This review looked at whether educating children and young people about asthma, either individually or in groups, is effective in improving their health. The review found that educating children and young people about asthma does improve their knowledge and understanding of the condition but does not seem to have an impact on their symptoms or lung function. However, it does reduce the number of emergency department presentations and hospital admissions for asthma.

We included 130 studies with 8341 participants. The types of surgery included ear, nose, and throat surgery; wisdom tooth extraction; open-chest surgery, spinal fusion surgery, or removal of a herniated disc; hip or knee joint replacement, or repair of a torn ligament; mastectomy, or removal of haemorrhoids; abdominal surgery, prostate surgery, or thyroid surgery; planned caesarean section, or keyhole surgery. Racemic ketamine (a mixture of the two enantiomers S-ketamine and R-ketamine) was always given by injection into a vein, at doses between 0.25 mg and 5 µg/kg/minute. Ten studies used only S-ketamine and one only R-ketamine. The studies continued for 24 hours after surgery, or until the participants asked for more medication. Opioids are the current standard treatment for pain after surgery, but are highly addictive. We found that ketamine together with opioids reduced the amount of opioid needed after surgery by 8 mg (19% less) over 24 hours, and by 13 mg (19% less) over 48 hours (moderate-quality evidence). This finding held true for all types of surgery, and for people with high or low pain, and in studies where ketamine was given just before the start of surgery, or during surgery, or both. Ketamine also reduced pain, measured on a scale of 1 to 100, by 5 points over 24 hours (19% less) and by 5 points over 48 hours (22% less, high-quality evidence). It also reduced pain on movement by 6 points over 24 hours (14% less) and by 6 points over 48 hours (16% less, moderate- to low-quality evidence). These results held true for all types of surgery, and for people with high or low pain, and when ketamine was given just before the start of surgery, or during surgery, or both. The need for additional painkillers was reduced by 54 minutes on average (moderate-quality evidence). The area of increased sensitivity to pain was reduced by 7 square centimetres (very low-quality evidence). Side effects of ketamine and the control treatment were similar, except that there were slightly fewer episodes of nausea and vomiting with ketamine (4% less, high-quality evidence). The quality of the evidence was moderate for most results. This means further research is very unlikely to change our estimates. However, we downgraded it to very low-quality for the result about the area of increased sensitivity to pain because of small numbers of participants, and low-quality evidence because the number of participants was too small for us to be sure whether small-study effects would affect the result.

This review found five randomised controlled trials of the Combination B vaccine, which includes the MSP2 protein from the 3D7 parasite strain and the RESA protein. A total of 217 volunteers were vaccinated. The vaccine was safe at the doses used, and caused short-term side effects like fever and dizziness. It did not affect the number of episodes of clinical malaria, but reduced the level of parasites in the blood of those who had not been previously treated for malaria. The vaccine worked better against one type of P. falciparum than another. This means that further research should focus on developing vaccines that include different parasite proteins. Vaccines that are effective against blood-stage parasites are also important because it has proven difficult to develop vaccines that work against the mosquito stage of the parasite.

The review authors searched the scientific literature up to January 2012 and identified four relevant studies, with 125 participants. Three studies involved the use of pulsed electromagnetic fields and one capacitive coupled electric field for the treatment of non-united tibial fractures. All four studies were well designed randomized controlled trials. However, each study had limitations in terms of design or implementation. The review authors concluded that while the available evidence suggested that electromagnetic field stimulation may be effective in the treatment of delayed union and non-union of long bone fractures, it is inconclusive and insufficient to inform current practice. More research is needed before its use can be recommended routinely in clinical practice.

We identified 12 randomised clinical trials with a total of 1831 participants in this review. The average age of participants in the trials ranged between 48 years and 63 years. The proportion of females ranged between 55.0% and 79.0%. Eight trials included only patients undergoing laparoscopic cholecystectomy and one trial included only patients undergoing laparoscopic cholecystectomy for acute cholecystitis. One trial included patients undergoing both open and laparoscopic cholecystectomy and one trial did not provide this information. There was no significant difference between the drain group and the 'no drain' group in short-term mortality, the percentage of patients who developed major complications, the number of patients who developed major complications, the quality of life, the length of hospital stay and the time taken to return to normal activity and to return to work. The operating time was significantly longer in the drain group than in the 'no drain' group by an average of 5 minutes. The cost of the procedure was not reported in any trial. We concluded that there is no evidence to support the routine use of drain after laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Further well designed randomised clinical trials are necessary. Only two trials including 199 participants were at a low risk of bias. The majority of the trials had either high or unclear risk of bias and the overall quality of evidence was very low.

This systematic review examined the effects of Chinese herbal medicines for diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Forty-nine randomised trials involving 3639 participants were included. The trials were of low quality and reported positive results of limited clinical importance. This review found insufficient evidence to support the use of Chinese herbal medicines for the treatment of diabetic neuropathy. Adverse events were poorly reported. Further high-quality research is required before any confident conclusions can be reached about the safety and effectiveness of Chinese herbal medicines for the treatment of diabetic neuropathy.

This review compared the effectiveness of IAS to CAS for treating prostate cancer and found limited information. Five studies with 1382 patients were included in this review. The studies were not long enough nor large enough to provide information on how these treatments affect overall survival or the spread of the cancer. However, they do provide some information on treatment side effects. The evidence is current to March 2011. Three trials reported on the effect of IAS compared to CAS for maintaining sexual function. Overall, IAS was as effective as CAS for maintaining sexual function, but there was a lower rate of episodes of impotence during the period of treatment with IAS. One trial found no difference between the treatments in terms of quality of life, pain, and bowel function. Two trials reported on the effect of IAS compared to CAS on the ability of the cancer to return. Neither study showed any difference between the treatments. One trial found that there was a lower rate of recurrence of cancer on imaging studies after IAS treatment than after CAS treatment. We could not find any reliable information about whether IAS is better or worse than CAS at extending a person's lifespan or delaying the spread of prostate cancer.

We did computer searches for randomized controlled trials looking at the effectiveness and safety of inserting an IUD during an abortion medical procedure done with drugs or a vacuum device. We looked at whether the IUD was still in place six months later. We wrote to researchers to find more studies we might have missed. The IUDs studied were types with the hormone levonorgestrel and those with no hormones. We also looked at whether the IUD caused problems and whether it worked to prevent pregnancy. We found 12 studies with 7,119 women. The overall quality of the studies was acceptable but not high. More women had their IUD taken out within the first three months if they had it put in straight away after an abortion compared to having it put in later. This difference could be due to chance. However, by six months, more women were still using their IUD if it had been inserted straight away. Inserting the IUD right after an abortion is safe and easy to do. It does not cause more bleeding or pain than putting it in at a later time. The IUD stayed in more often if it was put in straight away.

The aim of this review was to find out whether meditation could be beneficial for patients with blood cancers. We searched scientific databases such as Cochrane Haematology's specialised register, MEDLINE, EMBASE, PsycINFO and The Cochrane Library. We looked for randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) of meditation practices among adults (aged 18 years or older) with any type of blood cancer. We found only one small trial, which reported three measures of quality of life, depression and fatigue. The trial involved 90 people who had recently been admitted to hospital with acute leukaemia. The intervention group was divided into two subgroups, each receiving a different kind of meditation. The active control group received their usual care as well as a relaxation technique. The other group received their usual care only. The trial found that meditation might increase QoL scores; decrease depression rates; and it remains unclear whether meditation has an effect on fatigue, anxiety, quality of sleep and adverse events. However, as the trial authors did not report the characteristics of the people in the trial, nor the full results, it is difficult to assess the quality of the trial and the reliability of its findings. Therefore, we have rated the quality of the evidence as very low. Because of the lack of evidence, we cannot recommend meditation for patients with haematological malignancies. Future, larger, well-designed studies are needed to find out if meditation is beneficial for patients with blood cancers.

The evidence is current to August 2015. We found three randomised controlled trials that studied the effect of varicella vaccination in people who came into contact with someone with chickenpox. All three studies involved children (aged one month to 12 years) who were contacts of someone with varicella. The vaccine was administered within three days of exposure in all trials. The trials varied in other aspects: different vaccine products were assessed, the duration of follow-up was different, and the outcomes measured were not identical. As such, we could not combine the results of these trials. Overall, 13 out of 56 vaccine recipients (23%) developed varicella compared with 42 out of 54 placebo (or no vaccine) recipients (78%). Most of those who got chickenpox had a mild illness with fewer than 50 skin lesions. None of the trials reported on adverse events following immunisation. Given the lack of data for older children and adults, we cannot recommend the use of varicella vaccine for people of any age as PEP. For children between one month and 18 years who come into contact with someone with chickenpox, the administration of varicella vaccine within three days may reduce the chance of catching chickenpox, but more information is needed on side effects following vaccination. Two of the three included studies were of low methodological quality. The overall quality of the evidence was very low. Larger, well-designed trials are necessary to assess the benefits and harms of the varicella vaccine.

We identified 12 studies with 4704 participants in this review. Eleven studies compared different antibiotics against each other and one study compared an antibiotic regimen with placebo (a substance containing no medication). The quality of the trials was generally poor. It does not appear that MRSA was tested or eradicated before surgery in the majority of people undergoing surgery. There were no clear differences between the group of people who were given antibiotics and those who were not in terms of the number of people who died or had complications related to their surgery. There were no clear differences in the rate of MRSA infections in the comparisons of one antibiotic against another. However, the trial comparing an antibiotic regimen with placebo showed that the proportion of people who developed MRSA infections (and all infections) was significantly lower in the group that received antibiotics. This could be because giving antibiotics decreased the overall level of infection in the wound so that there were fewer bacteria present to cause subsequent MRSA infection. There was no clear difference in the rate of all infections (including MRSA infections) when different antibiotics were compared against each other but the trial comparing an antibiotic regimen with placebo showed that the proportion of people who developed infections was significantly lower in the group that received antibiotics. This suggests that the way that the trial was conducted may have decreased the likelihood of people developing infections. We do not think that the inclusion of people with cancer affected our results as only one of the trials mentioned that MRSA was present in the nose of all participants before they underwent surgery, although people with cancer may be more likely to carry MRSA in their noses. We do not think that the fact that half the people in the trial that compared an antibiotic regimen with placebo were given a second antibiotic affected our results as this antibiotic would not have contributed to the development of MRSA infections.

The review authors searched for evidence on 30 November 2014 and identified two randomised controlled trials (116 women) comparing planned home versus hospital management for women with preterm premature rupture of the membranes. The number of women included in each trial was too small to allow us to make any clear recommendations about the review's outcomes. One study was funded by the National Institute of Health Research (UK) and the other study was funded by the University of Sydney (Australia). In one trial perinatal deaths were reported but we could not be sure if they were different between the two groups. There was no clear difference between groups for serious problems experienced by the newborn including infection, gestational age at delivery and birthweight, and admission to neonatal intensive care. There was no information on serious problems experienced by mothers. Women who were cared for at home were more likely to have a normal vaginal birth. They spent approximately 10 days less time in hospital, were more satisfied with their care and the costs of their care were lower. However these findings need to be considered cautiously as the number of women in the included trials was too small to identify clearly significant differences between the home and hospital groups for many of the outcomes. Future trials with larger numbers of participants may be able to provide clearer guidance on the best place for women to be cared for after the waters have broken before 37 weeks' gestation.

This review examined the methods used to close the skin after a caesarean section and found that there are few studies comparing different techniques. The two most common methods are using either stitches or staples to close the skin. Staples were associated with similar outcomes in terms of wound infection, pain and cosmetic results when compared with using stitches, which are made of either synthetic or natural materials. However, if staples are removed on day three, the skin may separate, requiring the development of another wound. This problem was more likely with staples than with dissolving stitches.

This review aimed to find out if drinking green or black tea regularly reduced the risk of developing CVD. We searched scientific databases for randomised controlled trials (clinical trials where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) looking at the effects of green or black tea on the risk of developing CVD. We did not include non-randomised studies (where participants could choose which group they were in) or quasi-randomised studies (where participants were allocated to a group by a method that is not truly random such as date of birth or the last digit of their postcode). We found 11 randomised controlled trials with 821 participants. Two trials are awaiting classification (because we do not have enough information about them yet) and one trial is still underway. Seven of the trials looked at the effects of green tea and four at the effects of black tea. The trials were between two and 24 weeks long. Where reported, the age of the participants ranged from 19 to 87 years, and three trials included ethnic minorities. The amount of tea consumed in the trials varied. For green tea this ranged from 3 to 10 cups per day and for black tea from 2 to 5 cups per day. The strength of the tea also varied. For green tea this ranged from 250 mg of catechins per cup to unknown levels and for black tea from 20 mg of flavonoids per cup to unknown levels. Very few trials assessed adverse events, and those that did included a diagnosis of prostate cancer, hospitalisation for influenza, appendicitis and retinal detachment but these are unlikely to be caused by the tea. No trials reported any cardiovascular events. Drinking green or black tea appears to have a beneficial effect on blood lipids and blood pressure, both known risk factors for CVD. However, these findings should be treated with some caution as the number of trials in the analyses was small. The evidence is current to September 2013.

The aim of this review was to assess the effectiveness of azathioprine as a'steroid-sparing' agent in the treatment of patients with chronic asthma. Two small studies were included in the review. The results of these two studies showed no significant differences between the group of people who received azathioprine and the control group in the outcomes measured (lung function tests and oxygenation). Also, no significant difference was found in the number of exacerbations (flare-ups) during the trial period. However, due to the small numbers of people in the studies and some weaknesses in the way they were carried out, it is not possible to draw firm conclusions from the results. A search for studies carried out in August 2010 did not identify any additional studies for inclusion in the review. This review shows that there is currently not enough evidence to support the use of azathioprine as a steroid-sparing agent in the treatment of chronic asthma. More research is needed.

We included 56 studies in this review update, which examined the effect of exercise on fatigue in people with cancer compared to usual care (treatment as usual, standard treatment, daily activities of living etc). We found evidence to show that exercise is beneficial in reducing cancer-related fatigue in people with breast and prostate cancer and during and after cancer therapy. Exercise was defined as any physical activity, including walking, cycling, running, swimming, yoga, tai chi, dance, etc. The quality of the evidence was moderate. More research is needed to find out which type of exercise is best (aerobic, resistance, alternative) and how it should be given (how often, how long, etc.).

The evidence is current to January 2016. We found five randomised controlled trials with 3427 patients with classical Hodgkin's lymphoma aged between 16 and 65 years of age. The trials were published between 1998 and 2011 and were conducted in Austria, Belgium, France, Germany, Italy, Norway, Sweden and the UK. This update of a previous Cochrane Review includes the results from one new trial that has been added to the three trials included in the last version of the review. The new trial confirms that the so-called 'BEACOPP escalated' regimen cures more patients than the ABVD regimen, but at the cost of more toxicity. For instance, the chances that a person develops a second cancer is slightly increased, but the trial duration was too short to measure differences in second cancers that are expected after 15 years or more since treatment. Similarly, the chance of becoming infertile seems to be slightly increased, but the number of females in the trials was too small to allow a reliable comparison. Also, the impact of the treatment on quality of life was not sufficiently assessed. Overall, we conclude that for adult patients with early favourable and advanced-stage Hodgkin's lymphoma, the BEACOPP regimen leads to better survival than the ABVD regimen, but with more side effects. However, the results should be interpreted with care because of the relatively small number of patients included in the analyses and the fact that half of the patients were older than 40 years of age. Future studies should focus on the consequences of the use of BEACOPP for patients with a history of prior chemotherapy or with a strong desire to have children.

We searched for randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) looking at the effect of interventions aiming to improve work participation in adults with cancer. We found 15 randomised controlled trials including 1835 patients with cancer. The interventions used were divided into four categories: (1) psycho-educational interventions (interventions that aim at the mind of the patient, e.g. providing information and advice); (2) physical interventions (interventions that aim at the body of the patient, e.g. giving physical exercise); (3) medical interventions (interventions that aim at the tumour and the cancer causing disease); (4) multidisciplinary interventions (interventions that aim at the person as a whole; e.g. a combination of physical, psycho-educational and/or vocational components). Limitations of this review include the relatively small number of studies found, the small number of participants in many of the studies, the fact that many studies were poorly reported, and that all studies were performed in high-income countries. We found that psycho-educational interventions and physical interventions did not improve return to work in patients with cancer. Further research is needed to see if these types of interventions are ineffective because they do not meet the needs of patients with cancer. Medical interventions (e.g. less radical forms of surgery) and multidisciplinary interventions (e.g. including vocational counselling, patient education, patient counselling, biofeedback-assisted behavioral training and/or physical exercises) improved return to work in patients with cancer. However, it is unclear what elements of such an intervention are necessary to improve return to work. Future research should focus on the evaluation of the cost-effectiveness of interventions to improve work participation in patients with cancer.

We searched for evidence on 5 November 2015 and identified no new trials. We included four randomised controlled studies involving over 13,000 women in a previous Cochrane review and for this update. These studies were carried out in the UK and Ireland. The funding sources were the hospitals where three of the studies took place and the Scottish Government for the fourth. In this review, the number of women requiring caesarian sections was higher in women who had continuous monitoring than for women with intermittent auscultation but there was not enough evidence to show a significant difference. There was no clear difference between groups in the number of women having an instrumental birth or in the numbers of babies that died. Women who had continuous monitoring were more likely to have their babies monitored continuously throughout labour and have fetal blood samples taken, although the reasons for this were not clear. There was no difference between groups in terms of brain injury due to lack of oxygen and seizures in the newborn period. However, the review could not provide sufficient evidence on the importance of these outcomes. The evidence does not support the use of continuous monitoring for all women admitted to hospital prior to the onset of active labour. It may increase the likelihood that a woman will require a caesarian section without providing any benefits. The quality of the evidence varied from moderate to very low. The latter was due to the small numbers of women recruited to the included studies and the wide range of results within studies. The four included studies were carried out in developed Western European countries. One further study is ongoing. The relevance of these findings to less developed countries will depend on how widely used continuous fetal monitoring is for high-risk women in these countries. Future studies evaluating the effects of the admission CTG should include women admitted with signs of labour and before a formal diagnosis of labour. This would include a cohort of women currently having admission CTGs and not included in current trials.

The culture medium is a liquid solution that supports the growth of embryos in the laboratory. Many different types of culture medium exist and it is not clear which one is most beneficial to the embryo. We included 32 randomised controlled trials in this review. However, each trial studied a different comparison and had different experimental design. Therefore, we were unable to combine the results. Only seven studies reported live birth or ongoing pregnancy and four of these found no difference between the media compared. One study suggested that Quinn's Advantage was associated with higher clinical pregnancy rates than G5, but the evidence was poor. Three studies reported multiple pregnancies and six studies reported miscarriage, but neither found any evidence of a difference between the culture media used. None of the studies reported on the health of the babies. The overall quality of the evidence was rated as very low for nearly all comparisons. The main limitations were imprecision and poor reporting of study methods. More research is needed.

In this review, the authors set out to find studies that had been conducted to evaluate the best way to tell women they have breast cancer but did not find any suitable studies. It is not clear why there have been no studies conducted in this area, but the authors suggest it may be unethical to conduct such a study. The authors go on to suggest that a review looking at the information given to women at the first visit, when they first seek help for symptoms, may be an alternative way to look at this question.

This review examined the effectiveness of psychological treatments used to treat non-cardiac chest pain. We found 17 studies with a total of 1006 patients who received either a psychological treatment or usual care. The studies lasted between 1 and 13 months. Psychological treatments were shown to reduce the frequency and severity of chest pain in the short term. Those treatments that involved changing a person's thoughts and behaviours were more effective than other types of psychological treatment. An additional study, included in this updated review, showed that hypnosis may also be effective. However, these results should be treated with caution because different studies measured slightly different outcomes and some were not reported clearly enough. Also, more people knew which group they were in. Future trials need to monitor outcomes for longer periods of time, ideally over several years. More research is also needed to find out how long the effects of psychological treatments last. It is also important to establish whether certain types of psychological treatment are better than others in the long term.

We included 30 randomised controlled trials with 4344 participants. The following antihistamines and mast cell stabilisers were evaluated in at least one trial: nedocromil sodium or sodium cromoglycate, olopatadine, ketotifen, azelastine, emedastine, levocabastine (or levocabastine), mequitazine, bepotastine besilate, antazoline and tetryzoline, and a combination of levocabastine and pemirolast potassium. The most common comparison was between azelastine and placebo (nine studies). All trials evaluated only short-term effects, with a range of treatment of one to eight weeks. There was some evidence to support the use of topical antihistamines and mast cell stabilisers for reducing symptoms and signs of seasonal allergic conjunctivitis when compared with placebo. There were no reported serious adverse events related to the use of topical antihistamine and mast cell stabilisers treatment. It seems that all reported topical antihistamines and mast cell stabilisers reduce symptoms and signs of seasonal allergic conjunctivitis when compared with placebo in the short term. However, there is no long-term data on their efficacy. Direct comparisons of different antihistamines and mast cell stabilisers need to be interpreted with caution. Overall, topical antihistamines and mast cell stabilisers appear to be safe and well tolerated. The quality of the studies and reporting was variable, but overall the risk of bias was low.

The aim of this review was to find every study that has evaluated a tobacco prevention programme specifically designed for Indigenous youth. Two studies were included in the review, both from the USA, both working with Native American communities and both targeting sixth-graders. Both studies compared a combination of approaches to a control group, although the content of the programmes differed somewhat. One study used a skills-building approach, teaching young people how to resist social pressures to use tobacco, say no to tobacco use and refuse gifts of cigarettes. The other study combined skills training with a community component, including a 'Tobacco-Free School Team' and a 'Youth Advocacy Board'. Both studies showed no difference in the number of smokers in the intervention groups compared to the control groups. In one study, fewer students in the skills-only group used smokeless tobacco than in the control group. However, in the one study where tobacco use was measured at a later time point (six months), there was no longer any difference between the intervention and control groups. Given the high rate of smoking in Indigenous youth, and the fact that smoking starts at an early age, there is a huge burden of disease due to tobacco use in these populations. More research is needed to find an effective way to prevent Indigenous youth from taking up tobacco.

We searched for evidence on 30 November 2016 and found only one randomised controlled trial (72 women with sickle cell anaemia (HbSS) that compared the effects of giving all women with sickle cell anaemia who were pregnant blood transfusions to prevent complications against a policy of giving blood transfusions only to those with complications. This trial did not show any difference in the number of deaths of the mothers or their babies, or in the number with breathing problems, heart failure or acute chest syndrome between the two groups. Giving blood transfusions to all women with sickle cell anaemia during pregnancy may reduce the number of pain crises experienced by the mother but the overall effect of this intervention has to be balanced against the harms caused by the blood transfusion, including allergic reactions and iron overload. Other important outcomes, including how long women stayed in hospital after the birth, whether they had heavy bleeding after the baby was born and whether their babies needed special care, were not reported in the trial. We do not have enough evidence from randomised trials to advise on the best time to give blood transfusions to women who have other types of sickle cell disease (HbSC and HbSβThal). The quality of the evidence on this topic is very low because the only included trial was small and had several weaknesses that could affect the reliability of its findings.

The evidence is current to September 2015. We included 67 randomised clinical trials involving 6197 people. Sixty-four trials provided data for one or more measures (outcomes). The main measure of interest was how many people died within 30 days after surgery (morbidity), but we also looked at percentage of blood transfused, amount of blood transfused, amount of blood lost during surgery, operating time, total hospital stay, and complications (harm) caused by the operation. We did not look at quality of life or the time taken to return to work. Most of the trials had less than 100 participants. All compared two methods of cutting the liver. The methods compared were: (1) radiofrequency dissecting sealer versus clamp-crush, (2) low central venous pressure (reduced blood flow to the liver) versus acute normovolemic haemodilution plus low central venous pressure (removal of blood before the operation and reduction in blood flow to the liver), (3) fibrin sealant versus no sealant, (4) oxidised cellulose (sealing agent) versus fibrin sealant, (5) linear stapling versus endostapling, (6) laser versus no laser, and (7) microwave versus no microwave. We found no evidence of differences between the methods in terms of death, percentage of people who received a blood transfusion, amount of blood transfused, amount of blood lost during surgery, operating time, and total hospital stay. The only difference that we found was that the proportion of people requiring a blood transfusion was higher with low central venous pressure than with acute normovolemic haemodilution plus low central venous pressure; blood transfusion quantity (red blood cells) was lower with fibrin sealant than control; blood transfusion quantity (fresh frozen plasma) was higher with oxidised cellulose than with fibrin sealant; and blood loss, total hospital stay, and operating time were lower with low central venous pressure than with control. Also, the use of a radiofrequency dissecting sealer resulted in more complications than the clamp-crush method. There was no evidence to suggest that using special equipment for liver resection decreased the number of deaths or the blood transfusion requirements. We have classified the quality of the evidence as low or very low because all the trials had problems with their design, the trials were small, and the range of possible values for the difference between the methods was wide. This means that we are uncertain about the results and further studies are required. Use of the radiofrequency dissecting sealer is not recommended outside clinical trials because it causes more harm without any evidence of benefit.

This systematic review of 23 randomised controlled trials, which included 724 people, found that there are few well-designed studies to support any particular form of treatment. There is some evidence from two studies that the use of oral retinoids alone, or in conjunction with light therapy, may be effective at improving PPP. There is also some evidence from three studies that using a combination of PUVA and retinoids is more effective than either treatment alone. Applying a mild steroid cream under a hydrocolloid dressing was also found to be effective in one study. Other treatments that show some promise include grenz ray therapy, tetracycline antibiotics and ciclosporin A. However, the evidence for these treatments is less clear. There is no evidence that supports the use of colchicine, topical PUVA or hydroxycarbamide.

We developed a comprehensive search strategy of all relevant scientific databases to identify randomized controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) published up to March 2014. We identified nine trials that compared the blood pressure-lowering ability of five different loop diuretics (furosemide, cicletanine, piretanide, indacrinone, and etozolin). A total of 460 people participated in these nine trials. The average study duration was eight weeks. This review found that loop diuretics lowered blood pressure by about 8/4 mmHg. Because the included studies were small and had several methodological flaws, we believe that this estimate may be higher than what one would expect from real-world studies. There was no difference in the blood pressure lowering ability among the five different loop diuretics studied. Side effects were poorly reported in these studies, making it impossible to assess the harms associated with this class of drugs. It is important to note that the studies were only six weeks long, which is not enough time to evaluate the side effects of diuretics.

We identified 58 randomised clinical trials involving 3746 people undergoing laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Of these, 48 trials with 2849 participants compared local anaesthetic instillation (1558 participants) with control (1291 participants) for one or more of the outcomes. The evidence is current to September 2015. Most participants in the trials were low anaesthetic risk people undergoing planned laparoscopic cholecystectomy. There were no deaths in either group in the eight trials reporting deaths. One participant had a major complication in the trials of 446 participants reporting complications. The remaining trials reported no complications. None of the trials reported quality of life, time to return to normal activity, or time to return to work. The difference in the percentage of people discharged as day surgery was imprecise and was compatible with benefit and no difference between the two groups. The difference in length of hospital stay was imprecise and was compatible with benefit and no difference between the two groups. The pain was significantly lower in the participants who received local anaesthetic than those who did not receive local anaesthetic at four to eight hours and at nine to 24 hours, although the size of the reduction in pain was unknown. The quality of the evidence was very low. There were several problems with the design of the trials. First, the decision whether a participant received local anaesthetic or not was determined by a method similar to coin tossing so the two groups were like-wise balanced and there was no bias. Second, the fact that the surgeon knew whether the participant received local anaesthetic or not may have affected the way the surgeon performed the operation. However, the choice of whether to perform a difficult dissection or not would not affect the amount of local anaesthetic given and vice versa. Third, the difference in the number of participants in the two groups in many trials was large, which usually means that any difference between the two groups would be detected if it really existed. However, we could not detect even a moderate difference between the two groups. Finally, the results were not consistent across all the trials. We are reasonably confident that local anaesthetic instillation does not cause important complications. We are less certain about its effect on pain because the trials were not designed to measure pain well. We do not know whether local anaesthetic instillation has an important effect on the pain because the reduction in pain was small. In summary, there is very low quality evidence that local anaesthetic administered in the abdominal cavity during laparoscopic cholecystectomy reduces pain in low anaesthetic risk people undergoing planned laparoscopic cholecystectomy, but the importance of this reduction in pain is unknown. Further trials are necessary. Such trials should include outcomes such as quality of life, time to return to normal activity, and time to return to work that are important for the patients.

In this review, we summarized and analysed the performance of all available RDTs for the detection of P. falciparum malaria. We found 74 randomised controlled trials that evaluated 36 different RDT products based on five different antigen detection methods. All of these tests were qualitative, i.e. they produced a positive or negative result without indicating the level of the malaria infection. Qualitative RDTs are sufficient for the diagnosis of uncomplicated malaria because the threshold for treatment is set at any level of parasite DNA. However, it is important to know how well these tests perform because a false negative result might lead to a person not receiving antimalarial treatment, while a false positive result might lead to an unnecessary treatment with antimalarials and possibly serious side effects. The five different types of RDTs we evaluated differ in what they detect. Types 1 to 3 tests detect antibodies against the HRP-2 antigen from P. falciparum, either alone or combined with antibodies against other Plasmodium species. Types 4 and 5 tests detect antibodies against the pLDH enzyme from P. falciparum, again either alone or combined with antibodies against other Plasmodium species. We found that all types of RDTs are accurate enough to replace or complement microscopy for the diagnosis of uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria. There were large variations between the studies, though, which makes it difficult to predict how well individual tests will perform. This variation could be due to many factors, including the type of RDT used, the brand, the way the test was performed, the type of patients included in the study, etc. Also, the quality of the microscopy used as a reference was often unclear. Antibody-based RDTs targeting the HRP-2 antigen (Types 1 to 3) tend to be less specific than antibody-based RDTs targeting the pLDH antigen (Types 4 and 5), but the difference is small. Moreover, since the HRP-2 antigen persists in the blood for a long time, even after successful treatment, it is not useful for checking whether a patient has been cured by treatment.

We searched for studies up to September 2018. We found five studies that investigated whether changing the length of an appointment affects any aspect of general practice. These studies were all from the UK and focused on face-to-face appointments. The main things we looked at were: how long the consultation between a patient and doctor lasted; the number of times patients were referred to other services; the number of tests or further treatments prescribed; and how satisfied patients were with their consultation. We found no clear evidence that altering the length of an appointment changes any of these things. However, we are very unsure about this finding because the quality of the studies was generally poor. More research is needed before we can be certain. Future studies should look at the impact on patient health and the NHS, and involve a larger number of people.

This review assessed the effects of reducing the dose of stavudine in people on antiretroviral therapy. Three eligible studies were included in this review. These studies were published between 2005 and 2008 and involved 157 adults who had been on antiretroviral drugs for at least 12 months and had undetectable viral loads. Two studies compared a higher dose of stavudine (200mg twice per day) to a lower dose (150mg twice per day) and one study compared a higher dose (240mg twice per day) to a lower dose (120mg twice per day). No differences were detected in the proportion of people with an undetectable viral load over 24 weeks. Adverse effects were similar except for a trend towards increased lactate levels in the higher dose group in one study. Bone mineral density, limb fat and triglycerides were reduced in the higher dose groups in two studies. Reductions in bone mineral density were statistically significant but are unknown whether this has clinical significance. No serious adverse events required cessation of therapy. Individual results were imprecise and have not identified a clear advantage in viral suppression or tolerability between low and high dose stavudine. Furthermore, all the participants were already on treatment and virally suppressed when they entered the studies so the results can not be generalized to new users of stavudine, particularly in developing countries where stavudine is still widely used. Lowering the dose of stavudine may be worthwhile studying further in people starting antiretroviral therapy in developing countries as the phase out of stavudine that is recommended by the World Health Organization may not be immediately practical every where.

We searched the literature up to April 2016 and found 10 randomized controlled trials (RCTs) with 1658 participants. The RCTs compared the use of recruitment manoeuvres during the first 36 hours of mechanical ventilation in adults with ARDS. The mean age of participants ranged from 54 years to 69 years. The proportion of male participants ranged from 59% to 79%. The duration of ventilation in the included trials ranged from six days to 29 days. Six out of 10 studies reported funding by national agencies or charitable organizations. The application of recruitment manoeuvres resulted in a lower chance of dying within the first 24 hours of receiving mechanical ventilation, however there were no improvements in the chances of survival at 28 days or discharge from hospital. The application of recruitment manoeuvres did not result in an increased risk of complications related to high airway pressure. However, the results should be interpreted cautiously, since six out of 10 studies used the recruitment manoeuvre as part of a more comprehensive ventilation strategy, which also included changes in the level of pressure applied to the lungs at the end of expiration, the mode of ventilation and the volume of air pumped into the lungs with each breath. This makes it difficult to attribute the observed effects solely to the recruitment manoeuvre. Overall, we considered the quality of the evidence to be low.

This review examined the effectiveness of psychological therapies in the management of chronic asthma in adults. The majority of the studies included in the review had methodological flaws or were very small in terms of the number of people who participated. This makes it difficult to reach any definite conclusions about the role of psychological treatments in asthma. Some overall results were combined from individual studies. Relaxation therapy was associated with a reduction in the use of rescue medication "as needed" in two studies, although there was no significant improvement in lung function. However, these results need to be treated with caution as they were based on only 47 patients. Biofeedback therapy improved peak expiratory flow in two studies, also with small numbers of participants. Cognitive Behaviour Therapy (CBT) improved quality of life according to one study, also with small numbers of participants. Overall, there was no significant effect on anxiety or depressive symptoms. Further research is needed to assess the efficacy of these therapies before their use can be recommended.

The evidence is current to September 2016. This review included 35 randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups), with 6785 participants. However, not all trials addressed the comparisons of interest, so the number of participants in the analysis for each outcome is lower than the total number of participants. Antidepressants and benzodiazepines have similar effect sizes (i.e. the amount of improvement in anxiety symptoms) but this finding is based on a small number of studies with few participants. There is no evidence of difference in the number of patients showing clinical improvement, while there is low-quality evidence that suggests that fewer people taking benzodiazepines withdraw from the treatment they are assigned to compared to those taking antidepressants. Based on the small number of studies included in the analysis, we cannot be sure whether this difference is due to chance or not. More people taking tricyclic antidepressants experience side effects than those taking SSRIs, although both groups experience side effects equally often when taken for a short period of time. It is important to note that the quality of the evidence is low/very low, because the number of people in the analysis for each outcome is small, and the way the trials were designed and reported makes it difficult to draw firm conclusions. The quality of the evidence from the studies was low/very low, making it difficult to draw firm conclusions. The small number of studies included in the analysis for each outcome limits the strength of the results. The fact that for most of the studies the investigators did not report how they allocated participants to the treatment groups makes it difficult to be certain that the treatments were fairly compared. Finally, the lack of information on the methods used by the researchers to conduct the trial makes it difficult to assess the possibility of bias.

This review aimed to determine the best way to manage this problem using either surgery or stents made of metal or plastic. The review found that for patients with short-term survival, there is evidence that plastic stents are associated with a reduced risk of immediate complications, but have a higher risk of bile duct blockage before they die. For patients with longer predicted survival, endoscopic stenting with metal stents results in better quality of life and lower cost. Further trials are required to find the best type of stent to use.

The evidence is current to September 2016. We included five studies with a total of 444 participants. Four studies compared ultrasound with palpation, and one compared ultrasound with Doppler auditory assistance. All studies included only children aged one month to 12 years. Ultrasound guidance was more successful in achieving first-needle placement (four studies, 404 children, moderate-quality evidence), with fewer complications, such as haematomas (blood collects) at the site of insertion (two studies, 222 children, moderate-quality evidence), when compared with other types of guidance. Our results suggest, but do not confirm, that the advantage of ultrasound guidance for first-time success rate may be greater for infants and small children, in whom the anatomy makes it more difficult to find the artery, and that the advantage may be greater for experienced users of ultrasound. Our results also suggest, but do not confirm, that the advantage of ultrasound guidance for first-time success rate may be greater for experienced users of ultrasound. No studies reported on ischaemic damage (damage to the artery from the needle). The quality of the evidence was moderate because of imprecision, low number of events and small study groups.

We searched for studies that evaluated the effect of restricting the content or quantity of alcohol advertising on the amount of alcohol people drink. We found four suitable studies that met our inclusion criteria, including one randomised controlled trial (studies in which participants are assigned to treatment groups using a random method) and three interrupted-time-series (ITS) studies (where data are collected at regular intervals before and after an intervention). The randomised controlled trial, conducted in the Netherlands in 2009, showed that young men who were shown a movie with low-alcohol content drank less than those shown a movie with high-alcohol content (equivalent to about half a drink less). The ITS studies evaluated the introduction of a new law or policy to restrict the content or quantity of alcohol advertising. One study, also from the Netherlands, found that when advertisers were not allowed to show people drinking alcohol, there was no change in the amount people drank. Another study from Canada, which evaluated the introduction of a ban on all alcohol advertising, showed that the volume of all types of alcohol sold increased slightly after the ban was introduced. The third Canadian study evaluated the lifting of a ban on spirits advertising but not on beer and wine advertising. It showed that the volume of spirits sold decreased and the volume of beer and wine sold increased after the ban was lifted. None of these studies looked at the impact of policies on problem drinking, driving under the influence of alcohol, or whether people experienced any negative effects as a result of the introduction of content or quantity restrictions. None of the studies were funded by the alcohol or advertising industries. Overall, there is a lack of robust evidence for or against recommending the implementation of alcohol advertising restrictions. Such restrictions should be implemented within a high-quality, well-monitored research programme to ensure long-term evaluation of all relevant outcomes in order to build the evidence base. The quality of the evidence was rated as very low because of methodological problems in the studies and imprecise results, making it difficult to draw conclusions.

The review authors found eight randomized controlled studies studying a total of 182 infants. These studies compared medium-chain triglyceride (MCT) versus long-chain triglyceride (LCT) formulas for feeding preterm or very low birth weight infants. Medium-chain triglycerides are a type of fat that are more easily absorbed than long-chain fats, and so might be beneficial for nutrition in preterm or VLBW infants who have immature digestive systems. However, the review of trials found no evidence of differences between MCT and LCT formulas on short-term growth, on the incidence of severe bowel problems (necrotizing enterocolitis), or on tolerance to the formula. These studies did not provide any information about longer-term growth or development. More studies are needed, with larger numbers of babies, to look at the effects of MCT versus LCT on growth and development during the first months of life.

We searched for evidence on 30 November 2016 and identified only one randomised controlled study, which involved 34 women. This study compared amnioinfusion through the cervix with no amnioinfusion. The fluid used for amnioinfusion was room temperature saline, started at 10 mL per minute for 60 minutes then 3 mL per minute until delivery. All women received an intrauterine pressure catheter, acetaminophen and antibiotics (ampicillin or, if they were receiving Group B beta streptococcal prophylaxis, penicillin and gentamycin). No women in the study had a known Group B beta streptococcus infection. The study could not be analysed in conjunction with any other studies because it was so small, and individual results varied considerably. Amnioinfusion through the cervix was not shown to reduce the chance of the mother developing an infection after birth (low-quality evidence), nor was it shown to reduce the chance of the newborn baby developing an infection (low-quality evidence). The study did not provide any information on the number of babies that died or that experienced harm (including problems with brain development). The study did show that amnioinfusion lowered the mothers' body temperature at the time of birth when compared with no amnioinfusion. However, this was not something that the study had planned to measure. It is likely that the fluid used to cool the amniotic fluid has a direct cooling effect on the baby. The study did not measure the women's views on the experience. The included study was very small and the way it was run meant that we could not be sure how reliable its findings are. More research is needed to find out whether giving fluid into the amniotic cavity is helpful in the management of chorioamnionitis, and whether it is safe for the mother and her baby. The fluid used should be examined separately from the method of infusion. Infusion through the abdomen (transabdominal amnioinfusion) has also been proposed, but we did not find any completed studies examining this approach. Future research should aim to answer all of the questions posed by this review.

We searched for all articles evaluating the accuracy of the Mini-Cog as a cognitive screening test administered in the community. We found three studies with a total of 1620 participants. The studies showed good sensitivity (ability to correctly identify those with dementia), but poor specificity (ability to correctly identify those without dementia). However, these results were not consistent across the studies. The studies were also generally of low methodological quality. It is therefore not possible to make any conclusions about the accuracy of the Mini-Cog as a cognitive screening test in the community setting based on the current evidence. More research is needed.

This review included 19 randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) comparing amphetamines with placebo (a pretend treatment) or another drug type in 2521 adults with ADHD. Most were middle-aged, Caucasian men with combined-type ADHD. The amphetamines examined were dexamphetamine (10.2 mg/d to 21.8 mg/d), lisdexamfetamine (30 mg/d to 70 mg/d), and mixed amphetamine salts (MAS; 12.5 mg/d to 80 mg/d). Most studies were conducted in the USA, and one was conducted in both the USA and Canada. Most studies were short term, with a mean length of 5.3 weeks. We found no studies that had low risk of bias, mainly because amphetamines have powerful subjective effects that may reveal the assigned treatment, but also because we noted some other biases. Sixteen studies were funded by the pharmaceutical industry, one was publicly funded, and two studies did not report their funding sources. Amphetamines were associated with a reduction in the severity of ADHD symptoms as assessed by clinicians and patients, but were not associated with improvement in other outcomes. We found that amphetamines were associated with a higher percentage of study withdrawals due to side effects. There was little evidence on the effectiveness of dexamphetamine, whereas there was limited evidence to suggest that lisdexamfetamine and MAS may reduce ADHD symptoms. Different doses of amphetamines did not appear to be associated with differences in efficacy, but we only included a small number of studies in these analyses. We found no evidence that amphetamines were better than other drugs in improving ADHD symptoms. The quality of the evidence was low or very low, meaning that we cannot be certain about the results of this review. This review was limited by the lack of long-term studies, by the exclusion of studies with important outcomes, and by the fact that most studies were only partially blinded (i.e. both participants and researchers knew what treatment they were receiving).

This review assessed the efficacy and safety of vitamin D in treating chronic pain. We searched for clinical trials in which vitamin D was used to treat any chronic painful condition. The studies could be randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) or quasi-randomised controlled trials (clinical studies in which people receive one treatment by choice and the comparison treatment by default). The study could compare vitamin D against any other treatment or use an active comparator such as placebo (a pretend treatment). The evidence is current to January 2015. We found 10 studies that met our inclusion criteria, with a total of 811 participants. The studies were very different in terms of the type of pain being treated, the dose of vitamin D, the length of the study, and the outcomes that were measured. This makes it difficult to compare the studies and means that we cannot draw firm conclusions about whether vitamin D is effective for any particular type of chronic pain. However, the individual studies available do provide some information about the possible role of vitamin D in the management of certain types of chronic pain. Two studies reported that patients who received vitamin D had better pain relief than those in the control group after four weeks of treatment. However, the other eight studies did not show significant benefit from vitamin D. Vitamin D was generally well tolerated and few adverse events or serious adverse events occurred. Further research is required before the role of vitamin D in the management of chronic pain can be established. The quality of the evidence regarding adverse events was good, but the quality of the rest of the evidence was low. This review has identified several ongoing studies that should increase the strength of the evidence in future updates.

In this review we have summarised the results from 41 studies, which involved over 200 practices and 48,000 patients. The studies were very different, in terms of the interventions used, the participants, the clinical settings and the outcomes measured. However, many of the studies did find that certain types of interventions can improve the care provided to people with diabetes. These include making sure that health professionals are well trained and equipped to look after patients with diabetes, and making arrangements so that patients can be reminded easily of their appointments for a check-up or a review. Patients can also be helped by being given more information about diabetes and its management, and by being supported in using that information. Nurses can play an important part in improving the care for patients with diabetes, for example by giving them advice or help with their medication.

We did computer searches for randomized controlled trials that compared these kinds of birth control. We found five trials that studied 1503 women. The studies compared pills versus a new skin patch, a vaginal ring, or an implant. They also looked at differences between an intrauterine system with less hormone versus one with more hormone and an intrauterine device. The studies were not very large. The groups were not very different in the main outcomes of pregnancy, continued use, and leaving the method early. The only difference noted was that more women in the pill group chose to leave their method for other reasons than women with the intrauterine system. This difference may not be important in clinical practice. The studies evaluating different doses of the intrauterine system did not show any difference in pregnancy rates over time. Intrauterine systems also seemed to keep in place as long as birth control pills. We could not tell how well they worked and if women kept them on from the information available. We cannot say if hormonal birth control is better than an intrauterine device for young women because we did not have enough data.

This review examines whether allergy shots (also known as immunotherapy) are effective in the treatment of asthma. This review includes 88 studies with over 6000 people. A significant reduction in asthma symptoms and medication use is seen with this treatment. It may also reduce the person's allergic reaction to the allergen. However, more research needs to be done to determine how safe the treatment is.

Results of this systematic review show that the use of lower tidal volumes reduces the risk of death in adults with acute lung injury or acute respiratory distress syndrome. Based on the findings of this review, the optimal level of pressure remains uncertain. The effect on long-term outcome is unknown.

The evidence is current to June 2014. We found 15 randomized controlled trials with 1833 participants. The quality of the evidence was low. Our main findings are as follows: (1) the intravenous technique resulted in earlier emergence from anaesthesia than the inhalation technique using isoflurane, but not with sevoflurane; (2) there was no difference in the risk of experiencing side effects, such as nausea and vomiting, between the two techniques; and (3) the brain was less tense after administration of propofol than of isoflurane, but there was no difference between propofol and sevoflurane. The review shows that use of an intravenous anaesthetic agent can be comparable with the inhalation technique using sevoflurane to provide early emergence from anaesthesia. However, the quality of the evidence was low because of small numbers of participants and poor study designs. More research is needed.

The four randomised controlled trials included in this review had a total of 15,936 high blood pressure patients who were randomly assigned to either blood pressure lowering or continuation of treatment for an average period of two years. The average age of the participants was 75.4 years. The blood pressure at the start of the trials was 171/86 mmHg. Blood pressure was reduced by an average of 10/5 mmHg in the treated group. The number of patients suffering from dementia at the start of the trial was similar in both groups and there was no difference in the number of patients who developed dementia during the trial. A measure of cognitive function called the Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) was used to assess patients at the start and end of the trial. There was no overall benefit of treatment on MMSE but there was a suggestion that patients given placebo did better than those receiving active medication. Due to the nature of the way these trials were conducted it was difficult to analyse the data and some potential biases have been identified which may affect the validity of the findings.

This review showed that using a catheter to place stents in arteries narrowed by in-stent restenosis does not appear to improve the outcome compared to balloon angioplasty alone when the lesion is relatively simple. However, in more complex lesions, both options have similar patency rates (the state of being open) and neither is strongly better than the other. Furthermore, the addition of stents to balloons do not increase the risk of adverse heart-related events, although they are more likely to cause other complications such as narrowing of the artery, rupture of the artery and temporary blockage of the artery.

The aim of this review was to determine whether nebulised recombinant human deoxyribonuclease (rhDNase) is an effective treatment for bronchiolitis in infants. The review found three small randomised controlled trials that involved a total of 333 participants. Overall, the trials showed no benefit from the use of nebulised rhDNase in terms of shorter hospital stay or improved clinical scores. However, nebulised rhDNase may be beneficial in infants with bronchiolitis and atelectasis (a complication of bronchiolitis where there is collapse of lung tissue). Further trials are needed to establish its role in such cases. No significant differences in adverse effects were noted between the treated and the control groups. These included temporary desaturation, temporary coughing, increased coughing, facial rash, hoarseness, shortness of breath and bad taste.

The present review compared the benefits and risks of the two techniques and included two studies with a total of 181 participants, 116 of whom underwent the percutaneous technique and 65 by a cut-down incision in the thigh. Both studies showed no difference between the two approaches in terms of death, aneurysm exclusion, major complications, wound infection, and long-term complications. One study showed a significant difference in operation time, with the percutaneous approach being faster than the cut-down technique. There were several limitations with this review including the small number of studies and participants, with limited events. Further research is needed to strengthen the evidence. One such study is currently underway. Overall, the evidence for most outcomes was of moderate quality.

We did computer searches for studies of birth control methods with spermicides compared to other types of birth control. We also wrote to researchers and manufacturers to find other trials. We included randomized trials in any language. We found only two trials that looked at the sponge versus the diaphragm. The sponge did not prevent pregnancy as well as the diaphragm. More women dropped out within a year because of side effects. Reactions like itching and rash were more common with the sponge. However, some women dropped out because of discomfort with the diaphragm. We did not find any trials comparing spermicidal foam, jelly, or suppository to other methods. Spermicides are less effective than the diaphragm for birth control. Women using spermicides may get an allergic reaction. More women using the sponge left it because of side effects.

This review looked at the effect of interventions aimed at preventing or reducing the severity of cognitive problems in adults who had received radiotherapy to the brain. We searched for randomised controlled trials (RCTs), which are studies in which participants are assigned to one of two or more treatment groups using a random method. We were interested in RCTs of interventions that had a psychological, cognitive, behavioural or neurological component, or were drug based. We found six studies that met our inclusion criteria. Three of these studies looked at whether an intervention could prevent cognitive problems and three looked at whether an intervention could improve (or 'ameliorate') existing cognitive problems. Interventions assessed included those drugs called cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine), stimulants (methylphenidate, d-threo-methylphenidate HCL), and modafinil, as well as a rehabilitation program. Only two of the three studies, which looked at the drug memantine and the stimulant methylphenidate, found that an intervention may help to prevent cognitive problems. Only one study looking at the drug donepezil, which was used to treat established cognitive problems, found that it improved cognition. A combination of two different drugs (methylphenidate and modafinil) also showed an improvement in cognition. However, this study was small. More research is needed to replicate these findings. A number of limitations were noted across the studies, including the fact that many participants dropped out of each study. This means that our confidence in the results is lower than we would like it to be. However, we do have some evidence that suggests that interventions, specifically the drugs memantine and donepezil, may be able to help prevent and treat cognitive problems in adults who receive radiotherapy to the brain.

The two studies included in this review looked at whether laser therapy could improve feeling in the lip and chin after damage to the lower jaw nerves during tooth extractions. One study found that there might be a small benefit to using laser therapy to improve feeling in the chin but not the lip. However, the studies did not report any other outcomes we thought were important. As there are no studies looking at other treatments for nerve damage caused by tooth extractions, we do not know what the best way of treating this problem is. Further studies are needed to look at the effects of different ways of treating nerve damage to the lower jaw. These studies should look at the effects of treatment on feeling, pain, speech, eating and quality of life. They should also report if any side effects have been seen. We judged the quality of the available evidence to be very low. This is because the studies were small and had design problems that meant their findings could not be relied upon. Also, the studies only looked at two outcomes, which were both related to feeling, rather than looking at a range of outcomes that are important to patients.

The review included two small randomised controlled trials with a total of 281 pregnancies and 282 babies. One trial involved 161 women with a history of one or more miscarriages; the other, involving 120 women, was in pregnant women who had undergone in-vitro fertilization. The first trial compared daily tablets containing oestrogen and progestogen versus placebo (dummy pills) started at 12 weeks' gestation. The second trial compared daily injection of progesterone starting from the time of embryo transfer during in-vitro fertilization versus no treatment. Neither trial found any difference between the intervention and control groups in the number of babies lost before birth (miscarriage) but both showed an increase in the risk of developing a cancer of the reproductive system in the mother taking hormone treatment. Further studies are needed as the current evidence base is too small.

This systematic review aimed to find out how well they worked when used by people with depression who were already receiving primary care treatment. Fourteen studies were found, comparing amitriptyline, desipramine, fluoxetine and paroxetine with placebo. In total there were 1983 patients in the trials, 1364 in the drug groups and 919 in the placebo groups. Based on these results we would expect that one in nine patients with mild or moderate depression will improve more with a tricyclic antidepressant than with placebo, and one in seven with a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor. We cannot tell whether either type of drug is better than the other. Side effects were more common with the drugs than with placebo but not unpleasantly severe. It should be noted that most of the studies were of short term duration and so we cannot say what the results would be with longer use of these drugs.

In February 2015 we performed searches to look for clinical trials where ibuprofen had been used to treat migraine attacks. We found nine studies, most of them comparing ibuprofen (at doses between 200 mg and 600 mg) with placebo. One study compared ibuprofen with another type of painkiller (rofecoxib 25 mg). Two studies tested different forms of ibuprofen, one a soluble form that could be taken quickly and another a slow-release form intended to provide longer action. The results showed that ibuprofen was better than placebo at relieving migraine headache pain, although it did so for only a minority of people. About 1 in 7 people taking ibuprofen 400 mg had freedom from pain at 2 hours, compared with 1 in 10 taking placebo. Pain was reduced from moderate or severe to no pain by 2 hours in 5 in 10 with ibuprofen 400 mg, compared with 2 in 10 with placebo. Pain was reduced from moderate or severe to no worse than mild pain by 2 hours in 8 in 10 with ibuprofen 400 mg, compared with 5 in 10 with placebo. By 24 hours, 4 in 10 people taking ibuprofen 400 mg had freedom from pain, compared with 1 in 5 with placebo. Adverse events occurred at similar rates with ibuprofen and placebo, and were usually of short duration and mild to moderate in severity. The amount of benefit with ibuprofen was slightly less than that obtained with rofecoxib 25 mg, which is itself a relatively weak member of the COX-2 inhibitor class. This means that ibuprofen is unlikely to be effective against migraine pain that is severe. The soluble form of ibuprofen appeared to act more quickly than the ordinary tablet form, but the difference was small.

The searches of the literature were up-to-date as of August 2015. We found 43 randomized controlled trials that enrolled 3497 people with dry eye. In general, the studies had inconsistent results and did not clearly show that one artificial tear was better or worse than another. However, we did find that 0.2% polyacrylic acid-based artificial tears were better at treating dry eye than 1.4% polyvinyl alcohol-based artificial tears. The other comparisons we evaluated had either contradictory results or found no difference between the artificial tears being compared. We judged the quality of the evidence to be low. Many of the studies had design problems that could have affected their results. Additionally, many studies did not report enough information about their methods and findings for us to evaluate them properly. Also, we found 18 studies that were listed in clinical trial registers but did not have any published results. These studies had 2079 participants, but we do not know what the studies showed. If these studies had positive results, it could increase the likelihood that the artificial tears studied were effective. If they had negative results, it could decrease our confidence in the efficacy of those artificial tears. We do not know which came true.

This review assessed the effects of second-line therapy for people failing on an initial first-line regimen. One randomised trial and two observational studies evaluated second-line therapies in patients who had failed a World Health Organization-recommended first-line regimen. The trial showed similar rates of viral suppression at six months in patients continuing on lamivudine and those who changed to a different medication. The two observational studies showed similar proportions of patients with viral suppression and time to viral suppression in patients on lamivudine or emtricitabine versus those not on these medications. There were no trials of second-line therapies in patients who had failed a first-line regimen that included a protease inhibitor. Second-line regimens containing a boosted protease inhibitor appear to have good short-term response in populations starting antiretroviral therapy in resource-limited settings. However, there are no studies directly comparing boosted protease inhibitors versus other classes of drugs as second-line therapy. Current recommendations for second-line therapy are therefore made on the basis of availability of drugs and other health-care considerations.

We included 133 studies involving 844,206 participants. Sixty-two studies reported on the predictive values of the Mallampati classification alone, 79 reported on the modified Mallampati classification, 34 reported on the mouth opening test, 30 reported on the upper lip bite test, 18 reported on sternomental distance, 52 reported on thyromental distance, and six reported on the Wilson risk score. The ability of the airway examination tests to identify difficult airway cases varied between studies. The summary sensitivity (the ability of the test to correctly classify an individual as having a difficult airway) of the upper lip bite test was the highest at 67%, which means that 1000 individuals need to be examined to identify 185 people with a difficult airway. The summary specificity (the ability of the test to correctly classify an individual as not having a difficult airway) of the Wilson risk score was the highest at 95%, which means that 19 out of 20 people with a normal airway will be correctly identified. However, the summary sensitivity of all tests was low, meaning that many people with a difficult airway will remain undetected. The results suggest that the bedside airway examination is not effective in screening for difficult airway management. Future research is needed to develop tests with high sensitivity.

The review found that several species of probiotics given as supplements to patients with acute diarrhoea are effective at reducing the duration of illness and decreasing the number of stools per day.
