Generally, rehydration solutions alone were used in these studies but in four studies where antidiarrhoeal drugs were used as comparators, the probiotics were still effective. The different studies used a variety of species of probiotics, often in combination, so further research is required to determine which are the most effective combinations.

The review includes one trial with a total of 52 people. The treatment group received standard care plus nidotherapy, and the control group received standard care only. The duration of the study was 18 months. The study looked at the effects of the intervention at two time points: up to six months, and between six and 12 months. At both time points, those who had received nidotherapy showed some improvement in their social functioning and engagement with services, and were less likely to leave the study early, when compared with the control group. However, these improvements were not large enough to be statistically significant. There were no differences between groups in terms of mental health, service use, and general satisfaction with care, and no deaths occurred within the study. More research is needed before any firm conclusions can be drawn about the efficacy of nidotherapy. Currently, it cannot be recommended as an add-on to standard care for schizophrenia. The quality of the evidence in this review is very low/low. This summary has been written by Ben Gray, Senior Peer Researcher, McPin Foundation. Email: [bg@mcpin.org](mailto:bg@mcpin.org).

We searched scientific databases for clinical trials comparing pregabalin with placebo (a pretend treatment) or another medicine in adults with moderate or severe fibromyalgia. In July 2015 we found eight clinical trials involving 3736 people. Studies lasted 12 to 26 weeks. Studies were well conducted and well described. The main measure of whether pregabalin reduced pain was how many people had their pain reduced by at least half. Pain was measured on a scale of 0 (no pain) to 10 (worst imaginable pain). Results were similar with pregabalin 300, 450, or 600 mg, so further comparison was not possible. About 1 in 9 people benefited from this level of pain reduction with pregabalin, compared with 1 in 10 with placebo. About 1 in 6 people benefited with a pain reduction of at least 30%, which is considered moderate benefit, compared with 1 in 6 with placebo. A smaller study compared taking pregabalin at night only versus taking it twice a day, and did not find any difference in effect. Another study design randomly allocated people to continue their successful dose of pregabalin (300 to 600 mg) or reduce it quickly to placebo over 13 or 26 weeks. This allowed us to look at the chance of maintaining benefit without withdrawing from treatment. About 4 in 10 people maintained benefit with pregabalin, compared with 1 in 10 with placebo. Side effects were more common with pregabalin than placebo, especially dizziness, sleepiness, weight gain, and water retention. Serious side effects were uncommon with all treatments. More people withdrew from pregabalin treatment than placebo treatment, mainly because of side effects. We rate the quality of the evidence as high, which means that further research is very unlikely to change our estimates of the effect. However, the studies were small, so we cannot be certain about the size of the average effect.

We searched for evidence on 30 June 2016 and found only one trial involving 135 women with mild pre-eclampsia at term. The trial compared magnesium sulphate with a placebo and was at a low risk of bias. The trial did not report any of our this review's primary outcomes. No significant difference was shown between magnesium sulphate and placebo in Apgar score less than seven at five minutes (low quality evidence) or gestational age at birth (very low quality evidence). Significantly more women given magnesium sulphate felt warm and flushed compared with placebo but the numbers experiencing side effects severe enough to cease treatment were similar between groups. There were no significant differences between groups in the rates of postpartum haemorrhage and caesarean section. We need more data from high-quality trials to assess the effectiveness and safety of magnesium sulphate for the prevention of brain injury in the term fetus. It is important that we have strategies in place to reduce side effects for women. Six studies have been added to this review as awaiting further assessment.

This review aimed to identify randomised controlled trials which investigated smoking cessation interventions designed specifically for Indigenous populations. Four studies were included in the review, with all four showing a positive trend towards quit rates being higher in the intervention groups. Overall, the findings of an increased number of people quitting in the intervention groups compared with the control groups suggests that mass media campaigns, or interventions that provide a package of support including medication and counselling, or both, may be effective ways of helping Indigenous smokers to quit. However, more research is needed to confirm these findings.

The evidence is current to September 2014. We found 13 randomized controlled trials with 5686 critical care patients. The studies were conducted between 1990 and 2012 and were from Europe, the US, Australia and Canada. The studies were of mixed methods with 60% using invasive measurements (e.g. catheters) and 40% using non-invasive measurements (e.g. pulse contour analysis). Eight studies involved patients who had just undergone high-risk surgery and five studies involved general intensive care patients. For the group of high-risk surgery patients, five studies assessed the effects of changes made to patient care based on the information obtained from the PAC and three studies did not. For the general intensive care patients, only one study assessed the effects of changes in therapy. The inclusion of all studies in the meta-analysis was possible because results were similar. The use of a PAC did not affect the number of deaths, length of stay in the ICU or hospital, or the cost of care for the patients. Based on an analysis of subgroups, we found that the inclusion of studies with only high-risk surgery patients also did not show an effect of PAC on mortality. However, we cannot draw any conclusions from this subgroup analysis as it was not specified in our original review protocol. The quality of the evidence for the outcomes of mortality and length of stay was high, but low for cost analysis.

This review found that venepuncture is less painful than heel lance for newborn babies with a greater effect on their crying time. This finding holds particularly true when a glucose or sugar water after taste solution is used before the blood draw. However, the overall quality of the six studies was variable and future research is needed to confirm these results.

The purpose of this review was to assess whether re-stitching (resuturing) wounds that have broken down after the birth compared to leaving them to heal naturally has a beneficial effect on the rate of wound healing, as well as the level of pain, the women's satisfaction with the cosmetic appearance of the wound, if they were able to breast feed exclusively and their mental health. The review includes two poor quality trials involving 52 women with broken down wounds. One trial did report on the proportion of wounds healed by four weeks but it did not clearly state how healing was measured. There was a suggestion that resuturing may improve healing, but the result was not clear (statistically significant). We do not know from this trial what methods were used to measure the wounds, so we cannot be certain about the findings. The same trial also reported on the number of women who had resumed having sex by two months and six months. At two months, significantly more women in the resutured group had resumed having sex but by six months there was no significant difference between the two groups. The other trial did not provide any information on the proportion of healed wounds. It did report on the levels of pain, the women's satisfaction with the way their wounds looked and the number of women who had resumed sex at two months and six months. Resuturing did not make a significant difference to the levels of pain, nor to the women's satisfaction with the appearance of their wounds. More women in the resutured group had resumed sex by two months, but by six months there was no significant difference between the two groups. Neither trial provided any information on the adverse events associated with resuturing, such as infection, increased pain etc. Overall, there is not enough information available to show whether resuturing broken down perineal wounds is better than leaving them to heal naturally. More research is needed.

The evidence is current to May 2016. We included 18 randomised controlled trials (RCTs), which involved 2521 participants and used 37 kinds of Chinese herbal medicines. All studies compared Chinese herbs with antiviral drugs or compared different antiviral drugs. Most herbs showed similar effects to antiviral drugs for preventing or treating flu. Few were shown to be superior to antiviral drugs. No serious adverse events were reported. The methodological quality of most included studies was poor. The small number of trials and the limited number of people in each trial mean that we cannot be certain about the conclusions. Well-designed RCTs are urgently needed.

The aim of this review was to determine the best way to unblock blocked HD catheters. We searched the literature up to April 2016 and included 8 studies with a total of 580 participants. The included studies compared: clot-dissolving drugs versus a placebo; clot-dissolving drugs versus a different drug; clot-dissolving drugs versus physical cleaning; physical cleaning versus exchanging one catheter for another; and exchanging one catheter for another versus exchanging one catheter for another with additional disruption to the surrounding blood clot. Overall, we found that all three treatments were equally effective at restoring the function of blocked catheters. However, the results showed that the functional period of the catheter was shorter after the use of clot-dissolving drugs. Also, the results showed that there was a higher risk of bleeding associated with the use of clot-dissolving drugs. We concluded that all three treatments were equally effective at restoring the function of blocked HD catheters and all three treatments had similar effects on the ability of the catheter to provide adequate dialysis. More research is needed to fully investigate the topic. The quality of the included studies was generally considered to be low due to aspects of their study designs, small number of patients enrolled, and potential industry influence.

This review compared the effectiveness of adding one or more drugs to the standard treatment regimen versus using the standard treatment alone. This review includes information from 17 trials involving 2674 women. Adding one or more drugs to the standard treatment regime shrunk more tumours than standard treatment alone but it did not extend the lives of women with metastatic breast cancer or delay progression of their cancer. Unfortunately, it also caused more side effects.

The authors searched the literature and identified 10 relevant studies. The majority of the studies were of poor methodological quality although the more recent studies appeared to be of higher quality. Overall the studies suggested that patients who are cared for in a NLU are less likely to be discharged to an institution and have a better functional status on discharge. However, they do not appear to have a shorter length of inpatient stay, are not less likely to be readmitted to hospital and do not have a lower risk of dying. This would appear to contradict the original hypothesis that a NLU would improve outcomes. However, the authors suggest that the increased time spent in the NLU may be necessary in order to prepare patients for discharge and that a NLU may only be of benefit if it is part of a comprehensive rehabilitation programme. More high quality research is needed.

This review includes 11 studies with a total of 414 participants. Two studies compared therapeutic ultrasound with placebo, two compared one ultrasound regimen with another, two compared ultrasound with another non-surgical intervention, and six compared ultrasound as part of a multi-component intervention with another non-surgical intervention (for example, exercises and splint). The evidence is current to July 2015. Overall, there is insufficient evidence that one therapeutic ultrasound regimen is more efficacious than another. Overall, there is insufficient evidence that therapeutic ultrasound is more efficacious than another non-surgical intervention for the treatment of CTS. Only two studies reported the primary outcome of interest, short-term overall improvement (any measure in which patients indicate the intensity of their complaints compared with baseline). One low quality trial with 68 participants found that when compared with placebo, therapeutic ultrasound may increase the chance of experiencing short-term overall improvement at the end of seven weeks treatment. Another low quality trial with 60 participants found that at three months post-treatment therapeutic ultrasound plus splint increased the chance of short-term overall improvement (patient satisfaction) when compared with splint alone, but decreased the chance of short-term overall improvement when compared with low-level laser therapy plus splint. Differences between groups receiving different frequencies and intensities of ultrasound, and between ultrasound as part of a multi-component intervention versus other non-surgical interventions, were generally small and not statistically significant for symptoms, function, and neurophysiologic parameters (parameters that show signs of nerve damage or dysfunction). No studies reported any adverse effects of therapeutic ultrasound, but this outcome was only measured in three studies. More adverse effect data are required before any firm conclusions on the safety of therapeutic ultrasound can be made. The quality of the evidence ranged from low to unclear. Some studies had serious flaws in their methods, including their selection of participants and their conduct of the experiments. Other studies did not have enough participants to detect an evident difference between groups. Future research needs to focus on improving the quality of studies to allow for the detection of a true effect of therapeutic ultrasound.

This review aimed to find out if there was evidence from randomised controlled trials for giving statins to people who have had a stroke or TIA. We found eight studies involving around 10,000 participants looking at five different types of drug. Overall, there was no significant effect on the chance of having another stroke but those who had a statin did have less chance of having a heart attack or dying as a result of a blood clot. However, it could not be determined when statin treatment should start - immediately after the stroke or sometime later. It is likely that more research will become available in the near future. In the meantime, current guidelines suggest that people who have had a stroke or TIA should be considered for statin treatment, especially if they also have heart disease.

The review authors searched for randomised controlled trials and quasi-randomised controlled trials, uncontrolled (non-evaluated) studies, time-series studies and long-term follow-up studies of mass media campaigns. They also contacted study authors to obtain additional information. The authors included 11 studies in the review. These studies differed in design, settings, duration and content and intensity of the interventions, as well as in the way they measured whether people had stopped smoking and how often they smoked. The evidence shows that programmes that include mass media campaigns can change adult smoking habits. One state-wide programme in the USA showed positive results up to eight years after the campaign. Another state-wide programme in California showed positive results during the period when there was enough money to carry it out properly and in the final evaluation. At least six of the nine community-based programmes included in the review led to some improvement in smoking habits and decrease in the number of smokers. The intensity and duration of the mass media campaigns may affect how effective they are, but it is difficult to tell because most studies did not go on long enough and other factors changed at the same time. This makes it hard to know what caused any changes. The studies looked at different groups of people, in different places, with different types of campaigns over different periods of time. More research is needed to find out what really works best.

This review includes 24 trials with a total of 2166 participants. The average age of participants was 54 years and most of them had cancer of only one organ (breast, prostate, colorectal, head and neck). The duration of yoga sessions ranged from 30 minutes to 1 hour, and the number of sessions per week ranged from one to three over a period of six weeks to 10 weeks. The length of follow-up varied between four weeks and 14 months. We analysed the effects of yoga using different comparison groups (no therapy, psychological support, educational intervention and other exercise) separately. When compared with no therapy, yoga improved health-related quality of life, reduced fatigue and sleep problems in the short term. Yoga had no positive effects on depression or anxiety in the short term and had no medium-term effects on health-related quality of life, fatigue or sleep problems. When compared with psychosocial/educational interventions, yoga reduced symptoms of depression, anxiety and fatigue in the short term, but had no positive effects on health-related quality of life or sleep problems. When compared with other exercise, yoga was as effective as other exercise interventions in improving health-related quality of life and reducing fatigue. Researchers reported no severe side effects. The quality of the evidence from these studies was low to very low. Therefore, we are uncertain about the strength of our findings. Further research is likely to change results.

The evidence is current to 24 May 2018. We included one study that compared early post-operative MRI with no early post-operative MRI among adults with primary brain cancer called glioblastoma (GBM: a type of astrocytoma). Most participants in the study had undergone the removal of as much of the tumour as was possible (maximal surgical debulking), followed by radiotherapy and temozolomide chemotherapy. The study showed that there may be little or no difference in the number of people alive at one year and two years between those who received early post-operative imaging and those who did not receive early post-operative imaging. Other outcomes were not reported. We found no studies reporting the effects of other imaging schedules. We found no studies evaluating the cost-effectiveness of the different imaging strategies. Currently, the effect of different imaging strategies on the prognosis of people with glioma remains unclear. Our findings suggest that early post-operative imaging among GBM patients who will receive combined chemoradiation treatment may make little or no difference to their overall survival, but these results need to be researched further, especially as early post-operative imaging also serves as a quality control measure that may lead to an early reoperation if residual tumour is identified. Mathematical modelling of a large glioma patient database could help to identify the optimal time of surveillance imaging for different types of glioma, and this could be done on an individual basis according to the growth rate of each tumour. In addition, paediatric glioma study designs could be used to inform future research on imaging strategies among adults with glioma. The certainty of the evidence was very low, which means that the true effect of early post-operative imaging may be substantially different from what this review suggests.

The aim of this review was to assess how well metiaprine works compared to another common antipsychotic drug, chlorpromazine, for people with schizophrenia. An electronic search for relevant trials was carried out in 2012. Only two trials were found which randomised 161 people with schizophrenia. The trials did not provide much evidence to show any differences between chlorpromazine and metiaprine. However, there were some major problems with the way the trials were designed and run so it is difficult to be sure about the size of the difference between these two drugs. Furthermore, for many years chlorpromazine has been an inexpensive and widely available treatment for schizophrenia, and metiaprine is no longer used as a first-line antipsychotic. Therefore, despite the lack of good quality information comparing these two drugs, there does not seem to be a need to know if metiaprine is better or worse than chlorpromazine. This plain language summary has been written by Ben Gray, Senior Peer Researcher, McPin Foundation: http://mcpin.org/.

The researchers found that several drugs were effective at preventing the remission from coming back. These include: antibiotics (e.g. Rifaximin), anti-inflammatory drugs (e.g. Mesalamine/Aminosalicylates) and immunosuppressives (e.g. Azathioprine/6-Mercaptopurine). Each of these medications had similar effectiveness in preventing the remission from coming back. However, each medication also had side effects that need to be considered. For example, the use of antibiotics were associated with an increased risk of serious side effects. Immunomodulators (e.g. Infliximab) also decreased the risk of the remission coming back, but they are very expensive and have serious side effects. Patients who suffer from Crohn's disease may benefit from discussing the different drug options with their doctor in order to determine which drug is best for them based on their age, severity of the disease, side effect concerns and cost.

We searched scientific databases and internet resources to identify randomised controlled trials (where participants are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) of psychosocial interventions to reduce alcohol consumption among people who use illicit drugs. We included seven studies involving 825 participants, with four studies comparing brief intervention to another intervention. One study compared a cognitive-behavioral coping skills training to a 12-step programme. Another study compared motivational interviewing to an educational intervention only, and another compared motivational interviewing to assessment only. Finally, we identified a study that compared motivational interviewing to treatment as usual. Four of the included studies were funded by government agencies and one by a charitable organisation. The interventions were: – cognitive-behavioral coping skills training (one study), which taught skills to cope with triggers to drink and to deal with cravings; – a 12-step programme (one study), which is a recovery programme based on 12 steps that people affected by drug or alcohol misuse are expected to follow; – a brief intervention (three studies), which consisted of advice about alcohol use and help to set limits on drinking; – motivational interviewing (five studies), which aims to motivate people to change their behaviour by making them more aware of the reasons why they want to change, and helping them to make decisions about reducing or stopping drinking; and – brief motivational intervention (one study), which aimed to motivate people to cut down their drinking in a single session. The included studies did not report on alcohol-related harm. At one year, people who received cognitive-behavioral coping skills training drank just the same amount as those who attended a 12-step programme. Of those who started treatment, similar numbers completed treatment in both groups. The quality of the evidence was very low. When we combined the results from three studies, we found that there was no difference in the amount people drank, or in the number of people who completed treatment, between those who had a brief intervention and those who received treatment as usual. The quality of the evidence was low. When we combined the results from three studies that compared motivational interviewing to treatment as usual or to an educational intervention, we found that there was no difference in the amount people drank, or in the number of people who completed treatment, between the groups. The quality of the evidence was low. When we looked at the results from a single study that compared a brief motivational intervention to assessment only, we found that more people reduced their drinking in the brief intervention group than in the group that received only assessment. However, there was no difference between the groups in the number of people who completed treatment. The quality of the evidence was moderate. Finally, when we looked at the results from a single study that compared two forms of motivational interviewing, we found that there was no difference in the amount people drank, or in the number of people who completed treatment, between the groups. The quality of the evidence was low. We found no clear differences between the effects of different types of psychosocial interventions to reduce alcohol consumption among people who use illicit drugs. However, the quality of the evidence was low to very low, and the studies were small, so we cannot be certain.

This updated review included 23 trials with a total of 1586 participants. Twenty-one trials included people with essential tremor; one with Parkinson's disease and one with tetanus. The mean age of participants was 46 years and 59% were female. The participants received quinine at doses between 200 mg and 500 mg daily, usually taken after meals to reduce gut side effects. Studies took place in Thailand, Indonesia, Malaysia, India, Pakistan, Kenya, Tanzania, and USA. The duration of treatment was between two weeks and six months. Only three trials followed up with patients after they stopped taking quinine. Overall the quality of the trials was variable. This review provides evidence that quinine reduces the number of cramps experienced and the severity of cramping, but does not change how often cramps last. The numbers of people experiencing side effects on quinine were similar to those taking placebo, except for mild gastrointestinal symptoms which were more common with quinine. One person had low blood platelets (thrombocytopaenia) with quinine. These findings were based on short term trials so we do not know if long term use has risks. Other drugs such as vitamin E, quinine-vitamin E combination and theophylline combination with quinine appeared to be just as good as quinine, but there is not enough evidence to be sure. There is insufficient information about the best dose and length of treatment with quinine. More research is needed to find out how long the effects of quinine last for after stopping the medicine and whether quinine causes any long term harm. Trials should also test other medicines, such as vitamin E, quinine-vitamin E combination, and quinine-theophylline mixture against quinine. Trials of non-drug treatments like oral dantrolene and intravenous magnesium should also be conducted.

The review authors searched the medical literature up to August 2015. Seven randomized clinical trials (406 participants) met our inclusion criteria. Three included studies involved women who had leg cramps during pregnancy and four involved people who had cramps not related to any medication or medical condition (idiopathic cramps). Participants took either magnesium by mouth or a placebo, or no treatment, for 2 to 4 weeks. One small study showed some benefit of magnesium for frequency, severity and duration of idiopathic cramps while another small study showed no benefit. A third study found no benefit for any measure. The studies reporting on minor adverse events indicated that these occurred at similar rates with magnesium and placebo. There were no serious adverse event reports. None of the studies evaluated exercise-associated muscle cramps or muscle cramps associated with a medical condition such as an electrolyte imbalance. The review concluded that oral magnesium may or may not be effective in treating muscle cramps. However, there was no consistent evidence of benefit. Further research is required in non-pregnant adults and pregnant women with rest cramps.

The review authors found one study that recruited 14 people with SMA type 3 aged between 10 and 48 years. The study compared a home-based combined strength and cardio fitness training program with usual care over a period of six months. The training program involved both strength training, in which the participant lifted light weights 15 times per session, three sessions a day, and cardio exercise, in which the participant cycled slowly for 20 minutes, three times a day. However, half of the participants did not complete the cycling part of the program. The study measured the effect of the training on the ability to walk farthest in six minutes, cardiopulmonary fitness, muscle strength, self-reported fatigue and quality of life. The trial found no beneficial effects of the training on the ability to walk farthest in six minutes or cardiopulmonary fitness. There was a small improvement in muscle strength, but we do not know if this is meaningful for people with SMA type 3. The study reported no change in self-reported fatigue and quality of life, but the certainty in this result is very low. The study was well conducted, but it was small, which limits our ability to draw conclusions. The study used a protocol that was not fully described until after the study was completed, which also limits our ability to draw conclusions. The study did not assess the effect of the training on physical activity levels outside of the study. This evidence is up-to-date as of April 2018. We are very uncertain about the effects of exercise training in people with SMA type 3. Future studies should be designed to better reflect the needs of people with SMA type 3 and include larger numbers of participants.

We searched for all randomised controlled trials (RCTs) involving surgical and non-surgical treatments for these conditions. We found two RCTs, with a total of 149 participants. The first compared surgery with non-surgical interventions (conservative treatment and a soft collar) in people with neck pain and symptoms of nerve damage caused by a herniated disc or joint problem. The second compared surgery with non-surgical interventions (a soft collar and exercise) in people with neck pain and symptoms of spinal cord problems caused by a herniated disc, bone growths, or joint problems. Both trials were small and both had flaws that could affect the reliability of their results. The first trial showed that surgery relieved pain faster than non-surgical treatments, but the difference between groups was smaller than expected. The second trial did not show any difference between surgery and non-surgical treatments at three years. We cannot be confident about whether surgery is beneficial or not because of the small number of patients in the studies and the flawed methods used. However, the results of the two trials do suggest that surgery may relieve pain more quickly than non-surgical treatments.

The review found that telephone consultation with a nurse or doctor can reduce the number of visits to the surgery, but may not reduce the number of accidents and emergencies. There was not enough evidence about the effect on other aspects of healthcare, such as, repeat prescriptions, referrals to other services, hospital admissions, costs, patient satisfaction and potential errors.

We searched for studies that had been conducted in the past 25 years that included at least one group of people who were randomly allocated to either receive motivational interviewing or another type of intervention, or no intervention. The study had to include one or more measures of alcohol use or alcohol problems. We found 84 studies, involving 22,872 participants, that met the inclusion criteria. The studies included a variety of different types of participants, including young drivers, college students, employees, patients in general medical settings, and offenders. The length of the motivational interviewing intervention ranged from 10 to 60 minutes, with most lasting around 30 minutes. The present Cochrane Review found that Motivational Interviewing (MI) has a modest effect in reducing the amount and frequency of alcohol consumption, when assessed immediately after the intervention and in the short term. However, these effects reduce over time and are relatively small in size. The review also found that Motivational Interviewing has no substantial, meaningful effects in reducing alcohol misuse or alcohol-related problems, as measured by a range of questionnaires and interview-based assessments. Furthermore, the overall quality of the evidence supporting the findings was moderate or low. The studies did not report whether or not the interventions caused any harm. The review concludes that current evidence suggests that Motivational Interviewing does not have a large enough impact on alcohol use, misuse or alcohol-related problems to be of help in preventing them.

We found 29 studies including 2210 people with asthma. Most studies took place in a hospital or clinic. The length of the studies ranged from two weeks to six months. The techniques that we evaluated were either delivered face to face by a teacher or volunteer, through a machine such as a DVD or computer, or through a device attached to the inhaler that gave immediate feedback to the person using it. Volunteers were chosen at random to receive one treatment or another. We looked at the number of people who used their inhalers correctly and the number who had improvements in their asthma, as well as the number of emergency asthma attacks, days off school or work and potential side effects of inhalers. We found that most people found it easier to use their inhaler after an enhanced face-to-face training session, although it was harder to tell whether this benefit lasted long term. Using a machine to deliver the training had less benefit for adults but might help children to use their inhaler better. Giving people feedback on how they were using their inhaler also seemed to help them do it better. However, these different ways of teaching inhaler technique often were studied in different groups of people, making it difficult to combine the results. Another problem is that the way researchers checked inhaler use affected the results. This means that we are not sure whether these treatments really do improve inhaler technique or not. It is possible that future research will show that one or more of these treatments is effective. In the meantime, it seems sensible for doctors and nurses to continue to check their patients' inhaler technique and offer additional training if it is poor. We found no studies that looked at the cost of teaching people how to use their inhaler.

The review authors identified three trials which together included 226 participants. One trial involved people who had a stroke because of a clot in an artery leading to the brain (ischaemic stroke), and two trials involved people who had a stroke because of bleeding in the brain (intracerebral haemorrhage). In all three trials, one group of patients received mannitol and the rest did not. The results did not show any difference between the group that received mannitol and the group that did not receive mannitol in terms of the number of deaths, the amount of bleeding, or the change in the patients' mental and physical abilities. Side effects were either not found or not reported. The results of this review do not provide any evidence for or against the use of mannitol in people with acute stroke. More trials will need to be conducted before it can be established whether mannitol is beneficial or harmful in the treatment of acute stroke.

The objective of this review was to assess the short term efficacy of D-penicillamine, a drug used to treat rheumatoid arthritis, in a systematic way. Six studies were found, comparing 425 patients on D-penicillamine to 258 on placebo. These studies used a variety of different doses of D-penicillamine (500 mg to 1000 mg per day), and measured a number of different outcomes. Overall, the studies showed that D-penicillamine is effective in reducing the signs and symptoms of rheumatoid arthritis, and that these effects may be comparable to other drugs known to be effective in rheumatoid arthritis. However, the drug was also associated with a number of side-effects.

This review looked for randomised studies that investigated the effects of Chinese herbal medicines, specifically the Huangqi decoction, given in addition to chemotherapy, upon the common side effects of chemotherapy. Four studies were identified. These all used Huangqi decoctions, all compared chemotherapy plus Huangqi decoction with chemotherapy alone and followed up at least six weeks after treatment. One study was conducted in the US, one in China, one in Korea and one in Russia. The results showed that using Huangqi decoctions during chemotherapy significantly reduced the rates of nausea and vomiting, and decreased the rate of low white blood cell counts. It also increased the levels of some types of immune cells. However, these studies were all of low quality and so it is difficult to draw any firm conclusions from this review. Further studies are needed to confirm whether the use of Huangqi decoctions can reduce the side effects of chemotherapy.

The search is current to 25 March 2015. We found three randomised controlled trials (RCTs), which included 866 participants aged four to 55 years with RP of all forms of genetic predisposition. One trial evaluated the effect of vitamin A alone, one trial evaluated DHA alone, and a third trial evaluated DHA and vitamin A versus vitamin A alone. The three trials were well conducted overall. However, none of the RCTs had protocols available, so we could not assess whether any selective reporting of outcomes occurred. One trial did not specifically state how the randomisation was performed, but the rest of the RCTs were at a low risk of bias. The average loss to follow-up at one year was about 10%. There was no clear evidence of benefit of vitamin A and/or DHA for people with RP, in terms of the mean change in visual field and electroretinography (ERG) amplitudes (a measure of the eye's electrical activity) at one year and the mean change in visual acuity at five years follow-up. Two of the three trials reported positive results among some subgroups of participants, but these results have not been confirmed by other trials. No toxicity or adverse events were reported in these three trials. We do not recommend routine use of vitamin A or DHA for the treatment of RP based on the information in this review. More research is needed to determine if there are benefits of vitamin A or DHA for people with RP. Future trials should consider the changes observed in ERG amplitudes and other outcome measures from the trials included in this review when calculating the number of participants required to detect a meaningful difference between treatment arms.

We searched for studies up to May 2016. We included three randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) with 414 participants. Two studies tested interventions to prevent job loss and one study tested an intervention to reduce sickness absenteeism and improve work functioning. Participants had a median age of 52 years and mostly had rheumatoid arthritis. Interventions were given either as a single treatment or over a maximum period of six months. They consisted of various combinations of work adjustments, vocational counselling, education or health advice. Only one intervention consisted of workplace visits. Occupational physicians gave some of the interventions. We found that several interventions may be effective in reducing job loss, improving work attendance and work functioning among people with inflammatory arthritis. However, due to the small number of studies and the poor design of the studies, we are very unsure about these findings. We identified no adverse effects. We rated the quality of the evidence as very low. Reasons for downgrading the quality of the evidence were the small number of studies and the small number of participants, as well as important weaknesses in the way the studies were designed and reported. Further studies are needed to confirm whether these interventions can really prevent job loss and improve work participation and functioning.

We searched the Cochrane Review database until January 2015 and found three completed studies that met our inclusion criteria. These studies included a total of 285 infants; 140 received arginine, and 145 received neither arginine nor another amino acid. These three studies were conducted in Argentina, Israel and Scotland. One study was supported by a grant from a pharmaceutical company, and a second reported having received no funding. The third study did not report funding sources. Our analysis showed that arginine supplementation reduced the risk for developing NEC from 17% to 7%. The number of babies who would need to be supplemented with arginine to prevent one baby developing NEC was six. Arginine supplementation also reduced the rate of death caused by NEC. However, the overall number of deaths was not different between arginine- and non-arginine-supplemented infants. Studies detected no harmful effects of arginine supplementation, including low blood pressure or alterations in glucose homeostasis. Long-term follow-up of one trial at 36 months revealed no differences in developmental outcomes among arginine-supplemented and control infants. However, this trial involved only 54 of the 285 infants enrolled in the three studies. The overall quality of the included studies was good, but these studies were small. The relatively small number of included studies and the small number of infants enrolled limits the strength of our conclusions.

We identified four randomised controlled trials with a total of 1943 participants with acute sinusitis. All four studies were well-designed and double-blinded (nobody knew who was getting which treatment). Three of the four studies compared inhaled corticosteroids versus placebo (a substance containing no medication) for 15 or 21 days. The fourth study compared inhaled corticosteroids plus amoxicillin/clavulanic acid versus amoxicillin/clavulanic acid alone for nine days. The studies showed that inhaled corticosteroids reduced the number of patients with persistent symptoms at the end of the treatment period. However, the difference was small: 13 out of 100 patients still had symptoms after six weeks versus 12 out of 100 patients treated with placebo. Patients using a higher dose of inhaled steroid (400 microgram versus 200 microgram) had a greater benefit. Inhaled steroids did not cause significant side effects and did not increase the rate of recurrent episodes. This review shows that inhaled corticosteroids are a useful treatment option for patients with acute sinusitis. They are most likely to be of value in patients with persistent symptoms and those who cannot take antibiotics. As the benefit is modest, clinicians should balance this against possible, but rare, side effects.

The review found that the administration of prophylactic vancomycin reduces the incidence of bloodstream infection in newborn infants weighing less than 1500 grams when given before an expected need for a central line. However, the giving of prophylactic vancomycin does not reduce mortality or length of hospital stay. There are concerns about the development of vancomycin resistant organisms but more research is needed to find out if this is true.

The review authors searched the medical literature up to March 2015 and identified 14 relevant randomised controlled trials. The main comparison was between gauze and other dressings or topical treatments. The results of the review suggest that gauze is not the best dressing material for wounds healing by secondary intention. However, due to the small number of trials, and their poor quality, the evidence does not allow for any firm conclusions to be made about which dressing or topical treatment is most effective. One trial did find that foam dressings resulted in faster healing and less pain for patients than gauze.

Until 1 April 2015, we ran computer searches for randomized trials of a hormone method versus no birth control or another treatment with a different mechanism of action. Women could be breastfeeding when they started the study. We found 11 trials with a total of 1482 women. Five were old (before 1985), with limited information. Six were newer (2005 to 2014), with more details. Four looked at types of birth control pills, and six studied other methods. The latest studies had better information. The hormone methods tested were the etonogestrel implant, a levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS), and pills with only a progestin. Two other trials studied pills with both estrogen and progestin (combined oral contraceptives, or COCs). The IUD is a device placed inside the uterus. The implant is inserted into the skin of the arm or the upper forearm. These are long-acting reversible methods. The LNG-IUS stays in place for several years. The etonogestrel implant lasts for up to three years. The older studies were small and did not have good methods. The newer ones were larger and better run. With these, the groups were similar when starting out, and more women used their assigned method correctly. The LNG-IUS group had fewer women breastfeeding at 75 days but not at one year. An etonogestrel implant study showed no difference in breastfeeding duration. Two older COC studies reported a shorter time breastfeeding with the pill versus no method. Another showed less breast milk volume with the pill versus placebo (pretend pills). An etonogestrel implant study showed no difference in breast milk volume. Three studies reported no difference in infant growth, including one with the etonogestrel implant versus no method. One showed greater weight gain with the implant versus no method but less versus another hormone method called depot medroxyprogesterone acetate (DMPA). Two other studies showed no difference in infant growth with the LNG-IUS versus another type of birth control or with a COC versus a progestin-only pill (POP). The evidence was not strong enough to say how each method compares to no hormones in terms of breastfeeding duration, breast milk volume or composition, or infant growth. This review did not look at whether the effects differ for preterm or full-term babies.

We searched for studies evaluating the effectiveness of red-light cameras in terms of number of crashes, number of casualties and driver compliance with red lights. We found 10 suitable studies from Australia, Singapore and USA. Seven of these studies did not take into account the tendency for the number of crashes to vary randomly from one time period to the next (regression to the mean) or the possibility that cameras may have an impact on other roads in the surrounding area (spillover effect). When this was done, the studies showed that red-light cameras lead to a reduction in the total number of crashes but not in the total number of casualties. The reason for this is that cameras seem to reduce the number of right-angle crashes but not the number of other types of crashes. One study found that drivers exceed the speed limit at red lights more often than previously thought. There seems to be some support for the view that red-light cameras do reduce the number of red-light violations. However, most studies were too small and had design flaws, so caution is advised in interpreting the results.

We identified four randomized controlled trials involving 494 participants. Three studies, which included 383 participants, reported the proportion of people who had a therapeutic INR by day five. Considerable uncertainty surrounds the use of a 10-mg or a 5-mg loading dose for initiation of warfarin to achieve an INR of 2.0 to 3.0 on the fifth day of therapy. Warfarin nomograms of 10 mg versus 5 mg warfarin for the treatment of acute DVT or PE in adults Warfarin is an anticoagulant medication used to prevent formation of blood clots. An INR of 2.0 to 3.0 is considered therapeutic. It takes about two weeks for the effects of warfarin to reach their maximum. The review authors evaluated the effect of using a warfarin nomogram (a table or chart that shows the amount of drug that should be given based on the person's age, weight, and initial INR) of 10 mg versus one of 5 mg for initiating warfarin therapy in people with acute VTE. This review of four randomized controlled trials found that the choice of a 10-mg or 5-mg warfarin nomogram made little or no difference in the number of people who had a blood clot recurrence or bleeding complications. However, the review authors noted that these findings must be interpreted with caution because of differences among the included studies. Key results Warfarin nomograms of 10 mg versus 5 mg warfarin for the treatment of acute DVT or PE in adults People who received 10 mg warfarin were more likely than those who received 5 mg warfarin to have a therapeutic INR by day five (moderate quality evidence). The risk of bleeding with the 10-mg nomogram was not greater than with the 5-mg nomogram (moderate quality evidence). Quality of the evidence Because of differences in the types of study participants and the length of follow-up of the included studies, these findings are not very certain.

The evidence is current to October 2018. We looked for all studies that compared different models of care versus standard care for people with dementia who had had a hip fracture. We found seven relevant studies with a total of 555 participants. Three compared various models of care in hospital with standard care. Two compared models of care in hospital with further support at home to standard care. And two compared care led by a geriatrician (a specialist doctor who cares for older adults) with care led by the orthopaedic team (specialists in musculoskeletal disorders) in hospital. None of the interventions were designed specifically for people with dementia, but we included the data from the small number of participants with dementia or cognitive impairment in the review. The end of follow-up in the studies ranged from the point of acute hospital discharge to 24 months after discharge. Most of the studies were poorly conducted, and we are uncertain about the findings. There were too few participants in each analysis to determine whether the different models of care resulted in any real differences in outcomes. Some important differences in the characteristics of people receiving the different treatments were identified. For example, those who received the enhanced model of care were older on average than those receiving standard care. There were no data available for our primary outcomes of interest - activities of daily living and quality of life. There was only very-low certainty that enhanced care and rehabilitation in-hospital may reduce the rate of postoperative delirium (a type of acute confusion) and very low-certainty associating it with lower rates of some other complications. There was also low-certainty that, compared to orthopaedic team leadership, care led by a geriatrician may result in a shorter hospital stay. We found limited evidence that some of the models of enhanced rehabilitation and care used in the included trials may show benefits over usual care for preventing delirium and reducing length of stay for people with dementia who have been treated for hip fracture. However, the certainty of these results is low. Determining the optimal strategies to improve outcomes for this growing population of patients should be a research priority. The certainty of the evidence was low or very low, meaning that we cannot be sure of the results.

The aim of this review was to assess the effectiveness of home-based nursing services for children with acute and chronic illnesses. Seven randomised controlled trials (RCTs) met our inclusion criteria. We found that there is a significant decrease in length of hospitalisation for children who received home-based nursing care. However, the current evidence shows no clear reduction in hospital readmission rates or in the frequency of emergency department visits. This could be due to the small number of children included in the studies. There were some positive outcomes for parents including a reduction in their anxiety, an improvement in their ability to cope and in family functioning. Overall, increased parental satisfaction with the home-based care was reported. In one study, home care was reported as more costly for the service provider but there were substantial cost savings for the families. Further studies are needed to replicate these findings.

We ran an electronic search up to January 2015 for trials that randomised people with schizophrenia or other similar serious mental illnesses to receive Intensive Case Management or standard care, or to receive Intensive Case Management or another type of care. We found 40 randomised controlled trials (clinical trials where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) that meet our inclusion criteria. These trials involved 7524 people. We found that Intensive Case Management reduces the amount of time spent in hospital and improves social functioning compared to standard care. There was no clear effect on mental health or quality of life. Intensive Case Management also seems to reduce admission to hospital and improve engagement in therapy. However, we did not find that it reduces suicide or increases employment. We found that the effectiveness of Intensive Case Management varies between studies, and that this may be due to the way in which the teams delivering Intensive Case Management follow the original Intensive Case Management guidelines. Finally, we found that Intensive Case Management is expensive to set up and run, but cost savings seem to accrue over time as people spend less time in hospital. Given the lack of information about the effects of Intensive Case Management on mental health, further research is required. However, given the large body of evidence already available, we do not think that any more trials testing Intensive Case Management vs. standard care are necessary. What is missing is a comparison between standard care and non-ICM.

We included 25 trials in the review. A total of 846 participants were randomly allocated to either box training or no training (464 participants) or to one method of box training or another (382 participants). The number of participants in each study was between 12 and 66. The mean age of the participants was 28 years and 61% were female. The primary outcome was the impact of box model training on surgical skills as measured by error scores (number of errors committed during the task), accuracy scores (evaluation of how close the actual movement was to the correct movement), and completeness of the task (all components of the task completed). We also looked at the time taken to complete the task, the number of movements made, the distance moved by the instruments, and trainee satisfaction. The overall quality of the evidence was poor because of poorly conducted studies and small numbers of participants in most studies. Laparoscopic box model training appears to improve technical skills in trainees who have no previous laparoscopic experience compared with no training. However, we do not know what effect this has on patients or healthcare funders in terms of improved health care or decreased costs. There appears to be no significant difference in the improvement of technical skills between different types of box model training. Further well-designed studies are necessary. Such studies should assess the impacts of box model training on surgical skills in both the short and long term, as well as clinical outcomes when the trainee becomes competent to operate on patients.

Two small randomised clinical trials were included. One trial compared biliary lavage with hydrocortisone versus saline. Hydrocortisone tended to increase adverse events (pancreatitis, cholangitis with septicaemia, paranoid ideas, fluid retention) and did not improve the appearance of the bile ducts on special x-ray examination, which led to termination of the study. Another study compared two different kinds of glucocorticosteroids, budesonide versus prednisone, administered orally. Patients had significantly higher blood bilirubin concentration after treatment with prednisone compared with budesonide. For the other evaluated interventions there was no significant effect on clinical and biochemical outcomes. Therefore there is no evidence to support or refute peroral glucocorticosteroids for patients with primary sclerosing cholangitis.

This review looked at whether giving extra protein and calories to people with anorexia nervosa before they start to eat normally again might help them recover from the illness. Eleven studies with a total of 491 participants were included. Early feeding did seem to help people with anorexia nervosa recover from their illness. Those who were given early nutrition had less chance of dying and had less chance of being left disabled. However, the results are not conclusive as most of the studies were small and had design problems. Future research using larger groups of patients and reporting both nutritional outcomes and health outcomes is needed.

We found 57 randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) that tested 34,390 participants. On average, people who used digital programmes to help them cut down their drinking had about three fewer units of alcohol per week (or 23 grams of pure alcohol per week) than those who did not use a programme. They also had less than one drinking day per month, and had about one fewer binge drinking session per month. There was no difference in how much people drank at the end of the trial, on average, between people who used digital programmes and people in the control group. However, this result was based on only five studies. Most digital programmes used several different techniques to try to help people cut down their drinking. The most commonly-used techniques were based on motivational interviewing, the transtheoretical model and social norms theory. But over half of the digital programmes used no theory at all to guide what they did. We found that certain types of techniques were associated with being more effective than others, but we cannot tell whether theories were used because many studies did not report enough information. Overall, we think that digital programmes may help people to cut down their drinking, but we cannot be sure how well they work yet. More research is needed to find out how to make them as effective as possible.

The review includes 24 studies with 4233 people. The people taking part in the studies were split into two groups: one group was given benzodiazepines and the other group was given a placebo. More people in the benzodiazapine group recovered from their panic disorder than the people in the placebo group. Six more people out of 100 who took benzodiazepines recovered from panic disorder compared with placebo (62 out of 100 versus 56 out of 100). In addition, people who received benzodiazepines had fewer dropouts from treatment, 10 fewer out of 100 (14 out of 100 versus 24 out of 100). However, people who took benzodiazepines were more likely to experience side effects. This means that clinicians should carefully weigh the advantages and disadvantages of benzodiazepines against the advantages and disadvantages of other drugs that have similar effects, such as antidepressants. The quality of the evidence for the two main outcomes was low, meaning that we cannot be certain about the size of the difference between benzodiazepines and placebo. The evidence was even weaker for the other outcomes. Our analysis of the change scores for depression and social functioning was too imprecise to allow us to draw any conclusions. The included studies were small and had many flaws. It is likely that some people knew what treatment they were receiving, and several studies were never completed. Many people dropped out of the studies, often because of adverse effects. This means that the results may not be applicable to the general public and that the findings may not be correct. The included studies were only short-term studies and did not examine the long-term effectiveness of benzodiazepines or the risks of dependency and withdrawal symptoms. This limits the usefulness of our results for making decisions about treatment. The choice of drug should be guided by the patient's preference and should balance benefits and harms from treatment in a long-term perspective.

We searched for trials that compared tai chi to a control group (a pseudo-tai chi intervention, exercise or usual care) in adults at low-to-high risk of developing CVD. We found 13 completed randomised controlled trials (RCTs) and three ongoing trials. The completed trials involved 1520 participants, ranging in age from 40 to 92 years. Duration of tai chi programs ranged from four weeks to one year. The style of tai chi varied from soft-style tai chi to yang-style tai chi, with or without weapons. Follow-up ranged from three months to one year. Most trials were at some risk of bias, particularly in their methods, and some were at a high risk of bias. For example, one study did not hide group allocations, two studies selectively reported outcomes, and one study did not blind the outcome assessor. There were many differences between the studies: in the populations recruited, the duration and style of tai chi, and the way that outcomes were measured. This means that we could not combine results between studies. Only nine trials measured blood pressure and of these six found that systolic blood pressure was lower after taking part in tai chi, although only one study found a large reduction (-22 mmHg). Three studies found no clear evidence of a difference in systolic blood pressure, although neither of these studies found that systolic blood pressure decreased with the control intervention. A similar pattern was seen for diastolic blood pressure, with three studies finding a reduction and three finding no clear evidence of a difference. Three trials measured lipid levels: two found that total cholesterol, low-density lipoprotein-cholesterol (the 'bad' cholesterol) and triglycerides were lower after taking part in tai chi, and one study found no clear evidence of a difference in lipid levels. High-density lipoprotein-cholesterol (the 'good' cholesterol) was higher in the tai chi group compared to the control group in two studies. One study measured quality of life and found that it was better in the tai chi group than the control group after three months. None of the included studies reported on death, non-fatal endpoints, adverse events, costs or occurrence of type 2 diabetes. There were no long-term studies (over one year) in our review. There is insufficient evidence to assess the effects of tai chi on CVD event rates and all-cause mortality. Some studies showed beneficial effects of tai chi on CVD risk factors (blood pressure and lipid levels), but these results were not consistent. There were some problems with the quality of the evidence due to the small number of studies, the small number of participants in these studies, and some methodological problems with the ways that the studies were conducted. However, we do have some evidence of the potential benefits of tai chi and we expect that the inclusion of the results from the three ongoing trials will add to this evidence.

We identified 27 randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) that evaluated whether or not the interventions helped to prevent young people from starting to use tobacco. The interventions were quite different in the ways they approached the problem, the other health risks tackled, the age of children at the start of the study and the length of the study. More research has been done in the USA (23 studies) than anywhere else. The control groups, who received either nothing or 'usual care', or a school intervention, were considered separately. School interventions are expected to have an impact on all children in the class or year group, whereas family interventions target the family and aim to involve the whole family in the effort to keep children away from tobacco. The evidence from trials comparing family interventions to a control group, where the control group receives either nothing or 'usual care', suggests that family interventions can help children to avoid starting smoking. More research has been done on higher intensity programmes than on lower intensity ones, so the evidence is stronger for the former. Interventions typically try to improve family functioning and are most likely to be effective if delivered when children are between 11 and 14 years old. The best estimate we could make, based on nine studies, is that family interventions might reduce the number of young people taking up smoking by between 16% and 32%. However, our confidence in this estimate is moderate because it is based on a small proportion of the total number of studies identified. One study, which also compared a family intervention to a school intervention, suggested that the family intervention was more effective than the school intervention. Another study compared a family intervention to a school 'good behaviour' intervention and did not detect a difference between the two types of programme. No studies reported any harmful effects of the interventions.

We searched several databases to August 2016 and identified three randomised controlled trials that matched our review criteria. These studies included 451 participants with nausea and/or vomiting associated with cancer or its treatment. Two studies compared dexamethasone to a placebo, and the third study compared a number of additional treatments, including dexamethasone, in various combinations with other antiemetics. The duration of the studies ranged from seven to 14 days. The main outcome was the effect on nausea. We did not pool the results of the third study because the outcomes were measured differently. Dexamethasone had no significant effect on reducing nausea compared to placebo after eight days of treatment. The interventions were all well-tolerated, and frequency of side effects were similar in both arms. The quality of the evidence was very low due to the small number of participants in the included studies, changes in the populations in the included studies, and the way the outcomes were reported. We do not have enough data to assess the effects of steroids on nausea and vomiting in cancer patients. More studies are needed.

We identified ten randomised controlled trials (RCTs) of high-risk children using various antibiotics for the prevention of lower respiratory tract infections; three in HIV infection, four in cystic fibrosis, one each in sickle cell disease, cancer and low birth weight neonates with underlying respiratory disorders. The study duration ranged from seven days to three years. Antibiotics were not shown to be effective at reducing the risk of pulmonary tuberculosis (TB) in HIV-infected children but there was a trend towards fewer deaths in those receiving isoniazid. Co-trimoxazole reduced the number of new infections but not all studies showed a reduction in mortality. There was a significant decrease in the rates of hospital admissions with co-trimoxazole prophylaxis in one study. There was no evidence of increase in adverse events or antibiotic resistance. In children with cystic fibrosis, macrolide antibiotics (azithromycin and clarithromycin) reduced the number of pulmonary exacerbations but not the number of Pseudomonas infections. There was no improvement in lung function or quality of life but there was an increased risk of a gut infection (Clostridium difficile). In children with sickle cell disease, prophylactic penicillin had some effect on decreasing the risk of pneumococcal septicaemia. In children with cancer, co-trimoxazole decreased the risk of Pneumocystis carinii pneumonia. In low birth weight neonates with underlying respiratory disorders, there was no difference in the rate of pulmonary infection with vancomycin prophylaxis. Overall, there is inconclusive evidence that antibiotic prophylaxis can reduce pneumonia, pulmonary exacerbations, hospitalisation and mortality in these high-risk groups. Limitations in the current evidence base meant that the overall quality of the evidence was variable and more clinical trials are needed. Future trials should assess the effects of antibiotics for preventing LRTIs in children with congenital heart disease, metabolic disease, endocrine and kidney disorders, neurological disease or prematurity.

We searched the literature up to October 2017 and included nine trials with 519 participants. Three studies compared nitrous oxide with carbon dioxide, five studies compared helium with carbon dioxide, and one study compared room air with carbon dioxide. The methods used by all these trials were prone to bias. There were no differences between the groups in terms of age, body mass index, and percentage of patients with associated medical conditions. The concentration of gas used in all studies was 15% unless stated otherwise. The volume of gas used ranged from 8 L/hour to 20 mL/kg/min. The mean operating table pressure was maintained at 10 mmHg. The length of surgery varied from 48 minutes to 173 minutes. The duration of inflation ranged from 47 minutes to 200 minutes. The studied gases had no effect on cardiopulmonary complications (two studies, 140 participants), surgical complications (two studies, 143 participants), or death (one study, 70 participants). This evidence was of very low quality. There were no major complications related to nitrous oxide, helium, or room air. Two studies reported lower pain scores with nitrous oxide compared with carbon dioxide and one study showed lower pain scores with room air compared with carbon dioxide. However, the overall effect of these gases on pain is uncertain. This evidence was of very low quality. The use of nitrous oxide, helium, or room air instead of carbon dioxide to inflate the abdomen during laparoscopic surgery does not seem to change the rate of lung and heart complications, or wound infections. However, the safety of these gases has yet to be fully proven. The possible advantages of using room air include a reduction in cost and pain, but these findings are based on only one study of limited sample size. Further studies are needed to confirm the benefits and risks of other gases (nitrous oxide, helium, argon, and nitrogen) compared with carbon dioxide.

The aim of this review was to find out which corticosteroid regimen is the most effective in the prevention of BPD. We searched scientific databases for clinical trials in preterm infants at risk of BPD. The evidence is current to January 2016. We found 14 studies with a total of 1065 infants. All studies compared different corticosteroid regimens one against another. We did not identify any studies that compared dexamethasone phosphate against other corticosteroids. Based on our analysis of these studies, we would expect that of 1000 preterm infants who receive a moderate-dose steroid regimen, 145 infants will develop BPD, compared with 97 infants who receive either a low- or a high-dosage regimen. Furthermore, we would expect that of 1000 preterm infants who receive a moderate-dosage regimen, 40 infants will have abnormal neurodevelopmental outcome at eight months' postmenstrual age compared with 10 infants who receive either a low- or a high-dosage regimen. However, it must be noted that all studies were small and had a number of limitations that could have affected the reliability of the results. Therefore, further studies are needed before a final conclusion can be drawn.

Most of the eight included studies were conducted between 1950 and 1965. All studies compared corticosteroids (given by mouth, intramuscle or intravenously) or intravenous immunoglobulins versus aspirin or a placebo. No new trials have been conducted since 2001. Overall, corticosteroids and intravenous immunoglobulins were not better than aspirin in preventing heart problems. However, the quality of the trials was poor, so the results should be viewed with caution. Better quality trials are needed to assess the effects of newer corticosteroids and of other anti-inflammatory agents.

The review authors searched for all studies that evaluated the effects of environmental sanitation interventions on trachoma prevalence or incidence up to January 2010. We found six relevant studies. Two studies that assessed insecticide spray as a fly control measure showed that trachoma is reduced by at least 55% to 61% with this measure compared to no intervention. However, another study did not find insecticide spray to be effective in reducing trachoma. One study found that another fly control measure, provision of a latrine reduced trachoma by 29.5% compared to no intervention; this was, however, not statistically significantly different and findings have not been confirmed by a more recent study. Another study revealed that health education reduced the incidence of trachoma. These findings were not confirmed by a second study, however, which found that a modest health education programme with modest water supply did not reduce trachoma. All the studies have some methodological problems. The inclusion of a large number of people who did not have trachoma at the start of the study in the control arm of one study is likely to have biased the results of that study towards finding no difference in trachoma prevalence between the intervention and control arms. Another study was unable to confirm earlier findings of trachoma reduction with health education alone. Overall, there is very little data on the effectiveness of environmental sanitation measures for trachoma control.

This review set out to evaluate the effects of cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) in the management of CFS/ME. The review authors wanted to find out if CBT could improve the health status of people with CFS/ME by reducing tiredness, improving activity levels and quality of life, and to compare the effects of CBT with those of other psychological therapies. The authors also wanted to know if combining CBT with other treatments would be helpful. The review includes 15 studies with 1043 people with CFS/ME. The results showed that CBT reduced feelings of tiredness when compared with usual care and also when compared with other psychological therapies. However, it is not clear whether the reduction in tiredness lasted long term. There is little information about the effects of adding other treatments to CBT. More research is needed to answer this question.

The review authors searched the medical literature up to 1 November 2016 and found one small study with 39 people taking part. The study lasted six months and compared vitamin D supplements to a placebo (a substance containing no medication) in people with sickle cell disease. The study did not report funding sources. One person dropped out of the study because of the occurrence of painful episodes called vaso-occlusive crises. In the vitamin D group, 40% of the study participants dropped out, compared to 5% in the placebo group. The reasons for dropping out were not clear. The study measured blood levels of vitamin D and found that those taking vitamin D supplements had higher levels after four weeks. However, there were no differences in vitamin D levels between the two groups at 16 and 24 weeks. The study also looked at the level of pain that the participants experienced and found that those taking vitamin D supplements had less pain. However, the study also showed that their quality of life was worse than in those taking the placebo. The study did not find any differences in the frequency of adverse events (harms) between the vitamin D and placebo groups. Due to the small numbers of participants in the study, the review authors could not tell if vitamin D supplements cause harm. The study was of poor quality due to the large number of dropouts. The study had some other methodological problems that may have affected the results. The study was only able to provide the review authors with information on 37 of the 39 people taking part. For the rest of the participants, the study did not have any records of their vitamin D levels at the start of the study. The review authors judged the quality of the evidence provided by the study to be moderate for the blood levels of vitamin D, but low for the level of pain, quality of life and side effects. Moderate quality evidence means that more research may change our estimates of the effect. Low quality evidence means that we cannot be very sure about the accuracy of our estimates. The study was only able to provide the review authors with information on 25 of the 39 people taking part for the whole six month period. The study was too small to be able to tell if vitamin D supplements cause any harmful effects. Further high quality studies are needed to confirm whether vitamin D supplements are safe and effective for people with sickle cell disease.

We found one randomised controlled trial (a type of scientific experiment in which people are assigned to one of two or more treatments, one of which is a control treatment, using a random method), which compared two different treatments: the Nuffield Dyspraxia Programme-3 (NDP-3) and Rapid Syllable Transitions Treatment (ReST). The study took place in a university clinic in Australia and recruited 26 children aged 4 to 12 years with CAS. Children were allocated randomly to one of the two treatments. Treatments were delivered intensively in one-hour sessions four days a week for three weeks. Speech pathology students delivered the treatments in the English language. Outcomes were assessed before therapy, immediately after therapy, at one month and four months post-therapy. The study did not report funding sources. Our review looked at one-month post-therapy outcomes only. A number of children received further treatment from their usual therapist between one and four months post-therapy, meaning that we could not analyse the results for four-month outcomes without potential bias.
