Hence, our review only reports the results of the one study, which assessed outcomes at one month post-therapy. Both NDP-3 and ReST improved speech articulation (the correct formation of sounds) for both treated and untreated words, and speech consistency (leaving out or substituting sounds) in children with CAS. However, the study did not measure the effect on functional communication (the ability to meet the communication needs of everyday situations) and it is unclear whether these treatments may have an effect on this aspect of speech and language ability. As the study only included children with CAS without any other developmental difficulties, we are also unsure whether the intervention would be effective for children with CAS and other difficulties (e.g. autism spectrum disorder, Down syndrome). Only one study, involving 26 children, has been reported. While both NDP-3 and ReST showed improvement in speech articulation for both treated and untreated words, and speech consistency, we do not know yet if one treatment is better than the other, or whether either is better than no treatment. More studies of these and other treatments with a larger number of children are needed.

We included four studies in this review, treating participants either with antibiotics or a placebo (a substance which contains no medication) for up to 12 months. One study was from the US, one from the UK, and two from Brazil. A total of 268 participants were included. One small study reported the effect of treatment on vision; the rest reported the effect of treatment on the occurrence of toxoplasmal retinochoroiditis. Three studies used antibiotics and one used a combination of antibiotics. One study gave additional steroids to all participants. Antibiotic treatment may have little or no effect on vision. However, it probably decreases the risk of having a recurrence of toxoplasmosis from about 24 in 100 people per year down to six in 100 people per year. This estimate is based on three studies, including 227 participants. It makes little difference whether the toxoplasmosis is acute (recently developed) or chronic (long lasting). Two studies reported side effects. These were a fall in some blood cells (due to the way the body responds to infection), nausea, loss of appetite, rash, and joint pain. Giving steroids extra with the antibiotics did not increase the number of side effects. Further studies are needed to confirm that antibiotics do not improve vision in people with toxoplasmosis affecting the centre of the macula (the area at the back of the eye responsible for sharp central vision) or any other part of the retina. The evidence for the effect of antibiotics on preventing a recurrence of toxoplasmosis came from studies at low to unclear risk of bias (as long as the methods were well described). The evidence for the effect of antibiotics on vision came from only one small study at a high risk of bias (participants knew what treatment they were receiving).

This review included 43 controlled trials which together involved over 5000 participants. The mean age of the participants was in the mid-seventies. The majority of the participants had a simple extracapsular osteoporotic neck of femur fracture. The review looked at the results of using different devices to fix these fractures. The main comparisons were between two types of nail (intramedullary nail versus sliding hip screw) and between two types of plate (fixed angle plate versus sliding hip screw). The review found that the sliding hip screw performed better than the intramedullary nail. The sliding hip screw is relatively straightforward to use and has the advantage of allowing the bone to heal in a natural position. It also allows some movement at the fracture site, which reduces the risk of the bone breaking again (re-rupturing). However, it does not provide as strong a grip on the bone as an intramedullary nail and so the fracture can move slightly during weight-bearing activities. This can make it difficult for the bone to heal. The intramedullary nail holds the bone fragments more rigidly in place than the sliding hip screw, but this can make it more difficult for the bone to heal because of lack of blood supply. It can also cause problems in terms of the nail becoming loose in the canal of the thigh bone, requiring a second operation to remove it. However, there are concerns about the safety of the sliding hip screw. In particular, it can be difficult to know how much the fracture has moved back into place during surgery. If the fracture has not been properly realigned then the leg will be deformed and the patient will have difficulty walking. Furthermore, if the fracture moves back into an incorrect position after surgery then the bone will not heal and the patient will require another operation. This review found that the sliding hip screw was the best device for treating extracapsular proximal femoral fractures. More research is needed to find out whether newer designs of nail are safer and more reliable than the original ones.

We searched databases for randomised controlled trials (RCTs), which are regarded as the gold standard of research studies, and found one RCT that compared the effectiveness of surgery versus octreotide (a drug which reduces hormone levels) in women with bowel obstruction due to advanced ovarian cancer. The study analysed data for 47 women, of whom 27 had an operation and 20 had octreotide. Not all women were able to have an operation as they had to be well enough to undergo anaesthesia and recover from the operation. All women taking part in the study had their bowel obstruction treated by placing a stent (a thin tube) through their nose and into their bowel. If the stent became blocked it could be replaced. The study showed that the women who had an operation to remove as much of the tumour as possible lived longer than those who were given octreotide. However, the difference in survival was not clearly reported. The study did not report any information about how each group of women felt before and after their treatment and it is known that many women in the study had their bowel removed (a major operation). This means that we do not know whether women who have an operation feel better or worse in the long term compared with women who do not have an operation. The study was very small and was designed to look at the survival of the women rather than their quality of life (QoL). Further studies including more women are needed to confirm whether surgery is better than octreotide at improving the QoL of women with bowel obstruction due to ovarian cancer.

The review of four trials found that, although statins did improve cholesterol levels and lower testosterone, they did not make periods more regular or increase the chances of ovulation or improve hirsutism and acne. However, these results are based on only four small trials involving just 244 participants, so more research is needed.

We searched for relevant randomised controlled trials (RCTs) in July 2013. We did not find any RCTs that met our inclusion criteria. We found 22 reports of non-randomised studies, all of which were of low methodological quality. None of these studies provided any convincing evidence that any of the treatments were better than radiotherapy in reducing vaginal bleeding. Further good quality RCTs are needed to assess the best way of controlling vaginal bleeding in women with cervical cancer.

We searched four computerised databases for relevant trials. We included all randomised controlled trials (RCTs) looking at the effectiveness and toxicity of temozolomide in the treatment of newly diagnosed or recurrent brain cancer. Two RCTs were found that assessed the role of temozolomide in the management of newly diagnosed brain tumors, enrolling a total of 745 patients. Results showed that giving temozolomide in addition to radiotherapy improved how long people lived and delayed the tumour growing back compared to radiotherapy alone, but these benefits only appeared when the drug was given both during and after the radiotherapy phase of treatment. However, this treatment also increased side effects such as anaemia, fatigue and infection. Another two RCTs were found that assessed the role of temozolomide in the management of recurrent brain tumors, enrolling a total of 672 patients. Results showed that temozolomide did not improve how long people lived but it did delay the tumour growing back again (progression-free survival) in a subgroup of patients with a specific type of recurrent brain tumor called glioblastoma multiforme. Side effects were similar between arms. Two further RCTs were found that assessed the role of temozolomide in the management of elderly people (>65 years old) with newly diagnosed brain tumors, enrolling a total of 664 patients. Results showed that temozolomide was no better than radiotherapy at improving how long people lived or delaying the tumour growing back again, but some side effects were more common with temozolomide. Only one RCT reported quality of life measures and so these results are uncertain. Temozolomide, when given at the same time as and then after radiotherapy, is an effective treatment for most people with newly diagnosed brain tumors but it does increase side effects. In recurrent brain tumors, temozolomide is no better than standard chemotherapy at improving how long people live but it may be better at delaying the tumour growing back again without more side effects. In the elderly, temozolomide is no better than radiotherapy in terms of how long people live and how long the tumour remains stable but some side effects are more common with temozolomide.

We included two studies in this review. One of these studies was conducted in Cambodia and it examined whether or not contracting with international non-governmental organizations (NGOs) to manage health centers and clinics improved people’s access to and use of health care services. This study also examined the impact on the number of pregnant women who attended an antenatal care facility and used public facilities. The other study was conducted in three countries in Latin America and it evaluated the impact of intermittent training of district health system managers on their performance. We found that contracting with NGOs to manage health centers and clinics may increase the number of people who visit health care facilities but may not lead to better health outcomes. We also found that intermittent training courses may improve the performance of district health system managers. However, we do not have sufficient evidence from this review to recommend any specific type of intervention for improving the recruitment and retention of district health systems managers. It is important to note that the quality of the available evidence is low.

The authors searched the literature until 6 November 2014. We found three randomized controlled trials that compared surgical and non-surgical treatments. The number of people enrolled was 123. The methods used for preventing selection bias were not reported clearly. Two studies failed to conceal the randomization method. All three studies reported on mortality, with deaths occurring in two studies. There was no clear evidence of a difference in mortality between treatment groups. Six people died: the causes of death were pneumonia, lung blood clot, infection of the tissues between the lungs and the heart, and septic shock. Surgical treatment resulted in fewer cases of pneumonia, chest deformity, and the need for a tracheostomy (an opening created through the neck into the windpipe). Mechanical ventilation time, length of stay in the intensive care unit, and length of hospital stay were also reduced by surgery. As the number of people enrolled was small, our review was underpowered to detect a difference in mortality. The quality of the evidence was low. Further well-designed studies with a sufficient number of participants are needed to confirm these results and to see if surgery has an effect on mortality.

We found 50 studies that compared the blood cholesterol-lowering effect of cerivastatin with either placebo or another blood cholesterol-lowering drug. These studies included 12,877 participants who received either cerivastatin or placebo, or cerivastatin or another statin. The cerivastatin dose ranged from 0.025 mg to 80 mg daily and the duration of the studies ranged from three to 12 weeks. We judged the quality of the evidence to be high. Cerivastatin reduced blood cholesterol by 11% to 41% within three to 12 weeks. The amount of cholesterol reduction was closely related to the dose of cerivastatin. At very low doses (0.025 mg to 0.5 mg per day), cerivastatin had only small blood cholesterol-lowering effects, but at higher doses (2.5 mg to 80 mg per day) it had large blood cholesterol-lowering effects. Compared with other statins, cerivastatin was much more effective at lowering blood cholesterol. For example, cerivastatin was 250 times more effective than fluvastatin, 20 times more effective than atorvastatin and five times more effective than rosuvastatin at lowering blood cholesterol. Cerivastatin also increased the level of 'good' cholesterol (HDL) by five percent. The studies assessed the safety of cerivastatin and reported that 6% to 27% of people experienced adverse effects, but the actual number of people who experienced adverse effects may have been higher because some cases may not have been reported. The most common side effects were muscle pain, nausea and constipation. The short duration of the studies and the incomplete reporting of adverse effects made it difficult to assess the harms of cerivastatin.

The quality of the evidence from these trials was generally considered to be moderate to high. The main limitations of the evidence were imprecision (due to small numbers of patients in the included studies) and indirectness (the intervention and outcome measures used in the studies were not exactly the same as those used in the review). We found no clear evidence that remote ischaemic preconditioning prevents kidney damage after heart and vascular surgery. However, it may make little or no difference in the level of kidney function, the need for dialysis, the length of time spent in hospital, or the number of deaths. This means that we are moderately to highly certain that remote ischaemic preconditioning has little or no effect on the level of kidney function, the need for dialysis, length of time spent in hospital, or the number of deaths.

We searched for evidence from randomised controlled trials (RCTs) on the efficacy of treatments for post-stroke fatigue in adults after stroke. We found 12 RCTs with a total of 703 participants. Eight trials (546 participants) contributed to the analysis. The mean age of the participants ranged from 48 years to 76 years. The degree of disability ranged from severe to moderate. The duration of the interventions ranged from three weeks to several months. A range of treatments were compared, including drugs (fluoxetine, enerion, (-)-OSU6162, citicoline and a combination of Chinese herbs), a fatigue education programme and a mindfulness-based stress reduction programme. The overall result showed that the combined treatment group had significantly lower levels of fatigue than the control group, but this result was based on trials with different designs and may not be reliable. When we analysed the trials separately, the trials with low risk of bias did not show a beneficial effect, and neither did the trials where the assessors of outcome were aware of the group to which the participant belonged. This review shows that there is no clear evidence on which treatment might be most effective in treating or preventing fatigue after stroke. The trials were small, and some had a high risk of bias. Therefore, it is important that larger trials are conducted with a low risk of bias. It is also important that the outcomes are similar to those experienced by people after stroke.

We found three randomised controlled trials, enrolling 74 premature infants (outcome data available on 71 infants), that assessed interventions for hyperkalaemia. The sample sizes of the three trials were very small with 12 (Malone 1991), 19 (Singh 2002) and 40 infants enrolled (Hu 1999). The interventions and the outcome assessments could not be blinded to the clinical staff in two trials (Malone 1991; Hu 1999). One study (Malone 1991), the combination of insulin and glucose, compared to cation-exchange resin, caused a reduction in all cause mortality. The study by Hu (1999) showed a reduction in the incidence of intraventricular haemorrhbleeding (bleeding into the brain) ≥ grade 2 with the use of the combination of insulin and glucose. Albuterol inhalation versus saline inhalation changed serum potassium from baseline at four hours and at eight hours after initiation of treatment. No differences noted in mortality or other clinical outcomes were seen with albuterol inhalation versus saline inhalation (Singh 2002). No serious side effects were noted with either the combination of insulin and glucose or albuterol inhalation. Other interventions listed in our objectives have not been studied to date. In view of the limited information from small studies of uncertain quality, no firm recommendations for clinical practice can be made. It appears that the combination of insulin and glucose is preferably over treatment with rectal cation-resin for preterm infants with high levels of potassium. Both the combination of insulin and glucose and albuterol inhalation deserve further study. The two interventions could possibly be tested against each other. The effectiveness of other potentially effective interventions for high levels of potassium in the blood (diuretics, exchange transfusion, peritoneal dialysis and calcium) have not been tested in randomised controlled trials.

The authors searched for all the published and unpublished trials that compared IVIg with any other treatment in Guillain-Barré syndrome and included the ones that compared IVIg with plasma exchange (the standard treatment). We also contacted all the relevant companies to find any additional information. This review is an update of a previous one published in 2001. The original review showed that plasma exchange hastens recovery compared with supportive treatment alone. This review found that, in adults with severe disease, intravenous immunoglobulin started within two weeks from the onset of symptoms, hastens recovery as much as plasma exchange. Intravenous immunoglobulin is much more likely to be completed than plasma exchange and the combination of both does not give significant extra benefit. In children, intravenous immunoglobulin probably hastens recovery compared with supportive care alone. However, there were no trials in mild disease and we therefore do not know how useful the treatment is in this category. There were no trials where one treatment was compared directly with another. There were no significant differences in adverse effects, but this may have been due to the small number of participants in the trials. Further trials are needed to confirm these findings. One trial showing the effects of a new method of administration of immunoglobulin is underway.

The evidence is current to September 2015. We found 28 randomized clinical trials, involving 9330 adults who had general anaesthesia, of which 21 trials (19,597 participants) contributed data to this review. The trials compared a high or low inspiratory oxygen fraction with the guidelines-recommended fraction of inspired oxygen (between 21% and 30%). The highest fractions of inspired oxygen ranged from 50% to 100%, depending on the trial. We did not find evidence of an effect of a high fraction of inspired oxygen on the risk of death or of respiratory complications after surgery. Nor did we find evidence of an effect on the risk of wound infections. However, there was no evidence of a reduction in the risk of wound infections when a high fraction of inspired oxygen was administered in trials in which the antibiotics were given before the start of surgery (prophylactic antibiotics) and in trials in which the assessors of wound infections were unaware of which patients had received a high or low fraction of inspired oxygen. The quality of the evidence was low or very low because of study design problems and the small number of trials reporting on the outcomes of interest. It remains uncertain whether a high fraction of inspired oxygen should be used routinely during anaesthesia and surgery. Therefore, further randomized clinical trials are needed.

We included 25 studies involving 3663 children, aged one month to 16 years. However, two studies did not report on any of the outcomes of interest for this review, so we analysed the remaining 23 studies, which covered a range of antibiotics, types of participants, ways of measuring middle ear pressure and time points for assessment. We found that children who had received antibiotics were more likely to experience complete resolution of their OME symptoms by two to three months, but they were also more likely to experience side effects such as diarrhoea, vomiting or skin rash. However, it should be noted that some of the studies included in this analysis had elements that meant we could not regard them as particularly reliable. We found that antibiotics may also reduce the likelihood of further infections occurring over the next few months, although if we removed the two least reliable studies, this finding was no longer significant. Unfortunately, we do not yet know whether antibiotics have any beneficial effects on speech, language or cognitive development, nor on quality of life. Neither do we know whether antibiotics are linked to a reduction in the need for grommets to be inserted. The decision whether or not to prescribe antibiotics for children with glue ear therefore depends on an individualised clinical judgement about the possible benefits of antibiotic treatment, balanced against potential side effects and the possibility of encouraging bacterial resistance to these drugs. As one study showed parents were concerned about the safety of antibiotics, and another showed that doctors were influenced by parental expectations when deciding whether or not to prescribe antibiotics, open and honest discussion between parents and doctors about the possible benefits and harms of using antibiotics for glue ear is really important. Antibiotic resistance is a serious issue that has already had major implications for public health. Therefore, even though this review shows that there may be benefits of using antibiotics to clear up glue ear, we must consider the potential for increased antibiotic resistance associated with their use. Further research is needed to find new ways of treating glue ear that do not contribute to antibiotic resistance.

The aim of this review was to find out how much strict control of dietary protein intake reduces the risk of diabetic nephropathy in people with diabetes. We included twelve studies, nine randomised controlled trials and three before and after studies. One study showed that a low protein diet reduced the risk of kidney failure or death by 77%. Seven of the studies in patients with type 1 diabetes did not show a significant reduction in the loss of kidney function. Two studies in patients with type 2 diabetes showed an insignificant reduction in the loss of kidney function. One study reported that patients on a low protein diet had a lower body mass index and muscle mass. They also had less fat, which could be seen as a benefit, but the authors did not consider this likely enough to recommend this diet. Another study found that patients on a low protein diet were more likely to have inadequate protein intake than those given no special diet. This could lead to malnutrition and other health problems. We found no data on the effect of a low protein diet on health-related quality of life and costs. Reducing protein intake seems to slightly slow down the damage to kidneys caused by diabetes, but not enough to be statistically significant. It is not known what level of protein intake is needed to achieve this. More research is needed.

This review examined the effectiveness of various types of rehabilitation for people with brain injury. We included 19 studies involving 3480 people. The studies covered a wide range of interventions and settings. We found strong evidence that people with mild brain injury recover well and return to everyday activities without needing any specific treatment. However, those with more serious brain injuries do benefit from formal rehabilitation. This suggests that it is important to assess every person's needs for rehabilitation, regardless of severity, to ensure that each receives an appropriate level of treatment. We also found strong evidence that people who start rehabilitation soon after their injury make faster progress than those who wait for several weeks before starting rehabilitation. Furthermore, we found that people who engage in more hours of therapy each week make faster progress than those who have fewer hours of therapy. Finally, we found some evidence to suggest that people who are part of a group during rehabilitation make faster progress than those who receive one-to-one therapy.

We searched for evidence on 30 November 2015 and identified one randomised controlled trial (a type of experiment in which people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) involving 176 women. The trial investigated the effects of 'rooming-in' (i.e. mother and newborn are in the same room but not necessarily in contact with each other) on the success of establishing and maintaining breastfeeding. The mothers were randomly allocated to either the 'rooming-in' group (where the mother and her baby spent the first four days after birth together in the same room) or the'separate care' group (where the mother and her baby were cared for in different rooms during the first four days after birth). The designers of the trial used an unusual study design, known as a 'factorial design', which allowed them to examine two different interventions (i.e. 'rooming-in' and the type of infant apparel) within the trial. However, we could only use one arm of the trial for our review. The other arm examined the effect of different types of onesies on breastfeeding duration. The evidence is current to 30 November 2015. Breastfeeding duration was not different between the two groups, with the majority of women breastfeeding their infants for four months. There was no difference between the two groups in the proportion of infants still receiving any breastmilk at six months of age. On the fourth day after giving birth, the mean number of breastfeeds per day was just over eight times per day in the rooming-in group, although this figure was slightly lower than the scheduled seven feeds per day in the separate care group. The percentage of infants who received only breastmilk (i.e. exclusive breastfeeding) on the fourth day before discharge from the hospital was significantly higher in the rooming-in group, 86%, compared with 45% in the separate care group. No other comparisons between groups were significant. The quality of the evidence was low. The sample size was small and there was a high risk of bias due to the way the trial was designed.

The authors concluded that sanchi appears to be beneficial and safe for acute ischaemic stroke, but more well-designed randomised controlled trials are needed to confirm this conclusion. However, the small number of trials included in the review and their poor methodological quality mean that this conclusion should be interpreted cautiously.

The review looked at the effects of three techniques of early (immediate, immediate-delayed, delayed) placement of single tooth implants in the anterior (front) region of the mouth. The review found four studies on the placement of implants at the same time as the tooth was extracted, five studies on implants being placed after an interval of a few weeks and three studies on implants being placed months later. However, many of the studies were small and flawed by a lack of clear reporting, making it difficult to draw reliable conclusions. The limited available evidence suggested that there are no clear advantages of any of the three techniques, although it appeared that the aesthetic results (i.e. how natural they looked) may be improved by placing the implant at the same time as the tooth is removed. On the other hand, there appeared to be more potential problems with early placement, including failure of the implant and other complications such as infection and bleeding. Further well conducted research is needed to confirm these findings.

The review authors searched the medical literature up to March 2013 and found only two randomised controlled trials of one potential disease-modifying drug, clioquinol (PBT1), and its structural analogue, PBT2, which were eligible for inclusion in the review. Both trials were conducted in Australia by the same research group. The first trial compared clioquinol with placebo and the second trial compared a newer compound, PBT2, with placebo. In the first trial, which involved 36 participants, there was no difference in the level of cognitive impairment between the group who received clioquinol and those who received placebo after 24 and 36 weeks. A side effect that may have been caused by the drug was reported in the clioquinol group; it resolved when the medication was stopped. This trial did not find any effect on other aspects of dementia or on the clinical impression of the participants' condition. The second trial, which involved 78 participants, showed that after 12 weeks there was no difference in overall cognition or memory between the group of people who received PBT2 and those who received placebo. However, there were improvements in some aspects of executive function (a high-level brain function that enables us to plan, organise, and pay attention to detail) in the PBT2 group. These results need to be interpreted cautiously as the trial was small and the level of significance chosen was relatively low. No adverse effects were reported. The first drug tested, clioquinol, is no longer being developed because of lack of efficacy and safety concerns. Further trials are needed to determine whether the second drug, PBT2, is effective in treating Alzheimer’s disease.

This evidence is current to January 2017. We included 36 studies with 2999 participants in this review. Most studies were well-designed. We are confident in the findings in pulmonary hypertension due to groups 1 diseases (primary pulmonary hypertension, heritable disorders of connective tissue, and idiopathic interstitial lung disease). Pulmonary hypertension due to left-heart disease, lung disease, or blood clots in arteries was less well-studied. Pulmonary hypertension due to groups 1 diseases (primary pulmonary hypertension, heritable disorders of connective tissue, and idiopathic interstitial lung disease): People who took phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors had an improved World Health Organization functional class, could walk further in six minutes, and were less likely to die. They experienced more headaches, nausea, flushing, and muscle pain. Phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors appeared to work equally well whether they were used alone or with another medication. There was not enough information to compare them to other medications. Pulmonary hypertension due to left-heart disease: People who took phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors had an improved World Health Organization functional class, and could walk further in six minutes, but were not less likely to die. It was unclear which types of left-heart disease might benefit from treatment with phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors. Pulmonary hypertension due to lung disease: People who took phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors could walk slightly further in six minutes than those who took a placebo, but there was a lot of uncertainty around this finding. There was no change in oxygen levels. Pulmonary hypertension due to blood clots: There was no improvement in any measure for people who took phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors. We do not know how the different types of phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil, and vardenafil) compare to each other in terms of beneficial and harmful effects. This review suggests that phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors may be useful in the management of pulmonary hypertension due to groups 1 diseases, and may be useful in pulmonary hypertension due to left-heart disease, but the findings are less certain. There is no clear benefit of phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors in pulmonary hypertension due to lung disease or blood clots in arteries.

We searched several computerised databases up to June 2014 to find randomised controlled trials looking at the effects of single, double or multiple injections of local anaesthetic into the axillary brachial plexus to see how they compare in terms of the number of people with effective anaesthesia, side effects, level of patient satisfaction etc. We found 22 trials involving 2193 participants. The design of these trials was generally good, although two trials had serious weaknesses. One additional trial has been included in this update. The main outcome measures were the number of people with effective anaesthesia and the number with complete anaesthesia (no feeling in the arm). We combined the results from nine trials and showed that multiple injections are better than single injections as more people had effective anaesthesia (98% versus 90%). We also found that it took less time to perform a double injection than a multiple injection. Six trials reported the number of people with complete anaesthesia and again multiple injections were better than double (83% versus 70%). However, there were no clear differences between multiple and double injections in terms of the number of people with partial anaesthesia, pain due to the tourniquet, complications and patient discomfort. There was not enough information about the effects of different types of anaesthetic, or about the effects of multiple injections in children. We conclude that multiple injections of local anaesthetic into the axillary brachial plexus are more effective at producing anaesthesia of the arm than either single or double injections. However, there is not enough evidence about the safety of this procedure.

This study reviews the effect of inhaled corticosteroids on growth in children with asthma. It found that there are few studies available for review and that all were conducted using the same drug, beclomethasone. The results showed that this particular drug decreased growth in children with mild-moderate asthma. It is not known whether this would also occur with other inhaled steroids; some may be associated with fewer side effects. The review concludes that if inhaled steroids are necessary to control a child's asthma, the minimal effective dose should be used at all times and growth should be carefully followed.

This review aimed to compare these two forms of PD to see if there are any differences between them in terms of survival, ability to continue dialysis, quality of life, patient suitability, ease of use and costs. Three studies were identified. These studies did not show any difference between APD and CAPD with respect to mortality, peritonitis, dropouts from PD, hernias, PD fluid leaks, residual renal function or the need for hospital admission. However one study showed that patients on APD had significantly lower infection rates and hospitalisation when the results were analysed per year. Another study showed that patients on APD had more time for work, family and social activities.

This review compared four radiotherapy regimens used as primary treatment for Hodgkin's lymphoma: (1) involved field radiation (EF-RT) versus (2) extended field radiation (EF-RT), (3) combined radiation and chemotherapy (CRT) versus radiation alone (RT) and (4) CRT versus chemotherapy alone (CT). A meta-analysis is a statistical technique to combine the results of multiple single studies to calculate an average effect across all studies. This review includes information from 37 randomised controlled trials with 9312 patients. Results show that combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy are more effective than radiotherapy alone in terms of survival, disease-free period and side effects. Combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy are also more effective than chemotherapy alone in terms of disease-free period, but this result applies only to early stages of the disease. Radiotherapy alone seems to cause more secondary malignancies than combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy, probably because there is a greater need for further therapy after radiotherapy alone. However, combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy do not increase the risk of secondary malignancy compared to chemotherapy alone. Involved field radiation is superior to extended field radiation in terms of disease-free period, but no other differences between these two types of radiotherapy were found. The review concludes that combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy should be regarded as the optimal treatment for early-stage Hodgkin's lymphoma, that radiotherapy alone is not as good as combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy for advanced stages of the disease and that combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy cause fewer side effects than chemotherapy alone. However, these conclusions have to be applied cautiously to new drugs and techniques which have not yet been tested in randomised studies.

This review included 25 non-controlled studies (or controlled studies in which one or more arms used an opioid) that followed 4893 people who took opioids for more than four weeks. We also included one controlled trial that compared two opioids. The majority of people (82%) in the studies took opioids by mouth, 11% through a patch, and 7% through injections into the spinal canal. Most people in the studies had cancer; however, people with chronic non-cancer pain also were studied. Overall, people who take opioids long term experience substantial pain relief and improvement in their ability to function independently. Many people (22% to 28%) stop taking opioids because of side effects, 5% to 10% because of lack of benefit, and 0.3% because of addiction. Other common side effects, like constipation and insomnia, usually occur at low frequency. Serious side effects, including overdose and addiction, are rare. It is unclear whether the benefits of opioids are worth the risks.

Cochrane Review authors searched the medical literature up to 14 February 2019. We found only one randomised controlled trial (a type of experiment in which people are allocated at random to two or more interventions, possibly including a control intervention, and the results are compared). The 212 participants in the trial all had a spinal cord injury and pressure ulcers classed as stage III or IV (the most severe types of pressure ulcers). The average age of the participants in the anabolic steroid group was 58.4 years and in the placebo (inactive substance) group it was 57.3 years. The trial compared an oral anabolic steroid, oxandrolone (20 mg per day), with placebo (a dummy treatment). Participants received the treatment for 24 weeks or until their ulcer healed. The trial reported on the proportion of ulcers that had completely healed and on adverse events (harms). Anabolic steroids may make pressure ulcers slightly less likely to heal quickly. They may also cause harmful effects. This trial did not show any clear benefit or harm from using oxandrolone. The trial was stopped early because oxandrolone was not making ulcers heal faster than placebo. The certainty of the evidence was very low, meaning that we cannot be sure about the results. The trial was small and the wide range in ages of the participants means that the results may not apply to older or younger people. More research is needed to evaluate anabolic steroids for the treatment of pressure ulcers.

We found six randomised controlled trials involving 8372 people. Four of these trials compared email with standard mail and two compared email with usual care (usual care could include standard mail, or face-to-face visits from healthcare professionals). In all six trials, the information delivered by email did not contain any personal or identifying details. We found no clear evidence that email improved the uptake of screening tests, or helped people to understand the information provided, or supported them to make changes to their behaviour. There was also no clear evidence that email improved people's physical or mental health, or well-being. It is important to note that many factors affect whether or not people take up screening tests, and that the trials in this review had some limitations that might have affected our findings. Therefore further research is needed before we can draw firm conclusions.

This review found 11 clinical trials with a total of 855 participants with BPPV. Nine of these used a modified Epley manoeuvre with postural restrictions. Postural restrictions consisted of instructing patients to sleep for one night in the opposite ear position to the affected side, and to avoid bending forward or heavy lifting for several days. The addition of postural restrictions to the Epley manoeuvre increased the percentage of patients with a successful outcome from 79% to 89%. However, it only takes 10 patients to be treated with postural restrictions instead of the Epley manoeuvre alone to prevent one additional patient from having a recurrence of symptoms, which is a relatively modest benefit. The extra intervention does not subject the majority of patients to risk of harm, does not pose a major inconvenience, and can be routinely discussed and advised. Specifically, patients who experience discomfort due to wearing a cervical collar or other limitations on their activities can choose to have the Epley manoeuvre alone. This review also looked at whether adding extra movements to the Epley manoeuvre made it more effective, but did not find any evidence to support this. Finally, this review looked at whether applying a force to the outer ear through mastoid oscillation increases the success rate of the Epley manoeuvre, but did not find any evidence to support this. Both the Epley manoeuvre and the addition of postural restrictions are safe, with only a few reports of minor complications.

This review includes four studies with 231 participants who received either medical or surgical treatment for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps. All four studies were randomised controlled trials. The studies compared different types of surgery with various types and doses of systemic and topical steroids and antibiotics. Three studies looked at the effects of removing nasal polyps (polypectomy) versus giving oral steroids, giving oral steroids together with an intranasal steroid, or a combination of oral antibiotic and intranasal steroid. The fourth study looked at the effects of endoscopic sinus surgery combined with intranasal steroids versus oral steroids combined with high dose intranasal steroids. All four studies involved people who had nasal polyps and chronic rhinosinusitis; however, the severity of the disease varied between studies. The length of follow-up was between 6 weeks and 24 months. The main outcome measures were symptom scores and quality of life measures. The studies did not show any important differences between groups in either the patient-reported symptom scores or the quality of life scores. However, it should be noted that the study comparing endoscopic sinus surgery with systemic steroids showed a significant improvement in the endoscopic appearance of the sinuses after surgery. The studies did not provide information about the rate at which the polyps grew back (recurrence). The studies did not report complication rates but the included papers describe rates of up to 21% for medical treatment and 14.3% for surgical treatment. Nosebleeds (epistaxis) were the most commonly reported complication with both medical and surgical treatments, with severe complications reported rarely. The evidence was not strong enough to conclude that one form of treatment is better than another for patients with chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps. Further research is needed. The quality of the evidence was low or very low. This was due to problems with the way in which the studies were designed, the fact that the studies were too small, the wide variation in the types of treatments used, and the fact that we do not know how the symptoms of this condition affect people's quality of life.

We searched for trials up to 20 February 2018. We found eight randomised controlled trials. Seven of these were from Asia, Africa, Europe, and Latin America, which means that we have considered the potential for bias due to possible differences in participant characteristics. Four trials compared the effects of zinc-fortified foods versus unfortified foods, while the other four compared zinc-fortified foods versus the same foods with an additional nutrient or factor. One to nine months of consumption of zinc-fortified foods led to higher levels of zinc in the blood than foods without added zinc. However, there was no difference in the prevalence of growth problems (stunting, underweight) or in the level of anaemia between people eating foods with added zinc and those eating the same foods without zinc. Adding zinc to foods that already contained other vitamins and minerals did not change the level of zinc in the blood. We could not tell whether this was because the extra zinc was not absorbed or because the other vitamins and minerals neutralised the effect of the zinc. None of the studies reported any harmful effects of adding zinc to foods. Overall, the quality of the evidence was low. This means that we cannot be certain about the results. Further research may change our conclusions. What was studied in the review? We examined the effects of zinc fortification of staple foods (i.e. foods that are eaten every day) on zinc deficiency, growth, and other health problems. We looked at both children and adults. We examined two types of studies: (1) foods fortified with zinc compared with unfortified foods; and (2) foods fortified with zinc and other vitamins and minerals compared with the same foods fortified with other vitamins and minerals but without zinc. What are the main results of the review? We found eight randomised controlled trials. Four trials compared the effect of zinc-fortified staple foods with unfortified foods (comparison 1), and four compared zinc-fortified staple foods in combination with other nutrients/factors with the same foods containing other nutrients or factors without zinc (comparison 2). The interventions lasted between one and nine months. In comparison 1, foods fortified with zinc increased the level of zinc in the blood more than foods without added zinc. However, there was no difference in the level of anaemia, growth problems, or body weight between people eating foods with added zinc and those eating the same foods without zinc. A single trial of addition of zinc to iron in wheat flour did not reduce the proportion of people with zinc deficiency. In comparison 2, we did not find a difference in the level of zinc in the blood among participants consuming foods fortified with zinc plus other nutrients compared with participants consuming the same foods with micronutrients but no added zinc. No study in comparison 2 provided information about growth problems or body weight. There was no reported adverse effect of fortification of foods with zinc on indicators of iron or copper status. How up-to-date is this review? The review authors searched for studies up to 20 February 2018.

We found 11 randomised controlled trials that compared use of non-steroidal antiandrogens with medical or surgical castration in men with metastatic prostate cancer (3060 participants). The evidence is current to 12 November 2015. Non-steroidal antiandrogens had less effect on the prostate cancer than castration, and therefore did not improve overall survival. Moreover, the use of non-steroidal antiandrogens did not delay progression of the cancer. Furthermore, non-steroidal antiandrogens had less effect on reducing the size or spread of the tumor than castration, which also resulted in more treatment failures. Additionally, non-steroidal antiandrogens had worse side effects than castration, resulting in more participants stopping treatment. Common adverse events included breast pain, gynaecomasia (enlargement of the breast tissue in men), and tiredness. On the other hand, non-steroidal antiandrogens decreased some adverse events, including hot flashes, bleeding from the digestive tract, urinary problems such as bed wetting, tiredness and lack of interest in sex. We could not find evidence of differences between individual non-steroidal antiandrogens. The quality of evidence was moderate for overall survival, clinical progression and treatment failure. The quality of evidence for breast pain, gynaecomasia and hot flashes was moderate. The effects of non-steroidal antiandrogens on cancer-specific survival and biochemical progression remained unclear. We conclude that non-steroidal antiandrogens are less effective than castration for advanced prostate cancer, and therefore should no longer be investigated for men with metastatic disease. However, further research is needed for men with non-metastatic advanced prostate cancer.

The review identified eight relevant trials including 600,000 women in the age range 39 to 74 years. The trials were carried out between 1976 and 2003 in the UK, Sweden, Canada, the USA, Australia and the Netherlands. Information about the methods and results were published for 292,929 women. The results were similar when all women were included. This review shows that screening with mammography does reduce the number of deaths from breast cancer before the age of 85, but that the reduction may be smaller than previously thought. Overdiagnosis and overtreatment is a problem: for every 2000 women invited for screening throughout 10 years, one will avoid dying of breast cancer and 10 healthy women, who would not have been diagnosed if there had not been screening, will be treated unnecessarily. Moreover, more than 200 women will experience important psychological distress including anxiety and uncertainty for years because of false positive findings.

In January 2015, we searched medical databases for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) looking at the use of hCG priming in IVM. We found four RCTs, with a total of 522 women, which met our inclusion criteria. One trial did not report outcomes per woman randomised, and so was not included in the analysis. Three trials compared 10,000 units hCG priming with no priming, while one trial compared 20,000 units hCG with 10,000 units hCG. The quality of the evidence was low. The main limitations were lack of blinding (the process by which women are allocated to treatment is known to them and to the researchers analysing the data) and imprecision (variability in the results). When 10,000 units hCG priming was compared to no priming, there was no evidence of a difference in live birth rates (one trial; 82 women; low quality evidence), miscarriage rates (two trials; 282 women; low quality evidence), or clinical pregnancy rates (two trials; 282 women; low quality evidence). Our findings were inconclusive but they did suggest that hCG may be associated with a reduction in clinical pregnancy rates: 22% of women who received no priming achieved pregnancy, while between 7% and 23% of women who received hCG priming did so. The trial comparing 20,000 units hCG with 10,000 units hCG did not report sufficient data to enable us to calculate the odds of live birth, miscarriage or clinical pregnancy. Neither study reported on adverse events (other than miscarriage) or drug reactions. Overall, this review found no conclusive evidence that hCG priming had an effect on live birth, pregnancy, or miscarriage rates in IVM. There was low quality evidence that suggested that hCG priming may reduce clinical pregnancy rates, however, these findings were limited by the small number of data included. As no data were available on adverse events (other than miscarriage) or on drug reactions, we could not adequately assess the safety of hCG priming. We need further evidence from well-designed RCTs before we can come to definitive conclusions about the role of hCG priming, and the optimal dose and timing.

The review authors identified 16 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that assessed the effects of estrogen replacement therapy (ERT) or hormone replacement therapy (HRT) on cognition in healthy postmenopausal women. The majority of the trials had fewer than 1000 participants and lasted less than two years. Pooled results from these trials did not show any beneficial effects of either ERT or HRT on prevention of cognitive decline. In fact, the pooled results suggest that both therapies may even cause adverse effects on cognitive function. This systematic review shows that neither ERT nor HRT can be recommended for the maintenance of cognitive function in older postmenopausal women. More research is needed to find out if younger postmenopausal women can benefit from hormone therapy.

This systematic review included two studies with 880 participants. The studies lasted 12 weeks and compared a combination of the ICS/LABA inhaler (fluticasone and vilanterol) with the LAMA inhaler (tiotropium). Participants had an average age of 63 years and most had received a diagnosis of COPD for over 10 years. Both studies were sponsored by the pharmaceutical company that makes the ICS/LABA inhaler and both were published as full articles. Neither study was at low risk of bias because both were sponsored by the manufacturer of one of the inhalers and one was run at a research institute with close ties to the manufacturer. There were no deaths reported while taking the ICS/LABA inhaler, and four deaths reported while taking the LAMA inhaler. This does not mean that there were four more deaths in the LAMA group as people who died were taken out of the analysis. The number of exacerbations requiring additional treatment was similar in both groups. The studies reported that there were six cases of pneumonia (out of 440 people) in the ICS/LABA group and one case of pneumonia (out of 440 people) in the LAMA group. These numbers do not add up because one person was counted twice (once in each group) but this does not affect the analysis. The studies showed that there was no difference between the groups for other outcomes that we measured. The quality of the evidence was very poor because of the small number of studies and the small number of people in them, and because both studies were sponsored by the manufacturer of one of the inhalers. There was no clear benefit of either treatment over the other. More research is needed before we can be certain which inhaler provides the best treatment for people with COPD.

This systematic review investigated the efficacy of any antidepressant drug or any form of psychotherapy in the treatment of BDD. Four short-term randomised controlled trials, with a total of 169 participants, met the inclusion criteria. Three of the four studies showed a significant reduction in symptom severity, and one study demonstrated that fluoxetine was superior to placebo. However, the number of studies is too small to draw firm conclusions. Future research, including studies in adolescents, the use of other antidepressants, and a range of psychotherapies, is needed to supplement the limited data currently available.

The purpose of this systematic review was to evaluate the beneficial and harmful effects of the different regimens used for the administration of the desensitization protocol. Only three small trials could be included. These evaluated just one regimen for treating hypersensitivity to cotrimoxazole, and all were conducted in the USA. The results showed that the number of patients who had an adverse reaction to cotrimoxazole was reduced by 5% if desensitization is used rather than a rechallenge. Additionally, the number of patients who need to be treated with one protocol or the other before one person would experience an adverse event is 11.14. This information may be useful in clinical practice in settings where cotrimoxazole is widely used, however, there is not enough evidence to support any particular regimen. There is no data available on children, and no trials from developing countries. More research is needed.

We found three completed studies enrolling 148 newborns of very low birth weight. The search was updated in April 2016. Each study compared midazolam versus a placebo (a substance which has no treatment value) and was conducted in a neonatal intensive care unit. One study showed higher sedation levels with midazolam than with placebo but did not specify how the sedation level was measured. Another study reported that midazolam reduced pain, as assessed by the Premature Infant Pain Profile (PIPP) score, when given during routine care procedures. However, the PIPP score has not yet been validated in this population. Midazolam did not reduce the number of babies who experienced bleeding in the brain or other complications. In fact, it increased the risk of such complications. Midazolam did not improve growth, breathing or other aspects of newborn development. It did not reduce the duration of stay in the neonatal intensive care unit. The authors did not report funding from industry sources. Overall, the evidence does not support the use of midazolam to help newborn babies relax during initial intensive care. Its use may be associated with serious side effects. More research is needed to find an effective and safe drug to help newborn babies relax while they receive initial intensive care.

The review authors searched the medical literature up to 30 April 2015 and included 12 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) with 767 participants. RCTs compare treatments to see how they perform under similar conditions. In this case the treatments were either an antibiotic or a placebo (a substance containing no medication) or no treatment at all. The 12 studies included in this review compared quinolone antibiotics with placebo or no treatment in children and adults with non-invasive NTS infection. The studies were conducted in the USA, Canada, Finland, Thailand, Pakistan, India, Bangladesh, Kenya, Turkey, and Spain. Most of the included studies were of reasonable methodological quality but all studies had some limitations. No clear evidence shows that antibiotics make the symptoms of diarrhoea caused by NTS go away from two to four days earlier than no treatment or a placebo. This result was the same for both children and adults. However, there is some evidence that people treated with antibiotics are less likely to have bacteria in their stools during the first week of treatment. One month after treatment, there was an increased likelihood that the same Salmonella serovar had been passed on to others. This could be due to the fact that people who received antibiotics may have had a longer time to excrete the bacteria before the follow-up test. Antibiotics did not affect the outcome of whether the person developed invasive disease, including bacteraemia, meningitis, osteomyelitis, pneumonia, and arthritis. More side effects were seen with antibiotics than with placebo or no treatment. The most common side effects were nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and rash. Antibiotics may cause harmful side effects such as allergic reactions and blood disorders. Further research is needed to determine if antibiotics are safe and effective in certain groups of people, i.e. infants, the elderly, and those with severe disease.

The evidence is current to September 2015. We included 23 studies involving 4192 critical illness patients. The studies were very different in terms of the type of patients studied, types of infections, severity of the disease, laboratory tests used, cut-off values selected for the IL-6 test, and how the doctors diagnosed sepsis. The percentage of patients with sepsis also differed considerably across studies. Our analysis suggests that an IL-6 test might not be very accurate for the diagnosis of sepsis in critical illness. Based on our results, we would expect that in 1000 patients tested, 330 patients would receive appropriate and timely antibiotic therapy, while 130 patients would be wrongly considered to have sepsis. Additionally, 370 patients would avoid unnecessary antibiotic therapy, but 170 patients would be undiagnosed. These numbers should be interpreted with caution because of the differences across studies and the potential number of false positives and false negatives. There are 20 more studies that are pending publication, which may change the conclusions of the review. New studies that assess the accuracy of interleukin-6 for the diagnosis of sepsis in adults are needed.

The review authors searched the medical literature up to March 2018. We identified 29 relevant studies involving 5718 people with surgical wounds. Four of these studies compared the use of dressings with no dressing; the remaining 25 studies compared one type of dressing with another. A range of different dressings were included in the studies, including basic wound contact dressings, film dressings, hydrocolloid dressings, silver-containing dressings, and gauze dressings. The quality of the evidence was generally poor. Studies were small, reported few complications from wounds and were often not clearly reported. It is uncertain whether covering surgical wounds healing by primary intention with wound dressings reduces the risk of SSI, or whether any particular wound dressing is more effective than others in reducing the risk of SSI, improving scarring, reducing pain, improving acceptability to patients, or is easier to remove. Overall, there was no clear evidence that one dressing type was better than another, and it is uncertain whether wound exposure increases or decreases the risk of SSI. Therefore, decisions about how to dress wounds healing by primary intention after surgery could be made on the basis of cost, patient preference and other factors.

The authors searched the Cochrane Pregnancy and Childbirth Group's Trials Register. We looked at all randomised controlled studies assessing the effect of dietary supplements on the prevention of postnatal depression. The present review includes two studies with a total of 305 women. One study randomised 179 women to take a selenium yeast tablet (100 µg per day) or a placebo from the first trimester until birth. The other trial randomised 126 women at risk of postpartum depression to receive EPA and DHA supplementation (as fish oil), or placebo. These studies were carried out in the USA and Australia. Both studies were less than 30 months duration. The quality of both studies was good. Taking selenium yeast supplements during pregnancy may have a preventive effect on symptoms of postnatal depression but more research is needed to be sure. The included studies do not provide enough information about side effects. The studies did not report on the effect of other supplements. There is insufficient evidence to conclude that DHA or EPA rich fish oil prevents postnatal depression. More research is needed on the use of omega-3 supplements in the prevention of postnatal depression.

The review authors searched the literature and found 11 randomised controlled studies (803 participants) that met our inclusion criteria. Seven studies were included in the main analysis, which compared the effects on heart function of giving the anthracyclines by a short infusion (less than six hours) versus a longer infusion (six hours or more). These seven studies involved 557 patients and used a range of different drugs (doxorubicin, epirubicin, idarubicin, and liposomal doxorubicin). All patients had solid tumours, and most were over 18 years old. However, one study that included 40 children was also included. For the comparison of short versus long infusion, the risk of heart failure was reduced by 73% in those who received the drug by a long infusion, although the result was based on just five studies and 557 patients. If the anthracycline infusion is shortened, the risk of heart failure may increase. However, only three of the seven studies included patients with leukaemia, so this finding relates mainly to patients with solid tumours. There was no clear evidence of a difference in the chance of heart problems between patients given very high doses (over 60 mg/m2) and those given lower doses of doxorubicin or epirubicin. However, only one study addressed this issue for each drug, and the studies were small. For liposomal doxorubicin, the chance of heart problems was similar for all patients, irrespective of whether they were given a low or high dose. This was investigated in one small study. The authors of one study that tried to find out the optimal dose of doxorubicin for children reported that the chance of heart problems was very low, but this study was very small and needs to be confirmed in other studies. Overall, more studies are needed in both children and adults, and in patients with leukaemias and solid tumours.

We searched for relevant medical trials up to April 2017. We found 37 studies with a total of 3110 people who took part. These studies included both adults and children with brain injury caused by various types of trauma. The cooling process started either as soon as they arrived at the hospital or after a surgical operation to remove a blood clot or other material from the brain. All treatments were given for at least 24 hours. Some studies cooled the brain to 32°C, some to 33°C and one study cooled the brain to 35°C. One study repeated the hypothermia treatment cycle twice. Fourteen studies reported on the number of people who developed pneumonia. The temperature at which the brain is cooled may be an important factor; however, it was difficult to compare results between studies because different temperatures were used and the duration of treatment varied. The duration of follow-up also differed between studies. We could not combine the results from individual studies because of differences in study design, the type of brain injury and the treatments used. This means that we do not know whether cooling the brain is helpful or harmful for people with brain injury from trauma. More research is needed. The quality of the evidence was very low for studies reporting the effects of hypothermia on death and the chance of making a good recovery. The quality of the evidence was low for the outcome of pneumonia. The included studies were often not well described and it was not possible to tell whether they were conducted in a way that would produce reliable evidence.

This review investigated the effectiveness of family therapy for the treatment of depression. Studies were included if they involved a person who was depressed and at least one other participant in the social network of the depressed person. The studies had to be randomised, which means that the participants were divided into two groups by chance. One group received the intervention, while the other group received either no treatment, or another type of treatment or a waiting list. The results of the present review showed that family therapy may be as effective as other types of psychological therapy for the treatment of depression. However, due to differences between the studies, it is impossible to conclude whether family therapy is better, worse or equal to other types of psychological therapy. Therefore, other types of psychological therapy, for which there is already evidence, should be used instead of family therapy. This conclusion is based on only four small studies, so more research is needed to find out if family therapy is truly ineffective for the treatment of depression.

This review assessed the efficacy and safety of Duxil for people with dementia. The authors only included three trials with a total of 206 participants, all patients had vascular dementia. The trials showed some beneficial effects of Duxil on cognitive function but failed to show an improvement of activities of daily living. The included trials did not report any information about behaviour, death and quality of life of the patients. Small number of trials and probably publication bias limited the ability of this review to draw firm conclusions. More research is needed.

The authors searched the medical literature up to January 2015 and identified seven randomised controlled trials that compared the outcomes of patients who had a drain inserted after removal of lymph nodes in the axilla with outcomes of patients who did not have a drain. A total of 960 patients were included in these seven trials. The trials were generally of poor methodological quality, which limits the reliability of the evidence. The overall analysis showed that the odds of developing a seroma were reduced by 54% if a drain was inserted, although there was no reduction in the total amount of fluid expressed from the fluid collections, or the number of times the fluid collections needed to be aspirated. However, the drained group stayed in hospital for an average of 1.5 days longer than the un-drained group. No differences were observed in terms of infection, haematoma (collection of blood) or lymphoedema (excess fluid collection) between the two groups. The authors concluded that the use of drains reduces the risk of developing a seroma but increases the length of hospital stay.

This review examined the relationship between eating foods or supplements that contain flavonoids and the risk of bowel cancer or precancerous lesions in the bowel. We analysed eight studies that included over 390,000 participants in total. The studies assessed different types of flavonoids; isoflavones (found in soy products), flavanols (found in cocoa), flavanones (found in citrus fruits), flavonols (found in apples and onions) and anthocyanins (found in berries). We did not find any clear evidence that flavonoids as a group lowered the risk of bowel cancer or precancerous lesions. However there was some weak evidence that eating foods rich in flavanols may reduce the risk of bowel cancer. More research is needed to confirm this finding before we can recommend increasing our consumption of flavanols to lower bowel cancer risk. It is difficult to estimate how much flavonoids we eat and this limits the usefulness of observational studies like those included in this review. Future studies should focus on the effects of dietary interventions, such as taking flavonoid supplements or drinking flavonoid-rich beverages, on the risk of bowel cancer.

This review includes seven trials with 1369 participants. All trials were at high risk of bias. Only two trials reported on the proportion of patients with a sustained loss of virus from the blood. Extension of the treatment period from 48 to 72 weeks led to an increase in this proportion from 26.8% to 32.7% (absolute improvement 5.9 percentage points, number needed to treat 17) and from 41.7% to 53.1% (absolute improvement 11.4 percentage points, number needed to treat nine) according to two different definitions of slow responders. No significant differences were seen in the proportion of patients who experienced a relapse (reappearance of the virus in the blood) or in the proportion of patients with side effects. No deaths were reported. It is possible that these observed intervention effects were due to systematic errors (bias) as well as random errors (play of chance). Therefore, we conclude that more studies are needed to find out if the treatment period should be extended for patients who respond too slowly to standard treatment.

We included only two studies in this review. Both studies evaluated the ability of EUS to correctly identify pancreatic cancer as not resectable. One study included 25 participants and the other study included 9 participants. We are uncertain whether EUS can accurately identify all cancers that cannot be surgically removed because the results varied widely between the two studies. On average, 61 out of 100 people with what appears to be a surgically removable tumor on CT scan will actually have cancer that cannot be surgically removed according to EUS. This means that 39 out of 100 people with what appears to be a surgically removable tumor on CT scan will have cancer that can be surgically removed according to EUS. Similarly, we are also uncertain whether EUS can accurately identify all cancers that can be surgically removed because the results varied between the two studies. On average, 38 out of 100 people with what appears to be a surgically removable tumor on CT scan will actually have cancer that can be surgically removed according to EUS. This means that 62 out of 100 people with what appears to be a surgically removable tumor on CT scan will have cancer that cannot be surgically removed according to EUS. This means that in 13 out of 100 people with what appears to be a surgically removable tumor on CT scan, EUS will incorrectly indicate that the cancer cannot be surgically removed. In 20 out of 100 people with what appears to be a surgically removable tumor on CT scan, EUS will incorrectly indicate that the cancer can be surgically removed. We are uncertain as to whether EUS is useful in people with pancreatic cancer found to have a potentially surgically removable tumor on CT scan.

We found 34 studies that had recruited 2169 participants with blepharitis; 20 studies involved 1661 participants with anterior blepharitis and 14 studies involved 508 participants with posterior blepharitis. However, the quality and types of the studies, as well as the interventions being assessed, varied substantially. This made it difficult to combine the results from these studies in a useful way. We found that topical antibiotics were effective at eliminating bacteria from the eyelid margin but their effect on symptoms was less clear. The effectiveness of other treatments, such as topical corticosteroids and oral antibiotics, were not clearly demonstrated. Furthermore, many of the studies were only conducted for a short period of time, which makes it difficult to assess the long-term effects of treatments for this chronic condition. Our review highlights the need for more high-quality studies that involve a larger number of participants who have been followed up for a longer time. These studies should compare different treatments to lid hygiene measures, which are known to be effective, and should be designed so that each group of people with a similar form of blepharitis is compared. They should use instruments that measure symptoms and quality of life, rather than just counting the number of adverse events.

The Cochrane ENT authors searched for all available literature up to 30 November 2016. We found one eligible study with a total of 23 participants. The study compared the use of photodynamic therapy in addition to surgery versus surgery alone in patients with recurrent respiratory papillomatosis. However, this study had several major flaws that have resulted in very low-quality evidence. There were no differences between the group of people who received photodynamic therapy and the group who did not in terms of time to next recurrence of papillomas, number of surgeries performed, or amount of tissue removed during surgery. There was no significant difference in the size of the warts before or after treatment (as measured by laryngoscopy). A child in the photodynamic therapy group required intubation because of excessive swelling of the airway. More research is needed to see if using photodynamic therapy in addition to surgery is beneficial to people with recurrent respiratory papillomavirus. Future studies must be designed to measure outcomes that matter most to patients, such as improvements in breathing or voice quality, and quality of life. These studies need to be conducted over a longer period so we can get a true sense of any benefits or harms of this treatment.

This review included 42 studies with 4220 participants. The studies were of mixed methodological quality: only three studies fulfilled all the items of the Quality Assessment of Diagnostic Accuracy Studies (QUADAS) document, which outlines the criteria that a study should report in order to be considered of high quality. Of the 16 studies that evaluated the ability of CDUS to correctly identify an endoleak, only one study described the methods used to ensure that the person interpreting the images was unaware of the result of the other imaging test. Of the 11 studies that evaluated the ability of CE-CDUS to correctly identify an endoleak, four studies did not report clearly that the person interpreting the images was unaware of the result of the other imaging test. The studies that were included in this review were of different sizes: the smallest had 20 participants and the largest had 1500. The mean age of the participants ranged from 65.0 to 70.9 years. The percentage of participants with a previous diagnosis of an AAA varied from 73.7% to 100%. The results of this review suggest that both CDUS and CE-CDUS have good accuracy for identifying the presence or absence of an endoleak. However, CE-CDUS was found to be more sensitive (94% of endoleaks were identified with CE-CDUS but only 82% were identified with CDUS). This means that CE-CDUS will miss fewer false negative cases than CDUS. The addition of contrast to the ultrasound scan improves the ability to identify an endoleak but does not improve the ability to exclude an endoleak. Therefore, CE-CDUS followed by a CT scan could be a cost-effective way to follow up patients who have undergone an AAA repair.

The purpose of this review was to evaluate the effect of sterile water immersion for pain relief during labour. The review includes seven randomised controlled trials with a total of 766 women. Four used intracutaneous injections, two subcutaneous, and one both. All reported on low back pain in labour only. The maximum period that women were observed was 10 weeks postpartum. Immersion in sterile water was associated with greater reduction in pain, measured on a zero to 10 scale, than was placebo (salt water). More women given sterile water rated their pain as reduced by at least 4 points on the scale than those given placebo. However, the numbers with this level of pain reduction were still quite small, 50% to 60%, compared to 20% to 25% with placebo. This may be an over-estimation of the true effect as women knew they were getting an active treatment. Injection of sterile water caused brief, mild pain; some women refused further injections. There was no significant difference between sterile water and salt water for rates of caesarian section, instrumental delivery, use of epidural analgesia, timing of delivery, or Apgar scores. Only two studies reported that more women treated with sterile water would request the same analgesia in future.
