None reported on women's satisfaction with pain relief, women's sense of control in labour, women's satisfaction with the childbirth experience, mother/baby interaction, rates of breastfeeding, maternal illness, infant long-term outcomes, or cost. The outcomes reported severely limit conclusions for clinical practice. The review found little robust evidence that sterile water is effective for relieving pain in labour. Further large, methodologically rigorous studies are required.

We searched the medical literature in May 2015 for randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) comparing glue versus suture for mesh fixation in patients with a groin hernia. We identified 12 randomised controlled trials with a total of 1932 participants. The mean age of the participants was 58 years and 22% were smokers. The overall postoperative chronic pain in the glue group was reduced by 37% compared with the suture group. However, there was no difference in pain when we looked only at the studies using lightweight mesh. With heavyweight mesh, the benefit of using glue was more pronounced. There was no difference between the two groups regarding hernia recurrence, haematoma (localised collection of blood outside the blood vessels), or any adverse event including deep infection, superficial wound infection, seroma, and persisting numbness. Glue fixed mesh to the abdomen wall faster than suture, and resulted in a shorter operation time. It also led to a shorter hospital stay and earlier return to work. The quality of the evidence ranged from moderate to low. This was due to problems in the design of the studies as well as the small number of patients included in many of the trials. Further research with a larger number of patients and longer follow-up periods is needed.

Surprisingly few studies have rigorously evaluated the effects of technical editing. There is some evidence that the 'package' of technical editing used by biomedical journals improves papers. Many references in biomedical articles are cited or quoted incorrectly.

This review assessed the effectiveness and safety of codeine in treating cancer pain. We found 15 studies using codeine alone or in combination with other painkillers, with a total of 721 adult participants with cancer pain. Codeine was better than placebo, but the size of the studies and the reporting of results were not ideal. Codeine was also comparable to other painkillers (paracetamol, morphine, oxycodone, and tramadol). However, the studies were too small to be able to make any reliable comment on side effects. Based on this information, we would expect that one in 13 people would experience nausea, one in 20 would experience vomiting, and one in 20 would experience constipation when taking codeine. These numbers may be higher in people who take codeine for several days or longer. It is important to remember that these side effects are based on very low quality evidence. This means that we cannot be sure about how often they occur, or how severe they are. More research is needed to answer questions about the long-term safety and effectiveness of codeine.

We included 12 studies with 563 people with SCD between six and 35 years of age. Most were African-American. Sessions were held once or twice a month for up to eight weeks. The way the studies were carried out varied considerably. The studies were quite short and had few participants. We found some evidence that education improves patient knowledge about SCD and that this knowledge stays longer than the knowledge gained by caregivers. Education did not show an effect on the use of health care services, coping, family relations and quality of life. It was unclear whether education had an effect on the recognition of illness-related problems (e.g. pain crises) or adherence to medication. Education decreased patient depression, although the size of the decrease was small. Given the small size of the decrease in depression, it is unclear if this represents a meaningful clinical benefit. Limitations of the studies included a small number of participants and a high drop-out rate (many participants left the studies before the scheduled end). The studies were also very heterogeneous, meaning they were quite different in their design, the interventions and the measures used. The quality of the evidence was low for coping and moderate for patient knowledge, use of health services and depression. Moderate-quality evidence means that we are moderately confident in the results. Further research may change our findings.

This systematic review examined the efficacy and tolerability of brivaracetam as an antiepileptic medication in people with drug-resistant epilepsy. Six clinical trials met our inclusion criteria, comprising a total of 2411 participants. All six studies assessed brivaracetam as an add-on treatment, meaning that it was added to a participant's existing antiepileptic medication regimen. All six studies included adults aged 16 to 80 years old, and the length of each study was between 7 and 16 weeks. One study recruited participants with focal onset seizures, while the remaining five studies included participants with focal onset seizures only. Our results showed that participants receiving brivaracetam as an add-on treatment experienced a greater reduction in seizure frequency and were more likely to achieve freedom from seizures compared to a placebo group. However, participants receiving brivaracetam were also more likely to withdraw from the studies because of side effects than those receiving placebo. Overall, we regarded the quality of evidence provided by the six randomised controlled trials as moderate, which means that further research may change our estimates of how brivaracetam works. The six studies were limited by their small size, and three studies had some methodological weaknesses that could potentially impact on the reliability of their results. Additionally, one study failed to provide information on three of the outcomes that it reported, making it difficult to judge the overall quality of this study. Furthermore, none of the studies included participants younger than 16 years old, and only one included participants with generalised onset seizures. Therefore, our findings are mainly applicable to adult patients with drug-resistant focal epilepsy. Future research should focus on assessing the tolerability and efficacy of brivaracetam during longer treatment periods, and should investigate the efficacy and tolerability of add-on brivaracetam in managing other types of seizures and its use in other age groups.

We looked at existing studies that examined how parents make decisions about vaccination. We searched for randomised trials that evaluated the impact of the way in which vaccination information was given to parents. We also looked at non-randomised studies that explored parents' views and experiences of vaccination information. Most of the studies were from high-income countries, and some focused on the measles, mumps, rubella (MMR) vaccine. The studies showed that parents want more information than they are currently given. They want this information to be balanced, clear and simple, and relevant to their own situation. They want it to be available at a range of places, including outside health services, and in good time before each vaccination appointment. They view health workers as an important source of information, but sometimes have poor experiences with them. It can be difficult for parents to know which information source to trust, and hard to find information that seems unbiased and balanced. Our findings suggest that parents who are more hesitant about vaccination want more information than other parents. The studies reviewed by the project show that it is possible to give parents the kind of information they want. However, more research is needed to confirm some of the findings, particularly in poorer countries.

The aim of this review is to evaluate the effects of individual psychological therapies for anorexia nervosa in adults or older adolescents. We reviewed ten randomised controlled trials (clinical trials where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatments groups) with 599 people with anorexia nervosa. The trials compared various forms of psychological therapy to treatment as usual (TAU; the standard care that one would normally receive in the health service), dietary advice only, or other types of psychological therapy. We found no difference in recovery rates between those who received focal psychodynamic therapy and those treated as usual by a therapist who was not a specialist in eating disorders. There was no difference in weight gain between those who received cognitive analytic therapy and those treated as usual. There was no difference in the number of people completing treatment between any of the therapies and treatment as usual. The quality of the evidence was low or very low, which means we cannot be certain about the findings. Most trials were small, and half of the trials were judged to have a high risk of bias, often because the therapists conducting the outcome assessments knew what treatment group the participant was in. There was a suggestion that focal psychodynamic therapy might be superior to TAU, but this is in the context of TAU performing poorly. An alternative control condition of dietary advice alone appeared to be unacceptable, but this is based on just one trial. We need larger trials of better quality to evaluate the effects of individual psychological therapies for anorexia nervosa.

This systematic review included data from 516 participants from three randomized controlled trials that compared the effectiveness of oil or gas in the treatment of RDs with PVR. Two of the studies were from the USA, and one was a multi-center international study. The weight of the evidence suggests that the use of perfluoropropane gas is similar to that of silicone oil. Perfluoropropane gas may cause a slight improvement in vision, but this result should be interpreted cautiously as it was based on only two trials. Silicone oil produced better results than sulfur hexafluoride gas, especially with regard to the re-attachment of the retina. However, the difference between these two compounds was less obvious after two years. Heavy silicone oil did not produce better results than standard silicone oil. As there are few good quality studies, we cannot be certain whether one fluid is better than another. More research is needed to determine the best way to treat retinal detachment with PVR.

This review aimed to evaluate whether giving birth early would be more beneficial for the mother or baby, or both, in comparison with waiting for a natural birth. The search for studies was updated on 30 November 2016. We found five randomised controlled studies involving 1819 women. The quality of the studies was acceptable, although it was possible that the women and staff knew which treatment they were receiving. This may have affected aspects of their care and how decisions were made. One trial involved women with pre-eclampsia, the other four involved women with hypertension. The trials were quite small and had some limitations, but we found two very large, well-conducted trials that contributed most of the evidence. Women who had an early birth had a lower risk of combined problems of high blood pressure and protein in the urine (known as pre-eclampsia), of a stroke-like condition called HELLP syndrome, and of kidney failure. There was no clear difference in the number of babies that died, but there was a higher risk of breathing difficulties and admission to neonatal intensive care for the babies of mothers with hypertension or pre-eclampsia who gave birth early. Early birth did not make a difference to the number of women having a caesarian section, or to how long the mothers and babies stayed in hospital. Overall, the evidence showed that a planned early birth is safer for the mother than waiting for a natural birth, once the baby is at least 34 weeks old, but the evidence for the baby was inconclusive. More research is needed to find out if there are different time limits for the various conditions classified as hypertension disorders of pregnancy. Studies in the future should include information on the different types of hypertensive disorders and the best time for delivery for each condition, along with outcomes for the baby and mother, caesarean section, and length of hospital stay after delivery for the mother and baby.

This review included six randomised controlled studies and found that those studies which compared regular infusions of clotting factor concentrate (prophylaxis) with on-demand therapy (treatment after a bleeding event had occurred) demonstrated a clear benefit in terms of reduced bleeding frequencies and improved joint health in children with hemophilia. However, this review also highlighted the need for further research into the effects of clotting factor concentrate in people with existing joint disease and into the optimal dosages of clotting factor concentrate required.

This review included 13 clinical trials on 1824 people with Burkitt leukaemia or lymphoma. The trials were quite old. All used different drugs in different combinations and all compared these drug combinations with each other. It was difficult to compare these trials because they were so different from each other. Individual trials reported the results of the study in different ways, making it difficult to be sure how reliable the results were. Due to these problems, we could not find an overall result for the effect of chemotherapy on the length of time people lived. However, less intensive chemotherapy regimens seem to produce similar results with fewer side effects. Future trials should use the same drugs and measures of outcome so that results can be compared more easily. They should also report their methods clearly, so that the reliability of the results is clear.

The authors looked at whether there is a difference in the level of protection between IM and IV administration of anti-D immunoglobulin. Two randomised controlled trials with a total of 447 RhD negative women were included. The women received a 1500 IU (300 microgram) dose of Rhophylac during week 28 of gestation. The anti-D levels after IV and IM administration were similar from two to three weeks post-administration. It appears that IM and IV administration of anti-D are equally effective. The number of included studies and the number of participants are not enough to assess whether there are any differences. Anti-D can be administered by IM or IV injection. The choice of IM or IV route of administration will depend on the available preparations, the dose to be administered and also on the patients' preferences. This review found insufficient information upon which to guide practice due to the limited number of included studies, small sample sizes and methodological limitations.

This review aimed to assess the benefits and harms of antiplatelet drugs that block ADP receptors (clopidogrel and ticlopidine) for the prevention of death, cardiovascular diseases and other harmful events in people with diabetes. We identified eight studies involving 21,379 patients with diabetes. However, very few relevant outcomes for patients with diabetes were reported. One small study demonstrated no beneficial effect of ticlopidine, an older drug of this class, for reducing the risk of death, heart attack or stroke when compared to placebo. Two studies showed clopidogrel was at least as effective as aspirin in reducing the risk of stroke, but there were no data to show whether it was better or worse than aspirin at reducing the other outcomes. There were no data available on the effects of clopidogrel in combination with aspirin compared to aspirin alone, or the effects of clopidogrel versus other antiplatelet drugs in preventing death, heart attacks or strokes. There were also no data available on the side effects of these drugs in patients with diabetes, or the cost effectiveness of these drugs in this population. More research is needed to determine the effects of antiplatelet drugs that block ADP receptors for the primary prevention of cardiovascular disease in patients with diabetes.

The review included ten trials with 191 adults and children taking part. The trials assessed the effects of NIV on the ability to clear mucus from the lungs and on sleep quality. The review found that NIV may help people with cystic fibrosis to clear their airways, but more research is needed. When NIV was used instead of other chest physiotherapy techniques, lung function was better after treatment. People with cystic fibrosis also reported that they preferred NIV. However, these results were based on a small number of people who took part in the trial. Further studies with more people taking part are needed. The review also found that NIV during sleep may help to improve the exchange of gases in the lungs. This was evident when NIV was used instead of oxygen therapy. However, this was only seen in people who had moderate to severe lung disease. More research is needed to confirm this finding. The review also found that NIV does not seem to make a significant difference to exercise tolerance. This is based on one study with a small number of people taking part. More research is needed to reach a conclusion on this issue. Finally, the review found that NIV is generally safe. All three trials which reported side effects noted them to be relatively minor problems, including pain on breathing tests, inability to tolerate the NIV mask, decrease in appetite, abdominal distension, bloat and a pocket of air in the chest. These side effects occurred in people receiving NIV for both airway clearance and overnight support. The trial evaluating NIV for airway clearance reported that one person withdrew from the trial due to pain on respiratory muscle testing. The trial evaluating NIV for overnight support reported that one person could not tolerate an increase in positive airway pressure. A second trial, also evaluating NIV for overnight support, reported that one person did not tolerate the NIV mask, one person developed a pocket of air in the chest when breathing room air and two people experienced bloating, which got worse with an increase in positive airway pressure, but resolved when the pressure was decreased.

The aim of this review is to assess the benefit and harm of non-invasive ventilation at night in people with COPD. This review could include seven randomised controlled trials with a total of 245 participants. All studies were performed in out-patients who suffered from hypercapnia (elevated carbon dioxide levels in the blood) during sleep. The mean age of the participants was 68 years and the duration of the treatment was a minimum of three months. Non-invasive ventilation consisted of a nasal mask delivering a continuous positive airway pressure or a pressure support. The mean effect on the distance walked in six minutes was small at 27.7 metres. We are however unsure if this result is correct, as the 95% confidence interval was between -28.1 and 66.3 metres. This means that the actual effect of NIPPV may be anywhere between 28.1 metres less and 66.3 metres more than the control group. For the other outcomes no significant differences were found. The small number of studies included in this review prevents us from reaching definitive conclusions about the effect of NIPPV in COPD.

We searched for evidence on 30 November 2016 and found four randomised controlled studies, involving 1190 women. These studies compared women with suspected large babies who had an induction of labour against women with suspected large babies who went into labour spontaneously. Induction did not clearly reduce the risk of caesarian sections or instrumental deliveries, but it did reduce the risk of shoulder problems in the baby including fractures, and problems with the baby's shoulders getting stuck (shoulder dystocia). Birthweight was slightly lower in the induction group. In one study, more women in the induction group had serious perineal trauma, but this was not seen in the other studies. No clear difference was seen in Apgar scores less than seven at five minutes or cord blood pH between groups. The quality of the evidence was affected by the fact that women knew which group they were in, although we judged this not to be a major concern. Another factor which reduced our confidence in some findings was the wide variation in how much the studies weighed the babies at birth. Overall, although induction of labour does not seem to change the risk of brachial plexus injury, it may reduce the chance of a baby having a birth fracture. However, it may increase the chances of perineal trauma during childbirth. Induction of labour will remain a contentious issue until more information becomes available.

The review includes 159 trials, and 56 trials with 95,286 participants provided usable data on mortality. The age of participants ranged from 18 to 107 years. Most trials included women older than 70 years. The majority of the trials were well designed. All trials were conducted in high-income countries. Vitamin D administration lasted for a weighted mean of 4.4 years. More than 8% of participants dropped out. This systematic review suggests that vitamin D3 decreases mortality in elderly people living independently or in institutional care. Vitamin D2, alfacalcidol and calcitriol had no statistically significant beneficial effects on mortality. Vitamin D3 combined with calcium increased the risk of kidney stones. Both alfacalcidol and calcitriol increased the risk of high blood calcium levels. Further trials are needed.

The objective of this review was to evaluate the effectiveness and safety of medications for weight loss or weight loss maintenance in people who have type 2 diabetes mellitus. A comprehensive search of the literature was conducted, and twenty-two randomized controlled trials were found. A total of 296 individuals were included in the review of the antidepressant drug fluoxetine; 2036 individuals were included in the review of the anti-obesity drug orlistat; and 1047 individuals were included in the review of the anti-obesity drug sibutramine. These medications resulted in modest weight loss, when compared to placebo. The average amount of weight lost was 5.1 kg with fluoxetine, 2.0 kg with orlistat, and 5.1 kg with sibutramine. These drugs also modestly and significantly reduced blood sugar levels as measured by glycated hemoglobin A1c. However, all medications were associated with side effects. Most of the side effects were mild in severity, but gastrointestinal side effects were common with orlistat; tremor, somnolence and sweating with fluoxetine; and palpitations with sibutramine. Serious adverse events were infrequent. Sibutramine is currently not available in the United States. More research is needed on the safety of this medication. There was insufficient information available on other medications used for weight loss or weight control in people who have type 2 diabetes.

We searched scientific databases for clinical trials comparing the efficacy and safety of valproic acid versus carbamazepine as monotherapies in people with newly diagnosed focal epilepsy. The evidence is current to January 2015. We found five studies involving 734 participants. The quality of the included studies was variable; we assessed one study as high quality and the remaining four as low quality. Not all studies reported the outcomes we had selected for our review, making it difficult to pool the results. No clear difference between the two drugs was shown in terms of the time to withdrawal from the trial medication, but valproic acid was associated with a shorter time to the first seizure after randomisation. Valproic acid caused significantly more weight gain and less skin rash and drowsiness than carbamazepine. No differences in visual field defects and visual disturbances were observed. Overall, the data are insufficient to assess the balance of benefits and harms of valproic acid compared with carbamazepine as a treatment for epilepsy. Future studies should focus on investigating the causes of visual field defects in people taking valproic acid and exploring ways of preventing them. Moreover, future monotherapy studies of epilepsy should report their results according to the recommendations of the International League Against Epilepsy (ILAE) Commission, and their methodological quality should be improved.

The evidence is current to 20 February 2015. We found four randomised controlled trials (RCTs), but only two RCTs, including 600 adults with multiple myeloma or non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, could be included in the analysis. Both trials compared people who were given G-CSF and plerixafor to people who were given G-CSF and a placebo (pretend treatment). This review shows that the addition of plerixafor leads to increased stem cell collection in a shorter time, but there was insufficient evidence to prove that this also leads to longer survival or fewer adverse events. The two trials included in the review were published several times, e.g. because the publisher changed the layout. Two more RCTs examined the addition of plerixafor to a G-CSF mobilisation regimen, but terminated early and did not publish any results. Another RCT with 100 participants was completed, but has not yet published its outcomes. Because of the unpublished RCTs, it is possible that our review is affected by unpublished studies that favour plerixafor and therefore make the evidence seem more positive than it really is. The quality of the evidence for the assessed outcomes ranged from very low to high.

This review assessed the effectiveness of cones in the treatment of SUI in women. Evidence from this review shows that cones are better than no active treatment, and are possibly as effective as pelvic floor muscle training or electrical stimulation. More research is needed to confirm these findings. The review authors recommend that cones could be offered as one treatment option for SUI, if women find them acceptable.

We searched scientific databases and trial registers to identify randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) looking at the effect of education, decision aids, and self-monitoring in addition to usual care versus usual care alone in people with atrial fibrillation taking warfarin. We included 11 trials with a total of 2246 participants. The trials showed that there was not enough evidence to say whether the interventions improved the time spent in therapeutic range. There was some evidence that education interventions may decrease anxiety and depression and that decision aids may increase decisional conflict (unertainty about what was best decision for them), however all results were imprecise. Overall, there was not enough information to make firm conclusions about the impact of the interventions on anticoagulation control in AF patients and the mechanisms by which they are successful. It is also important to explore the psychological implications for patients suffering from this long-term chronic condition.

This review of 70 randomised controlled trials involving 11,487 women found that PGE2 reduced the need for caesarean section and increased the likelihood of vaginal delivery within 24 hours. PGE2 also increases uterine hyperstimulation with changes in the baby's heart rate, but does not further increase the operative delivery rate. This means that women may experience more pain, but their risk of needing an emergency caesarean section is reduced. The medication is equally effective in tablet, gel or pessary form. It can cause side effects, including vomiting, shivering, abdominal pain and diarrhoea.

Study characteristics: Five studies with a total of 247 very low birthweight infants were reviewed. The quality of the studies was variable. Results: When compared to an incubator, cot-nursing had no effect on mean body temperature though the one study reporting on episodes of overheating showed that these were more common in the cot-group. No studies reported on mortality before discharge from hospital. Cot-nursing resulted in less breastfeeding at discharge from hospital. The use of a heated waterbed instead of an incubator also had no effect on body temperature, weight gain or death in the first week after birth. It is not possible to make any recommendations for the care of these infants using cots in the nursery or the home on the basis of this review. Further research is needed.

The evidence in this review is up-to-date as of 20 February 2018. We searched scientific databases and found three studies including 146 participants. Both authors agreed on the results. The two surgical techniques may be equally successful at exposing PDCs. One study reported on complications following surgery and found two in the closed group: a post-operative infection requiring antibiotics and pain during alignment of the canine as the gold chain penetrated through the gum tissue of the palate. Individual studies did not find any differences between the surgical techniques for the other outcomes listed in this review. The quality of the evidence was low, which means we cannot be certain about our conclusions. This could be due to the small number of people in the studies, errors in how the studies were carried out or both. We suggest that further research is needed to strengthen the evidence.

We identified only three completed studies involving 244 women. The studies were considered to be at high risk of bias. The prostaglandins used were PGE2 (sulprostone) in 50 participants and PGE1 (misoprostol) in 194 participants at a dose of 250 mcg and 800 mcg respectively. There were no clear differences between the group of women who had prostaglandin and the group who had placebo, in terms of the number of women whose placentas were removed by the doctors, the number with heavy bleeding or the average amount of blood lost or the speed at which the afterbirth was delivered. Vomiting, headache, pain and nausea between injection and discharge from the labour ward were similar in both groups. Shivering was more common in the women receiving prostaglandin. There were no deaths among the women in either group. At present, there is insufficient evidence to show whether prostaglandins are effective in managing retained placenta following the birth of the baby. The included studies were of poor quality and the evidence obtained from them is not reliable. More research is needed.

This review found that in preterm infants with respiratory distress, the application of continuous distending pressure either via a face mask (six studies, 355 infants), pernasal catheter (two studies, 60 infants) or endotracheal tube (one study, 20 infants) is associated with a lower chance of treatment failure (the need for assisted ventilation). The use of continuous distending pressure also lowers the chances of dying. However, it increases the chances of air entering the lung tissue (pneumothorax). This review did not find any difference in chronic lung injury (bronchopulmonary dysplasia) and long-term follow-up (at nine to 14 years of age). Four out of six of these trials were conducted in the 1970s. Therefore, the applicability of these results to current practice is difficult to assess. Further research is required to determine whether the benefits outweigh the risks and to determine the best mode of applying continuous distending pressure.

This review (six studies involving 157 participants) found that there is no research evidence to suggest that foam wound dressings are more effective in healing diabetic foot ulcers than other types of dressing. However, all the studies were small and the results inconclusive.

This review compared different types of interventions for patients with chronic pancreatitis and a dilated pancreatic duct. We included three randomised controlled trials (RCTs): two trials compared endoscopic intervention with surgical intervention; one trial compared surgical intervention with conservative treatment (non-operative treatment). The two trials comparing endoscopic intervention with surgical intervention included a total of 111 participants: 55 in the endoscopic group and 56 in the surgical group. The review showed that surgical intervention relieved pain significantly better than endoscopic intervention in the short and long term. Surgical treatment also resulted in a better quality of life and better preservation of pancreatic function. The one trial comparing surgical intervention with non-operative treatment (conservative treatment) showed that surgery resulted in a higher percentage of participants with pain relief and better preservation of pancreatic function. However, this trial had several methodological limitations, and the number of participants was relatively low. More studies are needed to confirm whether surgery should be performed in an early stage of chronic pancreatitis.

We found only one randomised controlled trial which compared early post-operative showering or bathing with delayed washing of the dressing. The trial involved 857 patients who had had minor skin excisions in the primary care setting. The dressing was removed after 12 hours and normal bathing was resumed in half of the patients, while the other patients' dressings were not removed until at least 48 hours after surgery. The only outcome reported in the trial was surgical site infections. There were no significant differences between the two groups with respect to the number of patients who developed these infections (early bathing - 8.5%, delayed bathing - 8.8%). However, the results of this trial do not preclude there being a large benefit or harm associated with either strategy. In conclusion, we did not find any randomised controlled trials that measured our primary outcomes of interest (all-cause mortality, wound complication, allergic reaction to the wound dressing, length of hospital stay, quality of life or cost). We found that there was no clear evidence from the single included trial that early post-operative showering or bathing decreased the rate of surgical site infections. Further trials are needed to determine whether early post-operative showering or bathing is safe and beneficial for patients undergoing surgery.

We found two randomised controlled trials (RCTs), which involved 75 people with leprosy-type neuropathy. One trial compared oral prednisone with no treatment and followed up for 12 weeks. The other trial compared a short course of high-dose monthly oral dexamethasone with daily oral prednisolone and followed up for one year. The only RCT that we found, which compared oral prednisone with no treatment, showed improvement in 12 of 19 people treated with prednisone, and in five of 16 people who received no treatment. This supports the use of corticosteroids, but we are very unsure of this result because the RCT was small and had a high risk of bias. The trial did not report side effects in detail, but one person treated with prednisone died. A trial comparing a short course of high-dose monthly oral dexamethasone with daily oral prednisolone showed little or no difference between them in number of people whose symptoms improved, ability to perform activities of daily living, or strength in their hands. However, this trial was small and had a low risk of bias, so the results are unlikely to be due to chance. Side effects were similar with each regimen, except that sleeplessness was less common with monthly dexamethasone, and a condition called'moon facies' (which gives the face a rounded, moon-like shape) was probably less common with dexamethasone. We need more research to find out which people might respond to corticosteroids.

We included six studies involving 2100 people with asthma. Four studies compared telephone or email advice after a face-to-face consultation with a further face-to-face consultation. One large study looked at whether sending letters to people who had not recently attended a face-to-face appointment was as good as a phone call. One study compared the safety of reducing oral steroids using either a face-to-face or a remote consultation. The studies included people with varying degrees of asthma, including some with mild asthma, but most with more severe asthma. Studies were well conducted but it would have been better if more people had not left the studies early. It was difficult to combine the results because each study did different things. However, we found that having a remote check-up did not make people's asthma worse, and did not mean that they had more asthma attacks than with a face-to-face consultation. This was true for asthma attacks that needed additional medication, as well as those that did not. Remote check-ups were just as good as face-to-face consultations for controlling asthma and improving quality of life. However, we cannot be absolutely sure about this because the methods used in the trials meant that we could not be certain that the patients and clinicians knew which type of consultation they were having. The largest study showed that people were more likely to have an asthma review if they were offered a telephone call or letter. However, we do not know if their asthma was improved as a result of the telephone call. The study that reduced oral steroids showed that it was just as safe using a remote consultation as it was face-to-face. We conclude that there is currently no evidence to show whether remote consultations are as good as face-to-face consultations for asthma, but we think that this is unlikely to change when more studies are published. More research is needed to find out if remote consultations are as safe and effective as face-to-face consultations.

The authors included three studies with a total of 212 participants in this review. The mean age of the children was 12 years. Two studies compared an intensive exercise regimen with a control group and one compared an exercise regimen with no treatment. The duration of the exercise regimens varied from two weeks to six months. The exercises were supervised and ranged from 50 minutes to 1 hour per day. The intensity of the exercises was moderate, according to the studies. The outcomes of the studies were somewhat different, but all studies did show some benefit of exercise therapy for children with juvenile idiopathic arthritis. The methodological quality of all the included studies was good. However, the small number of studies limits the conclusions. Overall, the evidence shows that exercise therapy may not lead to improvement in function, quality of life, aerobic capacity or pain in children with juvenile idiopathic arthritis. However, these results should be interpreted with caution as the number of studies is low.

This review includes 19 studies involving 2663 people with acute bronchiolitis. All studies included children aged 24 months or younger. Outpatients were those who participated in studies that compared antibiotics versus placebo (a substance containing no medication) given at home (or in the emergency department) to children with mild or moderate disease. Inpatients were those who participated in studies that compared antibiotics versus placebo given while in the hospital, but not in the intensive care unit (ICU), and ICU patients were those participating in studies conducted in the ICU. Antibiotics had a small effect on reducing the chance of treatment failure (i.e. the need for additional treatment) at one month: the risk for treatment failure was 8% lower in the group treated with antibiotics. This result holds particularly true for older antibiotics no longer in use. There was no effect on mortality (death) nor on the number of exacerbations (worsening of illness) experienced by outpatients within two months. Health-related quality of life was assessed in only one study, which did not find a difference between the group treated with antibiotics and the placebo group. Two studies assessed the effect of antibiotics on inpatients, and their results were inconclusive. The only study conducted in the ICU showed a very large effect on treatment failure and a smaller effect on mortality and length of hospital stay. Overall, antibiotics did not cause more side effects than placebo. Reviewers judged the quality of the evidence as low for outpatients and inpatients, mainly because each outcome was reported in a small number of studies, and/or studies were not well designed. Quality of evidence was moderate for inpatients (excluding ICU patients), based on two well-designed studies, and for ICU patients, based on one large study. It was low for health-related quality of life and high for adverse events (harm). Antibiotics may reduce the chance of treatment failure in children with bronchiolitis, but this effect is small, and there is considerable uncertainty about whether antibiotics are effective in improving other outcomes (e.g. death, length of hospital stay). Effects of antibiotics on health-related quality of life are unknown. This review does not provide enough evidence to support or refute the routine use of antibiotics in the management of bronchiolitis in infants. Future research should focus on identifying clinical signs or biomarkers that can help clinicians decide which children will benefit from antibiotics.

We searched scientific databases for randomised controlled trials (clinical trials where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatments groups) looking at the effects of whole grain diets compared to diets with fewer whole grains or no whole grains on heart health in adults. We did not include studies in children as currently there is no recommended intake of whole grains for children. The evidence is current to September 2015. We found nine trials (1414 participants, aged 24 to 70 years) comparing whole grain diets with mostly white flour diets for 8 to 16 weeks. No studies assessed the effect of whole grain diets on rates of death due to heart disease or on heart attacks, strokes, or need for heart bypass operations. Some studies showed some beneficial changes in blood lipids (such as reduced triglycerides and increased HDL-C (the 'good' cholesterol)) and blood pressure, but other studies showed no benefits. Overall, we found no differences between whole grain and white flour groups for total cholesterol (low-quality evidence), and no studies assessed other CVD risk factors such as waist-to-hip girth ratio, apolipoprotein A1, or C-reactive protein. Four studies were funded by independent national and government funding bodies, and the other studies reported funding or partial funding by organisations with commercial interests in cereals. Currently, there is limited evidence from randomised controlled trials on which to base recommendations about the effects of whole grain diets for the purpose of improving heart health. More long-term studies are needed. The overall quality of the evidence was low as the included studies were small and had some methodological problems, and because only a few studies have looked at the effects of whole grain diets on CVD risk factors.

We searched the scientific literature up to July 2015 and identified 38 relevant randomised controlled trials, involving 1896 participants with acute ankle sprain, that met our inclusion criteria. The quality of the evidence was variable and only one trial was at low risk of bias. Three trials investigated rehabilitation interventions during the immobilisation period after conservative management (treatment such as ice, compression and elevation) of ankle sprain. Two trials showed that an air-stirrup was more effective than an orthosis (a device used to support the affected area) or a walking cast at reducing pain and improving function (measured by the time it takes to complete a self-care activity) in the short term. One trial found that 12 weeks of hypnosis did not reduce activity limitation or improve other outcomes. Ten trials compared the use of a removable type of immobilisation, such as a splint, with cast immobilisation during the immobilisation period after surgical fixation. Removable immobilisation allowed exercise and resulted in less reduction in range of movement, but it also led to more adverse events, most of which were minor. Starting weight-bearing exercises lighty during the immobilisation period after surgical fixation had a small positive effect on the range of movement at the ankle. One study showed that electrical stimulation may help relieve pain in the short term. There was little evidence on what type of support or immobilisation was the best. Five trials investigated different rehabilitation interventions following the immobilisation period. There was no evidence of effect for stretching or manual therapy when added to exercise, or exercise compared with usual care. One trial found that shortwave diathermy (a type of electrical therapy) did not make the ankle any bigger than it was before the injury. Overall, there is limited evidence to support the early re-establishment of weight-bearing and the use of a removable type of immobilisation during the immobilisation period after surgical fixation. It is important to consider potential harms when choosing between these interventions. There is little evidence for rehabilitation interventions during the immobilisation period after conservative management, and no evidence for stretching, manual therapy or exercise compared to usual care following the immobilisation period. Finally, there is little evidence for the effects of different types of splints or casts. Further research is needed to inform the treatment of ankle sprains.

We reviewed the literature on interventions designed to improve ART adherence among children and adolescents living in resource-limited countries. We identified four relevant studies. A home-based nursing programme appeared to improve a child's knowledge about their medications and resulted in more medication refills, but had no effect on the child's immunology. A study of caregiver medication diaries showed that children in the intervention group took fewer doses of their medications, but the difference was not statistically significant. Peer support group therapy for adolescents appeared to help adolescents take more of their medications, as measured by viral load, while a comparison of two different drug regimens showed that the lopinavir/ritonavir regimen was better at suppressing virus than the non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor regimen. Overall, there is little evidence on the effects of interventions designed to improve ART adherence in children and adolescents. Future well-designed evaluations of these interventions are needed.

This review assessed how well fenoprofen worked as an analgesic after an operation. It found that a single 200 mg dose reduced pain by half for 4 to 6 hours in one in three people. This was based on evidence from five studies in 696 people with moderate to severe pain after a variety of surgeries. The number of people who had side effects was similar with fenoprofen and placebo.

This review included six randomised controlled trials with a total of 975 women. Four trials compared pelvic floor muscle training against a control group (with 857 women) and two trials compared pelvic floor muscle training after surgery with surgery alone (with 118 women). Women who received pelvic floor muscle training showed greater improvement in their symptoms and in the severity of their prolapse compared to the control group. They also had better pelvic floor muscle function and fewer urinary problems. When looking specifically at the trials that measured the severity of the prolapse, it was shown that there was an increase in the number of women with an improved stage of prolapse in the pelvic floor muscle training group. However, it should be noted that two out of four trials in this comparison had a significant risk of bias. The two trials comparing pelvic floor muscle training after surgery with surgery alone did not measure prolapse, focusing instead on the effects of the treatment on the strength of the pelvic floor muscles and on urinary symptoms. They found no difference in muscle strength but there was conflicting evidence on the level of improvement in urinary symptoms. This means that we do not yet know whether adding pelvic floor muscle training to surgery is a useful intervention. Further research is needed. Overall, the review shows that pelvic floor muscle training can help to improve the symptoms of pelvic organ prolapse and to reduce the severity of the prolapse. It can be done at home, although regular supervision by a physiotherapist or similar professional may help to improve the technique and therefore the outcome. More research is needed to find out how effective pelvic floor muscle training is when it is used by itself over a longer period of time and how it compares to other treatments such as surgery.

This review identified 14 studies with 1,724 participants with chronic suppurative otitis media (chronic discharge from the ear). The quality of the research was variable and generally poor. The results showed that topical ciprofloxacin and ofloxacin are better at clearing discharge than no medication at all, and better than antiseptics. However, it is unclear how effective topical non-quinolone antibiotics are compared to no medication or antiseptics. There was insufficient evidence to show that quinolone antibiotics are better than non-quinolone antibiotics, but this does not rule out the possibility that they might be. Generally, the studies did not report on the safety of the interventions, or on any potential side effects. More research is needed to assess the safety of using these medications, especially as there is a potential risk of hearing loss associated with some antibiotics. More trials are also needed to see how effective topical antibiotics are at curing chronic suppurative otitis media, and to measure long-term outcomes such as resolution, healing, hearing, or complications.

The review looks at 21 studies with a total of 6253 people. The studies were carried out between 1974 and 2011. Most studies were carried out before 2000. The studies vary considerably in terms of the number of items used in the FRS, the cut points used to define a positive FRS and the type of setting in which the FRS was used. The studies were generally poor quality and it is difficult to relate the findings to current practice. Based on the studies in the review, the summary of results shows that FRS correctly identifies people with schizophrenia 75% to 95% of the time. This means that when someone has a positive FRS, they will actually have schizophrenia 75% to 95% of the time, but when someone does not have a positive FRS, they will not have schizophrenia 50% to 65% of the time. This means that there will be some people with schizophrenia who are missed and will not receive appropriate treatment. There will also be some people who do not have schizophrenia but who will be given a diagnosis of schizophrenia and will be wrongly treated for it. However, it must be remembered that the FRS is just one part of the process of diagnosing schizophrenia and empathetic, considerate use of FRS as a diagnostic aid - with known limitations - should avoid a good proportion of these errors. Newer tests - to be included in future Cochrane reviews - may show better results. However, symptoms of first rank can still be helpful where newer tests are not available - a situation which applies to the initial screening of most people with suspected schizophrenia. FRS remain a simple, quick and useful clinical indicator for an illness of enormous clinical variability.

As the original review identified only two randomised controlled trials (RCTs), we conducted a wide search for trials published up to January 2014. We found eight more relevant studies, making a total for this update of 10 RCTs. Of these, 9 were educational and 1 was a psychological study. The educational studies were delivered in primary- and secondary-care settings. All but one of the included studies used a comparison (control) group that received conventional treatment. One study compared an educational intervention with an additional conventional treatment, so this study was excluded from the meta-analysis. The largest study (n = 992) showed significant reduction in disease severity and improvement in quality of life for both nurse-led and dermatologist-led interventions, when compared with the control group, which received conventional treatment only. In three of five studies, the objective measure of atopic dermatitis severity was statistically significantly better in the intervention group compared with the control groups, although it must be remembered that these results were not all statistically significant when the results were combined across studies. This update includes five new studies, and the inclusion of these has not substantially altered the original review findings. The educational studies in both the original review and this update lack detail about how they were designed and what they consisted of. A lack of rigour in the way these interventions were developed means that we cannot be certain about whether they were effective. However, there is some evidence that both multiprofessional eczema interventions and nurse-led clinics may lead to improvements in disease severity and quality of life for children with atopic eczema. Further research is needed to develop these types of interventions. Furthermore, we do not know whether these interventions are cost-effective compared with other ways of managing atopic eczema. As we did not find any completed RCTs of psychological interventions, we do not know whether such interventions are effective in helping to manage atopic eczema in children.

We identified 14 randomised controlled trials, with a total of 753 participants. We found that people with LRTI who received hyperbaric oxygen were about 20% more likely to have complete coverage of the area treated with healthy tissue after surgery, and were about four times less likely to experience wound breakdown in the irradiated jaw after removal of teeth. There was also some evidence from individual trials of the benefits of hyperbaric oxygen for the treatment of radiation proctitis, and for use of tissue flaps and bone removal from the lower jaw to cover wounds. However, we did not find evidence of a benefit from the use of hyperbaric oxygen for patients with radiation injuries to their nerves. None of these studies reported on any harmful effects of hyperbaric oxygen. While the available evidence suggests that hyperbaric oxygen is effective in some ways for people with LRTI, more research is needed to establish which patients can best benefit from this treatment and when they should be treated. An economic evaluation should also be undertaken.

We included 10 randomised controlled trials, with a total of 2961 surgeons participating in the studies. The types of surgery concerned were abdominal closure, caesarean section, vaginal repair and hip replacement. The use of blunt needles reduced the number of puncture wounds of the surgical gloves by 54%. The use of blunt needles therefore prevents one glove being damaged in every six operations. Furthermore, the number of needle stick injuries to the fingers of the surgeons and their assistants was reduced by 69%. The force needed to pierce the tissue with blunt needles is higher than with sharp needles but all surgeons found it acceptable except for one study on vaginal repair. We conclude that the use of blunt needles reduces the risk of exposure to blood and bodily fluids for surgeons and their assistants and therefore we recommend their use in all operations where there is a risk of an injury to the surgeon or assistant. The quality of the evidence was rated as high.

This review wanted to find out if there is evidence to support that high- and medium potency antipsychotics are better than low-potency antipsychotics in terms of treatment efficacy, tolerability and cost. Seven small studies met the inclusion criteria. The number of participants was between 20 and 157 and the length of the included studies was between four and 52 weeks. The results suggest that high-potency antipsychotics like trifluoperazine may not be more effective than low-potency antipsychotics but they produce more side effects, in particular movement disorders. This evidence base has some weak points: it is based on only seven small studies, the quality of the evidence ranges from moderate to very low and all but one trial were published before 1980. So, there is a need for new studies comparing high- and low-potency antipsychotics.

We searched for evidence up to 19 September 2018. We found nine completed studies that met our inclusion criteria. These studies were small (involving 593 infants in total) and provided low quality evidence about the effects of responsive feeding in preterm babies. The main problems that we identified were that some studies did not use reliable methods, some information was missing, and the studies did not measure some key outcomes that we thought were important. The studies showed that preterm babies fed in response to feeding and satiety cues gained slightly less weight each day and reached full oral feeding earlier than babies fed at scheduled times. However, these studies were small and the studies had many weaknesses, so we cannot be sure about the results. The studies did not report any information about the views of parents or caregivers. We do not have enough evidence to say whether responsive feeding is better or worse than scheduled feeding for preterm babies. Further research is needed.

This review aimed to determine if treatment of hyperhomocysteinaemia is beneficial for people who have mild or moderate peripheral arterial disease. Two randomised trials with a total of 161 participants were included in this review. The studies did not report on mortality and rate of limb loss. One randomised trial with a total of 133 participants showed that there was a significant improvement in ankle brachial index (a measure of blood flow through the leg arteries) in participants who received folic acid compared with placebo, as well as in participants who received 5-methyltetrahydrofolate (5-MTHF) versus placebo. A second trial with a total of 28 participants showed that there was no difference in ankle brachial index in participants who received a multivitamin B supplement compared with placebo. No major events were reported. However, the two studies were small and had a short duration, so these results should be interpreted cautiously. More research is needed.

We searched scientific databases to 19 August 2015 and found two randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) that explored the impact of different payment systems on what services dentists provide. One study looked at whether the introduction of a fee-for-service payment system, compared to ordinary capitation, led to an increase in the number of dental sealants placed in children's back teeth. Sealant is a liquid coating which is brushed onto the back teeth to prevent tooth decay. The other study compared capitation with fee-for-service payment systems and reported on the number of preventive treatments provided, the number of visits made, the number of filled teeth, the number of decayed teeth, the number of teeth extracted and the total amount spent on care. In the first study, it was found that there was an increase in the number of dental sealants placed in the teeth of children in the group of practices that were incentivised with a fee-for-service payment. However, this study took place in some of the most deprived areas in Scotland, so we cannot be sure if these results apply to other areas. The second study found that, under a capitation system, teeth were treated at a later stage in the disease process than under a fee-for-service system. Dentists under the capitation system tended to see their patients less often, carried out fewer fillings and extractions, but gave more preventive advice. We do not have enough information about how cost-effective the different payment methods were. We judged both studies to be at a high risk of bias and the quality of the evidence was low/very low for all outcomes. This means we cannot draw any firm conclusions from the findings. However, it was shown that financial incentives within remuneration systems may produce changes to the treatment provided by primary care dentists and further experimental research in this area is recommended.

The review authors searched for evidence from randomised controlled studies on the effects of administering zinc supplements to women in early pregnancy. Twenty-one studies were included which involved over 17,000 women and their babies. Generally these studies were well conducted and had little risk of bias. Taking zinc supplements during pregnancy was found to reduce the chance of having a preterm birth by 6%, but there was no effect on the number of babies with a low birthweight. No other obvious differences were seen between the group of women who took their zinc supplements as prescribed and those who did not. However, most of the studies were done in areas where zinc supplements are known to be effective, such as in developing countries. In developed countries the additional benefit may be smaller. Overall, the evidence to support taking zinc supplements during pregnancy was not strong enough to recommend it routinely, although further research may provide more information. It is also possible that the benefit is too small to be relevant. More research needs to focus on ways of improving overall nutrition for pregnant women in disadvantaged groups.

We included ten randomized controlled trials (RCTs), with 1,656 participants, in this review. The trials were conducted between 1991 and 2011. None of the pharmaceutical industry funded the trials. The trials showed that the use of probiotics associated with antifungals could increase the rate of short-term clinical cure and mycological cure, and decrease the rate of relapse at one month. However, this benefit did not translate into a higher rate of long-term clinical cure or mycological cure. Probiotics use did not seem to increase the frequency of either major or minor adverse events. We found no RCTs addressing the outcomes time to first relapse, need for additional treatment at the end of therapy, patient satisfaction and cost effectiveness. The overall quality of the evidence was low or very low. Further well-designed RCTs are needed to confirm these results.

The search for studies was done on 30 May 2019. This review includes seven randomised controlled trials involving 696 women. The trials were conducted in different countries, covering the full spectrum of the World Bank's economic classification. Two trials were conducted in Germany and Italy which are high-income countries, while four trials were conducted in upper-middle income countries, two in Iran, one in Malaysia and the fourth in Turkey, and the seventh trial was conducted in Jordan, which is a lower-middle income country. In six of the trials all the participants met the inclusion criteria, and in the seventh study we included in the meta-analysis only the subgroup of participants who met the inclusion criteria. The trials compared treatment with progesterone or other progestogen drugs (such as 17α-hydroxyprogesterone, prometrium, and intramuscular progesterone) with an inactive substance (placebo) or no treatment, and the results were expressed in terms of odds ratio (OR). The overall quality of the evidence ranged from very low to moderate. Progestogens administered by mouth or by vagina compared to placebo or no treatment probably reduce the risk of miscarriage. Progestogens administered by mouth also probably reduce the miscarriage rate, whereas progestogens administered by vagina have little or no effect on the miscarriage rate. There was no significant difference in the reduction of miscarriage between progestogens administered by mouth compared to those administered by vagina. Progestogens may have little or no effect on the rate of preterm birth. We are uncertain if the treatment of threatened miscarriage with progestogens compared to placebo or no treatment has any effect on the rate of congenital abnormalities. The results of this Cochrane Review suggest that progestogens are probably effective in the treatment of threatened miscarriage but may have little or no effect in the rate of preterm birth. The evidence on congenital abnormalities is uncertain, because of the low number of studies and small number of babies with abnormality, and was found to be of very low quality.

We searched for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that compared laser treatment to no treatment or to deferred treatment (where the observation period was followed by laser treatment). We found five suitable RCTs, with a total of 4786 participants randomly assigned to receive laser treatment or no/deferred treatment. These studies had been published up to July 2015. Three were from the USA, one from the UK and one from Japan. The average age of participants was about 60 years and duration of diabetes varied from less than one year to over 20 years. The proportion of men and women recruited varied from 51% to 75%. In four of the studies most of the people recruited had an advanced stage of diabetic eye disease (proliferative retinopathy); one study mainly recruited people with an earlier stage of disease (non-proliferative retinopathy). The amount of type 1 diabetes in the population ranged from 61% to 100% in the studies. Four of the studies used the traditional technique of laser photocoagulation, known as panretinal photocoagulation, and one study used a different technique of laser treatment that targeted areas of the retina with poor blood supply. Two of the studies compared laser treatment to no treatment and three studies compared laser treatment to deferred treatment. The results showed that, at 12 months, there was little difference between groups in the risk of significant vision loss. However, there was a substantial benefit of laser treatment in reducing the risk of severe vision loss during this time. Furthermore, there was a 50% reduction in risk of progression of diabetic retinopathy and a similar reduction in risk of vitreous haemorrhage (bleeding into the centre of the eye). However, none of the studies reported any information on vision-related outcomes, quality of life, pain, loss of driving licence or adverse events such as retinal detachment. The quality of the evidence was rated as moderate or low, depending on the outcome measured. This is because some of the trials were old and did not report important details about the methods used. Also, it is likely that the fact that patients knew whether they were receiving treatment may have influenced the results. The evidence shows that laser photocoagulation is effective in reducing the risk of sight-threatening complications in people with proliferative diabetic retinopathy. However, new forms of treatment, such as antiangiogenic drugs, are now being used alongside laser photocoagulation. Therefore, future research should examine the addition of these new treatments to laser therapy.

We searched the literature up to September 2016 and identified two randomized controlled trials involving 269 participants. The participants were mostly men; the average age was 65 years. The trials were conducted in China and Italy. Both trials included adults with acute breathing failure after upper abdominal surgery. The findings of this review suggest that continuous or intermittent positive airway pressure or bilevel nasal mask ventilation may reduce the need for tracheal intubation (a tube inserted through the nose or mouth into the airway) and length of stay in the intensive care unit, without an increase in deaths or overall length of hospital stay. One trial showed that bilevel NPPV, compared to oxygen therapy, may improve blood gas levels and blood pH one hour after the intervention. The reliability of the evidence was low or very low. More good quality studies are needed to confirm these findings.

The purpose of this review was to evaluate which interventions are most effective at reducing the pain of forceps delivery for the mother and baby. Four randomised controlled studies were identified (388 women), all of which were at some risk of bias. Three compared diazepam with an alternative agent (ketamine; vinydan-ether; "other" anaesthetic agent) for the provision of general anaesthesia, and one trial compared spinal analgesia to pudendal nerve block (in both groups lignocaine was administered). Results could not be combined in a meta-analysis. Women receiving diazepam for forceps delivery in one small trial were more likely to regard their pain relief as effective compared with women receiving vinydan-ether. However, in a further small trial, no clear difference in the number of women judging their pain relief as effective was seen when diazepam was compared with ketamine. The trial comparing spinal analgesia to pudendal nerve block showed that women receiving spinal analgesia were significantly more likely to regard their analgesia as adequate and were less likely to report severe pain during forceps delivery. The trial that compared lignocaine for regional anaesthesia with lignocaine for pudendal nerve block did not show any difference in pain relief but did show that women in the regional anaesthesia group were more likely to have a low Apgar score at birth. The regional anaesthesia group were also more likely to have a low pH level in the umbilical artery. Neither of these outcomes are necessarily bad. The authors concluded that there is too little evidence to show which form of analgesia is most effective.

We included 15 randomised controlled trials (1048 participants). The trials were conducted in India, Europe, and the USA. The majority of participants were adults with mild to moderate asthma. Five studies included yoga breathing only, while the other studies assessed yoga interventions that included breathing, posture, and meditation. The length of the yoga programs ranged from two weeks to 54 months. In comparison with usual care, yoga had a small positive effect on quality of life (6 studies, 447 participants) and symptoms (3 studies, 243 participants), but had an uncertain effect on lung function (7 studies, 340 participants) and medication use (2 studies). No severe adverse events were reported, but the information on adverse events was limited. The number of participants in the included studies was small. The methods used in most of the studies were not as well described as we would like. Overall, we found that yoga probably leads to small improvements in quality of life and symptoms in people with asthma. However, we have less certainty about whether yoga improves lung function and reduces the need for asthma medication. More research is needed to be sure that yoga should be recommended for people with asthma.

We found 10 clinical trials that involved 249 participants who had been diagnosed with IBM. The average age of participants was 56 years; most (8/10) were male. Most (6/10) of the trials were conducted in Europe, and all were conducted in outpatient clinics. Treatment options were compared over a period of up to 24 months. Interferon beta-1a (a drug used to treat multiple sclerosis) was tested in two trials (62 participants), methotrexate (a drug commonly used to treat rheumatoid arthritis) in a single trial (51 participants), intravenous immunoglobulin (which involves regular blood transfusions) in three trials (94 participants), anti-T lymphocyte immunoglobulin (an antibody that targets T cells, which play an important role in the immune system) and methotrexate in a single trial (28 participants), oxandrolone (a steroid that is taken by mouth) versus placebo in a single trial (20 participants), azathioprine (an immunosuppressant) and methotrexate versus methotrexate alone in a single trial (49 participants), and arimoclomol (a drug that has an anti-senescent mechanism of action, which means it targets cells that have stopped dividing) versus placebo in a single trial (19 participants). Two trials were listed as ongoing. Compared to placebo, interferon beta-1a and methotrexate did not produce important results in slowing down muscle strength decline after six and 12 months, respectively. However, the trials were small and only 5 out of the 10 trials measured muscle strength. An open trial (where both the therapist and participant knew what treatment they were getting) of anti-T lymphocyte immunoglobulin plus methotrexate versus methotrexate showed an increase in muscle strength at 12 months, but there was a high risk of bias due to lack of blinding. Intravenous immunoglobulin was tested in three trials, but we could not combine these to get an overall result. Adverse events occurred, but none of the trials presented the information in a way that would be useful for understanding the safety of the different treatments. There is a lot of missing information about the effects of various treatments in IBM. More trials are needed that are larger, of longer duration, and that use fully validated, standardised, and responsive outcome measures. The quality of the evidence was moderate to very low. It was not possible to judge the quality of the evidence from the trials of intravenous immunoglobulin, as we did not have access to the original data.

This review found nine studies with 3144 participants, comparing linezolid against vancomycin. Most studies were sponsored by the pharmaceutical industry. The studies showed that linezolid is more effective than vancomycin for treating skin and soft tissue infections. This is particularly true for infections caused by methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus bacteria. However, the studies were small and had a number of other problems that could have affected the results. Therefore, further research is needed to confirm these findings.

We identified eight randomised controlled trials (RCT) with a total of 512 participants. The evidence is current to March 2016. Overall, we found no relevant differences in mortality, postoperative complications, and overall survival between both operation types. However, our analysis showed that open surgery was associated with a longer operation duration, more pronounced intraoperative blood loss, and increased risk of delayed gastric emptying. We could not identify sufficient high-quality evidence to determine the potential benefits or harms of one operation over the other. The quality of the included studies was variable, and study protocols were not always clear and well reported. The sample size was small and limited the possibility of detecting differences between groups. The number of participants enrolled in each trial was very low, and this limits the ability to draw firm conclusions. The characteristics of the included studies were diverse, and this limits the ability to combine study results. Overall, the quality of the included studies was low, and this limits the validity of our findings. Future high-quality RCTs of complex surgical interventions are needed. Future studies should focus on well-defined outcomes and report their methods in detail. The evidence does not support one operation over the other, but rather suggests that both operations should be considered when making decisions about treatment.

The review found six randomised controlled trials, with 1862 participants. The trials showed that overall, calcium channel blockers do not improve the outcome from traumatic brain injury. However, there is some evidence that one calcium channel blocker, nimodipine, improves the outcome for people whose brain has been damaged by bleeding. Nimodipine did, however, cause more side effects than the placebo.

We searched the literature up to January 2016 and identified four randomized controlled trials (RCTs) with a total of 3090 patients who had cardiac arrest in and out of the hospital. The studies had a low to moderate risk of bias. Two of the RCTs were part-funded by the manufacturer of the automated external defibrillator. Both RCTs were conducted in Europe, and two were conducted in the USA. One study was funded by the National Institutes of Health (NIH). Our primary outcomes of survival to hospital discharge and neurological outcome at hospital discharge were no different between the two treatments. The rate of successful resuscitation, as measured by the rate of return of spontaneous circulation, was also no different. There was no difference in the number of adverse events between the two treatments. Overall, the quality of the evidence for all outcomes was low. We are unsure whether giving one and one-half to three minutes of CPR before applying the Automated External Defibrillator is superior to immediately using the defibrillator. This result should be interpreted cautiously as the confidence intervals were wide. Additional studies with a larger number of patients and a longer follow-up period would be needed to draw more robust conclusions.

This review aimed to determine which medication(s) and dosage should be used to down regulate the pituitary gland; when and how much gonadotropin should be used during ovarian stimulation; what type of adjuvant(s) should be used and when they should be given in order to improve clinical outcome in poor responders to controlled ovarian stimulation in IVF. The review authors searched the medical literature and found 10 randomized controlled trials (RCTs), which met the inclusion criteria. However, only one RCT reported live birth rates. The control of ovarian stimulation in poor responders remains an open question. The evidence available does not show a clear advantage of one protocol over another. More research is needed.

The review looks at the effects of family therapy for people with schizophrenia. The main aim is to see if family therapy helps people stay out of hospital, reduces the recurrence of symptoms and improves their mental health functioning. The authors found that family therapy may help reduce the risk of relapse, hospital admission and non-adherence to medication for people with schizophrenia. It may also help to improve the general functioning of the individual and the family. The evidence for these outcomes is based on 53 studies with a total of 4826 participants. The methodological quality of the trials was variable and the overall strength of the evidence was low. More research is needed to evaluate the effectiveness of family therapy.

We searched the available literature until 20 February 2018. We identified three randomized controlled trials that compared drug-eluting balloons with uncoated balloons in patients with in-stent restenosis of the leg arteries. These trials involved 263 participants who received either a drug-eluting balloon or an uncoated balloon. Two of the three trials were sponsored by companies that make the drug-eluting balloons.
