One company also made the stents that were placed inside the artery. The other company only made the drug-eluting balloons. Both companies were involved in the design of the trials and in the analysis of data. Results from the trials showed little or no difference between groups in the number of participants who had an amputation or died. More participants in the group receiving the drug-eluting balloons had repeat balloon angioplasty performed on the blocked artery than those participants who received the uncoated balloon. This result was reported at six months and 24 months. At six and 12 months, more participants in the group receiving the drug-eluting balloons showed signs of improved blood flow than those participants given the uncoated balloon. The overall certainty of evidence provided by this review is very low. This was because the three trials included a small number of participants and contained design flaws that could affect the reliability of the results. Studies with more participants that do not allow companies making the devices tested to influence their design, conduct, analysis, or reporting of results are needed to assess the true benefits and harms of using drug-eluting balloons to treat blockages that have formed over stents in the leg arteries.

We searched for all available research up to 20 May 2016. We found seven studies with 922 participants that met our inclusion criteria. These studies were between 32 and 242 participants in size. Our analyses showed that corticosteroids can lead to fewer heart problems in children with Kawasaki disease, with no resulting serious side effects or deaths. In addition, corticosteroids reduced the duration of fever, time for blood tests to return to normal, and the length of hospital stay. However, there were not enough data available to show whether or not there were any harmful effects from the use of steroids. Further studies are needed to answer this question. Steroid treatment should therefore be considered for all children with Kawasaki disease until the results of these additional studies are available. The quality of the evidence was judged to be high for the incidence of serious adverse events, mortality, and time for laboratory parameters to normalise. The quality of the evidence was moderate for the incidence of coronary artery abnormalities, duration of clinical symptoms (fever, rash), and length of hospital stay. This means that we are reasonably confident that the true effect is close to that estimated in this work.

We searched the Cochrane Database of Trials in February 2019. We found eight randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) involving 846 participants that met our inclusion criteria. Four of these studies compared PIP with a control group (no treatment or treatment-as-usual) and four compared PIP with another treatment group (another PIP, video-interaction guidance, psychoeducation, counselling or cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT)). Our review includes men and women from a range of backgrounds including women with postpartum depression, anxious or insecure attachment, a history of being maltreated, and prisoners. The average age of the children was 18 months. We looked at the impact of PIP on the mental health of parents, the way in which parents and children interact, the type of attachment that develops between parent and child, the behaviour of the child, and the level of the child's cognitive development. Compared with no treatment, PIP made no difference to the level of parental depression but improved the security of infant attachment. It did this by increasing the number of infants classified as securely attached and reducing the number of infants classified as avoidant, disorganised or resistant. However, these conclusions were based on low quality evidence. This means we are uncertain about the true effect of PIP. The evidence was of low or very low quality, because the included studies were small, the results were imprecise and there were concerns about aspects of the way in which they were conducted. Furthermore, many of the studies had different methods and results from each other, which means that their results may not be comparable. More high quality research is needed to confirm whether PIP is effective in improving the mental health of parents and the development of their children.

We searched for evidence on 30 June 2016 and found one new included trial. Our updated review includes 11 randomised controlled studies with 753 women. Most of the studies were of low/very low quality. The main outcome measures were infant health, as assessed by Apgar scores at one and five minutes after birth, and if the mothers dropped in their oxygen levels during surgery. We found no clear evidence of a difference in Apgar scores at one and five minutes. However, the babies of mothers who received extra oxygen had significantly higher levels of oxygen in their blood than those babies born to mothers who breathed normal air. The mothers' oxygen levels also increased, but not as much as in the babies. However, the increase in the babies' oxygen levels may have adverse effects on the babies' health. Further research is needed to find out if the long-term benefits of this treatment outweigh its costs.

We searched the literature up to November 2010 and included twelve studies containing data on 2196 participants in this review. Four of these studies were new for this update. The studies tested a number of different types of packaging but all used a system whereby the packaging had a built-in reminder to take the medication at a particular time. We combined results from six studies using a variety of different reminder systems and showed an increase in the percentage of pills taken when a reminder was used. However, the studies were quite different from each other and we could not rule out chance as the reason for the result. We also combined results from two studies showing that patients taking their pills from a package with a reminder had lower blood pressure than a control group, while also combining results from two studies showing that patients using a reminder package had lower glycated haemoglobin levels (a measure of blood sugar control). The studies did not report any adverse effects from the use of the reminder packaging. One additional study found that people with low literacy levels preferred the reminder packaging. While these results are promising, it is important to note that many of the studies were performed in people with different health problems (e.g. asthma, tuberculosis, high blood pressure, diabetes) so the findings may not apply to all diseases. More research needs to be done to see how effective these packaging designs are in different populations.

The authors searched the literature and identified 15 randomised clinical trials evaluating 11 different pharmacological interventions (methylprednisolone, multivitamin antioxidant infusion, vitamin E infusion, amrinone, prostaglandin E1, pentoxifylline, mannitol, trimetazidine, dextrose, allopurinol, and OKY 046 [a thromboxane A2 synthetase inhibitor]). No significant reduction in mortality or postoperative complications was seen with the use of any of the drugs studied. Methylprednisolone, trimetazidine, and dextrose may have some protective effect on the liver by improving its function and reducing its injury, as demonstrated by biochemical markers. However, all the trials were small and had other methodological problems, so the results must be interpreted cautiously. Further studies are necessary to find out which drug is the most effective and safe to use in liver resections performed under vascular occlusion.

We systematically reviewed the literature on the prevalence of adverse renal effects in children treated for Wilms' tumour. We searched the scientific databases Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials, MEDLINE, EMBASE, ISI Web of Science and The LILACS, on 15 January 2016. We also cross-checked references of relevant articles. We considered all studies in the review, but performed analyses only on those studies that met our inclusion criteria. Our systematic review includes 61 studies, of which 52 studies evaluated the prevalence of adverse renal effects. These 52 studies included 13,327 patients, of whom at least 4499 had their kidney function tested. The studies used different outcome measures to report adverse renal effects. For this review, we grouped these outcomes into four categories: chronic kidney disease, decreased glomerular filtration rate (GFR; estimated with MDRD or CKiD equations), proteinuria and abnormal laboratory values of electrolytes (phosphate, magnesium). We defined chronic kidney disease as a decrease in GFR lasting for at least 6 months, or end-stage renal disease. Decreased GFR and proteinuria are markers of kidney damage. Abnormal levels of electrolytes are associated with poor kidney function. The prevalence of adverse renal effects varied from 0% to 84%, depending on the study population, the given treatment, the outcome measure, the duration of follow-up and the methodological quality of the studies. Seven studies, including 244 patients, reported the prevalence of chronic kidney disease, which ranged from 2.4% to 32%. Of 52 studies, 36 studies, including 432 CCS, reported a decreased (estimated) GFR, which was present in 0% to 74% of participants. Twenty-two studies, including 851 participants, reported proteinuria, which was present in 3.5% to 84% participants. Four studies, including 128 CCS, reported a low level of magnesium in blood, which was present in 13.2% to 28.6% participants. Eleven studies, including 287 participants, reported a low level of phosphate in blood or impaired tubular phosphate reabsorption (TPR), or both, which was present in 0% to 36.8% participants. The prevalence of hypertension ranged from 0% to 50% in 2464 participants. It is important to mention that the studies were very diverse in included patients, given treatment, reported outcome measures, follow-up duration and methodological quality. This diversity might explain the large differences in prevalence of adverse renal effects. Furthermore, the number of patients with adverse renal effects seems to increase with time, which underlines the need for long-term follow-up of CCS. We could not identify any risk factors for specific adverse renal effects. However, this does not necessarily mean that no risk factors exist. It might be related to the methodology of the studies. Therefore, we suggest that future studies should pay attention to study design and reporting, including the use of a proper control group and a large number of participants. In addition, these studies should investigate several risk factors simultaneously to correct for possible confounders. It is also important to research nephrotoxicity after new therapeutic agents. Until more evidence becomes available, all CCS should preferably be enrolled into long-term follow-up programmes to monitor their kidney function and blood pressure.

The review authors searched for clinical trials which compared the use of plant-based medicines versus placebo (a pretend treatment) or other treatments in people of all ages with sickle cell disease. We found two randomised controlled trials, one each for the two different plant-based medications, Niprisan® (also known as Nicosan®) and Ciklavit® (which contains Cajanus cajan seeds). The main outcome was the number of vaso-occlusive crises (painful sickle cell crises) experienced by each person over a set period of time. The trial of Niprisan® also measured the severity of these crises, the number of people who had to be admitted to hospital, the frequency of transfusions, and changes in haemoglobin levels (the amount of red blood cells in the blood). The trial of Ciklavit® measured the number of crises, the duration of these crises, the number of people requiring admission to hospital, and the level of anaemia. The trial of Niprisan® (83 participants) showed that this medication can reduce the number of vaso-occlusive crises over a six-month period without any serious side effects. However, we assessed the evidence from this trial as being of low quality. The trial of Ciklavit® (99 participants) did not show a significant reduction in the number of crises but suggested there might be a reduction in their duration. This trial reported a possible decrease in the level of anaemia and a possibly harmful effect on kidney function, although neither finding was statistically significant. We assessed this evidence as being of low quality. The two small trials included in this review suggest that Niprisan® and Ciklavit® may have some beneficial effects in reducing the number of painful sickle cell crises. However, the findings need to be confirmed in further trials before we can draw firm conclusions. More trials are needed to look at the effects of using plant-based therapies in sickle cell disease.

We searched important medical databases such as Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials and MEDLINE. We looked at all trials assessing the effect of PET-adapted therapy versus standard therapy in patients with Hodgkin's lymphoma, with a focus on those with early-stage disease. We included three randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatments groups) with 1999 participants. Two trials compared PET-adapted therapy (chemotherapy only) with standard therapy (chemotherapy plus radiotherapy) in patients with early-stage Hodgkin's lymphoma and a negative PET scan. In the third trial, patients with early-stage Hodgkin's lymphoma were divided into those with a favourable or unfavourable prognosis. They were then randomised to receive PET-adapted or standard treatment. Following a PET scan, patients were further divided into PET-positive and PET-negative groups. To date, data have been published for the PET-negative arms only. Of the 1999 participants included in the three trials, 1480 were analysed. The remaining 519 participants were either PET-positive or were excluded because they did not match the inclusion criteria. Only one trial reported no deaths. The other two trials reported two deaths in each group (all very low quality evidence). Progression-free survival was shorter in patients with PET-adapted therapy than in those receiving standard therapy. This difference was also apparent when comparing patients receiving no additional radiotherapy (PET-adapted therapy) versus radiotherapy (standard therapy), and in those receiving chemotherapy but no radiotherapy (PET-adapted therapy) versus standard radiotherapy (highly sensitive result). However, the difference in progression-free survival must be interpreted with caution, as it was based on less than 20% of the total number of patients with early-stage Hodgkin's lymphoma who would be expected to die within five years, according to the literature. Short-term adverse events were assessed in one trial, which showed no difference between the treatment arms. No data on long-term adverse events were reported in any of the trials. To date, no robust data on overall survival, response rate, treatment-related mortality, quality of life, or short- and long-term adverse events are available. There was moderate-quality evidence that progression-free survival was shorter in patients with early-stage Hodgkin's lymphoma and a negative PET scan receiving chemotherapy only (PET-adapted therapy) than in those receiving additional radiotherapy (standard therapy). More randomised controlled trials with longer follow up periods may lead to more precise results for adverse events, treatment-related mortality, and quality of life, and could evaluate whether this progression-free survival advantage will translate into an overall survival benefit. It is still uncertain whether patients with a positive PET scan benefit from PET-based treatment adaptation, and what the effect of such an approach is in patients with advanced Hodgkin's lymphoma.

Until 1 April 2014, we did computer searches for randomized controlled trials of birth control pills and other hormones tested against another hormone method or a placebo ('dummy') method. For this update, we also wrote to researchers to find other trials. The focus was on changes in blood sugar and insulin. We included 31 trials. Twenty-one trials compared types of birth control pills. Others looked at different pills, injections, a vaginal ring, and implants. None used a placebo. Of 34 groups compared, eight had larger or smaller average values for the same measure. Twelve trials studied pills with the hormone desogestrel versus levonorgestrel (a common pill hormone). Most differences were small or unclear. A combined analysis of two studies showed the desogestrel group had a higher mean fasting glucose (MD 0.20; 95% CI 0.00 to 0.41). Individually, one study showed lower means for fasting glucose (MD -0.40; 95% CI -0.72 to -0.08) and two showed higher means for two-hour glucose response (MD 1.08; 95% CI 0.45 to 1.71) and insulin area under the curve (MD 20.30; 95% CI 4.24 to 36.36). Three trials studied the etonogestrel ring and one the etonogestrel implant. One showed the ring group had lower mean area under curve insulin than the levonorgestrel pill group (MD -204.51; 95% CI -389.64 to -19.38). Of eight trials of norethisterone preparations, five compared pills and three compared injectables. In a pill study, a norethisterone group had a smaller mean change in glucose two-hour response than a levonorgestrel pill group (MD -0.30; 95% CI -0.54 to -0.06). An injectable study showed the group using depot medroxyprogesterone acetate had higher means than the group using norethisterone enanthate for fasting glucose (MD 10.05; 95% CI 3.16 to 16.94), glucose two-hour response (MD 17.00; 95% CI 5.67 to 28.33), and fasting insulin (MD 3.40; 95% CI 2.07 to 4.73). Five recent trials were included. Two examined newer pills with different estrogen types. One showed the group with nomegestrel acetate plus 17β-estradiol had lower means than the levonorgestrel group for incremental area under curve glucose (MD -1.43; 95% CI -2.55 to -0.31) and glycosylated hemoglobin (HbA1c) (MD -0.10; 95% CI -0.18 to -0.02). Another study showed an extended-use group had a greater mean change in area under curve glucose than the conventionally cycled group (MD 82.00; 95% CI 10.72 to 153.28). A small study using two levonorgestrel pills showed the lower-dose group had a smaller mean change in fasting glucose, but the obese and normal weight women did not differ much. We found no major differences in carbohydrate metabolism between different hormonal contraceptives in women without diabetes. Many trials had small numbers of participants and some had large losses. The earlier studies often reported methods poorly. We know little about women at risk for metabolic problems because they are overweight. Half or more of the trials had weight restrictions for inclusion. Only one small trial compared obese and normal-weight women.

This Cochrane Review is current to January 2015. We found 10 relevant randomised controlled trials (549 participants) that assessed the effects of land-based exercise for people with hip osteoarthritis. Participants were aged between 59 and 88 years. Most (75%) were women. Studies ranged in duration from four weeks to one year. All studies compared an exercise intervention with a non-exercise intervention. The exercise programmes ranged from 40 minutes to 60 minutes, twice to three times per day, up to five days per week. Exercises were mainly aimed at improving strength, flexibility and range-of-movement exercises, with some aerobic exercise. Most studies (75%) were funded by government health research grants. Key results Nine studies (438 participants) measured pain and physical function before and after the completion of the exercise programme. Pain (lower scores mean less pain) People who exercised had less pain: improved pain was eight points on a zero to 100 scale, compared with zero for those who did not exercise. • People who exercised rated their pain as 21 points on a zero to 100 scale. • People not exercising rated their pain as 29 points on a zero to 100 scale. Physical function (better scores mean better function) People who exercised had better physical function: improved function was seven points on a zero to 100 scale, compared with zero for those who did not exercise. • People who exercised rated their function as 22 points on a zero to 100 scale. • People not exercising rated their function as 29 points on a scale of zero to 100. Quality of life People who exercised did not have better quality of life: improved quality of life was zero points on a scale of zero to 100, compared with zero for those who did not exercise. • People who exercised rated their quality of life as 48 points on a scale of zero to 100. • People not exercising rated their quality of life as 50 points on a scale of zero to 100. Adverse events Five studies reported adverse events: one person suffered an injury due to the exercise programme. Two studies reported that the exercise did not cause any adverse events. The other studies did not report whether or not an adverse event occurred. Two studies reported that there were more withdrawals from the exercise group. One study showed more people completed the exercise programme than the control group. The other study reported the same number of people completed both the exercise and the control groups. The review authors rated the quality of the evidence as moderate for pain and physical function, and low for quality of life. Further research is likely to change the estimates of how much exercise improves pain and physical function, but is unlikely to change our confidence in what we have found about the effect of exercise on quality of life.

This review looked for evidence from randomised controlled trials (RCT) on the effects of exercise therapy in people with multiple sclerosis. We defined exercise as any activity that resulted in atherapeutic benefit, including walking, cycling, stretching or lifting weights. The nine studies included in this review had an overall methodological quality of 7.2 points out of 10.0. Analysis of the data showed strong evidence that exercise therapy improves muscle power, exercise tolerance and ability to perform daily activities. Furthermore, there is some evidence that exercise therapy improves mood. However, the number of studies found was limited and there was a small total number of participants. Future studies should include a larger number of participants and should measure outcomes over a longer period of time. In addition, the results should be confirmed by further well-designed RCTs.

We identified one randomized clinical trial, with a high risk of bias, that compared laparoscopic cholecystectomy performed less than 24 hours after the onset of symptoms (early laparoscopic cholecystectomy group) with laparoscopic cholecystectomy after an average waiting period of 4.2 months (delayed laparoscopic cholecystectomy group). Only 75 participants were included in this trial. The average age of the participants was 43 years and 10 more women than men participated. There were no deaths in the early group and one death in the delayed group. There were no cases of bile duct injury in either groups. The only complication that the participants in the delayed group suffered during the waiting period were pain, inflammation of the pancreas, empyema (pus-filled gallbladder), perforation of the gallbladder, inflammation of the gallbladder, infection of the bile duct system, obstruction of the flow of bile, and severe abdominal pain. A total of 14 participants were admitted to hospital because of these complications. All 14 participants were from the delayed group as all the participants in the early group underwent operation within 24 hours. The proportion of people who developed complications that required hospital admission was significantly lower in the early group compared with the delayed group. There was no significant difference between the two groups in terms of the proportion of people who required conversion to an open operation. However, this result should be interpreted cautiously as the number of participants was small. The mean length of hospital stay was significantly shorter in the early group than in the delayed group. The operating time was also significantly shorter in the early group than in the delayed group. There was no significant difference between the two groups in terms of the number of participants who developed minor complications, but this result should also be treated with caution as the number of participants was small. The trial did not report quality of life or the time taken to go back to work. We conclude that early laparoscopic cholecystectomy decreases the number of people who develop complications during the waiting period for planned laparoscopic cholecystectomy, the length of hospital stay, and the operating time. However, as the trial had a high risk of bias and included a small number of participants, further well-designed trials are necessary to confirm whether or not this conclusion holds true.

We searched several electronic databases until June 2017. We included 11 studies in this review. Seven of these studies provided evidence for our main outcome which was the number of superficial incisional surgical site infections. The other four studies only reported on other outcomes. Evidence from seven randomized controlled trials suggests that long-term antibiotic treatment (starting before the start of surgery and continued for 24 hours after the end of surgery) probably reduces the risk of SSI in people having corrective jaw surgery. It is uncertain whether short-term antibiotic treatment (given during the surgery only) reduces the risk of SSI compared with a single dose of antibiotic given at the beginning of the surgery. None of these trials reported any adverse effects related to the antibiotics. Other outcomes such as pain, days away from work, cost of medication and patient satisfaction were not assessed in any of the studies. Further research is needed to assess the effect of different types of antibiotics. The quality of the evidence for our outcomes was rated as low to moderate.

One randomised controlled trial (involving 40 infants and 42 women) was included in this review. The trial was too small to detect an important difference in outcomes between the two groups. There were no significant differences in survival, caesarian section rates or time spent on a ventilator between the two groups. We did find that babies born at 36 weeks' gestation had a higher chance of dying than those who remained in the womb until labour started spontaneously. This may be because the babies born at 36 weeks were more likely to have complications related to prematurity. The trial did not report on any maternal outcomes. One study is not enough to develop policy on when to deliver babies with gastroschisis. Further studies are needed.

In July 2016 we searched for clinical trials that compared paracetamol plus opioids with placebo (dummy treatment) plus opioids, or paracetamol alone versus placebo alone, for people with moderate or severe cancer pain. We found three studies involving 122 participants aged over 18 years, who had moderate to severe pain. These studies lasted up to one week only. Paracetamol was always used together with other painkillers. All studies were small, and all were badly designed, which makes it difficult to be sure if paracetamol works for cancer pain. The results did not show any important difference between paracetamol plus opioids and placebo plus opioids for any measure of pain, quality of life, or side effects. Because the studies were so small, and were of such poor quality, we cannot be certain whether paracetamol has any place in the management of cancer pain.

The small number of trials available all had shortcomings in their methods. This review found that there are too few trials to be sure which treatments are most effective. However, an antihistamine (astemizole and diphenhydramine), an anticholinergic (propantheline) and the Chinese herbal medicine 'Suo Quo Wan' have shown some short term benefit. A final conclusion cannot be made as the number of trials is too small and the quality of the way they were done is poor. More trials are needed.

We found six randomised controlled trials with a total of 195 participants with MS. Two studies investigated inspiratory muscle training with a threshold device; three studied expiratory muscle training with a threshold device, and one study looked at regular breathing exercises. A total of 18 participants (˜ 10%) dropped out from the studies, but this number is relatively low for this type of intervention. No serious adverse events were reported. Most studies were small, had some methodological limitations, and were poorly reported. The quality of the evidence was therefore low. The available evidence shows that inspiratory muscle training can improve the strength of the muscles used for breathing in people with mild to moderate MS. Training with a threshold device has no effect on the strength of the muscles used for breathing out. The effect of these trains on quality of life is unknown. More research is needed to find out if the benefits of inspiratory muscle training are sustainable over time.

The aim of this Cochrane review was to evaluate the effect of interventions for treating ITP during pregnancy. The review includes one randomised controlled trial (RCT) with 38 women randomly assigned to corticosteroid (betamethasone 1.5 mg daily) or no intervention. Data from 26 women (28 pregnancies) were analysed. The intervention did not affect the number of babies with low platelet count (thrombocytopaenia) or those with bleeding (haemorrhage). Other outcomes were not studied. Therefore there is insufficient evidence to support the use of betamethasone for treating ITP in pregnancy. This review highlights the need for more studies using RCTs to establish the effects of interventions for ITP in pregnancy. Future randomised trials should include a variety of important outcome measures, including death of the mother and her baby, post birth bleeding and babies with bleeding inside the brain.

The search for studies was carried out in May 2015. We included two studies with a total of 111 participants. Both studies were undertaken in Iran and were conducted in a similar manner. Participants had moderate to severe keratoconus and were randomly allocated to receive either deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty (DALK) or penetrating keratoplasty. Only one eye of each participant was treated as part of the trials. One study was too small to provide reliable data for analysis and results from this study did not affect the overall results of the review. Key results The visual outcomes of the two studies, measured on a pinhole visual acuity chart, were similar between the two techniques at all time points. This finding was the same when measured by the uncorrected visual acuity (the ability to see clearly without glasses) or corrected visual acuity (the ability to see clearly with glasses or contact lens correction). There was no difference between the two techniques in terms of the number of grafts that failed or were rejected. Complications, of varying severity, occurred frequently in both groups and included postoperative astigmatism (distortion of the front surface of the cornea), steroid induced raised eye pressure and persistent scratches to the outer layer of the cornea. In the DALK group, one participant had new blood vessel growth where the host and donor cornea came together and one had wrinkling of the membrane separating the stromal tissue from the inner cornea. In the penetrating keratoplasty group, one participant required repositioning of the graft and one had an atonic pupil, a condition in which the pupil cannot react to light. Quality of the evidence The quality of the evidence for both studies was poor, mainly due to the small number of studies included in the review and problems with the way they were conducted. It is not clear whether one surgical technique is better than the other in terms of final vision and time taken to achieve this, stability of the final refraction, risk of rejection or failure, or both, and risk of complications. More research is needed to compare the outcomes of these two techniques.

We searched for randomised controlled trials which compared daily iron supplements to control or no supplements in healthy women or women at a high risk of becoming iron deficient. We included 76 randomised controlled trials, with 67 studies providing usable data, and a total of 8506 women. The mean age of women ranged from 18 to 55 years old. The dose of iron given to women ranged from 50 mg to 100 mg per day. The duration of the interventions ranged from three months to one year. Most of the studies were conducted in Thailand. This review demonstrates that daily iron supplements are effective at reducing the number of women who are anaemic or iron deficient, and improve their haemoglobin concentration and iron stores. However, iron supplements also increase the frequency of side effects such as constipation, abdominal pain and diarrhoea. Whether iron supplements have any impact on physical or mental functioning is unclear. Adherence was not improved by giving women supplements. Overall, the quality of the evidence was variable and only a small number of studies were found to have a low risk of bias. More work needs to be done to assess the effectiveness of iron supplements in specific groups of women, including those with heavy menstrual bleeding, and in pregnancy.

We found five randomised controlled trials with a total of 1,726 patients with metastatic colorectal cancer who had progressed on prior therapy, who received either irinotecan and a fluoropyrimidine or a fluoropyrimidine only. Overall, we did not find evidence that the combination of irinotecan and a fluoropyrimidine improved survival over treatment with a fluoropyrimidine alone. However, there was some evidence that the combination may delay progression of the cancer. We also assessed the safety of the treatments and found that there was a higher risk of diarrhoea and hair loss with the combination treatment, but a lower risk of neutropenia (a decrease in white blood cells). The evidence for both survival and adverse events was generally considered to be of low to moderate quality. Therefore, both irinotecan and fluoropyrimidine treatment remain reasonable options in the treatment of patients with advanced or metastatic CRC, and future studies with more participants in each treatment arm are needed to clarify the benefit observed in progression-free survival with combination irinotecan and fluoropyrimidines.

The authors of this review wanted to compare the benefits and risks of different types of cut made through the abdomen to assess which is best. The main outcomes assessed were lung and wound complications, pain, length of hospital stay and cosmetic results. The authors found that all types of incisions have their own specific complications. In general, a straight 'T' shaped cut (known as an upper midline incision) is used so the surgeon can work comfortably inside the abdomen. However, this type of cut can cause problems with breathing after surgery because of the number of stitches through the abdominal muscles. A horizontal cut (known as a low midline incision) may be better for the lungs but can make it harder for the surgeon to operate. Other types of cuts are made at an angle across the stomach muscles (transverse or oblique incisions) and these may result in less damage to the underlying tissues. The authors found that a transverse or oblique cut is better for the lungs, and there are fewer wounds opening up again (wound dehiscence) or bulging tissue (incisional hernia) with these types of cuts. However, the findings on pain were mixed and the length of time spent in hospital was similar for all types of cut. Cosmetic results may be better with some types of cut than others but this will depend on the individual. The authors recommend further research to see how the type of cut affects the major complication of death after abdominal operations.

This updated review includes nine randomized controlled trials with 981 participants. Five studies were conducted in Europe and four in North America. The number of participants in the studies ranged from 33 to 351. The average age across trials ranged between 32.0 and 43.7 years. Most participants had sciatica (pain radiating down the leg) and had received some sort of treatment prior to participating in the studies. The duration of the interventions ranged from two weeks to three months. This review showed that people with subacute LBP who receive multidisciplinary treatment may experience slightly less pain and less disability than people who receive usual care. They also seem to return to work earlier and have fewer sick leave days, but these results should be interpreted with caution. However, when we compared multidisciplinary treatment to other types of treatment, we found no differences between the groups in terms of pain, disability or time away from work. None of the included studies reported on adverse events. Therefore, we cannot say if multidisciplinary treatment is safe. The quality of the evidence for all outcomes was low to very low, mostly due to problems in the conduct of the studies, but also due to the small numbers of participants and wide confidence intervals around the estimates. Therefore, we are not able to conclude whether multidisciplinary treatment is more effective than usual care or other types of treatment, and more research is needed.

Cochrane authors searched the literature up to 23 September 2015 and included 18 randomised controlled trials involving 4843 participants. This review showed that bisphosphonates may not improve the proportion of people having pain relief from bone metastases but they probably reduce the number of people who have fractures or need additional treatment because of bone problems. However, bisphosphonates may cause side effects like kidney problems and nausea. The evidence for these findings was of moderate or low quality. Reviewers defined quality of life and function as outcomes, but reported that the measures used to assess these were too different to combine in the analysis. They found no clear difference in the number of people who had jaw necrosis. This review did not find that bisphosphonates improved the pain response among men whose bones were secondarily invaded by prostate cancer, but they probably decreased other complications related to bone metastases. These results should be interpreted with caution due to the relatively small number of included trials and the low quality of the evidence. Future studies should use standardised measures to better compare the effects of bisphosphonates on symptoms and quality of life.

We searched scientific databases such as 'Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials' and MEDLINE to identify randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatments groups) in order to perform an analysis of the benefits and harms of this treatment in the treatment of follicular non-Hodgkin's lymphoma. We found five eligible trials which included altogether 1093 patients with follicular non-Hodgkin's lymphoma; four trials included only previously untreated patients and one trial included only patients with relapse. The risk of bias in all five trials was considered to be low or unclear. This review shows that the clinical use of high-dose therapy followed by autologous stem cell transplantation is more effective than conventional chemotherapy in terms of prolonging the time to progression in previously untreated patients with follicular non-Hodgkin's lymphoma. However, this result has to be verified in future trials, since the number of analysed trials and patients is still low. Moreover, overall survival was not improved by this treatment compared with conventional chemotherapy. Treatment-related mortality was comparable. There is evidence that high-dose therapy and autologous stem cell transplantation can be beneficial for patients with relapsed follicular non-Hodgkin's lymphoma.

The review includes 15 studies with 1437 people. The studies randomised people to receive either WDD, or standard care (which could include antipsychotic medicines) or WDD plus an antipsychotic or antipsychotic medicine alone. Most studies were small and poorly described, so it was difficult to be sure if they were well run. Some authors did not report funding sources; two studies were funded by private companies supplying the WDD machines. Treatment with WDD alone did improve the global state of participants in the short term when compared with those who received standard care but there is limited evidence to support this finding. When WDD was compared with an antipsychotic drug, there was no difference in the short-term global or mental health status of participants, but WDD was associated with fewer side effects. When WDD was used in addition to an antipsychotic, the combination produced better short-term global and mental health outcomes than antipsychotic alone, with fewer participants experiencing debilitating side effects. However, there was no significant difference in weight change. When WDD was used with a low dose of an antipsychotic, the combination produced better short-term global and mental health outcomes than a standard dose of antipsychotic alone, with fewer participants experiencing side effects. There were no studies examining the effect of WDD on the use of mental health services or costs. The quality of evidence from the studies was low because of their small size, poor description of methods, and the fact that participants were aware of which treatment they were receiving. This may affect the reliability of the findings. Further well-designed large studies are needed to properly evaluate the effectiveness and safety of WDD for people with schizophrenia.

Cochrane authors searched the literature up to 14 November 2018. We included 12 studies with 799 children aged from 9 to 18 years. The studies compared different types of arch wires against each other. Types of arch wire included those made of stainless steel, nickel-titanium (NiTi), thermoelastic NiTi and superelastic NiTi. Thermoelastic NiTi arch wires are more recent and thought to be more comfortable to use. Coaxial superelastic NiTi arch wires have two strands of wire helically wrapped around each other. They are thought to apply even forces to the teeth during treatment. Root resorption (shortening of the roots of the teeth) was not reported. Pain was measured on day 1 of wear by asking the child to rate how much pain they had on a scale of 0 (no pain) to 100 mm (worst pain imaginable). Five studies compared multistrand stainless steel arch wires with superelastic NiTi arch wires. Results showed that there may be no difference between them in terms of how far the teeth move (low-quality evidence) and that there may be little or no difference in pain on day 1 (moderate-quality evidence). However, the quality of the evidence was low. Three studies compared conventional NiTi and superelastic NiTi arch wires but did not show any difference between them in terms of how far the teeth moved or how much pain they caused. Two studies compared conventional NiTi and thermoelastic NiTi arch wires but did not show whether there was any difference between them in terms of how far the teeth moved. Only one study compared single-strand superelastic NiTi and coaxial superelastic NiTi arch wires. This study showed that the coaxial superelastic NiTi arch wire moved the teeth further than the single-strand superelastic NiTi arch wire (moderate-quality evidence). It did not measure the amount of pain the children experienced. Three studies compared superelastic NiTi and thermoelastic NiTi arch wires but did not show whether there was any difference between them in terms of how far the teeth moved or in terms of pain. Overall, there is some evidence about the effects of different arch wire materials on the amount of tooth movement and pain during orthodontic treatment. However, most of the evidence was low quality because the sample sizes of the relevant studies were small, and three of the 12 studies were poorly conducted.

We found only one randomised trial, which involved 306 elderly people with dementia living in nursing homes in France. The average age of participants was 86 years. The study compared the effects of de-escalation interventions versus usual care on agitated behaviour and measured the effect on the use of coercive interventions, but it did not report any of our primary outcomes. It did report improvements in some of the behaviours assessed using the Cohen-Mansfield Agitation Inventory, a measurement tool used to assess agitated behaviours, but did not report any improvement in the overall agitation score. The study did not report any improvement in the behaviours assessed by the Neuropsychiatric Inventory or the Observation Scale. The quality of the evidence was very low, meaning that we cannot be certain about how effective de-escalation interventions are. More research is needed. We found that there is insufficient evidence to determine whether de-escalation is an effective intervention for reducing aggressive incidents in nursing homes. More trials are needed to inform good practice and improve the quality of care for people with dementia who become agitated. We found one ongoing study.

We conducted this review to find out which treatments are most effective in preventing and treating IAD in adult patients who are unable to care for their own skin. We searched the medical literature up to 28 September 2016 for relevant, well-conducted studies. We identified 13 randomised controlled studies with 1316 participants. Most studies were small and focused on elderly people in nursing homes or hospitals. The studies tested a variety of skin cleansers and other skin products, as well as the frequency of applying these products. We found only two studies that measured our two primary outcomes. One study showed that cleansing with a skin cleanser might be more effective than cleansing with soap and water. Another study showed that washing with a washcloth might be more effective than soap and water. Several other studies showed that applying a leave-on product (such as a moisturiser or skin protectant, or a combination of these) might prevent IAD when compared to not applying any leave-on product. However, no study assessed the side effects of the treatments. Overall, the quality of the studies was low. This means that we cannot be sure about the findings. Well-designed, larger studies are needed to validate these results. Future studies should use standardised skin care regimens and measurements, and report on important outcomes such as patient satisfaction with the treatment.

The evidence is current to April 2015. We found seven randomised controlled trials with 333 participants. The age of participants ranged from two years to young adulthood. The type of antiviral, route of administration, and length of treatment varied between the trials. Antivirals used were acyclovir, valomaciclovir and valacyclovir. Treatment duration varied from five days to several weeks. Two studies took place in hospital settings, three in an outpatient setting, and the location of the third study was unclear. The diagnosis of IM was based on clinical symptoms and laboratory findings. Overall, we found no clear evidence that antiviral medications are effective in improving the symptoms and signs of IM caused by herpesviruses. However, it is possible that antiviral drugs might reduce the time taken for people to recover from their illness by five days. This amount of time may not be long enough for the body's natural immune system to clear the virus, as other studies suggest that it can take a month or more for the symptoms to resolve completely. Furthermore, this result should be treated with caution as the quality of the evidence was very low. Five studies provided information on adverse events and side effects of medication. They reported no serious adverse events or reactions, and mild side effects such as nausea, abdominal pain, headache and dizziness occurred at similar rates in both the treatment and control groups. These mild side effects generally resolved within a few days, once the medication was stopped. This finding was consistent with the known safety profile of these antivirals. Viral shedding (the presence of virus in bodily fluids) was reduced during the period of antiviral treatment, but continued after the end of treatment. For all the other outcomes that we examined, there was no difference between the group receiving antiviral medication and the control group. Therefore, we cannot recommend the routine use of antiviral medications to treat people with primary IM, until further studies with higher quality evidence become available. The quality of the evidence for all outcomes was very low. Further studies are needed to assess the effects of antiviral medications in IM caused by herpesviruses.

This review compared the long-acting insulins detemir and glargine. Four studies were included with a total of 2250 people treated during 24 to 52 weeks of treatment. There was no difference between the two insulins in their ability to lower blood sugar levels, to reduce the number of times blood sugar levels were too high or too low at night, or to reduce the number of nights when blood sugar levels were very low. However, insulin detemir was often administered twice daily in a higher dose, whereas insulin glargine was administered once daily. This resulted in less weight gain with insulin detemir. Insulin glargine was associated with a lower total daily insulin dose and fewer injection site reactions.

This review aimed to assess the effectiveness of sacral nerve stimulation (SNS) for treating faecal incontinence and constipation. Six studies were found that assessed SNS for faecal incontinence and two for constipation. The results showed that SNS can help to improve faecal incontinence but more research is needed to be sure of this effect. Some people had side effects, including pain at the site where the stimulator was inserted. In summary, there is only a small amount of evidence to support using SNS for the treatment of faecal incontinence. More trials need to be done before we can be certain about whether or not it is effective.

The two studies included in the review show important differences in the way the two interventions were carried out. The use of a light-cured adhesive with molar tubes resulted in significantly higher failure rates than using a glass ionomer cement to fix molar bands to teeth. We found no evidence of any other significant side effects associated with either treatment. The two studies included in the review were well designed and had a low risk of bias.

This review summarizes the accuracy of one of these imaging techniques, namely endoscopic ultrasonography (EUS), in the local staging of gastric cancer. We searched four computerized databases (MEDLINE, EMBASE, ISI Web of Science, and The Cochrane Library) for studies conducted on the accuracy of EUS in the local staging of gastric cancer. We included only studies published between 1988 and 2012. We included 66 studies (with a total of 7747 participants) in our review. The included studies were generally of good methodological quality. The results of our review showed that EUS can be an accurate tool for the local staging of gastric cancer, although with some limitations. In fact, the accuracy of EUS in the local staging of gastric cancer varied between studies, and this inconsistency makes difficult to propose a strong level of evidence. Nevertheless, the overall accuracy of EUS seems sufficient to guide physicians during the management of people with gastric cancer. Future studies need to clarify the factors that lead to the variability in the accuracy of EUS.

A total of six randomised clinical trials were included in this review. These included a total of 492 participants, with 239 having day-surgery and 253 having overnight stay surgery. The number of participants in each trial ranged from 28 to 150. The percentage of women in the trials varied between 74% and 84%. The average age in the trials varied between 40 and 47 years. One trial reported short-term deaths. There were no deaths in either group. Long-term deaths were not reported. There were no differences in the rates of complications between the two groups. There was also no difference in quality of life, pain, time to return to full activity or return to work. Finally, there was no difference in the rate of readmission to hospital or in the number of people who had to stay longer in hospital than planned. Overall, day-surgery appears just as safe as overnight stay surgery in laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Day-surgery does not appear to result in an improvement in any patient-oriented outcomes such as return to normal activity or earlier return to work. However, the trials that support these conclusions have important limitations due to their design. Therefore, more studies are needed. In particular, we need trials that better report the quality of life of patients.

In January 2017, we updated searches from an earlier Cochrane Review to look for clinical trials where topical capsaicin was used to treat nerve pain caused by diabetes, shingles, or HIV. We found eight studies, each comparing a high-concentration capsaicin cream or patch with a low-concentration capsaicin cream or patch, or placebo. There were 2488 participants, ranging in age from 39 to 89 years. The duration of the treatments was 6 to 24 weeks. Studies tested 0.025% to 0.05% capsaicin against placebo, or 0.025% capsaicin against 0.03% to 0.4% capsaicin. The main outcome was the number of people reporting that their pain was much improved or very much improved. We also looked at the amount of pain reduced over time, and the number of people who dropped out of the studies early, reporting a worsening of their pain. Four studies tested the effect of capsaicin on pain after shingles, two on pain because of HIV, and one on pain because of diabetes. Results were not always reported clearly, so most of our analyses included only about half of the participants. About 1 in 10 more people reported that their pain was much improved or very much improved with high-concentration capsaicin than with low-concentration capsaicin or placebo. This result was the same at 8 and 12 weeks. About 1 in 10 more people experienced a large (at least 30% and 50% reduction) reduction in pain with high-concentration capsaicin than with low-concentration capsaicin or placebo, again at 8 and 12 weeks. These results were consistent for pain after shingles, pain caused by HIV, and pain caused by diabetes. However, it's unclear whether these results would apply to other types of nerve pain. Serious problems were uncommon, and not more common with capsaicin than placebo. The quality of the evidence was moderate or very low, meaning that we cannot be certain about the results. Further research might change them.

The evidence is current to January 2017. The review included three randomised controlled trials with a total of 6343 participants who had recently had a stroke or transient ischaemic attack (TIA) because of narrowing of the carotid artery. Two of the trials assessed the risks and benefits of surgery in people with moderate narrowing (50% to 89% stenosis) of the carotid artery compared with no surgery, and one trial looked at the effects of surgery in people with very narrow arteries (70% to 99% stenosis) compared with no surgery. The three trials were similar in design, but measured the degree of narrowing differently, and used different definitions for their results. We combined the information from the trials in a meta-analysis, which involved 6092 participants living in Europe and North America. Carotid endarterectomy was of some benefit for participants with 50% to 69% stenosis of the carotid artery (moderate-quality evidence), and was highly beneficial for participants with 70% to 99% stenosis, as long as there was still a good amount of space in the carotid artery (not near-occluded) (moderate-quality evidence). There was no significant difference in the number of strokes for participants with near-occlusion (high-quality evidence). Overall, the quality of the evidence ranged from high to moderate. The main problems that affected the quality of the evidence were the lack of blinding of the participants and the inconsistent definitions used by the three included studies for what constituted a stroke or TIA.

We performed this review to find out which of the two types of surgery is better to perform a stoma. We searched the medical literature until May 2016 for all studies that compared these two types of surgery. We only found one randomized controlled trial (a type of study in which the participants are assigned to each group by chance) and 10 non-randomized studies (in which the participants were assigned to a group based on factors that might affect the results of the study). The participants had a stoma because they had colorectal cancer, ulcerative colitis or Crohn's disease, or because they had many large bowel polyps. The results of the included studies were inconsistent and the quality of the evidence was always low or very low. This means that we cannot be sure whether one of the operations is better than the other. The study that we mentioned above also looked at how both types of operations affect the quality of life of the participant, but this is not an important outcome as it was not mentioned in the original question. There was no information about stoma-related complications (e.g. stoma prolapse, bleeding from the stoma, pain or difficulty with bag change) or stoma-related deaths. Further well-designed studies are needed to compare these two types of surgery.

We searched for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) on the effects of supportive therapy compared to standard care or other types of psychological therapy for schizophrenia. We found 24 relevant studies with 2126 participants. Overall, the quality of the evidence was very low. There were no clear differences in effectiveness between supportive therapy and standard care. However, there were some small studies that suggested that other psychological therapies, such as cognitive behavioural therapy, may be more effective than supportive therapy. There were too few studies to say if family therapy and psychoeducation are better than supportive therapy. Given the importance of developing the most effective treatments for schizophrenia, further large scale research is needed. This summary has been written by Ben Gray, Senior Peer Researcher, McPin Foundation: http://mcpin.org/.

In July 2016 we searched for clinical trials in which NSAIDs were used to treat cancer pain. We found 11 studies, with 949 people who had cancer pain, most with moderate or severe pain. Studies lasted one week or longer. None of the studies used a placebo (a dummy treatment) group, and most did not blind (hide the type of treatment from the participant and the researcher assessing the pain) the participants or assessors correctly. The studies were not large enough, and many dropped out early, so it is difficult to be sure of the results. We cannot compare NSAIDs with other treatments, and we cannot compare one NSAID with another, so there is no clear overall result for NSAIDs. Individually, each study showed that with NSAID, pain was reduced by at least half for four in ten people (range 2 in 10 to 5 in 10) after one or two weeks. Side effects were quite common, with 1 in 10 people experiencing dry mouth, trouble sleeping, and indigestion. Two people had serious side effects, but these were not caused by their painkillers. Overall, there is currently no high-quality evidence to recommend NSAIDs as a first-line treatment for cancer pain, but there is low-quality evidence that some people may get good results.

We found two randomised controlled trials which together included 287 participants. One trial compared tinzaparin with a placebo and involved 253 participants. It showed that with tinzaparin there was a more rapid resolution of pain and a reduction in the length of time of the crisis. In addition, participants treated with tinzaparin had significantly fewer days in hospital. However, there were two reports of minor bleeding events in this group. The other study compared dalteparin with placebo and involved 34 participants and showed that pain intensity reduced more quickly in the dalteparin group than in the placebo group. Due to the small number of studies, and because of several weaknesses in the way they were designed, we cannot be sure of the quality of the evidence. Therefore, more research needs to be done before we can conclude whether or not low-molecular-weight heparins are effective in people with sickle cell disease.

This review found no trials comparing anticoagulants with antiplatelet drugs in people with neck artery (carotid artery) dissection. Non-randomised studies have been reported for this comparison but do not show any significant difference between the two treatments. More research is needed to determine the best form of treatment with anticoagulants or antiplatelet drugs for people who have had a neck artery dissection.

The review authors searched the medical literature up to March 2014, and identified 26 relevant randomised controlled trials, involving a total of 2066 participants. The trials were too different to combine, so the results are presented individually. The results do not provide clear evidence that silver-containing dressings or creams speed up wound healing, or prevent wound infection. In fact, silver sulphadiazine cream may be associated with an increased risk of infection.

The evidence is current to June 2016. This review includes 12 studies with 3571 participants. Four of the 12 studies compared two different antibiotic treatments and eight studies compared one antibiotic versus another. The primary outcome was all-cause mortality. Secondary outcomes were clinical cure, length of stay in the intensive care unit and frequency of adverse events. Studies had an average duration of 10 days (range 7 to 14 days). The quality of evidence from the four studies comparing two different antibiotic treatments was moderate for all-cause mortality, adverse events, and length of intensive care unit stay. The quality of evidence for clinical cure was very low. The quality of evidence from the three studies comparing one antibiotic against another was moderate for all-cause mortality and adverse events, and low for clinical cure. None of the studies identified patients with increased risk of resistant bacteria. We did not find any difference in all-cause mortality, clinical cure, length of stay in intensive care unit or adverse events between monotherapy and combination therapy. Our results support the safety of using effective monotherapy for the empiric treatment of VAP. However, more research is needed to determine which antibiotic is the most effective. Carbapenems appear to result in a higher clinical cure rate than other tested antibiotics.

This review examined the impact of policy interventions aimed at reducing drug expenditures through limiting the availability or increasing the costs of specific drugs. These types of policies have been shown to change drug use and expenditures in previous studies, but their impact on people's health is less clear. We found that policies that targeted certain kinds of anti-inflammatory drugs and acid-reducing drugs did decrease drug use and expenses; however, policies that targeted newer antipsychotic drugs had little effect on drug use but did increase the use of other healthcare services. Also, policies that removed restrictions on blood pressure-lowering and cholesterol-lowering drugs appeared to increase the use of these drugs among those for whom they were intended. Unfortunately, we do not have enough information about the health impact of these policies, and there was no information about how these policies affect different segments of the population, especially the most vulnerable. The review also highlighted the need for more research on the impacts of policies that aim to reduce drug expenditures. Such research should consider the health impacts of changes in drug use as well as the equity impacts, i.e. the effects on different groups in society.

We searched several electronic databases up to November 2015. We included 19 studies in the review, with a total of 4232 participants. The studies were divided into four groups according to the type of intervention being compared. 1. One-to-one OHA versus no OHA Four studies compared any form of one-to-one OHA with no OHA. There was some evidence from two studies that one-to-one OHA reduced plaque at all time points measured, but there were only a few participants in each study arm. There was also some evidence from one study that one-to-one OHA reduced dental caries after 12 months, although the study was not well conducted and the findings cannot be relied upon. There was no evidence of a benefit for one-to-one OHA in reducing gingivitis. 2. Personalised one-to-one OHA versus routine one-to-one OHA Four studies compared personalised OHA with routine OHA. There was little evidence that either intervention was better at reducing plaque, gingivitis or dental caries. 3. Self-management versus professional OHA Five studies compared some form of self-management with some form of professional OHA. There was little evidence that any of these interventions demonstrated a difference in reducing gingivitis or plaque. None of the studies measured dental caries. 4. Enhanced one-to-one OHA versus one-to-one OHA Seven studies compared some form of enhanced OHA with some form of routine OHA. There was little evidence that any of these interventions demonstrated a difference in reducing gingivitis, plaque or dental caries. There was not enough high-quality evidence to recommend any specific one-to-one OHA method as being effective in improving oral health or being more effective than any other method. High-quality trials are needed to find the most effective way to deliver one-to-one OHA.

This systematic review included five small trials with a total of 189 participants. Two trials compared oral steroids or placebo; one trial oral steroids or no treatment; one trial oral or intra-articular steroids and one trial manipulation under anaesthesia and intraarticular steroid injection with or without oral steroids. No meta-analysis could be undertaken because lack of data and different comparators. One trial showed significant benefit of oral steroids over placebo at four weeks with 48% more participants reporting success (NNT=2), significantly less pain, better shoulder mobility and improved shoulder scores. However, these benefits were not maintained at six weeks. A second trial found no difference between oral steroids and placebo in pain or range of movement but suggested that improvement occurred earlier in the steroid group. The third trial reported that oral steroids provided more rapid initial improvement in pain than no treatment but there was little difference by five months. Adverse effects were minimal. The limited available evidence shows that oral steroids can provide short term benefit in pain, range of movement and function in frozen shoulder but the effect is not maintained for long. Further research is needed to determine whether this technique is effective in the longer term and to assess potential side effects. This review is based on only five small trials and further studies have been identified since the last version was published. The evidence is current to February 2015.

We found three randomised controlled trials with a total of 50 participants. The trials were not similar enough to allow the pooling of results. One trial did provide usable data but no differences were found between repetitive (rTMS) and sham TMS (placebo) groups in their ALS Functional Rating Scale-Revised (ALSFRS-R) scores or manual muscle testing (MMT) scores at 12 months. None of the trials reported any adverse events associated with the use of rTMS. However, with only one trial providing usable data and a small number of participants, we cannot be sure whether rTMS is safe or not. Overall, there is currently insufficient evidence to support the use of TMS in the treatment of ALS.

This review examined the various treatments used in BP and found that very potent topical steroids are effective and safe, but their use in extensive disease may be limited by side-effects and practical factors. Milder regimens (using lower doses of steroids) are safe and effective in moderate BP. Prednisolone is the most widely used systemic steroid treatment for BP and there is evidence that higher doses do not result in improved disease control when compared with lower doses. The effectiveness of adding plasma exchange, azathioprine or mycophenolate mofetil to corticosteroids, and combination treatment with tetracycline and nicotinamide needs further investigation. There were no trials comparing any of these treatments with each other.

Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) is a functional disorder characterised by abdominal pain or discomfort, bloating, cramping, gas, diarrhea or constipation, or both. Symptoms are usually milder in nature than those experienced with inflammatory bowel disease. Patients often report a feeling of a bowel movement that is either incomplete or unaccompanied by defecation. The causes of IBS are unknown, but it is believed to involve interplay between emotional factors, changes in gut function, and sensitivity of the gut. Current therapies for IBS include light exercise, stress management techniques, and medications to slow gut contractions, relieve muscle spasms, or improve the balance of bacteria in the colon. Several herbal remedies are used for the treatment of IBS; however, the efficacy and safety of these treatments have not been well studied. This review examined all available clinical trials that assessed the effectiveness of herbal remedies for the treatment of IBS. Seventy-five randomised controlled trials, involving 7957 patients with irritable bowel syndrome, were included. Most of the trials had low methodological quality, which limits the strength of the evidence. Herbal medicines were compared to each other, to placebo, or to drug therapy. Based on those studies with higher methodological quality, several herbal medicines showed signs of benefit in improving overall IBS symptoms when compared to placebo. Furthermore, many herbs were shown to be as effective as conventional drug therapy. However, confirmation of these results in large, high-quality trials is needed before they can be recommended.

In January 2012, we did computer searches for randomized controlled trials of the pericoital method using hormones to prevent pregnancy. We also wrote to researchers to find other trials. The studies could compare the pericoital method with another birth control method, with no treatment, or with another type of hormone treatment. The pericoital method had to be used for birth control on the day of sex and the next one to two days. We found 22 trials that studied hormones for birth control after sex. The studies included 12,400 women who used different types and doses of hormones. The studies came from Europe, Asia, and the Americas. The focus of this review was on a progestin called levonorgestrel (LNG). Eleven studies used 0.75 mg of LNG, four used other doses of LNG, and seven used other hormones. The main outcomes were pregnancy rate, dropping out, side effects, and whether women liked the method. The LNG 0.75 mg dose worked well for birth control and caused few side effects. Women on two different schedules had similar pregnancy rates. Using LNG after sex for birth control caused bleeding problems in about one in seven women. However, this problem did not seem to be related to how often the pills were taken or how much hormone was used. Other hormones are being studied for use after sex to prevent pregnancy. However, we have no data on their effectiveness yet. Women liked the method even though it caused some bleeding problems. The studies of LNG had some problems in their methods. However, the large numbers of women studied, the low pregnancy rates, and the consistence of the results make the evidence moderate strength. More research is needed to see if using hormones after sex prevents pregnancy as planned.

We searched the Cochrane Oral Health and Dental E Evidence-Based Caries and Orthodontic Interventions Group's specialized register, which is up-to-date to May 2016. We included 15 studies reporting on 561 patients. These trials were published over a 25-year period and were carried out in Europe, India, China, South Korea and the USA. The age range of patients was commonly restricted to adolescents or young adults; however, the participants of two studies were from a much wider age range (12 to 54 years). The number of male and female patients was similar in most studies. One study recruited only female patients. Two other studies had a predominance of female patients. The majority of studies used a single anchor point (a fixed point in the jaw from which force is applied) with only four studies using multiple anchor points. Of the single anchor points, the use of mini-screws showed the greatest gain in orthodontic tooth movement. The main outcome we looked at was how far back the upper first molar moved towards the back of the mouth (mesial drift). We also looked at how long treatment took and if patients found it acceptable and tolerable. We found that surgical anchorage was more effective at preventing the movement of teeth at the back of the upper jaw than conventional anchorage. However, we found no evidence that any one surgical anchor point was better than another. The use of mini-screws showed particular promise. None of the studies reported on harm due to the use of surgical or conventional anchorage. The evidence relating to the effectiveness of surgical anchorage was assessed to be of moderate quality, due to some methodological problems with the way in which the studies were designed.

We examined the research published up to 15 January 2016. We looked for randomised controlled trials which compared statin therapy with no treatment, other blood lipid-lowering drugs, or another statin regimen in adults with CKD who were not yet on dialysis. We identified 50 studies which involved 45,285 people. Of these, 38 studies involving 37,274 people contributed data towards the analysis. The quality of the included studies was variable and we regarded seven studies as having a low risk of bias. Evidence from this review shows that statins reduce the risk of death, heart attack, and stroke in people with CKD who do not have heart disease. Statins also lowered the risk of heart disease in people with mild or moderate CKD. However, there is some uncertainty about whether they are beneficial for people with more severe CKD. We do not know whether statins are beneficial for people with CKD who have already had a heart attack or stroke (known as secondary prevention). There is insufficient evidence about side-effects associated with statin therapy in people with CKD. Limitations of this review include the fact that many studies were poorly reported and some were funded by companies supplying statins. More research is needed to assess benefits and harms of statins in people with CKD who have not had a heart attack or stroke.

In May 2015 we searched the literature and found nine studies that met our inclusion criteria. These studies included 379 children aged between 2 and 19 years. Five of the studies included children with cerebral palsy and the other four had osteogenesis imperfecta. We could not combine any of the results together, so it is not possible to say which drug is better at reducing pain. One study found that intramuscular baclofen reduced pain in children with cerebral palsy when assessed at two years but not at five years. The parents of the children in the trial reported some side effects, including swelling around the pump and muscle weakness. There were eight serious complications in the trial, including difficulty swallowing and a seizure, but it was not clear if these occurred in the treatment or the control arm of the study. Two studies found that botulinum toxin A did not reduce pain in children with cerebral palsy. The main complications in these trials were related to the intervention and included muscle weakness and neck pain. There were no dropouts due to adverse events. The three studies using bisphosphonates did not find that they reduced pain. The most common complication in the alendronate group was gastrointestinal (stomach pain, nausea and constipation). The study using pamidronate found no difference in pain compared to the control group and there were no adverse events reported. None of the studies looked at the effects of commonly used pain-relieving medications such as paracetamol or codeine. The quality of the evidence was variable and all the studies were small. More research is needed to find out how to improve pain management for children with life-limiting conditions.

We searched many electronic databases up to March 2015 to find all randomised controlled trials (clinical trials where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) of latrepirdine for treating AD. We found seven trials, six of which we analysed. The trials randomised 1697 patients with mild to moderate AD to receive either latrepirdine or a placebo. Treatment lasted for 6 months. Overall, we found only a small significant benefit of latrepirdine on the Clinician’s Interview-Based Impression of Change test, which is used to assess the clinical global impression of change in AD patients. However, our analysis also showed that there was no significant difference between the effects of latrepirdine and placebo on most other measures of disease severity (measures of cognition, or mental function) that we assessed. Furthermore, the magnitude of the significant effect on the Clinician’s Interview-Based Impression of Change test was small. These results were based on a small number of studies with a small number of patients, so our estimates of the effects of latrepirdine could change when more results from larger studies become available. In addition, our findings were imprecise, inconsistent between studies and at high risk of bias, which limits our confidence in the conclusions. Despite this, the evidence to date suggests that while latrepirdine is not associated with an increased risk of side effects, there is no effect of latrepirdine on cognition and function in mild-to-moderate AD patients, though there appears to be a modest benefit for behaviour.
