Further studies should investigate the potential benefit of latrepirdine on behavioural symptoms in AD.

We searched for all randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that investigated the effects of RIC in preventing and treating ischaemic stroke. RCTs compare treatments in which participants are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups. We found seven RCTs, involving 735 participants, that met our inclusion criteria. Three trials involved 371 participants who had recently had a stroke or were at high risk of having a stroke because of narrowing of an artery in the brain; four trials involved 364 participants who were being treated for a recent stroke with clot-busting drugs. The evidence is current to September 2016. We found low-quality evidence that RIC may reduce the risk of recurrent stroke in participants with narrowing of an artery in the brain, and reduce stroke severity in people undergoing carotid stenting (a procedure to open up the carotid artery), but it may increase death or dependence in people with a recent stroke who are undergoing clot-busting treatment. However, these conclusions were based on a small number of studies with few participants and so there is considerable uncertainty about them. Further research is needed to confirm whether any of these effects are real. Adverse events associated with RIC treatment were higher than in participants not given RIC, but no severe adverse events were related to RIC treatment. No participant was dead on arrival or during the period of the studies; and no trial reported haemorrhagic stroke or improvement in neurological, psychological or cognitive impairment. The quality of the evidence was low or very low. The main reasons were imprecision (small number of studies and small number of participants in each study arm), publication bias (studies with positive findings are more likely to be published than studies with negative findings), and selective reporting (studies reporting only the outcomes that showed an effect of RIC treatment).

We found six randomised controlled trials (studies in which participants are assigned to one of two or more treatment groups using a random method) involving 204 preterm babies who were being fed with mother's own milk. The other components of the milk varied between studies, but included standard feeds, formula, and other supplements. The protein supplements used were in the form of amino acid solutions. Three of the studies reported some support from manufacturers. One study did not report funding sources. Another study was funded by university grants. Another study was funded by a non-profit organization. Another study was funded by an academic grant. Another study did not report funding. The studies were conducted in high-income countries. We found that protein supplementation of mother’s own milk for preterm infants increased short-term growth. However, it did not clearly improve head growth, and had no clear effect on body proportions. It did not clearly increase the risk of feeding intolerance or the serious bowel problem called necrotising enterocolitis. However, the quality of the evidence was low. This means that we cannot be certain about these findings. The studies were too small, and the evidence about the safety outcomes was very low quality. Therefore, we have no confidence in these results. None of the studies followed up with the infants after they were discharged from hospital, so we do not know if there were any effects of protein supplementation on growth and development during childhood. Future studies need to look at the effects of different levels of protein in multi-nutrient supplements, and assess both short- and long-term outcomes. They also need to assess the effects on the mothers, including their ability to produce enough milk for their babies, and on the environment, including the cost of the intervention.

This review examined whether educational programs consisting of a set of slides with accompanying text and/or audio narration, either interactive or not, hosted on a CD-ROM, DVD, or website, influence healthcare professionals' knowledge, behaviour, and patient outcomes. We found that when professional practice is considered, computer-based education may improve professional practice, although the size of the improvement is small. However, we do not know if patients are affected by this small improvement in professional practice. We do not know whether these educational programs are more effective than other types of education, or whether they are more effective when delivered in combination with other interventions.

In this review we included 23 randomised controlled trials that evaluated a variety of interventions delivered to young women (aged 15-25 years) in a number of different settings. The trials had a variety of different approaches but most involved providing information about STIs and teaching skills related to safer sex such as how to use a condom, how to have open relationships with your partner( s) about sex. Sometimes some free condoms or other materials were also provided. We found that the interventions could increase the likelihood of a woman using a condom for protection against STIs during sex, but this effect was not consistent across all of the studies. We did not find any convincing evidence that interventions encouraged abstinence or reduced the number of sexual partners that young women had. We did not find any convincing evidence of a reduction in actual STI infections (for example chlamydia). None of the studies looked at whether the interventions reduced cervical cancer or warts (HPV infection). Therefore, given the importance of this issue for women's health, future studies should look at the impact of interventions on cervical cancer and HPV. It would be helpful if more studies were carried out in countries other than the USA, where most of the current research has been done.

The aim of this review is to evaluate the effects of brief psychoeducation for people with schizophrenia. Nineteen studies with a total number of 2337 participants were included in this review. Results from the included studies showed that brief psychoeducation can lead to an improvement in people taking their medication, at least in the short term. It may also help to prevent relapse in the medium term. However, the quality of the evidence base is low/very low, and the results should therefore be treated with caution. The authors conclude that there is some evidence that brief psychoeducation is helpful for patients with schizophrenia, but more studies are needed before it can be recommended routinely. Future research needs to focus on the content of brief psychoeducation, its effectiveness for the long term and cost-effectiveness.

This systematic review included 11 randomised controlled trials involving 9839 participants who had moderate to severe COPD. The trials followed people for between six months and two years. All but one trial was sponsored by pharmaceutical companies. This review found that LAMA+LABA inhalers reduced the number of exacerbations compared with ICS+LABA inhalers and improved quality of life. They also showed a small improvement in lung function, and a lower rate of pneumonias. However, these results were based on low quality evidence, which means that there may be potential for biassed results due to the majority of the studies being sponsored by pharmaceutical companies.

The evidence is current to July 2016. We found three small randomised controlled trials with 91 children aged between six months and four years. Two studies were conducted at children's hospitals in the USA, and one study was conducted at a university hospital in Spain. The length of the studies ranged from seven to 16 months. One study compared heliox to 30% humidified oxygen delivered through a mask for 20 minutes in 15 children with mild croup. Heliox may not improve croup symptoms, respiratory rate, or heart rate when compared to 30% humidified oxygen. The study did not report whether children were admitted to hospital, required further treatment, or returned to the emergency department. A second study gave 47 children with moderate croup one dose of oral dexamethasone (0.3 mg/kg) and either heliox for 60 minutes or no additional treatment. Heliox may improve croup symptoms, respiratory rate, and Taussig croup scores (a scale used to measure the severity of croup) when compared to no additional treatment. However, there may be no difference between groups after 120 minutes. There was no difference in the number of children admitted to hospital. The study did not report whether children required further treatment or returned to the emergency department, or whether any children were intubated. A third study gave 29 children with moderate to severe croup intramuscular dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg) and either heliox with one to two doses of nebulised saline, or 100% oxygen with one to two doses of adrenaline for three hours. Heliox may improve croup symptoms when compared to 100% oxygen and adrenaline, but the result was uncertain. The study did not report whether children were admitted to hospital or returned to the emergency department, or whether any children were intubated. The included studies did not report on adverse events, intensive care admissions, or how anxious parents were. The quality of the evidence was low, meaning that we are uncertain about the results. The studies were small, and the wide range of possible results means that we cannot be certain about whether heliox is more or less effective than other treatments. More research is needed to evaluate the role of heliox in the treatment of children with croup.

The purpose of this systematic review was to determine which, if any, of these'red flags' are useful screening tools in identifying fractures in people who present to all levels of care with low back pain. Eight studies were included in the review. The range of percentage of patients with a vertebral fracture in accident and emergency settings ranged from 6.5% to 11% and in primary care from 0.7% to 4.5%. Due to the variation in the types of red flags used, the level of the population sampled and the tests used to diagnose fracture, we could not combine results across studies. Many individual'red flags' identified through single studies had poor accuracy as indicated by the wide range of possible effects. However, some combinations of red flags appear to be more accurate than others. The authors concluded that there is little evidence currently to support the use of many red flags to screen for vertebral fracture in patients with LBP. Further research should focus on identifying smaller sets of red flags that are more accurate, and report the questions they ask, the answers given by patients and how the doctor/physiotherapist arrived at their diagnosis.

Two good quality controlled studies were identified. One study compared the results of optical urethrotomy and open surgery (urethroplasty) in 50 men who had stricture formation following trauma to the urethra during a pelvic fracture. The other study compared re-dilation of the stricture with optical urethrotomy in 210 men with non-traumatic stricture disease. Re-dilation was performed using either a cystoscope or a cutting balloon. The limited number of men included in these studies means that it is not possible to be sure what the true benefits and harms of each treatment are. Further research is needed to provide this information.

We included six studies with a total of 1211 participants, both children and adults. We did not include studies longer than one year. Our main result was whether there was a difference in the number of times people needed additional steroids, either because their symptoms worsened or because they had an asthma attack. There was no difference between groups. However, there were several differences in other measures of asthma that were important. Intermittent ICS was less good at improving most measures of lung function and airway inflammation than daily ICS. This is probably not clinically important, but it is difficult to know for sure without further research. On the other hand, daily ICS was slightly better at controlling symptoms and stopping the need for quick-relief bronchodilators. Growth was only measured in children and was reduced with daily ICS while there was no effect seen with intermittent ICS. Overall, both strategies seemed safe, but the short studies were not big enough to see small differences in side-effects. The studies were well designed and well reported. Most were randomised controlled trials, although one was a parallel-group study. The group sizes were similar and around half the patients knew which treatment they were getting. This review found that there was no difference between the two strategies for the risk of having an asthma attack that required additional oral steroids, but there were several differences in other outcomes that are important when making choices between these two strategies. More research is needed to find out if intermittent ICS is as good as daily ICS over a longer period of time, especially for children.

We found 17 studies involving 1639 people with CKD. Three studies involved 341 people treated with dialysis, four studies involved 168 kidney transplant recipients, and 10 studies involved 1130 people with CKD who were not treated with dialysis or a transplant. The average follow-up period was 12 months. Most studies did not report information about deaths or heart disease. Dieting may have little or no effect on the risk of death or heart disease in people with CKD, although the quality of the evidence was very low. The quality of the evidence was also low or very low for all other outcomes. We found some evidence that dieting may improve quality of life for an individual, but more research is needed to be sure that this was a consistent finding. Dieting may also improve kidney function and blood levels of albumin and cholesterol, and it may lower blood pressure. However, the quality of the evidence was low or very low, so we cannot be sure if these findings are accurate. We do not have any information from randomised controlled trials that can be used to make recommendations about specific diets for people with CKD. All of the studies in this review had problems with their design, methods, or reporting, which lowers our confidence in the findings. More research is needed to examine the effects of different diets for people with CKD.

One study was included in this review. The study compared nebulised beta-2 agonists, saline solution and mist in a tent. Nebulised beta-2 agonists improved clinical scores but there was no improvement when the children breathed in mist from a towel held over a bowl of hot water. More research is needed.

This review aimed to find out if statins are effective for treating people with dementia. We searched for clinical trials in which people with dementia were randomly allocated to receive either statins or placebo (or another type of drug). We found four trials with 1154 participants, aged between 50 and 90 years, who had mild or moderate dementia. These trials compared statin therapy with placebo. Two of the studies included people with Alzheimer’s disease and the other two included people with vascular dementia. Combining results from these trials did not show any significant benefit of statins on measures of cognitive function (memory and thinking) or ability to perform everyday activities. There was also no difference in the number of participants suffering a serious side effect. Although there is some evidence from randomized controlled trials that statins may be beneficial for the prevention of stroke in healthy individuals and those at high risk, there is no evidence currently available for their use in the treatment of an existing stroke or dementia. More research is needed.

This review included four studies involving 149 participants. Two studies looked at the effects of night splinting in 26 children and adults with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 1A; one study looked at the effects of prednisone in 103 boys with Duchenne muscular dystrophy; and one study looked at the effects of surgery in 20 boys with Duchenne muscular dystrophy. The review found that there was insufficient evidence to support or refute any of these interventions for improving ankle range of motion. Further research is needed.

The review set out to assess the effect of mobility and upright positions during labour compared with lying down for women giving birth 'normally'. The review includes 25 randomised controlled trials involving 5218 women. The trials show that women who walk and assume upright positions during the first stage of labour are more likely to give birth 'normally' with less need for interventions, and their babies are less likely to require admission to the neonatal nursery. However, it is not known whether the same benefits apply to women with abnormal labour progress, or if there are any risks. Current midwifery practice encourages women to assume whatever position they find comfortable. Assisting women to assume an upright position may help them to feel more in control and reduce their fear and anxiety. It is therefore recommended that women in low-risk labour should be informed of the benefits of upright positions and encouraged and assisted to assume whatever position they choose.

We searched for randomized trials that compared the outcomes of treatments for TOS with no treatment. We also looked at non-randomized studies that compared one treatment with another, as long as they had a follow-up of at least six months. The review authors wanted to look at the effect of treatments on pain, pins and needles, weakness, and restricted movement. They also looked at whether there were any side effects of the treatments. The review authors included two randomized controlled trials (clinical trials where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatments groups) and one non-randomized study. One trial compared surgery to relieve pressure on the brachial plexus (a nerve network in the shoulder region) with no treatment. It recruited 55 participants with the 'disputed type' of TOS, which means that there is some dispute among doctors about whether their symptoms are due to TOS or not. The other trial, which recruited 37 people with TOS, compared injecting botulinum toxin (which blocks nerve action) into the scalene muscles with injecting salt water into the same muscles. Botulinum toxin is sometimes used to treat muscle spasms. The results of both trials were not very clear because of problems in the way they were conducted. The trial of surgery reported a greater decrease in pain with surgery than with no treatment after six months. The trial of botulinum toxin injections reported no improvement in pain or disability at six months, but did report improvement in abnormal sensations such as pins and needles. There were no adverse events associated with any of the treatments. The review authors concluded that there is little evidence about whether treatments for TOS are effective. There is some evidence that surgery may decrease pain in people with 'disputed TOS', but we do not know if it is safe or effective in people with non-disputed TOS. There is some evidence that botulinum toxin injections have no great benefit over salt water injections. We do not have any evidence from randomized controlled trials for the use of other treatments for TOS, such as analgesics, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, corticosteroid injections, or physical therapies.

This review included 21 studies on 884 people with mild to moderate tendinitis. The studies used a range of non-surgical treatments and compared them to either placebos, usual care, another non-surgical treatment or no treatment. The results show that oral steroids reduce pain and improve function in the short term. A hand brace also improves symptoms in the short term. Ultrasound applied to the tendon for two weeks did not show significant benefit but benefit was evident at seven weeks. Yoga reduces pain in the short term. Mobilising the bones of the wrist also improves symptoms. There is little evidence to support using vitamin B6, diuretics, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, magnet therapy, laser acupuncture, exercise or chiropractic techniques. With the exception of oral steroids, the duration of benefit is unknown. Further research is required to determine this.

We found two randomised controlled trials (RCTs), which included a total of 708 participants with CRVO-ME. The studies compared steroid injections in the eye with no steroid injections. One study (SCORE) used steroid injections containing the drug triamcinolone acetonide, and the other (GENEVA) used a steroid implant. In SCORE, the steroid injections were given monthly for up to six months, whereas the GENEVA implant remained in the eye for up to 12 months. The follow-up period for both studies ranged from 6 to 14 months. The RCTs were funded by pharmaceutical companies. The review authors could not adequately assess the benefits of IVS in this population. The SCORE study had a high percentage of missing outcomes, and the GENEVA study selectively reported outcomes that showed no benefit of the treatment. Adverse effects were more common in the IVS groups, including an increase in eye pressure requiring medication, cataract development, and new blood vessels growing across the retina. The quality of the evidence was low. More research is required to find out if IVS is safe and effective for people with CRVO-ME.

The six studies included in this review involved a total of 394 children aged 6 to 18 years. Five of the six studies demonstrated a significant effect of intranasal steroids in improving nasal obstruction and in reducing the size of enlarged adenoids. However, one study did not demonstrate significant benefit. The greatest degree of improvement was seen with beclomethasone (a steroid used in two different doses in three trials), followed by fluticasone (used in one trial) and mometasone (used in one trial). These studies were only of short duration (1 to 8 weeks). Further studies of longer duration are needed to determine whether the beneficial effects of intranasal steroids are maintained over time. Adenoidectomy is a minor operation where the adenoids are removed. The review found that treatment with intranasal steroids can avoid the need for adenoidectomy in about one-quarter to one-third of children with moderately large or large adenoids who have symptoms of nasal obstruction.

The main aim of this review was to evaluate the effectiveness, safety and cost of epidural therapy for treating pre-eclampsia. We searched for evidence on 30 June 2016 and identified one randomised controlled trial (involving 24 women) that was at a low to unclear risk of bias. The study was conducted in Mexico and compared an anti-hypertensive drug regimen with the addition of lumbar epidural block using 0.25% bupivacaine, 10 mg by mouth through a tube directly into the stomach and 5 mg each hour by continuous mouth through a tube directly into the stomach for six hours. This study did not report on any of the important outcomes for the mother or baby. Although the study showed that there was no clear difference in the Apgar score of the baby at birth and after five minutes, there was a reduction in the diastolic arterial pressure in the mothers. However, the change in the mean arterial pressure and systolic arterial pressure were not significantly different between the two groups. There is currently insufficient evidence to support the use of epidural therapy for the treatment of pre-eclampsia. More high-quality trials are needed to find out if the use of epidural is beneficial for improving health for the mother and her baby.

We included 16 trials (4275 participants) in this review. The trials were short-term (the shortest lasted three days, and the longest 12 weeks). Most participants were men (65%), and they were between 25 and 70 years old. The evidence is current to May 2018. The review showed no beneficial effects of glucocorticosteroids on mortality (number of people who die) or quality of life for patients with alcoholic liver disease. However, we are very unsure about these results because the certainty of the evidence was very low. Furthermore, the results were imprecise, and therefore we cannot exclude that there might be relevant effects of glucocorticosteroids. In four trials, the team of researchers that assessed the outcomes at the end of the trial knew what treatment the participants had received (were randomized to glucocorticosteroids or to a control group), which may have biased the results. For the rest of the trials, it was unclear if the assessors knew which treatment the participants had received. Two trials ended their follow-up before the scheduled time, because a new relevant drug came onto the market. One trial stopped the trial early because of poor recruitment. Five trials did not report funding sources. Eleven trials reported adverse events (side effects); however, they used very different ways to report them, so we could not aggregate the data. One trial reported that 40% of the participants had adverse events, and another trial reported that 84% had adverse events. The rest of the trials reported percentages between 13% and 66%. The most common adverse events were gastrointestinal problems, renal problems, infections, cardiovascular problems, and psychiatric problems. One trial followed-up participants for more than two years, and found that the risk of death was still lower in the group that had received glucocorticosteroids compared to the control group. However, we are very unsure about this result because the certainty of the evidence was very low, and because there was only one trial that provided this long-term follow-up, and this trial was small. We do not know if glucocorticosteroids are effective in the treatment of alcoholic liver disease because the certainty of the evidence was very low, and because the results were imprecise. Therefore, we need well-designed clinical trials, where neither the researchers nor the participants know who is receiving glucocorticosteroids, and where the treatments are given for a longer period of time. Future trials should also report the individual data of all participants, so that we can analyse the effects of glucocorticosteroids in subgroups of people with different baseline characteristics.

This review included four studies with a total of 245 participants. Three studies compared a driver rehabilitation programme with a control group, and one study compared an additional training programme aimed at the retraining of speed of visual processing and visual motor skills. The three groups of people in the studies were similar in terms of age, gender, type of stroke and driving experience. However, the timing of the stroke was different: one group had had their stroke less than six months previously; one group between six and 12 months previously; and one group more than 12 months previously. The studies tested different types of driver rehabilitation programmes, hence it was not possible to combine the results of the individual studies. One study showed improvements in road sign recognition (a cognitive task) immediately after training with a driver rehabilitation programme using a driving simulator. The other studies did not show improvements in any outcomes directly related to driving (on-road assessment, driving tests, cognitive tasks, activities of daily living) immediately after training or at six months. Adverse effects were not reported. It is unclear from this review whether any aspects of driving ability can be improved by driver rehabilitation. Further research is needed to find out which aspects of stroke-related problems can be improved by driver rehabilitation, and the best way to deliver such a programme.

We looked for randomised controlled trials that compared two different durations of systemic corticosteroid treatment for adults with exacerbations of COPD. The studies included in this review had to be less than six months old when they were added to the review. The evidence is current up to June 2016. We included eight studies with 582 participants, of which five studies with 519 participants compared short-duration (three to seven days) and longer-duration (10 to 15 days) corticosteroid treatment and contributed to the meta-analysis. The mean age of participants was 65 to 73 years, the proportion of men ranged from 58% to 84% and exacerbations were severe or very severe. Five studies used oral prednisolone (30 mg daily in four studies, tapered in one) and two studies gave intravenous corticosteroid treatment. These studies appeared well designed and well reported, and we judged them all to be at low risk of bias. The studies included in this review did not include people with mild or moderate COPD; further studies including these groups of people are required. This review suggests that the likelihood is low that shorter courses of systemic corticosteroids (of around five days) lead to worse outcomes than are seen with longer (10 to 14 days) courses. However, a new large study has increased our confidence that five days of oral corticosteroids is likely to be sufficient for treatment of adults with acute exacerbations of COPD. We graded most available evidence as moderate in quality because of imprecision; further research may have an important impact on our confidence in the estimates or may change the estimates.

The evidence is current to February 2017. We found one randomised controlled trial (13 participants) and three ongoing trials. The only included study randomised participants with myelodysplastic syndrome (a type of MDS) to either a restrictive or liberal red blood cell transfusion policy. The study was too small to detect any differences between the two groups in terms of death, number of red blood cell transfusions, or effects on exercise ability. There were also no reports of any anaemia-related complications, including heart failure, or effects on quality of life. However, the study did not report on other important outcomes, such as mortality due to bleeding/infection/transfusion reactions or iron overload, frequency and length of hospital admissions, serious infections or serious bleeding. The quality of the evidence was very low across different outcomes according to GRADE methodology. The real-world implications of these findings are difficult to judge as we do not know if the results would be similar in a larger study. The one included study was very small and only in one subtype of bone marrow disorder (MDS). There are five other types of bone marrow disorders where the role of transfusions is even more uncertain. Therefore, further studies are needed in the various types of bone marrow failure disorders, particularly as the incidence of MDS rises with an ageing population.

We searched for studies evaluating the use of games as an educational strategy for the training of health professionals. We found only two studies that were of good methodological quality and addressed our research question. One study showed that game-based learning resulted in better knowledge about infection control, while the other study suggested that game-based learning may lead to similar levels of knowledge about stroke prevention and management as compared to traditional learning, but patients preferred the game-based approach. The findings of this review do not provide enough evidence to support or refute the use of games as an educational strategy for the training of health professionals. More research is needed to explore the impact of educational games on patient and outcome measures.

In this review we assessed the efficacy of lithium or antidepressants as maintenance treatment in unipolar depression. We found eight studies which met the inclusion criteria, involving 475 people. There is evidence that both lithium and antidepressants are effective as maintenance treatments for unipolar depression, but their relative efficacy is unknown. Due to the low number of studies, and small size of the studies, our confidence in the results is low. It is still not clear from these studies whether lithium or antidepressants are better for maintenance treatment for unipolar depression, or if they have different rates of adverse events. Larger studies are needed to give a more precise answer to the question of what is the best treatment to prevent relapse for people with unipolar depression.

We identified two randomised controlled trials with a total of 130 participants. The two studies compared amifostine versus placebo (no active medication) and showed no differences between the groups with respect to the incidence of dry mouth, the decrease in the uptake of technetium-99m by the salivary glands, and the reduction of blood pressure. Two patients in one study collapsed after initiation of amifostine therapy and had to be treated by withdrawing the infusion and volume substitution. Both patients recovered without sequelae. The authors did not report any information about deaths, side effects, health-related quality of life or costs. We conclude that there is insufficient evidence to support the routine use of amifostine to prevent radioxerostomia in patients treated with high-dose radioactive iodine for thyroid cancer. Future trials should include patient-oriented outcomes to guide decision making.

The evidence is current to January 2016. We found three studies involving 45 children with Down syndrome, aged between 29 months and six years. Two studies compared parent-mediated interventions versus usual care (treatment as usual), and the third compared a parent-mediated plus clinician-mediated intervention versus a clinician-mediated intervention alone. The length of treatment varied from 12 weeks to six months. One study provided nine group sessions and four home-based sessions over a 13-week period. Another study provided weekly, individual clinic-based or home-based sessions lasting 1.5 to 2 hours, over a six-month period. The third study provided one 2- to 3-hour group session followed by bi-weekly, individual clinic-based sessions plus once-weekly home-based sessions for 12 weeks. Two studies found no differences in expressive or receptive language abilities between the groups, whether measured by direct assessment or parent reports. However, they did find that children in the intervention group could use more targeted vocabulary items or utterances with language targets in certain contexts after the intervention, compared to those in the control group; this was not maintained 12 months later. The third study found gains for the intervention group on total-language measures immediately after the intervention. One study did not find any differences in parental stress scores between the groups at any time point up to 12 months after the intervention. All three studies noted differences in most measures of how the parents talked to and interacted with their children after the intervention, and in one study most strategies were still being used by parents in the intervention group 12 months after the intervention. One study looked at adherence to the treatment through attendance data, finding that mothers in the intervention group attended seven out of nine group sessions and were present for four home visits. No study measured parental use of the strategies outside of the intervention sessions. A grant from the Hospital for Sick Children Foundation (Toronto, Ontario, Canada) funded one study. Another received partial funding from the National Institute of Child Health and Human Development and the Department of Education in the USA. The remaining study did not specify any funding sources. Due to the methodological limitations of the studies and the small number of studies included, we considered the overall quality of the evidence to be very low. This means that we have very little confidence in the results, and further research is very likely to have an important impact on our confidence in the estimate of treatment effect.

We searched scientific databases from their inception to January 2015. We found two randomised controlled trials with a total of 148 participants who had heart valve surgery. The evidence is current to January 2015. One study was conducted in the USA and one in Canada. Participants were aged between 36 and 87 years. The duration of the interventions was four weeks in both trials. Both studies compared an exercise programme with no exercise. There was insufficient evidence at 3 to 6 months follow-up to judge the effect of exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation compared to no exercise on all-cause mortality and on serious adverse events. There was also insufficient evidence at 12 months follow-up for the return to work outcome. However, we did find that, compared with control (no exercise), exercise-based rehabilitation may increase exercise capacity. Neither of the included trials reported on health-related quality of life, New York Heart Association class, left ventricular ejection fraction and cost. The quality of the evidence was very low for all-cause mortality and serious adverse events at 3 to 6 months, and it was moderate for exercise capacity. The quality of the evidence was low for the return to work outcome at 12 months. The reasons for the very low quality of the evidence were the small number of participants and the high risk of bias. The reasons for the moderate quality of the evidence were the small number of studies and the small number of participants in the meta-analyses.

This review found five randomised controlled trials including 1130 participants. Two studied meditation, the other three studied multi-disciplinary palliative care programmes that included a chaplain or spiritual counsellor. The quality of the studies was poor. There is no evidence to support or refute the effectiveness of interventions with spiritual or religious components in improving quality of life or well-being at the end of life. More good quality research is needed.

We conducted a search for clinical trials comparing tDCS with sham tDCS (placebo), publishing up to August 2016. We found six studies with 137 participants. Two studies with 45 participants examined the effects of tDCS versus sham tDCS on the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (UPDRS), which measures impairment, disability, and overall health status. There was very low quality evidence that there is no effect of tDCS on overall UPDRS score, while there was evidence that there is an effect on the motor subscore of the UPDRS, which assesses the impact of Parkinson's disease on a person's muscular system. One study looked at the effect of tDCS on the amount of time spent in the immobile off state and the time spent having dyskinesia, but did not find any evidence of an effect. Two studies with a total of 41 participants examined the effect of tDCS on walking speed, and one study examined the effect of tDCS on physical and mental health-related quality of life, but none of these studies found any evidence of an effect. None of the included studies examined the effect of tDCS on activities of daily living. Two out of six studies reported dropouts, adverse events, or deaths occurring during the intervention phase. There was very low quality evidence that there is no difference in the number of people dropping out from the study, experiencing adverse events, or dying between those receiving tDCS and those receiving a sham tDCS intervention. Finally, we found one study with 16 participants examining the effects of tDCS added to movement therapy versus sham tDCS plus movement therapy on walking speed, and there was very low quality evidence that there was no effect. The quality of the evidence throughout all analyses was graded as very low. Further research is needed to determine the effects of tDCS for the treatment of PD.

As of July 2016, we identified an additional four randomised controlled trials, bringing the total number of studies included in this review to 12 (461 participants). We included studies written in any language. Participants were aged 18 years or older with CRPS type 1 diagnosed according to the International Association for the Study of Pain (IASP) criteria. The studies compared LASB with sham (placebo) treatment, other treatments, or no treatment. We did not include studies with fewer than 10 participants. Only two studies compared LASB to a sham procedure and did not show a significant difference in pain intensity immediately after treatment. One study showed a positive effect on pain intensity at one-year follow-up, but this study had a high risk of bias. One study compared thoracic sympathectomy (the surgical disruption of the sympathetic nerves) with injections of the same solution into the muscle, and did not show a significant difference in pain intensity in the short term. However, the solution used in the control group may have leaked out of the muscle and into the surrounding tissue, making the result less reliable. Another study examined whether adding LASB to a rehabilitation programme helped improve recovery. It did not show a significant difference in pain intensity. Eight studies compared sympathetic block to other treatments such as oral analgesics, intravenous analgesics, ketamine, or regional anaesthetic blocks. None of these studies showed a significant difference in pain intensity between the groups. One study compared using ultrasound to guide the needle placement for LASB with not using ultrasound, and did not show a significant difference in pain intensity. The maximum time that people were followed up for in these studies was 52 weeks. We rated the quality of the evidence from the studies using four levels: very low, low, moderate, or high. Very low-quality evidence means that we are very uncertain about the results. High-quality evidence means that we are very confident in the results. The quality of the evidence in this review ranged from very low to moderate. The reasons for downgrading the quality were limitations in how the studies were designed, inconsistencies between the studies, imprecise results, and the fact that the studies measured different outcomes. Because of the low quality of the evidence, we cannot be certain about the effectiveness or safety of LASB for the treatment of CRPS.

We identified 14 clinical trials, including 2488 participants, that investigated the effects of antivirals, corticosteroids, or both. Four of these studies were new for this update. Most were small, and most had significant problems in their design or implementation. We found that the addition of antivirals to corticosteroids may have had little or no effect on the proportion of people left with some facial weakness after three months, compared to using corticosteroids alone. However, we cannot be sure of this because the results were based on only three small studies. Corticosteroids alone were probably better than antivirals alone at treating Bell's palsy, and the combination of antivirals and corticosteroids was better than placebo (a pretend treatment) or no treatment. We found that the combination of antivirals and corticosteroids probably reduced long-term damage to the face nerve compared with corticosteroids alone, and further long-term damage was less likely with corticosteroids than with antivirals. We did not find clear differences in side effects between any of the treatments studied, but we cannot be certain of this because the certainty of the evidence was very low. We found no clear difference in side effects between antivirals and corticosteroids. Based on moderate-certainty evidence, motor synkinesis (unwanted facial movements) and crocodile tears (excessive tearing when eating or chewing) were probably less common in people who took antivirals and corticosteroids compared to those who took placebo and corticosteroids. They were much less common in people who took corticosteroids compared to people who took antivirals. We found no clear difference in side effects between antivirals and corticosteroids. The combination of antivirals and corticosteroids may have little or no effect on the proportion of people left with facial weakness after three months, compared to using corticosteroids alone. Corticosteroids alone were probably better than antivirals alone at treating Bell's palsy, and the combination of antivirals and corticosteroids was better than placebo or no treatment. The combination of antivirals and corticosteroids probably reduced long-term damage to the face nerve compared with corticosteroids alone, and further long-term damage was less likely with corticosteroids than with antivirals. We found no clear difference in side effects between any of the treatments studied, but we cannot be certain of this because the certainty of the evidence was very low.

We conducted a systematic search of the scientific literature to find randomised controlled trials (RCTs) of LHRH agonist therapy versus any other treatment in relapsed ovarian cancer. We found two RCTs, including 97 women, that compared LHRH agonist with either another drug (treosulfan) or a placebo (pretend treatment). One study showed no difference in survival for women taking leuprorelin compared with treosulfan but did report that hair loss, tiredness and some toxicities (nausea/vomiting, nerve damage and low white blood cell count) may be less with leuprorelin. The other trial showed that decapetyl prolonged the time to tumour growth by an average of five weeks when compared with placebo but did not report any information about survival or quality of life. It did report that a lower number of adverse events (mainly hot flushes and vaginal dryness) occurred with decapeptyl. Because the two studies were so different we could not pool the data. However, even if we had been able to combine the results, the numbers of women in the studies were too small to get a reliable picture of the true effect of LHRH agonists in the treatment of relapsed ovarian cancer. Furthermore, the evidence from the included studies were of very low quality, making it difficult to draw conclusions. Therefore, this review shows that there are too few data to assess the true efficacy and safety of LHRH agonists in the treatment of relapsed ovarian cancer. More high-quality clinical trials are needed to provide evidence to support the use of these drugs in the treatment of relapsed ovarian cancer.

We collected and analysed all relevant studies to answer this question, finding 17 randomised controlled trials that met our inclusion criteria. Most of these studies used initial concentrations of 20 parts per million (ppm), which is the current recommended starting dose for infants born at term or near term. No studies used more than 80 ppm. Studies were conducted in both preterm and term infants, but the benefits appeared to be limited to infants without a specific problem (congenital diaphragmatic hernia). Inhaled nitric oxide improves oxygenation (the amount of oxygen delivered to the body) in about half of infants treated and reduces the need for heart-lung bypass (extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO)) in about one in five infants. It does not affect the rate of death. Inhaled nitric oxide is safe, with no significant adverse effects; long-term follow-up of survivors shows no differences in developmental outcomes. The quality of the evidence supporting the use of iNO for the treatment of pulmonary hypertension in the full-term newborn with respiratory failure is high.

The authors searched the literature up to March 2012 and found 15 studies that satisfied the inclusion criteria. The methodological quality of all studies was rather low. The results of the review show that there is moderate evidence that lumbar supports are not more effective than no intervention or training in preventing low-back pain, and conflicting evidence whether lumbar supports are effective supplements to other preventive interventions. It remains unclear whether lumbar supports are more effective than no or other interventions for treating low-back pain.

The authors searched the literature on 20 May 2016 and included 57 randomized controlled trials with 16,784 catheters and 11 different types of antimicrobial impregnations. The total number of participants was unclear, as many studies did not provide this information. Most studies enrolled participants from the age of 18, including patients in intensive care units, oncology units and those receiving long-term total parenteral nutrition (where a nutrient mixture is administered intravenously through a catheter). Antimicrobial-impregnated CVCs were associated with a 2% absolute reduction in the risk of CRBSI, representing 50 cases needing a CRBSI avoided per 1000 catheters. They were also associated with a 9% absolute reduction in the risk of catheter colonization, representing 110 cases of colonization avoided per 1000 catheters. However, there were no significant effects on the risk of developing sepsis or death. Moreover, the current evidence showed no significant differences in the rates of adverse effects, including thrombosis/thrombophlebitis, bleeding, erythema and/or tenderness at the insertion site. We rated the quality of the evidence using the GRADE approach. According to this, the evidence for the effect of antimicrobial CVCs on CRBSI was high quality, while the evidence for the effect on catheter colonization was moderate. The evidence for the effects on sepsis and mortality was moderate to high. The reason for downgrading the quality of the evidence was mainly because of the lack of blinding (the process whereby participants and researchers are unaware of which treatment has been given), which introduced a risk of bias.

We searched for all studies up to March 2017. We found 15 studies conducted in Bangladesh, India, and Peru, including 9261 infants and children and 3664 pregnant women living in slums or poor urban or periurban areas. The studies included three types of interventions: supplementation (with vitamins, minerals, or energy-rich foods), nutrition education for pregnant women, and systems strengthening. Supplementation of pregnant women with zinc had no effect on birth weight or length at 24 months. Supplementation of children with vitamins and minerals had no effect on height-for-age or length at 18 months. Education of pregnant women had a beneficial effect on birth weight. Systems-strengthening interventions had a beneficial effect on length at 18 months. However, all these interventions could also have reduced stunting according to other research studies. The quality of the evidence was generally low to very low. The studies were not designed to measure inequality. Further research is needed to understand how to best address stunting and low birth weight in slums. It is important to test the effectiveness of multi-sectorial interventions that combine nutrition-specific and -sensitive approaches, as well as interventions targeting 'up-stream' practices and policies of governments, NGOs, and the business sector.

We evaluated the evidence from 12 randomised controlled trials, which reported on 14 different comparisons and involved a total of 1023 participants with venous leg ulcers. The main outcome was the percentage of ulcers healed, measured over a set time period (usually four to 20 weeks). We also recorded the number of adverse events (harms) experienced by participants. We found that there was no clear difference in the percentage of ulcers healed when foam dressings were compared with other types of dressings. This finding applies to all of the specific dressing types that we assessed, including: hydrocolloid dressings; paraffin gauze dressings; hydrocapillary dressings; knitted viscose dressings; and protease modulating matrix dressings. We did not detect any clear difference in the rate of adverse events among people wearing foam dressings when these were compared with other dressing types. Overall, we conclude that there is insufficient evidence to show that one type of foam dressing is better than another type of foam dressing, or that foam dressings are better than other types of dressings for treating venous leg ulcers. Further good quality studies have been identified that will be included in future updates of this review.

The researchers identified two randomized controlled trials that compared the COX-2 inhibitor celecoxib to placebo (a fake drug) and one trial that compared the COX-2 inhibitor etoricoxib to placebo. The celecoxib and etoricoxib trials enrolled a total of 381 patients with inflammatory bowel diseases and arthritis. The celecoxib trial showed no significant difference in flare-ups of ulcerative colitis between celecoxib and placebo (4% versus 6%). The etoricoxib trial showed no significant difference in flare-ups of IBD between etoricoxib and placebo (17% versus 19%). Serious side effects were uncommon with either celecoxib or etoricoxib. Non-serious side effects included increased frequency of bowel movements, rectal bleeding, and inflammation of the colon lining. These occurred at the same frequency in patients taking celecoxib or etoricoxib (11%) as it did in patients taking placebo (12%). The researchers could not combine the results of the two studies because of the different types of IBD in the patients being studied and the length of time patients were treated with the drugs. Also, the smaller number of patients in the studies limits our ability to draw definite conclusions about side effects. This systematic review shows that there are few studies looking at the side effects of celecoxib and etoricoxib in people with inflammatory bowel diseases and arthritis. More research is needed to be sure that these drugs do not cause side effects in these people.

This review includes 22 randomised clinical trials with a total of 2310 participants. One study did not report the number of participants. The studies varied greatly in size (ranging from just four to 317 participants) and duration (from 4 to 24 weeks). In the majority of studies, the level of ulcer infection was unclear, only six studies clearly stated that all of the ulcers were infected at the start of the study, and one study included people with both infected and uninfected ulcers. The included studies tested various antimicrobial compounds, including silver, iodides, silver sulphadiazine, tretinoin, pexiganan cream, chloramine, and others. Most studies used a simple 'active treatment versus control' design, but one study compared two different types of antibiotic and another compared three different antimicrobial creams against each other. The results from the few studies comparing topical treatments with systemic antibiotics were inconsistent and the overall quality of the evidence was very low. Overall, the review found that: 1) use of an antimicrobial dressing instead of a non-antimicrobial dressing may increase the number of diabetic foot ulcers healed (low-certainty evidence); 2) there is probably little difference in the risk of adverse events related to treatment between systemic antibiotics and topical antimicrobial treatments (moderate-certainty evidence); and 3) we are uncertain whether or not there are any differences between other types of topical antimicrobial treatments (all very-low certainty evidence). The certainty of the evidence was mainly affected by the small number of studies in each comparison, the small number of participants in the studies, poor descriptions of how the studies were conducted, and the wide range of results among the studies.

The review authors searched the available literature up to 19 November 2015 and included six randomised controlled trials (studies where people were allocated at random to one of two groups) involving 5193 children. The results of the review show that zinc supplementation reduces the occurrence of new episodes of pneumonia as well as the number of existing cases of pneumonia in children under five years of age. Supplementation had a greater impact on pneumonia confirmed by clinical examination while it had lesser effect on pneumonia based on faster breathing rate. The quality of the included studies was low. Future studies should aim to improve the methodological quality and report more detailed information about the type of zinc supplement used and its dosage.

The review authors searched the medical literature and identified 10 relevant studies enrolling 33,179 participants. The quality of the evidence was moderate. The results show that vitamin A does not have a large impact on the risk of developing acute lower respiratory tract infections (LRTIs) in the general population. However, there may be a beneficial effect in children with malnutrition. Supplementation had a greater effect on the risk of pneumonia in those with acute malnutrition than in well-nourished children. In one study vitamin A had no effect on pneumonia; in another it increased the risk of pneumonia. These two studies were both in well-nourished children. One study showed that vitamin A had an adverse effect on the risk of LRTI in children who were well-nourished. Further research is needed to assess the possible harmful effect of vitamin A in well-nourished children. The currently recommended dose of vitamin A for children is 100,000 IU. Lower doses may be just as effective with fewer side effects.

This review examines the effectiveness of the drug theophylline for treating people with COPD. Twenty trials were included in this review. The trials showed that theophylline improves lung function (forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV1) and vital capacity (FVC)) and resting blood gases (arterial oxygen tension (PaO2) and arterial carbon dioxide tension (PaCO2)). However, these improvements were relatively modest. The effects of theophylline on exercise tolerance and symptoms of breathlessness were variable. The exact role of theophylline as add-on therapy to inhaled steroids is not clear from this review.

We included 10 randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatments groups) with 439 children (249 in the treatment group and 190 in the control group), aged 1 year to 18 years. The trials used different amounts of egg powder added to milk or other drinks and different feeding schedules. None used a solid food form (such as an egg-based snack) or sublingual immunotherapy (where a dry form of the allergen is placed under the tongue). Most children (82%) in the group receiving oral immunotherapy could eat 1 gram to 7.5 grams of egg (about one-eighth to one-half of a large egg) without having a reaction compared to 10% of control group children. Half of the children in the oral immunotherapy group could eat a full serving of egg (30 grams) without reacting compared to 10% of the control group. Oral immunotherapy did not result in many children being completely free from egg allergy; however, this may have been due to the short duration of studies (most three years or less). Serious allergic reactions during oral immunotherapy were common, with 1 in 12 children experiencing a serious reaction in one study, and 1 in 20 across all studies. Some children stopped treatment because of adverse events. There were some problems with the way the trials were done. Different studies used different amounts of egg and ways of giving the treatment; some used a placebo (a substance containing no egg) for the control group and others used an egg-free diet. Also, some studies were not clearly explained or were not fair tests. This made it difficult to be sure how reliable the results were. We judged the quality of the evidence to be low. This means that we are uncertain about the results and they may change with more research. Future research may find that oral immunotherapy is safer and more effective than described in this review.

We searched scientific databases for clinical trials comparing an ILR-based diagnostic strategy versus a conventional diagnostic strategy in patients with syncope. We found four randomised controlled trials, including 579 patients with syncope who had undergone both ILR implant and conventional testing. The evidence is current to September 2016. There was no evidence of an impact on long-term or short-term deaths. Patients who underwent ILR implantation had more diagnoses made, but it remains unclear whether this improved their quality of life or reduced the number of syncopal relapses. Higher costs were associated with the ILR group, mainly due to the cost of the device itself. Our results are in line with the recently published European Society of Cardiology guidelines, which state that an ILR can be useful in selected patients, but does not provide evidence of long-term benefit. The quality of the evidence varied from very low to moderate. This was mainly due to the small number of patients included in the studies, the high number of dropouts and the fact that the two studies that reported long-term mortality did not include death as the primary endpoint.

We searched for all randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that compare the efficacy and safety of sclerotherapy versus surgery for the treatment of hydroceles. We included four RCTs in this review, which enrolled 275 patients with 282 hydroceles. The number of participants in each group varied between studies. The duration of follow-up also varied between 6 weeks to 12 months. In all four included studies the intervention was performed by a single surgeon. The sclerosing solution used in all four studies was hypertonic saline. The volume of the sclerosing solution injected also varied between 50ml to 200ml. The results showed that there was no significant difference between the two treatments in terms of clinical cure rate. However there was significant uncertainty in this result due to differences between the studies. There was a significant increase in recurrence in those who received sclerotherapy compared with surgery. One study reported a non-significant decrease in fever in the sclerotherapy group. There was an increased number of infections in the surgery group however this increase was not statistically significant. Three studies reported the frequency of pain in the surgery group was higher than aspiration and sclerotherapy group. Because of different measurement tools applied in these studies, we could not pool the results. There was no significant difference in haematoma formation between the two groups. Only one study reported patient satisfaction at three and six months; there was no significant difference between the two groups. Postoperative complications as well as cost and time to work resumption were less in the aspiration and sclerotherapy group; however the recurrence rate was higher. The limited number of studies included in this review precluded meaningful pooling of results for many outcomes. The quality of the included studies was moderate. The curation of hydrocele fluid collection and the amount of sclerosing solution injected may be important factors to consider when considering the two treatment options. More research is needed to determine the best management strategy for hydrocele.

The aim of this review was to find out which treatment is most effective in relieving symptoms and improving lung function in children with tracheomalacia. One randomised controlled trial comparing nebulised rhDNase with placebo in 40 children with airway malacia and a respiratory tract infection was included in the review. Nebulised recombinant human deoxyribonuclease (rhDNase) did not improve outcomes when compared to placebo. In fact, all outcomes favouring improvement were in the placebo group. This includes the primary outcome of cough-free breath at two weeks, night-time cough diaries, day-time cough diaries, dyspnoea and difficulty in expectorating sputum scores, and lung function tests. The only adverse event reported was a mild reaction to the nebuliser solution in one child. This review shows an absence of evidence to support any of the therapies currently being utilized for the management of tracheomalacia in children. More research is needed to identify the best mode of treatment for this condition.

The quality of the included studies was low to moderate. Many studies have methodological problems such as incomplete reporting of randomisation, or of the treatment received by each participant. Half of the studies were at high risk of bias due to lack of blinding of therapists, participants, or both. The interventions consisted of 12 hours of treatment over a period of one day up to nine months. Duration of follow-up was between two weeks and 24 months. Most studies included participants with long-standing symptoms. Psychological therapies were better than usual care or a waiting list in reducing the severity of physical symptoms. However, the size of the difference was small. This finding holds for all types of psychological therapy together and for cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) separately. The positive effect of psychological therapy was present immediately after the end of treatment and remained stable up to one year later. Compared with enhanced or structured care, psychological therapies were not clearly more effective. More people dropped out of psychological therapy than they dropped out of enhanced care. This was true for all types of psychological therapy and for CBT. Drop-out rates were comparable to those reported in other mental health outcomes. The numbers of patients reporting side effects were low, but many studies did not report this information. No serious side effects were reported. Limitations of our review include a low number of studies on physical symptoms and somatisation disorder, and a low number of studies on children and adolescents. In addition, we do not know what percentage of patients are not willing to receive psychological treatment. Our results may not apply to this group.

We found 63 relevant randomized controlled trials (studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) looking at whether regional anaesthesia can stop patients having PPP after surgery. We included 39 of these studies in our main analysis, with 3027 participants. The studies involved adults over 18 years old with no history of nerve damage or chronic pain. They had had one of five types of surgery: caesarian section, mastectomy for breast cancer, thoracotomy (opening up the chest wall to access the lungs), iliac crest bone graft harvesting (harvesting bone from the hip to use in another part of the body) or limb amputation. We did not include studies involving children. The type of regional anaesthesia used was either a local anaesthetic injected into the area of the operation, an epidural (a catheter is placed in the epidural space around the spinal cord and local anaesthetic is given through it) or intravenous infusion of local anaesthetic (the anaesthetic is given directly into a vein). We looked at the development of PPP three to 12 months after surgery. We found that there is a 57% reduction in the risk of developing PPP if you have an epidural for thoracotomy, a 58% reduction in the risk of developing PPP if you have an epidural for a caesarean section and a 57% reduction in the risk of developing PPP if you have an injection of local anaesthetic into the tissue around a breast tumor removed for breast cancer. We also found a 76% reduction in the risk of developing PPP if you receive an intravenous infusion of local anaesthetic after breast cancer surgery. However, these results should be viewed with some caution as the quality of the evidence was only moderate to low. There were problems with the design of many of the studies which means that we cannot be completely sure of the size of the true effect. Also, the studies were very small, making it difficult to tell if the result is precise or imprecise. We do not have enough evidence to say whether any type of regional anaesthesia can prevent PPP after any of the five surgeries investigated in this review. We do not know if regional anaesthesia can prevent PPP after other types of surgery and we cannot recommend any particular technique of regional anaesthesia. There are seven ongoing studies and 12 studies waiting further assessment that may change the conclusions of the review once they are published.

This review aimed at investigating the efficacy and acceptability of pharmacological treatments for borderline personality disorder (BPD). We searched scientific databases for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) of pharmacological interventions targeting BPD. Twenty-eight studies were identified as suitable for inclusion, comprising a total of 1742 participants. A wide range of medications were tested, including antidepressants, mood stabilisers, antipsychotics and dietary supplementation. The quality of the evidence base was limited, with few studies reporting outcome data for the core BPD symptoms of chronic feelings of emptiness, identity disturbance and abandonment. Furthermore, very few studies reported adequate information about the treatments administered prior to the RCTs, and drop-out rates were low suggesting that adverse events were rare. Overall, the findings were suggestive of the potential benefits of second-generation antipsychotics, mood stabilisers, and omega-3 fatty acids, but these results need confirmation in additional, larger studies. Antidepressants are not widely supported for BPD treatment, but may be helpful in the presence of comorbid conditions. Total BPD severity was not significantly influenced by any drug.

The evidence is current to September 2017. We found seven trials that compared different policies for keeping the catheter in place after surgery. These included a total of 349 participants, of whom 217 completed the studies. In four trials people had a catheter for less than a week and in three trials it was for longer. We found that we do not have enough evidence to say whether using a washout policy after surgery reduces the chance of having a urinary tract infection while the catheter is in place or how long the catheter needs to stay in to reduce the chance of infection. We also do not know what effects our proposed interventions have on the length of time someone needs the catheter or on their overall recovery from the operation. Some people experienced side effects from the washout solutions, such as seeing blood in the washout solution, increased bladder spasms, and changes in blood pressure readings. More research is needed to answer the questions of this review. The quality of the evidence from the included studies was low or very low. This was due to problems in the way the studies were designed, small numbers of participants in the studies, and poor reporting of study details.

The evidence is current to September 2015. We found 30 relevant studies, including 18 studies (with 14,573 participants) that answered our main question. We also included 22 studies in our review, which addressed our secondary questions. The quality of the evidence was low. We do not know exactly how many childhood cancer survivors are tired. This is because the definition of 'tiredness' varied between studies, as did the timing of assessments, and the way in which severity of tiredness was measured. Also, the treatments given to children with cancer differed greatly between the studies, as did their age, and the type of the cancer they had. In addition, the way in which the different studies assessed other factors that might be related to tiredness, such as psychological (e.g. anxiety, depression) and physical (e.g. obesity, muscle function) factors, differed greatly. This is why we could not combine the results from these studies in an analysis. Instead, we describe the range of results across studies. Overall, 4% to 62% of the children in the studies we looked at were severely tired at some point during follow-up. This percentage was higher in older children and young people, and in those whose cancer was treated with a combination of chemotherapy and radiotherapy. It was lower in those whose cancer was treated with radiotherapy only. In comparison, 3% to 10% of the siblings or people in the community who were assessed were severely tired. This suggests that survivors may be more likely to feel tired than others, although more research is needed to show this clearly. Only one study followed a group of 102 survivors over time (2.7 years). This study showed that 33 of the survivors (32%) were still severely tired at different times during follow-up. More research is needed to understand how long children and young people may remain tired after treatment for cancer, and what the effect is on their quality of life.

The review found 36 trials with 6914 participants. The trials showed that the use of prophylactic antibiotics reduced the rate of respiratory tract infections and overall mortality in critically ill adults. However, the evidence for the use of prophylactic antibiotics is not strong. There were differences between the trials in the types of bacteria that were studied, the dose and duration of antibiotic therapy and the intensity of the critical illness. Furthermore, the number of deaths was low indicating that the interventions used were effective. The review also found that there was no increase in the rate of antibiotic resistance.

This review set out to find all studies which evaluated the effect of education on the use of physical restraints in elderly people in nursing homes and other long-term care settings. Five randomised controlled trials were identified. They had to be randomised because the reviewers wanted to reduce the risk of bias as much as possible. Randomised controlled trials are often used to evaluate the effects of interventions. In these five studies, the intervention was education of nursing staff about alternative ways to manage behaviour problems. Four of the studies took place in nursing homes and one in group dwelling units. Education was given to the nursing staff working with the participants. In one study additional consultation was also provided. Another study offered guidance for nursing staff in addition to education. What the included studies found The five studies showed inconsistent results. One nursing home study found that the use of physical restraints increased in both the intervention and the control group after eight months. The other three nursing home studies found reduced use of physical restraints in the intervention groups and no reduction or even a slight increase in the control groups after seven and 12 months, respectively. The study in group dwelling units found no change in the use of physical restraints in the intervention group and a significant increase in the control group after six months. Quality of the evidence The quality of the studies was low. It is not possible to conclude from these five studies whether education of nursing staff alone reduces or increases the use of physical restraints in geriatric long-term care.

Two randomised controlled trials with 310 women compared glucocorticoid supplementation during IVF stimulation versus placebo. We were able to include data from both trials in the analysis for the outcomes of clinical pregnancy and live birth. However, when including the outcome of multiple births, we were only able to use data from one trial with 20 women. One of the two studies had serious design weaknesses, with possible over-estimation of benefits and under-estimation of harms. The evidence from the other high-quality trial suggested that glucocorticoids might increase the likelihood of a clinical pregnancy, but may have little or no effect on the likelihood of a live birth. Due to the small number of studies and low event rates, the overall quality of the evidence was assessed as low or very low. Suggested chances of live birth with and without glucocorticoid supplementation, assuming a 15% chance of live birth without supplementation, were between 7% and 31%. Suggested chances of clinical pregnancy with and without glucocorticoid supplementation, assuming a 24% chance of clinical pregnancy without supplementation, were between 23% and 47%. Currently, more research is needed to clarify the effects of glucocorticoids on live birth rate.

The review includes eight trials with 291 participants (aged between five and 23 years) who received either rhGH or a placebo (no treatment). Six trials lasted for one year and two trials for six months. This review did not find any trials longer than one year. This review did not receive any funding. Growth hormone therapy appears to improve height, weight and lean body mass in children with cystic fibrosis, but these findings are based on only a small number of patients. There was no evidence of an improvement in lung function or clinical status during treatment. No significant side effects were seen other than an increase in the level of fasting blood glucose, which did not rise to the level of being diabetic. However, these trials were only six to 12 months long, which is not long enough to measure any potential side effects of long-term treatment. Longer-term trials are needed before its use can be recommended routinely in people with cystic fibrosis. Overall the quality of the evidence was low to very low. The main limitations were the small number of participants, the short duration of the trials and the fact that the results were not consistent across all trials.

This review includes 26 studies with 1,695 participants who were exposed to chemicals at work and had asthma because of their work. We looked at three different types of workplace interventions: removal from exposure, reduction of exposure, and reduction of personal exposure. Removal from exposure means that the worker stopped working in the job that caused the asthma. Reduction of exposure means that the worker either changed to another job that did not cause asthma, or stayed in the same job but reduced the amount of time spent in the exposure situation, improved ventilation in the workspace, or used personal protective equipment such as masks. The studies we found showed that all three types of interventions may improve symptoms and lung function. However, removal from exposure may improve lung function more than reduction of exposure, especially for people with asthma caused by high molecular weight agents (> 30 kDa). On the other hand, reduction of exposure may be better for preventing unemployment. Also, reduction of exposure may be less expensive than removing the person from exposure. The quality of the studies was very low. More research is needed to evaluate the effect of workplace interventions for occupational asthma.

We included six randomized controlled trials with a total of 1758 participants who received either continuous proton pump inhibitor therapy or on-demand therapy (five trials) or abrupt discontinuation of proton pump inhibitors (one trial). The participants were aged between 48 and 57 years, except for one trial where the average age was 73 years. All participants had mild reflux disease or inflammation of the esophagus. Five trials used a 'double-dummy design,' i.e. both groups took a proton pump inhibitor, but those in the on-demand group only took it when they experienced symptoms.
