One study compared a double-dummy approach with actual cessation of proton pump inhibitor therapy. Proton pump inhibitors are effective anti-acid drugs widely used to treat gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GERD) and other related disorders. They work by reducing gastric acid secretion. Many people taking these drugs for several months or longer report side effects such as headache, constipation, and nausea. Some people also develop a secondary problem called Clostridium difficile diarrhea. This systematic review found that people who take a proton pump inhibitor continuously may have fewer symptoms and side effects than those who only take the drug when they experience symptoms. However, people who take the drug only when they have symptoms may be less satisfied with their treatment. We did not find any evidence about the effects of stopping this type of drug after several weeks of use, but two of the included studies reported an assessment of the lining of the esophagus. Further research is needed to determine whether there are benefits of stopping this class of drug after several weeks of use and whether these benefits outweigh the risks. The included studies were generally of reasonable methodological quality; however, some were more well-conducted than others. Overall, the quality of evidence was low to moderate.

The review authors searched for randomised controlled trials which compared social skills training to standard care or to other interventions. They found 13 trials, with 975 participants either receiving social skills training or standard care; or social skills training or participation in a discussion group. The results of the review show that social skills training seems to lead to an improvement in social functioning when compared to standard care. However, the quality of the evidence was very low. When social skills training was compared with another intervention (in this case, a discussion group) there was no difference in patient outcomes. This review was conducted from a Western perspective, however it has been noted that most of the included studies were carried out in China. Therefore the generalisability of the results to other cultures is limited. In order to strengthen the evidence base regarding the effects of social skills training for people with schizophrenia, there would have to be more trials performed, including a large multi-centre trial.

We searched for clinical trials of treatments for gouty tophi. One study (published in 2010) was found that compared three treatments: biweekly infusions of 8 mg pegloticase, monthly infusions of 8 mg pegloticase, and a control treatment (placebo - a fake infusion). The study included 225 people with gout and tophi; 145 had tophi at the start of the trial. Results showed that biweekly pegloticase improved tophi by removing them completely in 5 in 10 people, compared with 1 in 10 with the control treatment. However, it was noted that some people experienced a severe reaction during or shortly after their treatments, known as an infusion reaction, and these people were more likely to stop treatment early. Monthly pegloticase also improved tophi, but to a lesser extent, with no complete resolution in any participants. Quality of life, participant-reported pain relief and serum uric acid levels were improved for all participants, but not analysed separately for those with tophi. As this is the only study included in this review, the results should be viewed with caution. The study was well designed, with low risk of bias, so this is moderate-quality evidence.

This review found only three randomized controlled trials with a total of 2095 newborns comparing oral immunoglobulins (IgG and IgG/IgA) with placebo for preventing NEC. The evidence is current to August 2016. Oral immunoglobulins did not reduce the rate of NEC, NEC with a high degree of certainty, need for surgery or death from NEC. There were no adverse events reported. There were no completed studies using oral immunoglobulin A (IgA) monotherapy. The sample size was too small to detect differences in outcomes. The quality of the included studies was low. Further studies evaluating oral immunoglobulins for the prevention of NEC with a higher number of newborns are needed.

This review found five randomised controlled trials which compared no additional treatment with additional chemotherapy or radiotherapy after hysterectomy in women with early stage endometrial cancer. It also included four trials that compared platinum-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy. Combining the results of these trials showed a small but significant improvement in survival among women who had chemotherapy. Further analyses of the trials found that this was due to a reduction in the risk of dying from any cause by a quarter and a reduction in the risk of developing a secondary cancer elsewhere in the body. However, the risk of developing a secondary cancer in the pelvis was not reduced as effectively by chemotherapy as by radiotherapy. Adverse effects were higher with chemotherapy than with radiotherapy. Chemotherapy reduces the risk of dying from endometrial cancer by a small amount when given after surgery to remove the womb. It may reduce the risk of the cancer recurring by a small amount and does not add much extra risk of side effects.

This review focused on the potential of using electronic systems to send messages to patients or clients, for example to remind them of an appointment, to provide them with health information, or to encourage them to change their behaviour. We included 35 studies from a wide range of countries on six continents. The studies covered a range of digital targeted client communication, for example, medication or appointment reminders, prenatal health information, support for stopping smoking during pregnancy, or general sexual health information. Our synthesis showed that people's experiences of these types of programmes were mixed. Some felt that these programmes gave them feelings of support and connectedness. They also shared the messages with their friends and family. However, some had poor access to cell networks and to the internet, or could not afford to pay for credit for their phone. Women and young people often had their access to phones controlled by others. The cost of messages could also be a problem, and many thought that messages should be free of charge. Language issues as well as skills in reading, writing, and using mobile phones could also be a problem. People with stigmatised or personal health conditions, such as HIV, family planning, or abortion care, were also concerned about privacy and confidentiality. Some suggested ways to improve these problems, such as using neutral language and tailoring the content, start time, and how often the messages were sent. They also had preferences for different ways to send the messages and for the content of the messages, including new knowledge, reminders, solutions, and suggestions about health issues. Whether people trusted the person sending the messages could affect their views of the programme. We found that many of the studies did not try to address these problems, although it may have been reported inadequately in the published papers. Our synthesis identified several factors that can influence the successful implementation of targeted client communication programmes using mobile devices. These include barriers to use that have equity implications. Programme planners should take these factors into account when designing and implementing programmes. Future trial authors also need to actively address these factors and to report their efforts in their trial publications.

The review authors searched the medical literature and identified 23 randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people were allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) published up to April 2012. The studies involved a total of 2467 opioid-dependent participants who were all treated with the same protocol except for the medication used. The comparison was made between methadone and: (1) other pharmacological treatments (22 studies); (2) placebo (two studies). There were no clear differences between the various pharmacological treatments in terms of the number of people who completed the scheduled period of maintenance, the number of people abstinent at follow-up, the level of discomfort due to withdrawal symptoms and side effects. The only difference found was in the number of dropouts from the studies, with the placebo group showing significantly higher dropout rates than the methadone group. This finding may be due to the greater severity of the withdrawal symptoms in the placebo group. The lack of uniformity in the way the studies were carried out meant that we could not combine the data from individual studies. However, even if the studies had been able to provide data which could have been combined, the futility of using one pharmacological substance as a substitute for another has already been shown by the failure of the international community to eradicate the use of illicit drugs of abuse over the past 30 years. In conclusion, all the medications tested produced similar results in terms of the number of people who completed the scheduled period of maintenance and the number of people abstinent at follow-up. All the medications caused some side effects, although the nature of these varied depending on the type of drug used.

The review found nine controlled trials, involving 1109 opioid-dependent participants. Best estimate was that 67 people would need to be treated with anaesthetic block for one person to experience an adverse event. This number is likely to be an underestimate as some studies did not report adverse events adequately. The high rate of adverse events means that there are serious ethical concerns about putting people in hospital for the purpose of inducing opioid withdrawal under anaesthesia. Withdrawal can also be precipitated by using an opioid antagonist such as naloxone or naltrexone. Precipitating withdrawal with an antagonist is more intense but shorter than withdrawal produced by reducing the dose of a longer-acting opioid like methadone. Precipitating withdrawal can cause very uncomfortable symptoms, but it is unlikely to be as severe as withdrawal from heroin or morphine. It can be managed with symptomatic medication and is likely to be less expensive and safer than withdrawal under anaesthesia.

This review examines the effects of molindole for schizophrenia compared to placebo and other antipsychotic drugs. The review includes 14 studies with a total of 269 people. The results of the review show that molindole is as good as other typical antipsychotic drugs but causes significantly more weight loss. However, the number of people in the studies was too small, and the quality of the research too poor, to be sure of this.

The review authors evaluated the efficacy and safety of treatments for melasma. We included 20 studies with a total of 2125 participants covering 23 different treatments. These can be roughly divided into three groups: 1) treatments using a bleaching agent such as hydroquinone; 2) triple-combination creams (hydroquinone, tretinoin, and fluocinolone acetonide); and 3) combination therapies (hydroquinone cream and glycolic acid peels), as well as less conventional therapies including rucinol, vitamin C iontophoresis, and skin-lightening complexes like Thiospot and Gigawhite. The limited evidence found did not allow us to make any clear recommendations for the treatment of melasma. However, it did show that triple-combination cream was more effective at lightening melasma than hydroquinone alone or in combination with other treatments. Also azelaic acid may be more effective than low concentration hydroquinone, while higher concentrations of hydroquinone may cause more side effects. Further research is needed to find an optimal treatment for melasma.

We searched the literature in July 2013 and identified 12 randomised controlled trials that compared 5-FU to control in the first year after surgery. These included 1319 participants whose average age ranged from 61 to 75 years. 83% of participants were white and 40% were male. The duration of the trials was a minimum of one year. We found that anti-VEGF causes a small but significant decrease in the risk of failure in the first year after surgery, with an average of 14 fewer failures per 1000 operations. This translates to an average of four fewer failures per 1000 operations in the high risk of failure group and five fewer per 1000 in the primary trabeculectomy group. Anti-VEGF also reduces intraocular pressure by an average of 4.7 mmHg in the first year after surgery in those having their first cataract operation together with their glaucoma surgery, and by an average of 16.3 mmHg in those with a high risk of failure. However, it increases the risk of some complications. None of the trials asked the participants about their views on the treatment. Not all trials reported on the outcomes we considered important. There appears to be little or no difference in the risk of serious complications, but other complications are more common in those receiving 5-FU. The quality of the evidence was variable and was lowest for combined surgery and low-dose postoperative 5-FU. Currently 5-FU is only used on a selective basis postoperatively, perhaps because of its side effects. However, none of the trials addressed the issue of patient preference.

This review of trials found that long-term treatment with ICS does not consistently slow the rate of decline in lung function, although it does improve symptoms and reduces the number of exacerbations suffered by people with COPD. Inhaled steroids do have side effects, which include thrush in the mouth and throat, hoarseness and an increased risk of pneumonia. It is therefore important for people with COPD and their doctors to carefully weigh up these potential benefits and side effects when considering whether or not to start using ICS.

Cochrane authors searched for clinical trials in July 2013. We found 80 randomised controlled trials (5820 women) comparing 20 different NSAIDs against each other, or against a placebo (pretend treatment), paracetamol (a common pain reliever) or other drugs. NSAIDs were found to be slightly better at reducing pain than placebo, but caused more side effects. The comparison between individual NSAIDs was less clear, although naproxen appears to work well with fewer side effects. NSAIDs were found to be better at reducing pain than paracetamol, but there was not enough information about side effects. Most of the people who did the research were paid by drug companies. Low-quality evidence suggests that NSAIDs are better at reducing pain than placebo, but cause more side effects. High-quality NSAIDs, such as naproxen, may cause fewer side effects. NSAIDs are better at reducing pain than paracetamol, but we don't know if they have similar side effects.

The evidence is current to September 2016. We found seven randomized controlled trials that compared high versus low levels of positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) in adults with acute respiratory distress syndrome. A total of 2565 participants were enrolled in these seven studies. Five of the studies involved 2417 participants and examined the impact of high versus low PEEP in combination with the same tidal volume (the volume of air displaced by breathing) in both groups. Two other studies, which included 148 participants, examined the effect of high versus low PEEP in conjunction with different tidal volumes in the high- and low-PEEP groups. Studies were of variable quality, and we identified some concerns regarding methodology in three of the studies. The main analysis included five studies that compared high versus low PEEP in conjunction with the same tidal volume in both groups. No clear evidence showed a reduction in mortality before hospital discharge, and no increased risk of barotrauma was observed in the high-PEEP group. Oxygenation was improved in the high-PEEP group. The number of ventilator-free days showed no significant difference between the two groups. Limited information was available on length of stay in the intensive care unit. Among participants with ARDS, however, those receiving high levels of PEEP had a lower chance of dying in the intensive care unit. Included studies were diverse in terms of the populations they examined and the other treatments provided. Current evidence suggests that high levels of PEEP, as compared with low levels, did not reduce mortality before hospital discharge. High levels of PEEP made little or no difference in the risk of barotrauma but improved oxygenation on the first, third, and seventh days.

The aim of this updated systematic review was to determine if there are any studies that show a difference in kidney function between people receiving HES and those who received alternative fluid types (e.g. crystalloids, albumin) during the same time period. Twenty three new studies were added to this review, giving a total of 42 studies (11,399 patients). The studies were generally of good methodological quality. Analysis of all studies combined showed an increased risk of AKI in patients receiving HES as well as an increased risk of requiring dialysis. This was the case for both high and low molecular weight HES solutions, as well as for both small and large volumes of HES. The reason for this injury appears to be due to a direct toxic effect of HES on the kidneys rather than due to a compensatory response from the kidneys in an attempt to maintain blood flow. This toxicity may be less in patients with severe sepsis, explaining why no significant differences were seen in the subgroup analysis of patients with sepsis. We conclude that HES solutions cause AKI and therefore should not be used as a volume expander. Alternate fluid types such as crystalloids or albumin should be used instead.

This systematic review examined the effects of Rheum officinale on improving kidney function in patients with CKD. We found nine randomised controlled trials that involved 682 patients. These studies did not blind the patients or the clinicians to which treatment the patients received. The quality of the trials was variable but it was possible to show that Rheum officinale improved some measures of kidney function. However, the studies were few in number, small in size, and were assessed as having a low or unclear risk of bias so the overall quality of the evidence was low. This means that we cannot be certain about the findings. There was insufficient information to make any recommendations about using Rheum officinale to treat patients with CKD. More research is needed.

We searched existing literature on the accuracy of IQCODE for identifying dementia, and combined the results in a meta-analysis. We found that IQCODE accurately identifies people with dementia about 91% of the time, but incorrectly suggests that people do not have dementia 66% of the time. This means that an IQCODE test result indicating 'no dementia' has a chance of being wrong 34% of the time. We also found that IQCODE may be less accurate in specialist memory clinics compared to general hospitals. We think that IQCODE could be useful in the general hospital as a screening tool to identify patients who need further assessment for possible dementia, but it cannot be used alone. It is also important that researchers and clinicians report IQCODE results more clearly, including whether they were unsure about a person's cognitive decline.

This review report is current to September 2016. We found three studies, with 91 participants, which investigated whether any form of multidisciplinary rehabilitation could improve arm function, muscle tone or arm movement in people who had a stroke and lived in the community. In all three studies, the intervention was a therapy programme aimed at improving the person's ability to use their arm. No study examined a therapy programme that focused on reducing carer burden or the person's desire to have an easier arm to care for. While one study showed some improvement in arm movement and function, due to the poor quality of the evidence, we cannot be certain if multidisciplinary rehabilitation improves function, muscle tone and arm movement in people who have had a stroke and live in the community. More research is needed.

This review found four small randomised clinical trials which did not provide enough information for meta-analysis. The four arms tested three different regimens: lamivudine alone, human immunoglobulin alone, lamivudine plus human immunoglobulin, lamivudine plus adefovir or no intervention. Due to the small number of patients and the lack of power to detect differences between the groups, this review cannot recommend any particular treatment. Future trials should focus on testing the effects of long-term combination therapy with the new antiviral drugs on the outcome of graft loss, mortality, morbidity, quality of life and cost-effectiveness.

The authors searched the literature up to July 2014 and found 15 articles from 10 different studies. These involved 1019 participants who received a wide variety of treatments (oral agents, insulin, Chinese herbal medicines, vitamin D) for a period ranging from three months to 10 years. Comparing studies was difficult as there was a great deal of variation in the studies and in their selection criteria. There is some evidence that sulfonylureas lead to earlier insulin dependence, and that insulin maintains better glycaemic control than sulfonylurea. Vitamin D with insulin showed steady fasting C-peptide levels. One study using a novel treatment, glutamic acid decarboxylase (GAD65) formulated with aluminium hydroxide, showed improvement in fasting and stimulated C-peptide levels, which were maintained at five years. There is no significant evidence for or against the use of any particular intervention in LADA. Limitations of the review include the small number of studies available and the small number of participants in these studies, and the fact that most of the studies were open label, which may have biased the results. The reporting of the studies was also poor, with incomplete data and different ways of reporting outcomes.

We identified 70 randomised controlled trials (around 45,000 students) that evaluated the effects of social norms interventions. The studies could be classified into one of five groups based on how the intervention was delivered: (1) web-based self-completion questionnaire (WF); (2) interactive feedback format (IFF); (3) group format (GFF); (4) multimedia (MC); or (5) multiple delivery modes (MF). The results of the review show that social norms interventions do not have a large impact on alcohol misuse. Some significant effects were found, but these were small and inconsistent across outcome measures. Furthermore, the interventions did not produce significant effects in the longer term. The quality of the evidence was rated as low or moderate. In general, it is difficult to obtain high-quality evidence in this area, as it is challenging to design studies that can accurately measure alcohol misuse and intervene effectively. Future research should focus on developing and evaluating more effective interventions.

This review includes evidence identified up to 20 September 2016. We found three relevant studies with 492 participants who had undergone a total of 530 THAs. Each participant gave their consent to be part of the trial. The first study (81 participants) compared the provision of hip precautions, equipment and functional restrictions versus no provision of these. It showed no clear difference in function or quality of life between the two groups at 12 months. There were no hip dislocations or adverse events in either group. The second study (265 participants) compared the provision of hip precautions with or without the prescription of postoperative equipment and restrictions to functional activities. It showed no clear difference in the rate of recovery between the two groups. Only one person out of 152 people given equipment and restrictions had a hip dislocation compared to none out of 151 people who were not given equipment or restrictions. The third study (146 participants) compared an enhanced postoperative education and rehabilitation service versus a conventional rehabilitation intervention in the community. It showed no clear difference in functional ability between the two groups at six months. The studies did not report on pain, health-related quality of life, re-operation rates or total adverse events. The quality of the evidence was very low, making it difficult to draw any conclusions. We are uncertain if the provision of hip precautions, equipment and functional restrictions improve function and quality of life after THA, or if an enhanced postoperative education and rehabilitation service improves functional ability compared to a conventional rehabilitation intervention. There is insufficient evidence to support or refute the adoption of a postoperative community rehabilitation programme consisting of functional reintegration and education compared to conventional rehabilitation strategies. Based on the very low quality of the current evidence, we are unable to determine which occupational therapy interventions are the most beneficial for people who have had a THA. Future research should aim to provide better quality evidence on the effectiveness of various occupational therapy interventions for people who have had a THA. These interventions should focus on improving pain-free activity, and independence in personal and instrumental activities of daily living, with a particular emphasis on functional integration-type interventions rather than just hip precautions, equipment and restrictions.

The review identified three small randomised controlled trials. Two trials compared treatment with an intrauterine device (IUD) releasing the progestagen hormone levonorgestrel (LNG) with 'watchful waiting' and one trial compared LNG-IUD with gonadotrophin-releasing hormone agonist. The results showed that the LNG-IUD reduced the risk of having further period pain by about 78%. The LNG-IUD also increased the number of women who reported a change in their menstrual bleeding, although it did not make them stop completely. It did not cause more side effects or make women abandon treatment earlier than other treatments. This review found that an LNG-IUD may be an effective way to treat painful periods caused by endometriosis, however the only two studies were small and had some limitations so more research is needed to confirm these findings.

We searched for relevant studies in January 2019, and found 24 studies that tested whether changing the environment around food affects how much people select and consume unhealthy foods/drinks. All of the included studies investigated food, but we also developed an extension protocol for studies investigating alcohol and tobacco. None of the studies investigated alcohol or tobacco. The majority of studies were of reasonable quality, although some had some methodological limitations that could have biased their results. The studies generally investigated the effects of changing the number of available food options or the distance at which foods were placed, but there were some differences between studies in relation to what foods were investigated and who was studied. The foods most often investigated were snacks and fast food. The majority of studies were conducted in laboratories, but some were in schools, workplaces, or hospitals. The participants were usually adults, but one study only included children. The studies were conducted in high-income countries, mostly in the USA. We combined the results from studies that investigated the same outcome, using the same measure, to obtain our overall findings. There were six studies looking at the effect of changing the number of available options. We found that people who were given fewer choices chose and consumed significantly less of the targeted foods. We combined the results from 15 studies looking at the effect of changing the distance at which foods were placed, and found that people who were offered foods that were placed farther away from them chose and consumed significantly less of the targeted foods. Furthermore, the farther away the food was placed, the greater the reduction in the amount of that food chosen or consumed. This evidence suggests that changing the number and positioning of foods could help people to eat more healthily. However, our analysis also showed that the certainty of this evidence was either low or very low, meaning that we cannot be sure how large an impact these interventions have on people's eating habits. Further studies are needed in real-world settings, examining a wide range of foods - including alcohol and tobacco - and over longer time periods.

No randomized controlled trials were identified, so a single prospective case-control study informed this review. This found that HIV transmission was associated with deeper injuries, presence of blood on the needle, needles being placed in the source patient's blood vessel, and the source patient having a terminal illness. However, there was no difference between cases and controls in the rate at which people were offered post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) with zidovudine, but cases were less likely to take the medication they were offered. No studies evaluated the effect of two or more antiretroviral drugs for occupational PEP. Adverse effects of PEP were higher in those who took a three-drug regimen, especially one containing indinavir, but the rates of dropping out of this treatment were not significantly different. In conclusion, the use of PEP is based on limited direct evidence of effect. However, because it is highly unlikely that a definitive placebo-controlled trial will ever be conducted, on the basis of results from a single case-control study, a four-week regimen of PEP should be initiated as soon as possible after a known exposure, depending on the risk of becoming infected. We recommend that future studies include a detailed assessment of adverse events, especially in the developing world. Also, due to the success of combination therapies in treating HIV-infected individuals, a combination of antiretroviral drugs should be used for PEP. Healthcare workers should be counseled about the expected adverse events and strategies for managing these, as well as the fact that PEP is not 100% effective in preventing HIV infection.

This review examined the effectiveness of psychological therapies for people with chronic pain. We included 13 trials, with a total of 1,008 patients. The trials compared various forms of psychological therapy with either other psychological therapies, standard medical care, waiting list control or treatment as usual. Most trials were of poor quality and lacked sufficient information about the specific details of the psychological therapy being delivered. Overall, we found that Cognitive Behaviour Therapy (CBT) was the most popular form of psychological therapy used in the trials. The results showed that CBT produced a small reduction in pain and disability and a large reduction in catastrophic thinking and depression when compared with treatment as usual or waiting list. However, these benefits were mostly seen straight after the end of treatment and not six months later. Behaviour therapy produced a small reduction in disability and a large reduction in catastrophic thinking immediately after the end of treatment when compared with an active control. Neither therapy produced any change in pain. We conclude that CBT is a useful approach to the management of chronic pain, but more research is needed to find out which parts of CBT are essential for which patients.

We found 15 initiatives that met our inclusion criteria, including more than 260,000 people. All but one initiative took place in higher-middle- or high-income countries. Most initiatives involved several different types of intervention, including both individual and environmental (i.e. structural) interventions. Examples of environmental interventions include guidelines for health care workers to provide dietary advice to patients, and policies that require food manufacturers to reduce the amount of salt in their products. Examples of individual interventions include helping people to eat less salt by providing them with low-salt products or cookbooks. Only 10 initiatives provided enough information to calculate changes in average salt intake from before the initiative started to after. Across these 10 initiatives, the average amount of salt reduced varied between 0.35 g per day less (Ireland) to 1.15 g per day less (Finland). In two countries (Canada and Switzerland) the average amount of salt increased from pre-intervention to post-intervention, but in both cases the pre-intervention data point was from several years before the start of the initiative. Seven of the 10 initiatives that we looked at were multi-component initiatives, meaning they included more than one type of intervention. Of those seven, four showed a significant decrease in salt intake from pre-intervention to post-intervention and one showed a significant increase in salt intake (Switzerland). Nine initiatives gave us information on how much salt was reduced by men and women separately. For women, three initiatives (China, Finland, France) showed a significant decrease, four (Austria, Netherlands, Switzerland, United Kingdom) showed no significant change and two (Canada, United States) showed a significant increase in salt intake from pre-intervention to post-intervention. For men, five initiatives (Austria, China, Finland, France, United Kingdom) showed a significant decrease, three (Netherlands, Switzerland, United States) showed no significant change and one (Canada) showed a significant increase in salt intake from pre-intervention to post-intervention. The initiatives we studied did not report any harmful effects. Due to problems with the way the studies were designed, we are uncertain about the overall effectiveness of these initiatives. However, we do know that multi-component initiatives that include activities of an environmental (structural) nature have the potential to be effective, especially for men.

This review evaluated the effects of drug treatments for sleep bruxism. We included seven randomised controlled trials (RCTs), all with a cross-over design, which means that each participant received different treatments in different periods of the study. The drugs tested were: amitriptyline (three studies), bromocriptine (one study), clonidine (one study), propranolol (one study), levodopa (one study) and L-tryptophan (one study). The comparative medications were placebo (a pretend treatment) or no treatment. The studies had a small number of participants, ranging from seven to 16 people. There were three studies in which the treatment group and the placebo group appeared similar at the beginning of the study in terms of demographics and bruxism severity. Four studies did not clearly report this information. The amitriptyline studies showed no clear differences between the treatment and placebo groups in terms of bruxism severity, sleep quality or pain. One study reported that clonidine reduced the number of REM sleep stages and increased the second stage of sleep. The other studies did not show a clear difference between the treatment and placebo groups in terms of sleep quality. Propranolol reduced the number of bruxism episodes in one study, but the other studies did not clearly show a reduction in bruxism severity. The same was observed for bromocriptine and levodopa, which also improved some measures of bruxism severity. However, the small number of studies and the small number of participants in these studies made it impossible to determine whether these drugs were effective. Only two studies reported adverse effects: amitriptyline caused symptoms such as drowsiness, difficulty awakening in the morning, insomnia and dry mouth; and propranolol caused mild-to-severe dry mouth. Clonidine was associated with prolonged morning hypotension (low blood pressure) in three of 16 participants. Bromocriptine and levodopa did not cause any side effects during the treatment period. More studies are needed to evaluate the effects of drug treatments for sleep bruxism. Future studies should use parallel designs, in which each person receives only one treatment, and should have sufficient numbers of participants and follow-up time to allow us to draw solid conclusions about the effects of treatments. A consensus is needed on how to measure the severity of sleep bruxism and other aspects of the condition.

The evidence is current to September 2019. We found 10 randomized controlled trials with 1015 participants. These studies were often small, short term, and had weaknesses in their design, making their results less reliable. The studies were not similar in terms of types of immunonutrition and other treatments used, length of study, and outcomes measured. We found no clear evidence that immunonutrition with omega-3 fatty acids and antioxidants reduced death from any cause. We are uncertain whether this treatment improved time spent on a ventilator and in the intensive care unit, or the level of oxygen in the blood. We are also uncertain whether this treatment increases adverse events, including heart problems, gut problems, and overall adverse events. The reliability of the evidence ranged from low to very low. The quality of the evidence was limited by small numbers of studies and participants, and by poor study designs. More studies are needed before we can determine whether immunonutrition with omega-3 fatty acids and antioxidants are beneficial or harmful for adults with ARDS.

We reviewed the literature to see whether one way of asking about AEs was better than another. We found 33 studies that met our inclusion criteria. These studies were quite different, and it was difficult to compare them. There were problems with the design of some studies, and the ways in which AEs were defined and recorded. We found that when researchers asked more specific questions about AEs, then more AEs were reported. It did not seem to make a difference what type of question was used first, although it was not clear what types of AEs were missed by a general question. Sometimes serious AEs were only detected by a detailed interview, but sometimes all AEs were equally likely to be detected by any method. We do not know if using one method instead of another affects the number of AEs that are reported, but it could affect how many AEs are detected overall. This could make it harder to pool AE data from different trials.

The evidence is current to September 2017. We searched scientific databases for clinical trials and other relevant studies. We also contacted researchers and looked through the titles and abstracts of potentially relevant papers to ensure all relevant studies were included. We found 582 records from the databases and added 10 more after looking through books and other resources. We removed 211 duplicate records and screened 381 records. We then read the summaries of 17 papers in detail and assessed them for inclusion in the review. We excluded 15 studies: eight studies did not assess interventions to prevent sudden death; five studies measured how sensitive devices are at detecting seizures but did not directly measure sudden death; and two studies assessed risk factors for sudden death but not interventions for preventing sudden death. One study is still waiting for assessment. We included one study where the authors compared people with epilepsy who had died suddenly with those who had not. They found that having someone present during the night could reduce the risk of dying suddenly. The quality of the evidence was very low, meaning we cannot be certain about these findings. Other potential treatments to prevent sudden death include seizure detection devices, safety pillows, antidepressants, early surgical evaluation, educational programmes, and drugs to block pain and adrenaline. We do not know if any of these treatments are effective in preventing sudden death because there were no trials. More research is needed to find out which treatments might help prevent sudden death in people with epilepsy.

The review included seven studies with 208 participants aged between seven and 63 years of age. The studies lasted from four days to two years. Six studies involved people who were clinically stable at the start of the trial and one study involved individuals who were hospitalised due to a chest infection. The studies compared autogenic drainage to another type of airway clearance technique. No studies compared autogenic drainage to exercise. The review did not find any difference between autogenic drainage and any other type of airway clearance technique. This includes the technique known as postural drainage where the individual is positioned in a specific way to allow the mucus to drain out of the lung. In one study autogenic drainage was the preferred technique used by the participants, but this was not a direct comparison with exercise. One study reported on side effects and described a decrease in oxygen levels during an active cycle of breathing techniques, but not with autogenic drainage. The review found that while autogenic drainage is widely taught to people with cystic fibrosis, there is little evidence to support its use over other techniques. More research is needed to compare autogenic drainage with other types of airway clearance techniques. We do not know how well autogenic drainage works in people with mild disease, as the studies included in the review primarily enrolled those with more severe disease. The studies were of mixed design and had a small number of participants. The studies were not designed to show that one treatment was better than another, they were simply designed to compare one treatment to another. The methods used in the studies were not as well reported as we would like and it was not clear how many patients dropped out of the studies. We cannot be sure how representative these findings are of the wider cystic fibrosis population.

This review aimed to determine the efficacy of psychological therapies for generalised anxiety disorder (GAD). Twenty five randomised controlled trials (RCTs) were included, with 13 contributing to the main analysis. The review found that psychological therapy based on cognitive-behavioural principles is effective in reducing anxiety symptoms from short-term treatment of GAD. However, there were no studies examining the long-term effectiveness of this approach. There were too few studies of other types of psychological therapy to draw conclusions about their effectiveness.

Cochrane authors searched for clinical trials which used progesterone or a similar medication (progestogen) in women with unexplained recurrent pregnancy loss. We found 12 completed randomised controlled trials, with a total of 1856 women who were diagnosed with unexplained recurrent miscarriage. The trials were from India, Jordan, UK and USA. Women were allocated randomly to receive a progestogen by mouth (through a tablet), by injection or through a vaginal pessary (a device placed in the vagina) or to a control group (who received either no treatment, placebo (pretend tablets) or an oil. The current evidence is up-to-date as of January 2018. Most of the trials were well designed but one trial was at a high risk of bias because it was possible that the clinicians knew which intervention the women were receiving. One trial did not use a control group and so this trial could not be included in the meta-analysis but the trial itself reported no difference in miscarriage rates. The review of 10 trials, involving 1684 women, found that progestogen supplementation may reduce the chance of miscarriage. However, the result is not conclusive. The odds of having a miscarriage were reduced by, on average, 27% with progestogen supplementation. The odds of having a live birth were increased by, on average, 7%. Progestogen did not have a clear effect on the chances of a preterm birth, stillbirth or the number of babies that died within the first 28 days of life. This review does not provide enough information to conclude whether progestogen is effective in the management of women with unexplained recurrent miscarriage to prevent another miscarriage. Further studies are needed.

We searched for randomised controlled trials (where participants are allocated at random to either a treatment or a control arm) looking at the effectiveness of psychosocial interventions in treating substance-using pregnant women. We found 14 studies with 1298 participants. Nine of these studies (with 704 participants) compared counseling plus medication versus control, and five studies (with 594 participants) compared behavioral interventions versus control. We did not find any studies that assessed other types of psychosocial interventions. The quality of the included studies was low to moderate. For the most part, it was unclear if the included studies adequately controlled for potential biases in the conduct of the studies. The included studies rarely captured mother and newborn outcomes. For studies that did measure such outcomes, no difference was observed in pre-term birth rates, maternal toxicity at delivery, or low birth weight. However, the results showed that newborns remained in hospital for fewer days after delivery in counseling plus medication intervention groups. At the end of the studies, there was no difference in the number of women who completed treatment or remained abstinent (as measured by a positive drug test at the end of treatment) between any psychosocial intervention group and the control group. These results apply to both counseling plus medication and behavioral interventions. The review concluded that there is no difference in treatment outcomes to address drug use in pregnant women with the use of psychosocial interventions, when taken in the presence of other comprehensive care options. However, few studies evaluated childbirth or newborn outcomes, and rarely did so in a systematic way, making it difficult to assess the effect of psychosocial interventions on these important outcomes. It is important to develop a better evidence base to evaluate psychosocial interventions for the treatment of substance use during pregnancy.

The aim of this review was to compare the benefits and risks of the radial versus femoral approach in patients undergoing coronary angiography or angioplasty (or both) with potential use of coronary stents. We searched the literature until 15 May 2017. This review included 31 randomised controlled trials involving 27,071 participants. The risk of bias in the studies was low or unclear for several important aspects. For the radial approach compared to the femoral approach, we found fewer combined adverse events (e.g. myocardial infarction, stroke, bleeding), cardiac death, all-cause mortality, bleeding, and access site complications. The difference in efficacy (i.e. successful cannulation) was negligible. The quality of the evidence was low to high across the outcomes, with the evidence being moderate for most of the combined adverse events and high for cardiac death and all-cause mortality.

In July 2016 we searched for clinical trials of palliative care interventions for people with advanced dementia. We found two randomised controlled trials that met our inclusion criteria. One trial, from the USA, allocated 99 people with dementia who were admitted to hospital to either a palliative care team or standard care, and followed them for the duration of their hospital stay. The other, from Canada, allocated 256 family carers of people with dementia to one of two groups. One group were given an intervention to assist with making decisions about tube feeding, while the other group received usual care. Both studies were small and both were at some risk of bias; for example, it would have been impossible for the researchers to keep the treatment allocation of some participants hidden from those assessing the outcomes. Because the two studies were so different, we were unable to combine the results for any outcome. One study showed that people with advanced dementia who were allocated to the palliative care team were more likely to have a palliative care plan created for them while they were in hospital. However, this plan was put in place for only two people, and neither of them were able to make decisions using the plan as their wishes may have changed since the plan was written. The other study showed that family carers who used a decision aid to make decisions about feeding were less uncertain about their decision than carers in the control group, but were more likely to have discussed the decision with doctors and nurses. However, there was not enough information to be sure that this finding applies to people with advanced dementia rather than all people with dementia. Overall, there was not enough evidence to show whether or not palliative care interventions are effective for people with advanced dementia, but we are hoping that the six ongoing studies will provide some answers to this question in the future updates of this review.

This review found that there are few studies that compare the effectiveness of these two treatments in terms of reducing the chance of the breast cancer coming back. This review found only three randomised controlled trials that were able to answer this question, and they suggested that the two ways of giving the treatments are equally effective. However, it should be noted that these results are not yet conclusive as the number of women in the trials is relatively small. The review also found that the trials provide some information on side effects. It appears that when radiotherapy is given first, more women experience low white blood cell counts and infection than when chemotherapy is given first. Radiotherapy given first also causes more skin reactions and changes in skin pigmentation than chemotherapy given first. Women treated with chemotherapy and radiotherapy at the same time did not report worse side effects than those who had the treatments sequentially, although doctors reported worse cosmetic outcomes in those who had the therapies concurrently.

We included nine randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) with 622 participants. The trials were conducted in the community setting, with interventions mainly delivered by health professionals, and had a short- to medium-term follow-up (up to 24 weeks). Three of the included studies compared CrP plus exercise with placebo plus exercise, the other studies compared CrP with placebo. The daily doses of CrP investigated were 200 µg, 400 µg, 500 µg and 1000 µg. Compared with placebo, all doses combined resulted in a small decrease in body weight, body mass index and percentage of body fat, but the size of the difference was small and may not be relevant for the patient. We found no clear differences between the different doses of CrP. Three studies reported on side effects: two participants experienced side effects (one serious) during treatment with 1000 µg CrP, and one participant experienced a serious side effect during treatment with 400 mg CrP. Two participants discontinued placebo treatment because of side effects (one event was serious). No study reported on death, morbidity, health-related quality of life or socioeconomic effects. The quality of the evidence was low, meaning that we have limited confidence in the results. The main reasons for downgrading the quality were imprecision (small number of participants and events), publication bias (we suspect that studies with negative results were not published) and industry sponsorship (two studies were sponsored by the supplement industry).

The review authors searched the medical literature up to March 2015 for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) looking at the use of antiseptics, antibiotics or anti-bacterial products in the management of SWHSI. We included 11 studies with a total of 886 participants in this review. The studies presented results for a variety of different products making it difficult to make direct comparisons between them. Most studies were small and two were described as having possible conflicts of interest in their design or implementation. Overall, there was little clear evidence on the most effective way to manage SWHSI but there was some evidence that dressings impregnated with silver, such as Dermacyn®, may increase the rate of healing in foot ulcers in people with diabetes and that sucralfate cream may increase the rate of healing of wounds after surgery to treat piles. There was also some evidence to suggest that triclosan may reduce the time to heal wounds after surgery. However, both of these results come from small studies so more research is needed to confirm these findings. There was no clear evidence that any particular antiseptic or antibiotic preparation was better than no antiseptic or antibiotic. It should be noted that the quality of the evidence for all these findings was either low or moderate, meaning that we can not be sure of these results and that future research may well change our conclusions.

This review of five randomised controlled trials found no clear evidence that any specific technique during amniocentesis or chorionic villus sampling is better than another. When a woman's amniotic fluid is sampled, it can be done through her abdomen (amniocentesis) or through her vagina (percutaneous umbilical blood sampling). If the sample taken contains too few cells, the test has to be repeated. This can cause stress for the mother and baby, and there is a small risk of miscarriage after each test. There are several ways to improve the chances of getting a good sample but the review of trials found only limited evidence on the best way to do this.

We searched for articles from different databases for relevant studies. We found 10 studies that met our inclusion criteria: four studies provided data for analysis, five studies involved randomised controlled trials, three studies involved non-randomised controlled group comparisons, and two studies used a before-and-after design to examine changes over time. Six studies took place in upper-middle-income countries (China, Mexico, South Africa), one study was conducted in a lower-middle-income country (Bangladesh), and three studies were conducted in a high-income country (Canada). The quality of included studies was generally unclear. The evidence is current to January 2018. Folic acid fortification of wheat flour may reduce the risk of neural tube defects; however, this result comes from just one study. Folic acid fortification of either wheat or maize flour may increase blood concentrations of folic acid. It is uncertain whether folic acid fortification of wheat or maize flour affects haemoglobin levels or risk of anaemia. We do not know whether folic acid fortification of wheat or maize flour has any effect on other health problems measured by this review because the studies did not report on these outcomes. None of the included studies reported on side effects. Overall, the certainty of evidence was low/very low. This was due to limitations in the way studies were designed, small numbers of studies and participants, and poor reporting of study methods and results. Future research should aim to provide more certain evidence on the effects of folic acid fortification of wheat or maize flour.

The review found two good quality studies including 1,124,483 newborn babies, of whom 210 were diagnosed with cystic fibrosis. One study included all babies born in 13 major obstetric hospitals in Victoria, Australia, over a 14 month period, and the other was conducted in the UK. The results showed that those who were screened were healthier and had better nutritional levels than those who were not screened. The health of both groups was monitored over time. At the age of seven, 88% of those who were screened and 75% of those who were not had healthy lungs. However, the lung health of those who were screened declined more rapidly over time than those who were not screened. This means that although initial benefits of screening may be seen, the two groups would be expected to have similar lung health at older ages. We recommend further research to see if there are ways to improve the benefits of screening.

We found five randomized trials, recruiting a total of 7314 participants and with a mean follow-up of 4.5 years. One trial, ACCORD, compared outcomes associated with 'lower' (< 120 mmHg) or'standard' (< 140 mmHg) systolic blood pressure targets in 4734 participants. Despite achieving a significantly lower blood pressure (119.3/64.4 mmHg vs 133.5/70.5 mmHg), and using more antihypertensive medications, the only significant benefit in the group assigned to 'lower' systolic blood pressure (SBP) was a reduction in the incidence of stroke: risk ratio (RR) 0.58, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.39 to 0.88, P = 0.009, absolute risk reduction 1.1%. The effect of SBP targets on mortality was compatible with both a reduction and increase in risk: RR 1.05 CI 0.84 to 1.30, low quality evidence. Trying to achieve the 'lower' SBP target was associated with a significant increase in the number of other serious adverse events: RR 2.58, 95% CI 1.70 to 3.91, P < 0.00001, absolute risk increase 2.0%. Four trials (ABCD-H, ABCD-N, ABCD-2V, and a subgroup of HOT) compared clinical outcomes associated with 'lower' versus'standard' targets for diastolic blood pressure (DBP) in people with diabetes. The total number of participants included in the DBP target analysis was 2580. Participants assigned to 'lower' DBP had a significantly lower achieved BP: 128/76 mmHg vs 135/83 mmHg. There was a trend towards reduction in total mortality in the group assigned to the 'lower' DBP target (RR 0.73, 95% CI 0.53 to 1.01), mainly due to a trend to lower non-cardiovascular mortality.There was no difference in stroke (RR 0.67, 95% CI 0.42 to 1.05), in myocardial infarction (RR 0.95, 95% CI 0.64 to 1.40) or in congestive heart failure (RR 1.06, 95% CI 0.58 to 1.92), low quality evidence. End-stage renal failure and total serious adverse events were not reported in any of the trials. A sensitivity analysis of trials comparing DBP targets < 80 mmHg (as suggested in clinical guidelines) versus < 90 mmHg showed similar results. We conclude that current evidence does not support blood pressure targets lower than the standard targets in people with elevated blood pressure and diabetes. More randomized controlled trials are needed, with future trials reporting total mortality, total serious adverse events as well as cardiovascular and renal events.

This review of 14 studies (709 participants) found that walking casts are more effective at healing foot ulcers than dressings alone, but not more effective than other types of dressings. When an Achilles tendon lengthening operation is performed, walking casts are more effective at preventing ulcers from recurring than if no casting is used after surgery. The review also found evidence that non-removable casts are more effective at healing foot ulcers than removable casts.

We searched the literature in April 2015 for all randomised controlled trials (RCTs) of non-drug interventions aimed at the prevention or reduction of cognitive impairment in adults diagnosed with cancer which had been published up until March 2015. Five studies, with a total of 235 participants, were included. These five studies comprised two each of cognitive training (n = 100) and compensatory strategy training (n = 95) interventions, one meditation intervention (n = 47) and one physical activity intervention (n = 19). All five of the studies involved women who had undergone treatment for breast cancer. Our main measure of interest was cognitive function. The evidence is current to April 2015. The data for our primary outcome of interest, cognitive function, could not be combined across studies. This was due to differences in the way that each study measured this outcome. However, each individual study did demonstrate improvement in aspects of cognition following the delivery of the intervention. Additionally, each study demonstrated improvements in at least one aspect of the participants' quality of life. The group-based cognitive training intervention improved cognitive function, as assessed by both objective testing and self-report, while the compensatory strategy training intervention improved cognitive function, as assessed by objective testing and self report, and also improved spiritual well-being. Meditation and physical activity each improved aspects of cognitive function, although the consistency between studies was not sufficient to conclude a overall positive effect. Compensatory strategy training also improved self-reported cognitive function. However, the group-based cognitive training intervention was only effective in improving self-reported cognitive function. The lack of benefit of compensatory strategy training on physical well-being is noted. The relatively small number of studies eligible for inclusion in this review limits the conclusions that can be drawn. Each of the five studies was small in terms of the number of participants and each was at significant risk of bias. The quality of the evidence was low, therefore the findings should be interpreted with caution.

The Information Specialist of Cochrane Schizophrenia ran an electronic search for clinical trials comparing chlorpromazine or piperacetazine versus other antipsychotics in August 2015. We found and checked 144 potential articles. Only five trials met our inclusion criteria. These included 343 participants who received either chlorpromazine or piperacetazine. The trials were old and the methods used were not as good as today’s standards. There was only short term information available. Based on the results of the included trials we found no clear difference between chlorpromazine and piperacetazine in improving the overall symptomatology of schizophrenia. One trial reported on changes in mental state and again no clear difference was found. Chlorpromazine and piperacetazine did cause similar rates of side effects, with about 60% of participants experiencing some side effect and 40% experiencing a movement disorder. The quality of the evidence was very low, meaning that we cannot be sure whether chlorpromazine is better or worse than piperacetazine. Further research with newer antipsychotics and larger number of participants is required.

We included five randomised controlled trails with a total of 207 participants with malignant large bowel (colon) blockage. One hundred and two participants were treated with stent and 105 participants were treated with emergency surgery. The stent placement was successful in 86% of attempts. The average time for relief of symptoms was around half a day in the stent group compared to three and a half days in the surgery group. There was no significant difference in the number of deaths within 30 days after the treatment in both groups. The death rate at 30 days was just under 3% in both groups. The stent caused a hole through the wall of the bowel in 6% of cases. The stent had to be repositioned or replaced because it had moved out of place in 2% of cases and clogged in 2% of cases. There was no significant difference in overall complication rate in both groups. The complication rate was 39% in the stent group and 46% in the surgery group. The average length of hospital stay was 11 days in the stent group compared to 17 days in the surgery group. The average procedure time was just under two hours in the stent group compared to one hour and 44 minutes in the surgery group. The amount of blood lost during the procedure was minimal in both groups, with a median of 50 ml in the stent group and 350 ml in the surgery group. This review shows that using a stent or surgery to relieve bowel blockage in people with advanced cancer is equally safe with no difference in the number of deaths or complications. Stent placement is a relatively simple procedure to perform and does not require a large incision through the abdomen. It also results in a shorter hospital stay, less blood loss and a shorter operation duration. However, more research is needed to confirm the findings of this review.

The review identified nine randomised controlled trials (RCTs), conducted between 1980 and 2010, including 1867 women. Seven trials involved human albumin, two involved HES and one involved mannitol. The sample size of the included studies varied from 24 to 150 women. In three of the studies, all women received gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist and down-regulation was achieved with GnRH-antagonist in the other six. The dose of human albumin used varied between 10 mg/kg/min over 30 minutes to 50 g given as a single bolus. The dose of HES used was similar, ranging from 6 mg/kg/min over 10 minutes to 33 g given as a single bolus. The dose of mannitol used was 1 g/kg body weight every six hours as needed. The overall quality of the evidence was very low to moderate. There was evidence that all three fluids reduced rates of moderate and severe OHSS. However, there was also evidence that human albumin may have a detrimental effect on pregnancy rates. There was no evidence that either HES or mannitol had any influence on pregnancy rates, however the number of women in the studies was small. There were no live births reported in any of the studies. Adverse events appeared to be uncommon, but were too poorly reported to reach any firm conclusions. There were no deaths. The evidence for the use of human albumin, HES or mannitol for prevention of OHSS was based on a small number of RCTs, which need to be confirmed in additional larger RCTs before they should be considered for routine use in clinical practice.

The review authors identified 10 randomised controlled trials, which involved 484 patients and six different types of sclerosing agent. The trials showed that any active agent was better than normal saline, but they provided little evidence of a difference between the various types of sclerosing agent. Patient satisfaction appeared to be similar for the different agents used. Adverse events such as an allergic reaction, thromboembolism, pain, itching and necrosis were reported, but it was difficult to relate them to a specific agent. The trial reports were poorly written, and lacked information about other important factors such as the size and composition of the telangiectasiae, the timing and technique of injection, and the duration of follow-up. More research is needed to find the most effective agent and technique to treat telangiectasias.

No studies were found that examined the effect of intravenous calcium salts or potassium binding resins on the level of serum potassium. This review found that all of the drugs tested decreased the level of potassium in the blood. However, it must be noted that none of the studies looked at whether the reduction of potassium was associated with an improvement in heart function. Furthermore, safety data regarding the use of these medications were incomplete. The authors conclude that while some medications, such as intravenous dextrose and salbutamol, may effectively reduce the level of potassium, more research is needed to determine which drug is the most effective.

The authors searched the literature up to April 2017. We identified 39 randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) comparing antimicrobial locks with standard locking solutions in people receiving haemodialysis. These studies involved 4216 participants, but information suitable for analysing was available from 3392 participants. Most studies had low or unclear risks of bias (which means there are few grounds for questioning the results). We found that antimicrobial locks containing antibiotics, non-antibiotics, or the combination of both, significantly reduced the number of CRIs compared to the standard locking solution (heparin). However, there was no significant reduction in the number of cases of blood clots around the catheter with any type of antimicrobial lock solution. We found low and very low quality evidence that antimicrobial locks containing antibiotics and the combination of both antibiotics and non-antibiotics reduced CRIs. We also found that non-antibiotic antimicrobial locks reduced CRIs in patients with tunnelled CVC, but not in patients with non-tunnelled CVC. Therefore, we conclude that there is limited evidence to support the use of an antimicrobial lock solution in haemodialysis catheters to prevent CRIs. More research is needed to confirm whether using antimicrobial locks is safe and effective.

Searches to January 2015 identified 15 randomised controlled trials (1098 participants). Six investigated pre-emptive treatment versus treatment of CMV when the virus was detected in the blood. Eight looked at pre-emptive treatment versus antiviral prophylaxis and one compared two different routes of administration of pre-emptive treatment. The methods used to allocate participants to different treatments were at some risk of bias in all studies. It was unclear whether the entire outcome set was complete for all participants in any studies. Seven studies were at low risk of selective reporting, although one study was at high risk of selective reporting because it was funded by a pharmaceutical company. Only one study reported that both participants and personnel were blinded to which treatment they received. None reported blinding of the person assessing outcomes. Pre-emptive treatment significantly reduced the risk of CMV disease compared with placebo or standard care but did not affect the risks of acute rejection or death. Studies comparing pre-emptive therapy versus prophylaxis had inconsistent results and did not show a difference in the risk of CMV disease. Leucopenia (a decrease in the number of white blood cells) was significantly less common with pre-emptive therapy compared with prophylaxis. Other adverse effects did not differ significantly or were not reported. There were no significant differences in the risks of all-cause mortality, graft loss, acute rejection or other infections. In summary, few studies have evaluated the effects of pre-emptive therapy to prevent CMV disease. Pre-emptive therapy is more effective than placebo or standard care. More studies are required to determine how pre-emptive therapy compares with prophylaxis to prevent CMV disease in solid organ transplant recipients.

We included three randomised controlled trials which investigated a total of 140 participants and lasted from six weeks to five months. All three studies were conducted by the same investigator. The trials compared the effect of sweet potato preparations with placebo on glycaemic control in people with type 2 diabetes mellitus. There was a statistically significant improvement in glycosylated haemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) at three to five months with 4 g/day sweet potato preparation compared to placebo. No serious adverse effects were observed. The authors did not report any information on diabetic complications and morbidity, death from any cause, health-related quality of life, well-being, functional outcomes and costs. Currently, there is insufficient evidence about the use of sweet potato for type 2 diabetes mellitus. Further studies are needed to assess the benefits and harms of sweet potato for type 2 diabetes mellitus.

The review included 19 studies with a total of 440 adults and children who had CF. The studies compared the active cycle of breathing technique with another type of airways clearance treatment, or the active cycle of breathing technique combined with another treatment. The other treatments were autogenic drainage, airway oscillating devices, high frequency chest compression devices, conventional chest physiotherapy, and positive expiratory pressure. More people preferred the active cycle of breathing technique over autogenic drainage, over airway oscillating devices, and were more comfortable with the active cycle of breathing technique than with high frequency chest compression. However, when looking at clinical outcomes, we did not find any difference between the active cycle of breathing technique and any of the other techniques. This means that we cannot say whether the active cycle of breathing technique is better or worse than any of the other techniques, and that each has its own role in the management of individuals with CF. We recommend that longer-term studies are carried out to see if the active cycle of breathing technique improves quality of life and preference in the longer term. We also recommend that more information is published on how the studies were designed so that we can be sure that they were well conducted.

The evidence is current to July 2015. We included eight randomised controlled trials with 733 women in total. These trials compared the use of brief co-incubation with the standard insemination protocol. Ongoing pregnancy, clinical pregnancy and miscarriage rates were investigated. The evidence from this review suggests that brief co-incubation of sperm and oocytes may be better than the standard insemination protocol for improving the chances of ongoing and clinical pregnancy in infertile women undergoing IVF. However, as the number of live births was not reported, we cannot make any conclusions about the effect on live birth rate. Furthermore, the evidence about the safety of brief co-incubation is inconclusive. The overall quality of the evidence is low. More randomised controlled trials are needed to confirm these results.

The authors of this Cochrane review questioned whether the long versus short inspiratory time is more effective at providing ventilation for newborn babies with lung problems. The evidence from five studies showed that long inspiratory times can cause low airway pressure during expiration, resulting in air leaking through the vocal cords. This can lead to lung injury and death. Therefore, short inspiratory times are preferable, especially for babies with poor lung compliance. It is worth noting that the five studies reviewed were carried out before the introduction of antenatal steroids, postnatal surfactant and the use of synchronized modes of ventilation, which are now standard treatments for many babies with breathing difficulties.

The review authors searched the literature up to January 2012 and analysed 33 studies with 5110 patients. The main findings of the review are that TFU has been applied to many different patient groups, in many different ways. The review authors found no clear evidence that TFU improves functional recovery or health status compared to usual care. Adverse effects were rare. The review authors were unable to draw firm conclusions about the effects of TFU on health-related quality of life, pain, or length of hospital stay. The majority of the studies were of low methodological quality, which limits the strength of our conclusions.

We searched for all randomised controlled trials that evaluated the effects of any strategy to improve recruitment and retention in studies measuring patient outcomes in free-living patients. We found 38 randomised controlled trials, which evaluated six broad types of strategies to improve recruitment and retention. These were: (1) incentives, (2) communication strategies, (3) new questionnaire formats, (4) participant case management, (5) behavioural interventions and (6) methodological interventions. Most of the trials we found were of low methodological quality and reported retention as the number of people returning questionnaires. A small number of trials reported loss to follow-up. In about half of the trials there were problems retaining participants and dropout was greater at later time points. Addition of a monetary incentive to the cover letter of a postal questionnaire increased the number of returned questionnaires. However, it is not known what size of incentive is most cost-effective. An offer of a £20 shopping voucher also increased the number of returned postal questionnaires. When an electronic questionnaire was added to a postal questionnaire the number of returned questionnaires increased. However, it is not clear how much of an increase. A personal letter signed by a senior researcher increased the number of returned questionnaires. Recorded delivery of questionnaires seemed to be more effective than telephone reminders and a 'package' of postal communication strategies with reminder letters appeared to be better than standard procedures used by the study team. It may also be more effective to make sure that participants know that their responses will be kept confidential. Each of these findings is based on the results of a single trial. Therefore, they need to be treated with caution. There was no evidence that any particular type of questionnaire was better than any other for return by postal questionnaire. However, this was also based on the results from a single trial. In only one trial was the effect of the use of a non-monetary incentive evaluated and the results were inconclusive. A trial in which participants knew the treatment they were receiving seemed to be better than one where they did not know. However, this was also based on a single trial. There was insufficient information to compare the effects of different types of questionnaire on the number of people returning to trial sites for follow-up.

The evidence is current to September 2018. We found eight randomized controlled trials that compared these two devices in adults. A total of 829 participants were studied. Six studies were still underway when we updated our search. These studies will be incorporated into future updates of this review. The studies that we included were well designed; however, a large proportion reported no events in either study arms, making it difficult to draw conclusions. No studies showed that one device was better than the other in terms of ability to provide adequate ventilation. The ProSeal laryngeal mask airway took longer to place than the Classic laryngeal mask airway, but the difference (10 seconds) is so small that this would not be meaningful to patients. The Classic laryngeal mask airway requires slightly more pressure to deliver oxygen. The ProSeal laryngeal mask airway allows less air to leak back up into the stomach. We rated the quality of the evidence as low, particularly because many of the studies had design problems and a small number of events were reported. We do not know whether one device is better than the other for providing adequate ventilation and preventing complications during general anaesthesia. The ProSeal laryngeal mask airway may make a better seal than the Classic laryngeal mask airway. The Classic laryngeal mask airway may be quicker to insert, but this does not matter.
