The researchers searched for randomised controlled trials (where participants are assigned to treatment groups by chance) of dietary interventions for the management of abdominal pain in children with functional gastrointestinal disorders. We assessed the inclusion of participants, the kind of intervention being studied, and the duration of the intervention. We looked at the reporting of pain, as well as broader impacts on children's health and wellbeing. The search is current to January 2018. We found 19 studies which met our inclusion criteria. These studies included a total of 1453 children aged between four and 18 years. Most studies recruited large numbers of girls and boys, although the gender split varied between studies. Fourteen out of 19 studies recruited children with a variety of functional gut disorders, including irritable bowel type symptoms; five studies only recruited children with irritable bowel syndrome. Studies tested the effects of giving children probiotics (13 studies), extra fibre (four studies), low FODMAP diets (one study), or fructose restriction (one study). Probiotics are live bacteria that help to digest food and keep the gut healthy; they are often taken as a supplement in yoghurt or a drink. Some studies suggested that probiotics might improve pain, but it was difficult to tell whether this was just chance rather than a genuine effect. This is because there were differences between the studies, and some studies did not report enough information about how they were conducted. However, based on the available evidence, we could say that one child in eight who takes probiotics will experience improvement in pain, while one child in five will experience improvement if they take a placebo. Children with irritable bowel syndrome who took probiotics were more likely to experience improvement in pain than those given a placebo. We found that fibre supplements did not improve pain, and that other types of dietary change are not yet supported by sufficient evidence. It is important to note that all studies assessing probiotics were short term; we do not know whether the benefits of probiotics are sustained over time. Also, we do not know whether some strains of probiotics are better than others, or whether one probiotic is better than another. More research is needed to answer these questions. We rated the quality of the evidence as moderate to low. This means that future studies have the potential to change our estimates of the size of the effect, and we cannot be very confident in our findings.

The review authors searched for randomised controlled trials (where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) of mass media interventions designed to reduce mental illness stigma and/or promote mental health. We found 22 studies involving 4490 participants. Three of these studies were cluster-randomised (the whole school or workplace was allocated to the intervention group). In the others, people were allocated at random to the intervention or control group. Most of the studies were conducted in English-speaking countries, using a variety of different types of mass media, including film, video, interactive websites and mobile phone texts. Almost all of the studies involved the general public, four involved employers, three involved health professionals and two involved both the public and health professionals. One study each involved both employers and health professionals. Fourteen of the studies were funded by government or non-government organisations and eight by academic or research institutions. Funding sources were unclear for four studies. Findings from five studies with discrimination outcomes were mixed, with effects showing a reduction, increase or consistent with no evidence of effect. For the three studies with continuous outcomes, the average effect was a small reduction in stigmatised attitudes. The two studies with dichotomous discrimination outcomes (reporting the number of people who discriminated against people with mental illness) showed no evidence of an intervention effect. The 19 studies with prejudice outcomes had average effects showing a moderate reduction in stigmatised attitudes. The studies varied considerably in terms of the type of mass media used, the duration and intensity of the intervention and the timing of assessments. There was little information about the impact of mass media interventions on other outcomes, such as mental health service engagement or self-esteem. None of the studies reported any harmful effects of the interventions. Limited information was available to assess value for money. Overall, the quality of evidence was low or very low, meaning we cannot be certain about the findings. The lack of evidence about the effects of mass media interventions on discrimination makes it difficult to draw conclusions about their overall effectiveness. However, the evidence suggests that mass media interventions may reduce prejudice against people with mental illness. More research is needed to understand which types of mass media intervention are most effective, particularly those that reach specific groups, such as employers or children. Future research should be of higher quality, have larger sample sizes, and measure more outcomes. It should also report data more consistently using recognised reporting guidelines.

We found 85 studies that met the review criteria, with 46 exploring the views and experiences of women (40 of these involved women only, four included both women and health-care providers, and two included all three groups: women, health-care providers and policy-makers). Seventeen studies explored the views of health-care providers (two of these included both women and health-care providers) and one study included all three groups. The studies were conducted in 41 countries, in rural, urban and semi-urban settings. The quality of the evidence was assessed as moderate to high. Our analysis led to the development of 52 findings, which we grouped into three themes: 'Socio-cultural context' (five moderate- or high-quality findings), 'Service design and provision' (15 moderate- or high-quality findings) and 'What matters to women and staff' (11 moderate- or high-quality findings). Within the 'What matters to women and staff' theme we further divided our findings into two areas: 'Personalised supportive care' (eight findings) and 'Information and safety' (three findings). From our findings we developed two lines of argument. One line of argument is about what affects women's use of ANC. Women's initial or continued use of ANC depends on a perception that doing so will be a positive experience. This is a result of the provision of good-quality local services that are not dependent on the payment of informal fees and that include continuity of care that is authentically personalised, kind, caring, supportive, culturally sensitive, flexible, and respectful of women’s need for privacy. They must allow staff to take the time needed to provide relevant support, information and clinical safety for the woman and the baby, as and when they need it. Women's perceptions of the value of ANC depend on their general beliefs about pregnancy as a healthy or a risky state, and on their reaction to being pregnant, as well as on local socio-cultural norms relating to the advantages or otherwise of antenatal care for healthy pregnancies, and for those with complications. Whether they continue to use ANC or not depends on their experience of ANC design and provision when they access it for the first time. Another line of argument is about what affects the provision of good-quality ANC. Good-quality ANC depends on the availability of sufficient resources and staffing to provide flexible, personalised, private appointments that are not overloaded with organisational tasks. Such provision also depends on organisational norms and values that overtly value kind, caring staff who make effective links with local communities, who respect women’s belief that pregnancy is usually a normal life event, but who can recognise and respond to complications when they arise. Staff also need sufficient training and education to do their job well, as well as an adequate salary, so that they do not need to demand extra informal funds from women and families, to supplement their income, or to fund essential supplies. Our review has identified key factors that affect women's use of ANC services and the quality of the ANC they receive. These factors relate to the socio-cultural context in which women live, how ANC services are designed and provided, and what matters to women and their staff. Our findings are useful to anyone who provides or commissions ANC services, including policy-makers, to improve the quality of ANC services and increase the number of women who choose to attend.

This review found that several animal studies have shown that calcium antagonists can reduce the risk of brain damage after SAH. This review looked at the evidence from 16 controlled trials which involved 3361 people. The review found that calcium antagonists could also reduce the risk of a poor outcome after SAH. However, the review found that there is relatively little evidence about the effects of other calcium antagonists. There is moderate evidence that nimodipine improves the outcome after SAH, but the evidence is not conclusive. Calcium antagonists may cause side-effects, such as ankle oedema and nausea and vomiting, but these are generally short-lived. It is therefore concluded that calcium antagonists should be used routinely in patients with SAH, but more research is needed to find out how effective they are and to settle outstanding questions about adverse effects.

In this review we found three randomised studies which compared T&M versus conventional chemotherapy (with or without radiotherapy) in 739 patients. The results of the meta-analyses suggest that the number of events (including relapses and deaths) and the number of deaths are lower in the T&M group than in the group of patients who did not receive T&M. However, the number of deaths was not lower than in the conventional chemotherapy group. Moreover, the number of deaths was even slightly lower in the conventional chemotherapy group than in the T&M group after additional follow-up data were included. This means that at the moment there is no evidence that T&M improves overall survival. Adverse effects were reported differently across the studies, but overall it seems that T&M has more adverse effects than conventional chemotherapy. No information on quality of life was reported. Limitations of the studies include the fact that the definition of high-risk patients has changed since the studies were performed and that therefore the results may not be relevant for the current practice. Furthermore, in two of the studies the way the randomisation was performed has been considered inadequate, although it is likely that this inadequacy had no major impact on the results. Finally, the relatively small number of patients included in the analysis leads to uncertainty about the results.

We searched the major databases until 17 July 2019. We found eight randomised clinical trials (632 participants), with an average age ranging from 16 years to 78 years. The percentage of men ranged from 60% to 75%, and the percentage of patients with advanced liver cancer ranged from 22% to 85%. The average follow-up time ranged from two months to six years. The primary outcomes were all-cause mortality (death from any cause) and tumour response (reduction in size of the tumour). Secondary outcomes were health-related quality of life, serious adverse events, and non-serious adverse events. TACE followed by 3-DCRT may decrease the number of patients who die from any cause within three years, and may increase the number of patients whose tumour shrinks or disappears. However, TACE followed by 3-DCRT may cause an increase in the level of bilirubin in the blood, which can lead to jaundice. One study reported that the quality of life was better in the TACE followed by 3-DCRT group than in the TAE group, but the information provided was not clear. No trials reported on serious adverse events. Based on the observed increases in bilirubin levels, we would expect that one additional patient will experience this adverse event. However, we are very unsure about this estimate because of the weak study designs and small numbers of patients. The certainty of evidence was low to very low. This means that the true effect of TACE followed by 3-DCRT may be substantially different from what this review shows. More studies with low-risk of bias and large numbers of patients are needed to assess the role of TACE followed by 3-DCRT for advanced liver cancer.

To find out whether giving more energy than usual is beneficial or harmful to babies born preterm with chronic lung disease we reviewed all the published and unpublished work that could be found worldwide. We searched for any randomised controlled trial that compared extra energy given by milk feeds or intravenous nutrition with the usual amount of energy given. We were interested in the effect on survival, breathing problems, growth, side effects and quality of life. We found none of the studies that we were looking for. However, two studies provided some information. One study showed that preterm babies with chronic lung disease who were fed formula enriched with protein and minerals had better growth up until three months of age. Another study compared a high-energy-density formula with a low-energy-density formula but gave them the same amount of energy. Both groups were not able to reach the intended total daily energy intake and there were no differences in growth, respiratory problems, swelling or diuretics between the groups. This review shows that there is no evidence available from randomised controlled trials to answer our question. Future trials should aim to provide answers to this question and include important outcomes such as survival, breathing difficulties, growth and development. It would be useful to know which methods of increasing energy intake are best, i.e. by increasing the energy content of milk feeds, by increasing the volume of milk feeds, by giving additional intravenous nutrition and whether different sources of energy (i.e. different amounts of protein, carbohydrates and fats) are equally effective and safe.

We included 13 randomized controlled trials (involving 2341 participants and 2360 procedures) in this review. Nine studies involved the subclavian vein (in the area of the first rib, just above the collarbone); four involved the femoral vein (in the groin area). For the subclavian approach, the quality of evidence was high for two outcomes (one measure of success and one measure of complication), moderate for another two (both measures of complication) and low for the remaining three outcomes (all measures of success). For the femoral approach, the quality of evidence was low for all outcomes except one (which was moderate). The main problems that we looked at were whether the wrong vessel was hit (artery instead of vein) and whether there were complications such as a collection of blood outside the vessel (haematoma). We also looked at the number of attempts needed to succeed, the overall success rate (percentage of successful insertions), the number of first-time successes (those inserted on the first attempt) and the time taken to insert the catheter. For the subclavian vein approach, the risk of hitting the artery instead of the vein was lower and the risk of haematoma formation was also lower with ultrasound guidance than with an anatomical landmark technique. No clear differences were seen for other complications, number of attempts required, overall success rate, first-time success rate or time taken to insert the catheter. For the femoral vein approach, no clear differences were seen in risks of hitting the artery instead of the vein and of haematoma formation. However, a higher success rate on the first attempt (98% vs 90%) and a slightly higher overall success rate (99% vs 97%) were reported with ultrasound guidance than with an anatomical landmark technique. No data on mortality or patient-reported outcomes were available. We concluded that ultrasound guidance offers small benefits in terms of safety (fewer arterial punctures and haematomas) and quality (higher success rate on the first attempt, overall) when compared with an anatomical landmark technique for inserting a central line via the subclavian or femoral vein. More research is needed to determine whether ultrasound guidance is beneficial when used by less experienced users or in patients with certain anatomical characteristics (e.g. large veins, multiple venous access points). The results for Doppler ultrasound techniques versus anatomical landmark techniques are still uncertain.

This review assessed the efficacy and safety of physostigmine for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease. Fifteen randomized controlled trials were found. These used four different methods of administering physostigmine: by intravenous infusion, an oral conventional form, an oral controlled release form or as a skin patch. Intravenous infusion and oral forms of physostigmine have been withdrawn from the market and so are not available to use. The results from the trials of the skin patches showed that the drug was no better than placebo for the symptoms of Alzheimer’s but it caused more side effects. The controlled release formulation had no effect on memory but did cause side effects. These findings are based upon relatively small numbers of patients.

Evidence from this updated review support the use of fibrates for the secondary prevention of coronary heart disease, especially in those at high risk, but with some caveats. This review included 13 trials involving 16,112 participants who had a stroke or heart attack in the past, or had a high risk of developing them. The people taking part were randomly assigned to take either a fibrate or a placebo (or usual treatment). The results showed that fibrates reduced the combined risk of having another heart attack or stroke or dying from a blood vessel disease by 12%. They also reduced the risk of having a heart attack by 14%. However, fibrates did not reduce the risk of death due to any cause or due to a blood vessel disease or stroke. When we divided the studies according to whether they used clofibrate or other fibrates, the benefit of fibrates was mostly due to the inclusion of clofibrate trials. Clofibrate is not used anymore in clinical practice because of its harmful effects. The other fibrates (fenofibrate and gemfibrozil) are still used and have fewer side effects. These results are based on moderate quality evidence. More research is needed to find out if fibrates are still effective in people who have already had a stroke. It would also be useful to know how fibrates interact with statins, which are now the first choice treatment for preventing another heart attack or stroke in people at high risk.

We included 12 randomised controlled trials involving 3285 women at low risk of excessive bleeding after childbirth. The women received oxytocin and/or ergometrine (both are drugs that cause the uterus to contract thereby reducing bleeding) according to their local guidelines and were randomly assigned to receive transabdominal massage or to no massage. The overall quality of the included studies was acceptable, although the numbers of women in the included studies were small. The chance of experiencing a blood loss of more than one litre was decreased by 57% in women who received abdominal massage; the chance of losing more than 500 mL or 400 mL of blood was decreased by 60% and 51%, respectively. The mean blood loss from the time of birth until two hours afterwards was decreased by 78 mL in women who received the massage. The number of women requiring additional medication to stop the bleeding (including infusion of fluids and blood products) was also decreased in the group of women who received the massage. On the other hand, the chances of experiencing side effects such as nausea, vomiting and dizziness were increased by 10 times, 13 times and three times, respectively. The chances of experiencing more serious complications (including death, infection, problems with the functioning of the kidneys or heart, and formation of blood clots) could not be determined in this review. We did not find any differences in the effect of the massage whether the woman gave birth normally or by caesarian section. However, the number of women in the larger studies was small. This means that we cannot be absolutely certain of this finding. Abdominal massage does decrease blood loss and stops bleeding in women at low risk of excessive bleeding who give birth normally or have a caesarian section. It does, however, increase the chances of experiencing some mild side effects. We are not certain of its effects on the chances of experiencing very serious side effects including death, because the information on this outcome was not clearly reported in most studies. Future trials should report this information carefully. Future research should also look at the usefulness of the massage in women at high risk of excessive bleeding (for example, those with high blood pressure, multiple pregnancy, if the baby is large, if the placenta has covered part of the cervix during pregnancy etc).

The evidence is current to February 2014. We included 12 studies with 2494 participants (1586 children aged one month to 18 years and 908 elderly people aged 65 years or older) comparing amantadine or rimantadine with a placebo (a pretend treatment) or paracetamol (one study with 69 children) or zanamivir (two studies with 545 elderly people) to treat influenza A. Amantadine was slightly effective in preventing influenza A in children, but there were only three studies with small numbers of participants. Rimantadine had a very small effect in reducing the number of days with a raised temperature in children on day three of treatment. Rimantadine had no effect on preventing influenza A in the elderly. There were no serious adverse events caused by treatment with amantadine or rimantadine. There were no studies assessing amantadine in the elderly. The quality of the evidence for prevention and treatment in children and elderly people was low or very low because the studies were poorly conducted, had few participants and used different doses of amantadine or rimantadine. Therefore, we cannot recommend amantadine or rimantadine as a useful medicine for preventing or treating influenza A in children or the elderly.

We found nine randomised controlled trials, including 1512 women, that investigated the effects of endometrial injury in women with unexplained infertility. The evidence is current to April 2017. Intentional endometrial injury versus no intervention or a 'placebo' procedure There was no evidence that endometrial injury increases the chance of a live birth/ongoing pregnancy, however the quality of the evidence for this outcome was very low. The average pain experienced by participants was 6 out of 10 on a zero-10 scale but only one study reported this outcome. Intentional endometrial injury may increase the chance of a clinical pregnancy. The quality of this evidence was low. Intentional endometrial injury versus a lower degree of endometrial injury There was no evidence that a more aggressive endometrial injury technique resulted in a greater chance of a live birth/ongoing pregnancy or clinical pregnancy. The study that provided data for these outcomes also did not report the average pain experienced by participants. Intentional endometrial injury in the cycle before IUI versus intentional endometrial injury in the IUI cycle There was no evidence that performing the endometrial injury in the cycle before the IUI increased the chances of a live birth/ongoing pregnancy or clinical pregnancy. Neither of the studies that provided data for these outcomes reported the average pain experienced by participants. Adverse events There was no evidence to suggest that endometrial injury increases the chance of miscarriage, ectopic pregnancy or multiple pregnancy. None of the studies reported bleeding secondary to the procedure. Quality of the evidence The quality of the evidence was either low or very low. As such, the conclusions of this review should be interpreted with caution. The main reasons we downgraded the quality of the evidence were because most included studies were at a high risk of bias, the wide range of results seen between the included studies and small numbers of participants in each comparison. Future studies that are larger and of better methodological quality may change our conclusions.

The evidence is current to January 2018. We included four studies with a total of 3905 participants. All four studies compared professional oral care versus usual oral care. One study with 834 participants did not show any reduction in infections or deaths between the two groups and was therefore not included in the analyses. The remaining three studies, with 2871 participants, suggested that professional oral care may reduce the risk of pneumonia-associated death and may have little or no effect on the risk of having a first episode of pneumonia or the overall risk of death. However, the quality of these findings was low or very low. One study reported on adverse events. It identified 64 non-serious events, including problems with teeth and mouth, and staining. The quality of the evidence was low or very low. Therefore, we cannot be certain about the true effects of professional oral care for preventing nursing home-acquired pneumonia. Further research is needed to find out which type of oral care is most effective.

The researchers identified five randomised controlled trials (RCTs) with a total of 149 participants. The studies compared bismuth subsalicylate versus placebo, budesonide versus placebo, mesalazine versus mesalazine plus cholestyramine and beclometasone dipropionate versus mesalazine. The study which assessed mesalazine versus mesalazine plus cholestyramine and the study which assessed beclometasone dipropionate versus mesalazine were judged to be of poor methodological quality due to lack of blinding. The study which compared bismuth subsalicylate versus placebo was judged to be of low quality due to a very small number of patients and limited data. The other three studies were judged to be of high quality. Budesonide (9 mg/day for 6 to 8 weeks) resulted in clinical improvement and healing of mucosal ulcers in most patients. Clinical response was noted in 88% of budesonide patients compared to 38% of placebo patients. Histological response was noted in 78% of budesonide patients compared to 33% of placebo patients. Mesalazine (2.4 g/day) led to clinical improvement in 85% of patients. There were no differences in clinical response between patients who received mesalazine together with cholestyramine (4 g/day) and those who received mesalazine alone. Five patients were enrolled in the very small study that evaluated bismuth subsalicylate (nine 262 mg tablets daily for 8 weeks) compared with placebo. There were no differences in clinical and histological responses in these patients. Forty-six patients were enrolled in the study that compared beclometasone dipropionate (5 mg/day or 10 mg/day) versus mesalazine (2.4 g/day). There were no differences in clinical remission at 8 weeks and 12 months of treatment. Patients receiving beclometasone dipropionate (84%) and mesalazine (86%) achieved clinical remission at 8 weeks but this was not maintained at 12 months (26% and 20%, respectively). Side effects reported in the budesonide studies included nausea, vomiting, neck pain, abdominal pain, excessive sweating and headache. Nausea and skin rash were reported as side effects in the mesalazine study. Sleepiness and change of mood were noted with beclometasone dipropionate. No side effects were reported with bismuth subsalicylate. The researchers concluded that budesonide may be an effective treatment for active lymphocytic colitis. This result needs to be confirmed by a larger study. Also, the results from the two mesalazine studies suggest that mesalazine may be beneficial for the treatment of lymphocytic colitis, however these results need to be confirmed by large studies. No conclusion could be made from the very small study of bismuth subsalicylate. More studies evaluating interventions for the treatment of lymphocytic colitis are needed.

We reviewed the literature on self monitoring of blood glucose in people with type 2 diabetes which lasted longer than six months. We found 12 randomised controlled trials with a total of 3259 randomised patients. Patients monitored their blood glucose levels between six months up to two years. Self monitoring of blood glucose was compared with 'usual care' (no self monitoring), self monitoring of urine glucose, another form of self monitoring or a more or less intensive self monitoring regimen. Self monitoring of blood glucose has a small beneficial effect on glycaemic control after six months, but not after a year. There was no evidence that self monitoring of blood glucose affected patient satisfaction, general well-being or general health-related quality of life. Data on adverse events were sparse and long term complications were not reported.

We found 11 randomised controlled trials, which involved 471 individuals with antisocial personality disorder, although information was available from only five of these studies, which involved 276 individuals. Most of the interventions used with individuals who misuse substances were also helpful in reducing substance misuse or improving social function in those with antisocial personality disorder. However, many important outcomes were not reported, including reconvictions and aggressive behaviour. There is an urgent need for more studies that look specifically at the effectiveness of psychological interventions for individuals with antisocial personality disorder.

The evidence is current to April 2016. This review found six studies with a total of 1047 participants testing several different antibiotics against placebo for the common cold. Five studies tested several different antibiotics versus placebo in people with acute purulent rhinitis. One additional study could not be included in the meta-analysis because it presented only summary statistics. Most of the studies were of reasonable quality but one was very poorly conducted and had a high drop-out rate. Another study had a very small number of participants. All of the studies took place in just two countries, the US and Japan, so the results may not apply to other countries. The studies were of short duration (just three days) and only 12 studies out of 1000 participants reported taking the entire course of medication. Antibiotics do not help to cure a nose infection or reduce the severity of its symptoms. Furthermore, they are associated with side effects, especially in adults. People should not take antibiotics if they have a runny nose, sore throat or cough.

This review examined whether antistreptococcal treatments are effective and safe for treating guttate and chronic plaque psoriasis in children and young adults. We searched for clinical trials up to 1 April 2018. We found five randomised controlled trials, with 162 participants. However, only three trials assessed our outcomes of interest. One study compared penicillin to no treatment in children with guttate psoriasis; another compared azithromycin to vitamin C in children with chronic plaque psoriasis; and the third compared tonsillectomy to no treatment in children with chronic plaque psoriasis. The other two studies assessed the effects of erythromycin versus no treatment and rifampicin versus a placebo, but they did not measure our outcomes. Overall, we found no evidence for the effectiveness of antistreptococcal treatments in improving psoriasis in children and young adults. There was no information on side effects. The quality of the evidence was very low, because the included trials were small, and the way they were designed and reported meant that their results may be biased. Moreover, it is possible that the true effect of antistreptococcal treatments is quite different from what this review shows. Further studies, in particular on penicillin V or amoxicillin, are needed to assess the effects of antistreptococcal treatments before considering them as a routine treatment option for guttate and chronic plaque psoriasis in children and young adults.

Cochrane researchers searched for all randomised controlled trials (where participants are allocated at random to either a treatment or a control arm) comparing vitamin D against any other treatment or no treatment in adults with multiple sclerosis. They looked for evidence on recurrences, brain lesions, disability, relapses, hospitalisations, quality of life, cognition (memory and thinking), and harmful effects. They found 12 studies including a total of 933 participants. Most studies used vitamin D₃ (11 studies), and one study used vitamin D₂. The average duration of the included studies was one year, and the dose of vitamin D ranged from 2000 IU/day to 50,000 IU/week. Vitamin D had no effect on the number of participants who experienced a relapse, on worsening of disability, or on brain lesions as shown by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Three studies assessed quality of life, and results were inconsistent. Two studies found no effect of vitamin D on fatigue, and one study showed improvement of fatigue. The seven included studies that measured immune cells as a surrogate marker of the effects of vitamin D showed no clear changes, although some measurements were affected by small numbers of participants. No study provided information on time to first treated relapse, number of participants who were hospitalised because of their MS, proportion of participants who remained relapse-free, cognitive function, or psychological symptoms. Vitamin D appeared safe at the doses and treatment durations used in the included studies. The overall quality of the evidence was very low because of small numbers of participants, poor methodological quality of most included studies, and lack of blinding of both participants and researchers. The evidence is current to February 2016.

In this updated review we set out to assess the effectiveness and tolerability of oral morphine for cancer pain using the same methods as the original review. We searched the Cochrane Pain Consortium's Cancer Pain database in January 2015. We include 62 studies with 4241 participants. Oral morphine provided good pain relief for many patients, although a small proportion could not tolerate the side effects. Our findings confirm that morphine is an effective analgesic for cancer pain. Studies showed that morphine was effective in reducing pain, but were relatively small, old, and were largely carried out for registration purposes. Therefore they were only designed to show that one drug was similar to another and not designed to show whether or not the drugs were effective. The studies assessed in this review were of variable quality, with a high risk of bias. The evidence for the effectiveness of oral morphine for cancer pain is therefore poor.

We searched for all studies that compared different treatments for trigger digit (trigger finger) on 19 November 2015. We found 14 randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) with 1260 participants (1361 trigger fingers) that assessed the effects of open surgery, percutaneous surgery (through the skin), endoscopic surgery (using a camera to visualise the inside of the finger) and steroid injection (where steroid medication is injected into the affected tendon) in adults with trigger finger. The age of participants included in the studies ranged from 16 to 88 years. The majority of participants were women (approximately 70%). The average duration of symptoms ranged from three to 15 months. The follow-up after the procedure ranged from eight weeks to 23 months. This review compared open surgery versus steroid injection, because open surgery is the oldest and the most widely used treatment method and steroid injection is the least invasive treatment method. Open surgery reduced the likelihood of the condition coming back (recurrence) from six to 12 months, but caused more pain in the first week after the procedure, when compared with steroid injection. However, we are uncertain about the effect of open surgery on the proportion of people who had no more symptoms (resolution rate) from six to 12 months, as results were variable and based on a small number of participants. We are also uncertain whether open surgery increase the risk of complications (infection, tendon injury, flare (inflammation), cutaneous discomfort and fat necrosis (death of body fat)) and nerve and blood vessel injury. Studies did not report any information on hand function and participant satisfaction. Overall, the quality of the evidence was low or very low. Limitations included problems in the way the studies were designed, differences between the studies, and the small numbers of participants.

In this updated review, we have identified three randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that compared the use of RT alone versus RT and chemotherapy (combined modality therapy) in 931 adults with anaplastic oligodendroglioma (AO) or anaplastic oligoastrocytoma (AOA). Two of the RCTs used the chemotherapeutic agents procarbazine, lomustine and vincristine (PCV), and the third used temozolomide. Only one of the RCTs, however, demonstrated a significant improvement in overall survival (OS) for participants receiving combined therapy with PCV and RT, as compared with RT alone. Furthermore, the addition of PCV to RT significantly increased the rate of severe side effects. Analysis of tumour tissue from the participants enrolled in two of the RCTs identified a group of patients who may benefit more from the addition of PCV to RT. These patients had specific changes in the structure of their tumours, specifically deletion of the short arm of chromosome 1 and the long arm of chromosome 19, with or without a specific mutation in the isocitrate dehydrogenase (IDH) 1 or -2 gene. It is likely that further studies will focus on using these biomarkers to select patients with AO/AOA who may benefit from the addition of chemotherapy to RT, while avoiding the severe side effects experienced by all patients. Overall, the evidence for the use of combined modality therapy, as it relates to improved OS, is not yet clear. However, the information obtained regarding the role of tumour biomarkers has been very valuable, and should be incorporated into future studies.

The evidence in this review is current to August 2019. We found five randomised controlled trials (with a total of 240 children aged one to 18 years) comparing either intranasal steroids or oral montelukast with a placebo (dummy pill) in children with OSA. The children performed the study over several nights in a sleep laboratory and the duration of the intervention lasted from six weeks up to four months. This review shows that there is insufficient evidence for the efficacy of intranasal steroids for the treatment of OSA in children. Intranasal steroids may have short-term beneficial effects on some symptoms, but we are uncertain about the effect on most measures of OSA. Montelukast, on the other hand, has short-term beneficial treatment effects for OSA in otherwise healthy, non-obese, and surgically untreated children by significantly reducing the number of breathing pauses and awakenings during sleep. Montelukast was well tolerated in the children studied. However, the minimum level of change in these measures that would be meaningful for the child's health has not yet been established. Therefore, it is unclear if the observed changes are relevant for the child's overall condition. In addition, we did not find any information on long-term effects of these drugs or effects on quality of life, concentration ability, vigilance, or school performance. Overall, the certainty of the evidence was low to high, depending on the outcome measure. The main limitations of the evidence were the small number of children included in the analysis for each outcome, inconsistent results across studies, and the fact that the children were not blinded to their treatment (i.e. they knew if they received active medication or placebo).

We searched scientific databases for clinical trials and cohort studies in which complete blood clotting disorders were present in children with cancer who had a central venous catheter. We found six studies with 1291 participants. Five studies compared systemic treatment versus no intervention, and one study compared two different types of systemic treatment. Systemic treatment consisted of low-molecular-weight heparin, antithrombin supplementation, warfarin, cryoprecipitate and fresh frozen plasma. Low-molecular-weight heparin and antithrombin were given when there was a complete deficiency of antithrombin. Cryoprecipitate and fresh frozen plasma were given when there was a deficiency of all clotting factors or only of fibrinogen. We found no significant effects of systemic treatment compared with no intervention in preventing both symptomatic and asymptomatic clots. One study showed a positive effect of adding low-molecular-weight heparin to antithrombin supplementation without bleeding complications. Asymptomatic clots, thrombocytopenia, heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, heparin-induced thrombocytopenia with thrombosis, death as a result of clots, removal of central venous catheters because of clots, central venous catheter-related infections and post-thrombotic syndrome were not assessed in the included studies. Bleeding was not more common in any of the groups. Overall, the quality of the included studies was low. Differences were noted between the studies, making it difficult to draw definite conclusions. Our results might be affected by the small number of included participants, which makes the chances of finding a significant result less likely. It might be useful in future studies to focus on children with a higher risk of developing clots or to investigate asymptomatic clots instead.

The review authors identified three randomized controlled trials, with a combined total of 287 participants who had open surgical repair of thoracoabdominal aortic aneurysms. Two trials involved administration of CSFD during surgery to protect the spinal cord. The other trial gave the participant both CSFD and papaverine (a vasodilator) during surgery. The two trials that examined the role of CSFD alone found no benefit in preventing neurological deficit. However, the third trial, which used a combination of CSFD and papaverine, demonstrated a significant reduction in the rate of postoperative neurological deficit. This study, however, was stopped early, before it had recruited the intended number of participants. The review authors concluded that there is limited evidence to support the use of CSFD to prevent paralysis during surgery to correct an aneurysm of the thoracic or thoracoabdominal aorta. More research is needed.

A review of the research on narrowband UVB (NB-UVB) found that it is useful for treating moderate to severe chronic plaque psoriasis in people who cannot tolerate or do not respond to other treatments. It is not clear whether it is better or worse than other types of light therapy. Some studies suggest that NB-UVB may be as good as other forms of UV radiation for treating chronic plaque psoriasis, but more research is needed.

This review examined the effectiveness of pelvic floor muscle training and biofeedback in adults with faecal incontinence. Twenty-one studies were included in the review. There is some evidence that biofeedback and electrical stimulation may improve the results of pelvic floor muscle training. It is uncertain whether there is a difference in outcome between using different types of biofeedback. There is also insufficient evidence about whether there is a difference between biofeedback and pelvic floor muscle training alone. Rectal volume discrimination training appears to be more effective than no training but the quality of the two studies assessing this was poor. There were other problems with the way in which the studies were conducted which means that we cannot be certain about these findings.

This review aimed to find out which type of catheter, made of what material, is best and what the best policy is regarding how long it should be left in. The review found 39 relevant trials but there were too many variables to consider. For example, whether or not to use a catheter at all; the type of catheter (e.g. Foley, fixed vs detachable balloon); catheter material (e.g. latex vs silicone); and how long the catheter was left in. Few firm conclusions could be drawn, mainly due to the poor quality of reports, the small number of people studied in the trials and the large variation in findings. It is a clinical judgement as to whether or not the benefits of using a catheter outweigh the risks.

The review authors searched the Cochrane Schizophrenia Group's trial register in April 2014. We found 10 studies that could be included. All 10 studies randomised participants to receive either aripiprazole or another treatment. Seven studies compared aripiprazole versus a placebo; two studies assessed aripiprazole as an add-on to valproate or lithium versus placebo as an add-on to valproate or lithium; and one small study compared aripiprazale versus haloperidol. Aripiprazole was better than placebo in reducing the symptoms of mania from week three onwards. However, the difference between aripiprazole and placebo was small. Aripiprazole was no better than other drugs in reducing the symptoms of mania. Treatment with aripiprazole was associated with more movement side effects than placebo, leading to more people receiving additional medications to treat their symptoms. For example, more people taking aripiprazole received biperiden. Aripiprazole also caused more nausea and constipation than placebo. It had no effect on weight. The quality of evidence from the studies was generally unclear, which means we cannot be certain about the findings. The high number of people who dropped out of the trials may affect the accuracy of the results. One study provided some information about service user preference and suggested that it may prefer not to take aripiprazole.

In this review we aimed to find out whether giving antibiotics through the CVC is more effective than giving them systemically (into a vein elsewhere) in children with cancer who have a CVC and an infection. We also looked at the complications that may occur, such as lack of function of the CVC, and side effects. We searched scientific databases for clinical trials comparing antibiotic locks with systemic antibiotics, or comparing one type of antibiotic lock with another, in children with cancer and a CVC. We assessed the quality of the trials and combined their results. We found six eligible studies, but only two randomized controlled trials (RCTs) which compared antibiotic locks combined with systemic antibiotics with systemic antibiotics alone, and one controlled before-and-after study (CCT) which compared antibiotic locks combined with systemic antibiotics with systemic antibiotics alone. The other studies were of lower quality and had some methodological problems. The two RCTs showed no differences in effect between the intervention and control groups. The one CCT showed a statistically significant reduction in the number of days until the first negative blood culture in the group treated with antibiotics through the CVC, but not all participants were included in this analysis and the significance of this result is uncertain. No adverse events occurred, but there were some cases of the CVC not functioning properly. There were no RCTs or CCTs evaluating antibiotic locks alone. The small number of studies included in this review and their low methodological quality mean that we cannot draw any firm conclusions from the results. More RCTs are needed to evaluate the use of antibiotic locks in children with cancer and an infected CVC.

This updated review included 15 randomised trials published up to November 2014. The studies included people with acute whiplash, over three months but less than a year after the injury, with or without a diagnosis of whiplash-associated disorders. The studies compared various types of education with a control group that received either no treatment, or treatment as usual, or minimal information. The studies tested whether the education resulted in reduced pain, improved function, or better general satisfaction with their condition. We found only one study that provided good-quality evidence that education to ‘activate’ people with acute whiplash, to help them get back to normal activity, might reduce pain in the short term. However, we found no evidence that any type of educational intervention was effective in the longer term. The studies were too short to measure long-term effects effectively. Overall, the quality of the evidence ranged from low to very low. Further research is needed to find out which type of education might be most helpful.

We searched for studies evaluating interventions to notify people of their possible risk of contracting a fatal brain disease (Creutzfeld-Jakob Disease (CJD) or variant Creutzfeld-Jakob Disease (vCJD)) and found no suitable randomised controlled trials. We found 49 relevant studies and pieces of literature (meeting the same population, intervention and outcome criteria as the Cochrane review, but not of sufficient quality for inclusion in the review) and included these in a thematic synthesis. The synthesis suggests that notification might be more effective if it was part of a longer-term process, and messages and contact were tailored and responsive to individuals' needs. It also highlights potential harms caused by poor communication, such as people reporting they had been stigmatised because of their risk status. Overall, there is not enough evidence to show whether or not notification is effective, or what the most effective approach might be. However, the findings from the included studies can still be used to guide policy and practice decisions for communicating with people at risk of a fatal brain disease.

We found one small trial (N = 304 randomised; 204 completed; 276 analysed) comparing opioids (prolonged release oxycodone/naloxone) with placebo. The mean age of the participants was 51 years and the duration of the trial was 12 weeks. The treatment reduced RLS symptoms and improved quality of life and sleep. More people in the active treatment group felt their symptoms had improved and were better able to function normally. However, the drug was no more effective at improving sleep than placebo. The most common side effects were tiredness, constipation, and abdominal pain. It is not clear from this single study whether opioids are safe for long-term use. The quality of the evidence was low as it was based on a single small trial with many dropouts. Further research is needed to find out if the benefits of opioids are maintained over time and to better assess any risks associated with their use.

This review included 15 trials, which involved 1022 adults with displaced and potentially or definitely unstable distal radial fractures. The methods used by all these trials were flawed; however, this reflects the nature of the condition being studied. External fixation was better at maintaining the position of the bone and preventing deformity than plaster casting. However, the external fixators were associated with a high number of complications, such as pin-track infection. Many of these complications could be avoided by improving the technique of inserting the pins. There was not enough evidence to show that any of the other ways of fixing the fracture were superior to plaster casting.

We found five studies with 1127 participants that compared early stent removal to late stent removal after kidney transplantation. These studies were generally well conducted. Some studies did not clearly define what was meant by 'early' and 'late' stent removal but where this information was clear it was usually between one and two weeks. The majority of the stents used were made of plastic (polyurethane) and were placed inside the ureter from the kidney to the bladder. In some studies the stent was also wrapped around the ureter to keep it in place (antegrade stent). The main findings of the review were that the number of urinary tract infections may be reduced if the stent is removed within one week of surgery, but this only applies to stents made of plastic and placed inside the ureter. There was insufficient data to show whether there was any effect on kidney stones or pain. We did not find any studies that looked at how the different types of stents compared. The review authors recommend further research looking at the best type of stent to use and how long they should be left in.

This review includes five studies evaluating three treatments: filling and capping versus crowning, crowns versus another type of crown and crowns versus no restoration. However, the main question we asked was not answered by any study. Filling and capping versus crowning Four studies compared filling and capping with crowning. Two studies used the Hall Technique for crowning and two used the conventional method. One of these four studies also compared crowns fitted using the Hall Technique with no further treatment. The evidence from two studies showed that crowns are less likely to cause pain and are less likely to fail than fillings after 12 months. The quality of the evidence for these two outcomes was moderate. When the Hall Technique is used, it is likely to cause less discomfort than filling and capping. The quality of this evidence was moderate. There was insufficient evidence to conclude whether or not there was a difference between the two interventions in terms of bleeding of the gums or the number of people experiencing side effects. Crowns versus other crowns One study compared preformed metal crowns with aesthetic crowns (stainless steel with white veneers). The quality of the evidence was very low, meaning that we cannot be sure if one crown is better than the other. Crowns versus no restoration One study compared preformed metal crowns (fitted using the Hall Technique) with no restoration. The quality of the evidence was very low, meaning that we cannot be sure if one treatment is better than the other. There were no studies that compared different ways of fitting preformed metal crowns (Hall Technique versus conventional technique). Future work should aim to answer the main question of this review. Future studies should also evaluate the effect of different materials and techniques on the surrounding tooth and gum tissue, as well as the child's opinion of the appearance and feel of the crown.

The review included 28 studies with 788 adult and paediatric participants. Eighteen of the studies had 296 participants who took part in more than one treatment. Not many studies provided enough information for us to be able to combine their results. We could not do any analysis for most studies, but we were able to analyse the results from six studies in the comparison of PEP versus ACBT. The peristalsis device was used in three of the studies. In these studies, there was little or no difference in the effect of PEP compared with ACBT on lung function. In studies longer than three months, the results were uncertain. In studies longer than one year, there were fewer exacerbations in the PEP group. When analysed together, the studies showed that there was a lower rate of exacerbations in the PEP group compared to other techniques when used with a mask for at least one year. In one study, which used PEP with a mouthpiece, there was no difference in the number of exacerbations. In 10 studies, participant preference was reported after a period of at least one month and in all studies this was in favour of PEP. The review found that there was few studies that reported on adverse events, but those that did report them measured them in sufficient detail to show variability in the type of adverse event reported. For example, in infants with reflux during PEP or whole lung breathing, the severity of the reflux was worse during whole lung breathing. In PEP versus oscillating PEP, some participants felt dizzy when using the flutter device and this improved after further instructions on its proper use. In PEP versus high-frequency chest wall oscillation, there was little or no difference in the number of adverse events, but those in the PEP group had fewer adverse events affecting the lower airways than those in the high-frequency chest wall oscillation group. The quality of the evidence provided by this review varies, but suggests that all the techniques and devices described may have a place in the clinical treatment of people with cystic fibrosis. It is important to note that airway clearance techniques should be individualised throughout life according to developmental stages, patient preferences, pulmonary symptoms and lung function. This also applies as conditions vary between baseline function and pulmonary exacerbations.

The researchers found four studies that included a total of 1485 participants with moderate to severe Crohn's disease. These studies compared CZP against a placebo (a fake medicine). The drug was given by intravenous infusion (through a catheter into a vein) every two to four weeks. Infusions lasted 30 minutes to six hours. The studies showed that CZP is better than placebo for inducing remission and improving symptoms in people with active Crohn's disease. Remission is when all symptoms become minimal. This review shows that 23 in 100 people had remission after eight weeks with CZP and 17 in 100 had remission with placebo. This means that 6 more people had remission with CZP than with placebo. The studies also showed that CZP improves symptoms (decreases disease activity) in 42 out of 100 people and placebo improves symptoms in 32 out of 100 people after eight weeks. This means that 10 more people improved with CZP than with placebo. However, CZP did not make a significant difference in terms of side effects. Side effects were serious in 9 out of 100 people with CZP and 6 out of 100 people with placebo after eight weeks. More research is needed to know how well CZP works in the long term.

We found 112 randomised controlled trials which were eligible for inclusion in the review. Of these, 31 studies (23,762 participants) had data for people with CKD, but only 26 studies (19,612 participants) provided usable data for our review. The risk of bias in all 112 studies was frequently high or unclear. The quality of the evidence was low to very low. Few trials assessed heart failure treatments in people with CKD and few studies provided data for those with CKD, making it difficult to determine whether heart failure treatments are effective and safe in people with CKD. We were unable to determine whether treatment with adenosine A1-receptor antagonists, dopamine, nesiritide, or serelaxin improves outcomes in people with acute heart failure. We were also unable to determine whether treatment with angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers, aldosterone antagonists or vasopressin receptor antagonists reduces the risk of death, as results were imprecise. Treatment with beta-blockers may reduce the risk of death. We were uncertain whether treatment with angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers increases hospitalisations for heart failure, as results were imprecise. Treatment with beta-blockers may decrease hospitalisations for heart failure. Aldosterone antagonists may increase the risk of increased potassium levels. We were uncertain whether treatment with renin inhibitors increases the risk of increased potassium levels. Whether treatment with sinus node inhibitors increases the risk of increased potassium levels was unclear, as we were not able to combine study data. Increased potassium levels were not reported for the CKD subgroup in studies investigating other therapies. We were uncertain whether treatment with angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers, or aldosterone antagonists affects the risk of heart failure getting worse or kidney function getting worse, low blood pressure, or quality of life, due to a lack of data. The effects of anti-arrhythmic agents, digoxin, phosphodiesterase inhibitors, renin inhibitors, sinus node inhibitors, vasodilators, and vasopressin receptor antagonists were very uncertain due to the small number of studies. There is insufficient evidence from randomised controlled trials to guide treatment of people with heart failure and CKD. More research is needed.

This review aimed to assess whether internal or external monitoring is more effective for women receiving oxytocin. Three randomised controlled studies were identified. These involved a total of 1945 women. The studies were of moderate quality. The studies did not find any differences between internal and external monitoring of labour progress in women receiving oxytocin. Both methods were equally likely to result in a normal birth with no adverse effects for mother or baby. Further studies are needed to confirm these findings.

We identified two randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) comparing physical exercise with non-specific control interventions for people with chronic venous insufficiency. The two studies involved a total of 54 people with CVI; however, many of our outcomes of interest were not reported or were reported in single studies only. One study found no difference in the symptoms of CVI while the other found a benefit in the exercise group. One study showed that exercise increased blood flow and heart function when compared with a control group. No difference was found between groups in terms of quality of life, ankle mobility or muscle strength. The included studies were small and both were judged to have a high risk of bias. Therefore, the results should be interpreted cautiously. The overall quality of the evidence was very low. The review authors do not recommend the use of physical exercise over non-exercise until further evidence becomes available. Future research into the effects of physical exercise should take into account the type of exercise, its intensity, how often it is performed and for how long, as well as the characteristics of the people with CVI (e.g. severity of disease).

This review considered only randomised controlled trials (RCTs) of HA or hylan injected into the joint space of patients with symptomatic knee OA. We divided these trials into four groups according to the type of comparison used in the trial: (1) hyaluronan/hylan versus placebo, (2) hyaluronan/hylan versus non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), (3) hyaluronan/hylan versus corticosteroid, (4) other treatments versus hyaluronan/hylan. We found 76 RCTs eligible for inclusion in the review. Generally, the quality of the trials was good although many did not blind the patients to the type of injection they received. This means that the patients knew whether they were getting the HA injection or the control substance and this could have influenced their assessment of outcomes, particularly those that involved a subjective measure (e.g. pain, global assessment). We combined the results of the trials using methods that take account of differences in the design of the studies. Overall, the results support the use of these products in the treatment of knee OA. At 5 to 13 weeks after an injection, patients receiving hyaluronan or hylan injections reported significant improvements in pain and function (by about 30%) compared to the control group. These results were based on a number of high-quality trials of different products so we are reasonably confident about them. We also found that hyaluronan/hylan injections work at least as well as NSAIDs and corticosteroid injections, although these results are based on fewer trials. Finally, we found that some hyaluronans may be more effective than others, but we cannot say exactly which ones because we did not find any trials that directly compared different brands of HA.

We searched for randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and found 10 trials involving 4052 women. Four trials compared misoprostol with placebo (dummy treatment) and six compared different treatments for women with primary PPH. Four trials showed that adding misoprostol to oxytocin does not help reduce bleeding, and may cause more side effects. It may also increase the amount of blood lost by women. Two trials showed that oxytocin is better than misoprostol alone for reducing bleeding, and is less likely to cause side effects. However, when given to women who had already received uterotonic drugs, misoprostol and oxytocin worked equally well. Two trials looked at non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) and tranexamic acid, but there were not enough women studied to make any meaningful conclusions. One trial tested whole body hypothermia but did not report any data for our outcomes. Overall, we conclude that oxytocin is the drug of choice for the treatment of primary PPH and that the addition of misoprostol has no extra benefit. More research is needed to find the best way of treating women whose bleeding has not responded to initial therapy.

The review authors searched the medical literature and found six randomized controlled trials that met their entry criteria. Most of these trials included children born before 34 weeks' gestation. Two studies showed that giving cysteine supplements improved the body's use of protein. In one study in which very low birth weight infants were given N-acetylcysteine with their nutrition, no benefit was shown. Giving cysteine supplements increased the level of cysteine in the blood, but this did not occur when N-acetylcysteine was used. N-acetylcysteine did not improve the body's use of cysteine and had no effect on major complications of preterm birth. These results are based on a small number of infants studied. Further studies are needed to confirm these findings.

Cochrane researchers searched the literature up to July 2016 and included 77 studies with 6287 participants. Forty-one of these studies provided information for this review. The trials lasted between one month and two years. The interventions tested were antioxidants (a type of vitamin), bile acids, and thiazolidinediones (which increase the body's sensitivity to insulin). Antioxidants versus no intervention There was no mortality in either group. None of the participants developed serious complications. There was no evidence of difference in the number of people who developed serious complications between antioxidants and no intervention. None of the trials reported health-related quality of life. Bile acids versus no intervention There was no evidence of difference in mortality, the number of people with serious complications, or the total number of complications between bile acids and no intervention. None of the trials reported health-related quality of life. Thiazolidinediones versus no intervention There was no mortality in either group. None of the participants developed serious complications. There was no evidence of difference in the number of people who developed serious complications between thiazolidinediones and no intervention. None of the trials reported health-related quality of life. Source of funding Twenty-six trials were partially- or fully-funded by pharmaceutical companies that would benefit, based on the results of the trial. Twelve trials did not receive any additional funding or were funded by parties with no vested interest in the results. The source of funding was not provided in 39 trials. The overall quality of the evidence was very low. Further well-designed randomised clinical trials with larger numbers of participants are needed.

We searched many databases up to January 2016 and found 15 studies which assessed the accuracy of cerebrospinal fluid tests for the presence of beta-amyloid protein (ABeta), tau protein (t-tau) or both. There were 1282 people taking part, with 430 developing Alzheimer’s disease and 130 developing other forms of dementia during the study period. The results were not very consistent across the studies. This is likely due to differences in how the studies were carried out, how the results were analysed and the cut-off points used to determine a positive result. It was also unclear what impact these differences had on the overall results. We did not find any studies that looked at the accuracy of cerebrospinal fluid tests for other forms of dementia. Due to this lack of information, we cannot be certain about the value of these tests in clinical practice. It appears that a positive result may be useful in ruling out Alzheimer’s disease, but a negative result does not rule out Alzheimer's disease. The tests do not provide any information about other forms of dementia. It is important that further studies are carried out to improve the situation.

We searched the literature up to October 2017 and analysed three randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that compared total intravenous anaesthesia versus gas-based anaesthesia for 149 participants. One study is still underway. The participants were 50 to 75 years of age and had no contraindications to either type of anaesthesia. The evidence is current to 3 October 2017. No relevant outcomes were reported for respiratory or circulatory complications, cognitive function, length of hospital stay or costs. We found that both anaesthetic techniques resulted in similar levels of pain after surgery and that propofol reduced nausea and vomiting when compared with inhalational anaesthesia. The elevation of intraocular pressure that occurs with carbon dioxide insufflation and steep Trendelenburg position did not differ between the anaesthetic techniques. The quality of the evidence was low, as the included studies were small and provided weak information about their methods. More research is needed to evaluate the benefits and harms of the different anaesthetic techniques in various surgical procedures performed through a cut in the abdomen.

This review includes 14 studies, with more than 1.6 million pregnant women. All studies were observational (a type of study in which the investigators do not assign participants to treatment groups but instead look at the outcomes among people receiving different treatments). Seven of these studies looked at the use of topical corticosteroids versus no use of topical corticosteroids; six looked at different potencies of topical steroids versus no use of topical corticosteroids. Overall, the evidence shows no harmful effects of topical corticosteroids on pregnancy outcomes. Topical corticosteroids are not associated with an increased risk of mode of delivery, congenital abnormalities, preterm delivery, foetal death, or low Apgar scores. However, there may be an association between the maternal use of potent to very potent topical corticosteroids and low birth weight. The possible protective effect of mild to moderate potency topical corticosteroids against foetal death needs to be investigated further. The quality of the evidence varied from low to very low. The main limitations of the evidence were imprecision (variability in the estimates) for the outcome of low birth weight and inconsistency (variation in the effect across studies) for the outcome of foetal death.

We searched the literature in July 2014 and identified four randomized controlled trials that fit our inclusion criteria. These included a total of 611 participants with an average age of 31 years. The included studies were conducted in the USA, Canada, India, and South Africa and had a follow-up period of two months to one year. None of the included studies found any important difference in visual acuity between those who received and those who did not receive corticosteroids. This could be due to too few participants in each study or because corticosteroids have a very small effect. Three of the studies were small, and the largest study was still recruiting participants when the search date was current. Only one study reported the time it took for the cornea to heal, and it showed no difference between the two treatment groups. For adverse events, the three small studies found no important difference between the two treatment groups. The largest study found that more participants in the control group developed an increase in eye pressure than those treated with corticosteroids. One study reported quality of life and found no difference between the two groups. None of the included studies reported cost outcomes. Given the lack of evidence, we cannot recommend the use of corticosteroids as an adjunctive treatment in bacterial keratitis. Future trials should address this question.

This review includes four trials with 450 participants. The mean age of participants ranged from 60 years to 70 years. The proportion of male participants ranged from 51% to 100%. The time from onset of stroke symptoms to start of treatment ranged from less than six hours to 24 hours. Thrombolysis using clots-busting drugs given by injection into a vein was compared with percutaneous vascular intervention in all four trials. No other comparisons were included. The primary outcome was the proportion of participants with minimal or no disability one month after the stroke. Secondary outcomes were the proportion of participants with any type of bleeding inside the brain, the proportion of participants who died, the proportion of participants with recanalization (opening up of the blocked vessel), and health-related quality of life. The main result of interest was the proportion of participants with minimal or no disability one month after the stroke. This review showed no evidence that percutaneous vascular interventions are more effective than intravenous thrombolic treatment with respect to the proportion of participants with minimal or no disability one month after the stroke. A possible reason might be that the number of participants in the included trials was too small. The quality of evidence was low because assessors were aware of the treatment group, but not the clinicians or participants. Furthermore, the wide confidence intervals indicate considerable imprecision. Therefore, further studies with larger numbers of participants are needed.

We searched the medical literature up to July 2016 and identified one randomised trial that met our inclusion criteria. It involved 120 families with children aged 18 months to two years who were randomly assigned to either centre-based day care or care at home. The study was conducted in the UK. This trial provided some limited information on the effects of centre-based day care for children younger than five years of age and their families in high-income countries. Evidence was insufficient to assess the effectiveness of centre-based day care for young children and their families. Further research is needed. In particular, more studies are required that compare centre-based day care with other forms of day care that provide special attention to children from low-income families.

The researchers found 126 reports describing 30 different ways to score the appearance of biopsies from people with ulcerative colitis. Only 11 of these ways of scoring biopsies have been tested in any way. The researchers recommend that more work needs to be done to validate the best way of scoring biopsies in order to measure healing of the gut in people with ulcerative colitis.

We searched the scientific literature until May 2016 and identified three relevant studies. These studies compared the use of either nails or plates in 213 participants, of whom 173 had useable data. The average age of participants in the individual studies ranged from 41 to 44 years. All studies were at risk of bias, which means that their results may not be reliable. We found that the three studies together showed little or no difference between nails and plates in terms of function, re-operations, and complications. However, the quality of the evidence was very low, so we are very uncertain about these findings. One study of 85 participants measured pain using a visual analogue scale and found no difference between the two groups. The review concluded that there was either no or insufficient evidence to support either nailing or plating in the treatment of these fractures. Further research is needed, but it will not be possible to conduct this until the results of two ongoing studies are available. Future research should first seek to identify the most important questions to be answered.

We identified 11 studies involving 38,742 participants: eight studies compared BPLDs versus placebo or no treatment (35,110 participants), and three studies compared different blood pressure targets (3632 participants). The quality of the included studies was variable. One study was funded by a government organisation, four studies were funded by pharmaceutical companies, and six studies did not report their funding source. The combined analysis of eight studies involving 35,110 participants showed that BPLDs reduced the risk of having a recurrent stroke by 19% (moderate-quality evidence), the risk of a major vascular event such as heart attack or stroke by 10% (high-quality evidence), and the risk of dementia by 12% (high-quality evidence). The combined analysis of three studies involving 3632 participants showed uncertain effects of different blood pressure targets on the risk of recurrent stroke or major vascular events. The subgroup analysis of five studies involving 24,577 participants showed that the protective effect of BPLDs against recurrent stroke was more evident in participants treated with an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor (49% reduction in risk) or a diuretic (36% reduction in risk). The subgroup analysis of two studies involving 1676 participants showed similar effects of BPLDs on the risk of dementia across subgroups of age, sex, and baseline systolic blood pressure.

This review of five randomized trials showed that taking beta-sitosterol does improve urinary symptoms and flow but the long-term effects, side-effects and risk of complications have not been determined. Five hundred nineteen men from four randomized, placebo-controlled, double-blind trials, lasting 4 to 26 weeks, were assessed. Three trials used non-glucosidic beta-sitosterols and one utilized a preparation that contained 100% beta-sitosteryl-B-D-glucoside. Beta-sitosterols improved urinary score measures and flow. The trial using 100% beta-sitosteryl-B-D-glucoside also showed improvement in urinary flow measures. Beta-sitosterols did not reduce prostate size.
