Withdrawal rates for men assigned to beta-sitosterol and placebo were 7.8% and 8.0%, respectively.

This review compared long-acting beta-agonists with placebo and other bronchodilators and found that salmeterol caused a small increase in all-cause mortality compared with placebo. The increase in asthma-related deaths was much larger in patients not using inhaled steroids and was not significant in patients taking inhaled steroids. However, we could not exclude an increase in asthma-related mortality in patients taking inhaled steroids. This means that a decision on whether salmeterol is beneficial or harmful in patients taking inhaled steroids cannot be made with current evidence. The safety of salmeterol in children is unknown because there were few children in the studies. Salmeterol did not cause more exacerbations than salbutamol or placebo but this may reflect the fact that it is taken four times a day and salbutamol is taken only as needed. It is possible that taking salmeterol daily leads to more serious side-effects including asthma-related death.

Triiodothyronine (T3) and thyroxine (T4) are natural (endogenous) hormones produced by the thyroid gland. Thyroid hormones play an important role in the normal growth and maturation of the central nervous system, heart and other tissues. It has been suggested that giving thyroid hormones to babies born early (preterm) might help them overcome problems of breathing and other organ immaturity. Two small randomised controlled studies have been conducted to assess the effects of thyroid hormone administration to preterm infants with respiratory distress syndrome. These studies did not find any reduction in the use of mechanical ventilation or length of ventilation; incidence of air leak; incidence of chronic lung disease at 28 days; patent ductus arteriosus; intraventricular haemorrhage or necrotising enterocolitis. There were also no differences in death rate to discharge.

Evidence from this review shows that non-absorbable disaccharides may be associated with a beneficial effect on mortality and hepatic encephalopathy. Furthermore, non-absorbable disaccharides may be associated with a beneficial effect on serious adverse events associated with liver diseases. No differences were found between lactulose and lactitol. The evidence is based on moderate quality evidence. The quality of the evidence was downgraded because of problems with the design of the RCTs. The preventive effect of non-absorbable disaccharides needs further evaluation. In general, the interventions were well tolerated, and non-absorbable disaccharides caused few side effects.

We found 65 studies which were eligible for inclusion in the review. Fifty-six of these studies included adults aged 18 years or older and nine included children and adolescents younger than 18 years. The number of study participants was 3598. We found that bisphosphonate therapy (a class of drugs commonly used to strengthen bones) given over 12 months may prevent fractures after kidney transplantation. However, it is also possible that bisphosphonates have little or no effect on reducing fractures. Whether or not any other drug treatments for bone disease after kidney transplantation prevent fractures was uncertain. None of the studies assessed the effects of bone therapies on death, heart disease, or kidney transplant failure. Bisphosphonates may decrease the number of cases of bone pain, but whether they prevent problems of bone tissue dying because of poor blood supply was very uncertain. Bisphosphonates may increase the risk of an abnormally low level of calcium in the blood. It was very uncertain whether vitamin D compounds had any effects on bone health or other important outcomes after kidney transplantation. For children and adolescents, there was only one small study (60 children, 37 of whom were given bisphosphonate therapy) providing very uncertain evidence on the effects of bone therapy. The certainty of the evidence was either low (for fracture and abnormal blood calcium levels) or very low (for all other outcomes). Low certainty evidence means that we are reasonably certain about the results. Very low certainty evidence means that we are very unsure of the results. Further research could change these assessments of certainty.

We searched scientific databases and trials registers to 30 November 2017. We found four randomised controlled trials that involved 317 participants. The trials were small, with a significant difference between them in terms of design, quality and the way that they were reported. We did not find any trials on the use of intravenous or intramuscular magnesium. Overall, we found no clear evidence that oral or intravenous magnesium is effective in the management of AWS. Further research is needed to determine the effectiveness of magnesium as a treatment for AWS.

This review includes 15 trials with 3057 participants. All included trials compared antibiotics with placebo (a substance which contains no medication) or no treatment. Participants were aged between 12 and 80 years, and most (70%) were women. Most studies were well designed, and we judged the overall quality of the included studies as adequate to high. In this review, we found that without antibiotics, 46% of people with rhinosinusitis were cured after 1 week and 64% after 14 days. With antibiotics, 51% were cured after 1 week and 71% after 14 days. So, antibiotics can shorten time to cure, but only 5 to 11 more people out of 100 will be cured faster with antibiotics instead of placebo or no treatment. Furthermore, 13 more people out of 100 experienced side effects with antibiotics compared to placebo or no treatment. Therefore, the potential benefit of antibiotics is very small compared to the risk of side effects. Moreover, three out of four people in the studies had a normal X-ray at the start of the study, so the results cannot be applied to people with more severe sinusitis. Two studies reported cultures of bacteria from nasal secretions before and during treatment. One study showed that antibiotics did not eradicate all bacteria, and another study showed that antibiotics changed the type of bacteria present. However, we do not know what this means for the patient. Antibiotic resistance is an issue, but in the few cases where it was reported, it did not lead to treatment failure. There was one case of a brain abscess (a serious complication) in a person who received open antibiotic therapy (i.e. not via a drip). The conclusion is that there is no place for antibiotics in the treatment of uncomplicated rhinosinusitis.

A total of eight trials including 2515 patients were included in this review. This review of trials found that both D1 and D2 are equally effective in terms of overall survival, but that D2 is more effective at preventing the spread of the disease. However, D2 is also associated with an increase in the number of deaths after surgery. The authors conclude that D2 lymphadenectomy can improve disease-free survival in patients with resectable gastric cancer, although the increased occurrence of postoperative mortality reduces its beneficial effects.

The aim of this review is to evaluate the efficacy of naltrexone in the maintenance of abstinence in previously detoxified opioid dependent people. The review includes 13 randomised controlled trials with a total of 1158 participants. No clear evidence of effectiveness of naltrexone in preventing relapse to opioid use and in maintaining abstinence from opioid drugs has been found. Further research is needed before it can be established if naltrexone is a useful intervention for the maintenance of abstinence in opioid dependence.

We searched the literature up to March 2016 and found two studies that investigated the effect of altering the consistency of fluids for people with dementia. These studies were part of a larger trial and one study invited only those who had already taken part in the first study. The first study looked at the immediate effect of the alteration of fluids in 351 people with dementia. Fluids were altered to regular consistency, to 'nectar thick' consistency, to 'honey thick' consistency and the individuals were given each fluid type in a random order. A chin tuck (whereby the individual is asked to stand with their head held down) was also tested. The second study then followed on from this to see what effect the use of nectar thick and honey thick fluids had after three months in 260 people with dementia. The conclusion of the review is that more research is needed to determine whether or not the alteration of the consistency of fluids is beneficial for people with dementia. It may be beneficial in the short term as it may improve the safety of eating and drinking but it may have the effect of causing constipation. This review does not consider the views of the individual with dementia; it is based solely on research. More research needs to be done before a clear answer can be given regarding the effects of altered fluids for people with dementia.

The evidence is current to January 2015. We included six randomised controlled trials with a total of 788 women. The largest trial had 396 women. The main outcome measured was live birth rate. Other outcomes included clinical pregnancy rate, ongoing pregnancy rate, multiple pregnancy rate, gestational abnormalities, ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) and treatment cancellation rate. For live birth, the evidence shows no clear difference between natural cycle IVF and standard IVF. With standard IVF, the chances of a live birth per cycle are typically around 50%. Using natural cycle IVF, the chances of a live birth per cycle ranged from 30% to 50%. For all other outcomes there was no clear difference between the treatments. However, the evidence was often imprecise. The largest trial has not yet been fully completed but over 95% of women have been followed up. More evidence from well conducted large trials is needed. The quality of the evidence was moderate for live birth and ongoing pregnancy, and was low or very low for all other outcomes.

A total of 984 participants from 12 studies (23 references) were analysed in this review. Only 811 participants who received either Tai Chi or were in the control group were included in the final analysis. The number of participants in each study ranged from 10 to 206, and average age ranged from 61 to 74 years. Duration of programmes ranged from six weeks to one year. No adverse events were reported. Three studies used methods that aimed to reduce bias, and six studies ensured that those assessing outcomes were unaware of group allocation. Half of the studies used an approach that ensured all participants were included in the analyses. Comparison of Tai Chi versus usual care revealed that Tai Chi improved some aspects of health, including the six-minute walk distance (a measure of exercise capacity), lung function and breathlessness, but it remains uncertain whether Tai Chi has an effect on quality of life. No differences were found when Tai Chi combined with another intervention was compared with the same combination of interventions, suggesting that Tai Chi did not add anything extra in terms of benefits. Furthermore, when individual components of Tai Chi were examined separately, they did not show any advantage over the same component without the Tai Chi context. This review provides evidence that taking part in a supervised and structured Tai Chi programme improves some aspects of health in people with COPD, but does not provide enough evidence of its complex nature and the diversity of existing practices. Future research needs to address these issues. Overall, the quality of evidence ranged from very low to moderate. The main reasons for downgrading the quality of evidence were small numbers of participants in the included studies, poor details about how the studies were performed and large differences between the studies.

This updated review included 72 studies with a total of 2470 participants. The average age of participants was 57 years. The degree of impairment ranged from people who had only mild difficulties carrying out everyday tasks to those with severe problems with movement and daily functioning. The length of the inclusion period for studies ranged from 2000-2012. We did not exclude studies based on funding sources; however, we noted that four studies declared some level of industrial sponsorship. The main comparison was between virtual reality and other types of therapy (usual care, sham therapy, etc.). We also compared types of virtual reality, including customised versus commercial games and differences in dosage (hours of therapy provided). We did not include self-exercise at home using virtual reality devices. Overall there is low-quality evidence that virtual reality is no more effective than other therapies in improving arm function. However, when virtual reality was used in addition to usual care, it resulted in a larger improvement in arm function than usual care alone. We found that virtual reality has an important place in the rehabilitation of individuals with stroke but its role is not yet clear. It is necessary for more research to establish the best way(s) to provide virtual reality, especially in terms of dosage and type of device. More information is needed regarding the best ways to assess outcomes, particularly for activities of daily living, cognition, and participation. Adverse events were rare and were related to the type of device used. They included eye strain, headaches, and pain. Further research is required to determine the potential for serious harm from the use of virtual reality. The quality of the evidence for each outcome was low. Many studies had flaws in their design or how they were conducted. There were just a limited number of studies for some outcomes, often with small numbers of participants.

We found five randomised controlled trials, enrolling 1330 people with tuberculosis, that compared fluoroquinolone antibiotics (ofloxacin, levofloxacin, moxifloxacin, or gatifloxacin) with other antibiotics (ethambutol, isoniazid, or both) as part of the standard treatment for pulmonary tuberculosis. Single trials compared adding fluoroquinolone to the standard treatment or substituting a fluoroquinolone for one of the other antibiotics. The evidence is current to March 2017. Adding fluoroquinolone to the standard treatment did not result in any additional side effects but we are unsure if it had an effect on death, treatment failure, or relapse. Substituting a fluoroquinolone for ethambutol or isoniazid may have little or no effect on death, treatment failure, or relapse, and we are also unsure about its effect on improving the bacteria tests at eight weeks. We rated the evidence as very low quality. Larger studies are currently being done testing fluoroquinolones in treatments of only four months.
